topics.title: Introduction to Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mobile Robot** is an autonomous or semi-autonomous mechanical system equipped with sensors, actuators, a controller (computing unit), and a power supply, capable of locomotion through its environment without being fixed to a single physical location. Mobile robots are classified as a sub-field of **Robotics and Autonomous Systems (RAS)**, formally described in ISO 8373:2021 as *"an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes."*

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT866 — Underwater Robotics), a *mobile robot* is any robotic platform that can translate (and often rotate) freely through its operational medium. Mobile robots are broadly categorized by the medium they operate in:

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Classification of Mobile Robots by Operating Medium
> - **Terrestrial / Ground Mobile Robots (UGV)** – Unmanned Ground Vehicles operating on land (e.g., wheeled, legged, tracked).
> - **Aerial Mobile Robots (UAV / Drones)** – Unmanned Aerial Vehicles operating in atmospheric airspace.
> - **Surface Mobile Robots (USV)** – Unmanned Surface Vehicles operating on water surfaces.
> - **Underwater Mobile Robots (UUV / ROV / AUV)** – Unmanned Underwater Vehicles operating in submerged environments. This is the central focus of PERAT866.

The course PERAT866 — *Under Water Robotics* — falls under the broader umbrella of mobile robotics, since Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) and Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) are essentially **underwater mobile robots** designed to navigate the aquatic domain.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you have a remote-controlled toy car. It has:
- **Wheels (locomotion)** — the "legs" that allow it to move.
- **A battery (power)** — the "stomach" giving it energy.
- **A small chip inside (controller)** — the "brain" telling it what to do.
- **Sensors (e.g., a camera, an ultrasonic sensor)** — the "eyes and ears" helping it perceive the world.

A mobile robot is essentially this toy car — but smarter. It is *autonomous* (makes its own decisions using pre-programmed logic and sensor feedback), or *tele-operated* (controlled remotely by a human). When we place such a robot in **water** instead of on land, it becomes an underwater mobile robot.

> [!NOTE] The Three Universal Capabilities of Any Mobile Robot
> 1. **SENSE** — Perceive its environment using onboard sensors (cameras, sonar, IMU, depth sensors).
> 2. **THINK** — Process sensor data via embedded controllers (microcontrollers, FPGAs, onboard PCs).
> 3. **ACT** — Execute motion commands through actuators (thrusters, wheels, fins, manipulators).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Mobile Robotics

The following standard metrics are universally used in mobile robotics literature and are essential KTU terminology:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — The number of independent motions a robot can perform. A rigid body in 3D space has 6 DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations). A differential-drive ground robot has 3 DOF $(x, y, \theta)$ in 2D.
- **Payload Capacity** — The maximum additional mass (in **kg** or **N**) the robot can carry beyond its own structural weight.
- **Operational Range / Endurance** — Maximum mission duration (in **hours**) or distance (in **km**) on a single charge.
- **Maximum Operating Depth (MOD)** — The deepest depth (in **meters**) at which an underwater robot can safely operate. Commercial ROVs: $\sim 300$ m; deep-sea AUVs: $> 6000$ m.
- **Degrees of Autonomy** — From *tele-operated* (Level 0) to *fully autonomous* (Level 5), as per the Autonomy Levels for Unmanned Systems (ALFUS) framework.
- **Localization Accuracy** — The precision (in **meters** or **radians**) with which the robot estimates its pose (position + orientation) in a global reference frame.

> [!TIP] KTU Exam Tip
> Examiners often expect students to differentiate clearly between **autonomous** and **tele-operated** robots, and between the four classes (UGV, UAV, USV, UUV). Memorize the 6-DOF rigid body property — it is a **high-yield** board question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Architecture of a Mobile Robot

Every mobile robot, irrespective of the medium it operates in, consists of the same five core subsystems. Understanding these is critical for KTU Module 1 questions.

### 2.1.1 Locomotion Subsystem
The mechanism by which the robot physically moves. Selection depends on the operational medium and terrain.

- **Terrestrial**: wheels (differential drive, Ackermann steering, omnidirectional), tracks, legs (bipedal, quadrupedal, hexapodal).
- **Aerial**: fixed wings, rotary wings (quadrotor, hexacopter), flapping wings.
- **Surface**: propellers, water jets, sails.
- **Underwater**: thrusters (ducted, open-frame), buoyancy-driven gliders, biomimetic fins (fish-like).

### 2.1.2 Perception Subsystem (Sensors)
Sensors convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.

- **Proprioceptive sensors** — measure the robot's *internal* state (battery voltage, wheel encoders, IMU — Inertial Measurement Unit).
- **Exteroceptive sensors** — measure the *external* environment (cameras, LiDAR, sonar, GPS, pressure/depth sensors).
- Underwater robots *cannot* use GPS (electromagnetic waves attenuate rapidly in water), so they rely on **Doppler Velocity Logs (DVL)**, **inertial navigation**, and **acoustic positioning (LBL, USBL)**.

### 2.1.3 Cognition / Control Subsystem
The "brain" of the robot. Architectures range from simple reactive (subsumption) to deliberative (sense–plan–act) to hybrid.

- **Reactive control**: Direct sensor-to-actuator mapping. Fast but no memory (e.g., Braitenberg vehicles).
- **Deliberative control**: Plan → Execute. Slow but globally optimal.
- **Hybrid control**: Combines both (e.g., the *three-layer architecture*: reactive / executive / deliberative).

### 2.1.4 Communication Subsystem
- **Terrestrial/Aerial**: Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), 4G/5G, radio (RF).
- **Underwater**: **Acoustic modems** (since RF attenuates in water). Data rates typically $< 100$ kbps, latency in **seconds**.

### 2.1.5 Power Subsystem
- **Terrestrial**: Lithium-ion battery packs.
- **Aerial**: High energy-density Li-Po batteries.
- **Underwater**: Rechargeable Li-ion banks, sometimes supplemented by surface tethers.

## 2.2 Locomotion Principle: How Mobile Robots Move

For a mobile robot to move, it must overcome **resistive forces** in its medium. Newton's second law governs all locomotion:

$$F_{propulsion} - F_{resistive} = m \cdot a$$

For ground robots: $F_{resistive}$ includes rolling friction and aerodynamic drag.
For underwater robots: $F_{resistive}$ is dominated by **hydrodynamic drag**:

$$F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of fluid (**$1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for seawater**)
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless, $\sim 0.5$ to $1.0$ for typical AUV hulls)
- $A$ = frontal cross-sectional area (in m$^2$)
- $v$ = relative velocity (in m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT] Engineering Insight
> The dominant $v^2$ dependence explains why underwater robots are *very* drag-sensitive and why most AUVs are designed with streamlined, torpedo-shaped hulls and operate at slow speeds (1–3 m/s).

## 2.3 Coordinate Frames and Robot Pose

A mobile robot's pose (configuration) is described relative to a global reference frame $\mathcal{F}_W$ (world frame). For a planar ground robot:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x, y)$ is the position (in **meters**) and $\theta$ is the heading angle (in **radians**), measured counter-clockwise from the x-axis of $\mathcal{F}_W$.

For a 3D underwater robot, the pose is **6-dimensional** (pose = position + orientation):

$$\boldsymbol{\eta} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{p} \\ \boldsymbol{\Theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ \phi \\ \theta \\ \psi \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\boldsymbol{p} = (x, y, z)^T$ is position and $\boldsymbol{\Theta} = (\phi, \theta, \psi)^T$ is roll–pitch–yaw (orientation expressed as Euler angles).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all the **high-yield equations and constants** required for Module 1 questions.

| Concept / Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rigid-body DOF (3D) | $\text{DOF} = 6$ | dimensionless | 3 translations + 3 rotations |
| Planar robot DOF | $\text{DOF} = 3 \;(x, y, \theta)$ | dimensionless | Ground robots in 2D plane |
| Drag force (quadratic) | $F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ | N (Newton) | Dominant in underwater motion |
| Drag force (linear / Stokes) | $F_{drag} = 6\pi\mu r v$ | N | For very low Reynolds number flow |
| Newton's 2nd law | $F = m a$ | N | Foundation of robot dynamics |
| Buoyancy force | $F_b = \rho g V$ | N | Must equal weight for neutral buoyancy |
| Hydrostatic pressure | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa | $P_0$ = atmospheric pressure |
| Seawater density | $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ | kg/m$^3$ | Standard KTU value |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g = 9.81$ | m/s$^2$ | Universal constant |
| Robot pose (2D) | $q = (x, y, \theta)^T$ | m, m, rad | Planar configuration |
| Robot pose (3D) | $\boldsymbol{\eta} = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)^T$ | m, rad | 6-DOF configuration |
| Homogeneous transform | $T \in SE(3)$ | $4 \times 4$ matrix | Special Euclidean group |
| Rotation matrix 2D | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | dimensionless | 2D orientation |

> [!CAUTION] LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance
> In all KTU examinations, when you write $|x|$, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in your LaTeX. The unescaped pipe `$\vert x \vert$` is safe; the `$\vert x \vert$` in plain markdown tables can break the table layout.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mobile robots have transformed multiple engineering sectors:

- **Marine/Underwater**: Pipeline inspection, coral reef mapping, mine countermeasures, oceanographic data collection, search-and-recovery (e.g., the *Titan* search).
- **Terrestrial**: Warehouse automation (Amazon Kiva robots), planetary exploration (NASA's *Perseverance* rover, 6-DOF rocker-bogie suspension), agricultural automation.
- **Aerial**: Aerial surveying, last-mile delivery, military reconnaissance, precision agriculture.
- **Medical/Service**: Surgical robots (Da Vinci system), assistive robots, domestic vacuum cleaners (Roomba).

The **design and control of underwater mobile robots (AUVs/ROVs)** is the most challenging branch of mobile robotics because the underwater environment is *GPS-denied, optically murky, acoustically noisy*, and *high-pressure*, demanding robust autonomy and pressure-tolerant hardware.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since Module 1 is *introductory*, the primary mathematical content involves: (i) deriving the kinematic model of a differential-drive mobile robot, and (ii) deriving the buoyancy / hydrostatic equations used in underwater robotics. Both are foundational for the rest of PERAT866.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kinematic Model of a Differential-Drive Mobile Robot

A differential-drive robot has two independently driven wheels on a common axis. The robot's pose in the world frame is $q = (x, y, \theta)^T$. Let $v$ be the linear velocity of the robot's center and $\omega$ be the angular velocity.

**Step 1**: Write the position of the robot as a function of time:

$$\boldsymbol{p}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \theta(t) = \text{orientation}$$

**Step 2**: Express the velocity in the *body frame* of the robot. The body frame is rotated by angle $\theta$ relative to the world frame. The rotation matrix from body to world is:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3**: The robot moves forward along its local x-axis with linear velocity $v$ and rotates with angular velocity $\omega$. The velocity vector in the body frame is $(v, 0, \omega)^T$.

**Step 4**: Transform the body-frame linear velocity into the world frame by applying $R(\theta)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = R(\theta) \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5**: The angular velocity is invariant under rotation (rotations about the vertical axis commute with world-frame yaw):

$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**Step 6**: Combine the three equations into the standard differential-drive forward kinematics:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7**: Express in terms of the two wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ (with wheel radius $r$ and wheelbase $b$):

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}$$

**Step 8**: Substitute back into the kinematic model:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= \frac{r}{2}\left(\omega_R + \omega_L\right)\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= \frac{r}{2}\left(\omega_R + \omega_L\right)\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{r}{b}\left(\omega_R - \omega_L\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **complete kinematic model** of a differential-drive mobile robot and is the foundation for trajectory-tracking and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) algorithms.

> [!NOTE] Why This Matters for PERAT866
> The same kinematic structure (forward velocity + angular velocity = pose update) is used to describe *underwater* thruster-driven vehicles, where the thrusters replace the wheels. The math is identical.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Buoyancy and Hydrostatic Pressure for Underwater Robots

**Step 1**: State Archimedes' Principle. An object submerged in a fluid experiences an upward **buoyant force** equal to the weight of the displaced fluid:

$$F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**$1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$** for seawater)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**)
- $V_{displaced}$ = volume of fluid displaced (in m$^3$)

**Step 2**: State the weight of the robot:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 3**: Compare $F_b$ and $W$ to determine the *buoyancy state*:

- $F_b > W \Rightarrow$ **positively buoyant** (robot floats up).
- $F_b = W \Rightarrow$ **neutrally buoyant** (robot hovers in place) — *ideal* for AUVs.
- $F_b < W \Rightarrow$ **negatively buoyant** (robot sinks).

**Step 4**: For the hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ below the surface, the pressure increases linearly with depth:

$$P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h$$

where $P_0$ is the atmospheric pressure at the surface ($\approx 101325$ Pa).

**Step 5**: Example numerical evaluation — at the average ocean depth of the Mariana Trench ($\sim 11000$ m):

$$\begin{aligned}
P(11000) &= 101325 + (1025)(9.81)(11000) \\
&= 101325 + 110,683,275 \\
&\approx 1.108 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \approx 1108 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

This is why deep-sea AUVs require *pressure-tolerant* hulls (typically titanium spheres).

> [!TIP] Common Exam Numerical
> A common KTU problem: *"Compute the buoyancy force on an AUV displacing 0.05 m$^3$ of seawater."* Answer:
> $F_b = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 502.76 \text{ N}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Mobile Robot Types

| Property | UGV (Ground) | UAV (Aerial) | USV (Surface) | UUV (Underwater) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating medium | Solid terrain | Air | Air–water interface | Submerged water |
| Primary locomotion | Wheels / tracks / legs | Propellers / rotors | Propellers / water jets | Thrusters / gliders |
| Typical max speed | 0.5–20 m/s | 5–100 m/s | 2–15 m/s | 0.5–5 m/s |
| Dominant resistive force | Rolling friction | Aerodynamic drag | Wave drag | Hydrodynamic drag |
| GPS availability | Yes | Yes | Yes (at surface) | No (GPS-denied) |
| Communication | Wi-Fi / RF | Wi-Fi / RF | Wi-Fi / RF | Acoustic (slow) |
| Autonomy challenge | Obstacle avoidance | Wind gusts | Wave motion | Localization, pressure |
| KTU example | Mars Rover | Quadcopter drone | Wave Glider | REMUS AUV |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Mobile Robot

Below is a Mermaid block diagram showing the five core subsystems of a mobile robot and their interconnections. Notice that all five subsystems are interdependent and feed back into the controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[POWER SUBSYSTEM\nBattery / Energy Source] -->|Electrical energy| B[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM\nMicrocontroller / SBC]
    C[PERCEPTION SUBSYSTEM\nSensors: Camera, IMU, Sonar] -->|Sensor data| B
    B -->|Control signals PWM UART CAN| D[ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM\nMotors / Thrusters / Wheels]
    D -->|Motion commands| E[LOCOMOTION MECHANISM\nWheels / Propellers / Fins]
    E -->|Physical interaction| F[OPERATIONAL ENVIRONMENT\nLand / Air / Water]
    F -->|Feedback stimuli| C
    B <-->|Mission data telemetry| G[COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM\nWiFi Radio Acoustic]
    G <-->|Operator commands status| H[HUMAN OPERATOR\nSupervisory Control]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Mobile Robot Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MR[MOBILE ROBOTS]
    MR --> UGV[UGV\nUnmanned Ground Vehicle]
    MR --> UAV[UAV\nUnmanned Aerial Vehicle]
    MR --> USV[USV\nUnmanned Surface Vehicle]
    MR --> UUV[UUV\nUnmanned Underwater Vehicle]
    UGV --> W[Wheels]
    UGV --> T[Tracks]
    UGV --> L[Legs]
    UAV --> FW[Fixed Wing]
    UAV --> RW[Rotary Wing]
    UAV --> FW2[Flapping Wing]
    USV --> PROP[Propeller]
    USV --> JET[Water Jet]
    UUV --> ROV[ROV Tethered]
    UUV --> AUV[AUV Autonomous]
    UUV --> GLIDER[Buoyancy Glider]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Sense–Think–Act Feedback Loop

This is the canonical control loop used in every mobile robot, from a Roomba vacuum cleaner to a deep-sea AUV.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[SENSE\nAcquire sensor data] --> T1[THINK\nProcess and decide]
    T1 --> A1[ACT\nSend actuator commands]
    A1 --> ENV[ENVIRONMENT\nPhysical world response]
    ENV --> S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT] Interpretation for KTU
> The cycle *Sense → Think → Act → Environment → Sense* is a *closed-loop* feedback system. In control theory, it is equivalent to a discrete-time feedback controller with the robot as the *plant* and the controller in the *feedback path*.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Underwater Robot (AUV) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSE["SENSE LAYER"]
        IMU[IMU Accel Gyro]
        DVL[Doppler Velocity Log]
        DEPTH[Depth Pressure Sensor]
        SONAR[Side Scan Sonar]
    end
    subgraph THINK["THINK LAYER"]
        NAV[Navigation Filter EKF]
        PLANNER[Mission Planner]
        CTRL[Low Level Controller PID]
    end
    subgraph ACT["ACT LAYER"]
        THR[Thruster Drivers]
        FINS[Control Surfaces Fins]
    end
    IMU --> NAV
    DVL --> NAV
    DEPTH --> NAV
    SONAR --> PLANNER
    PLANNER --> NAV
    NAV --> CTRL
    CTRL --> THR
    CTRL --> FINS
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a mobile robot. List any four major components of a mobile robot.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A mobile robot is a programmable mechanical device capable of *autonomous or semi-autonomous locomotion* in its environment, equipped with sensors, a controller, actuators, a power source, and a communication interface (2 marks for definition).
**Four major components** (1 mark — ¼ mark each):
1. Locomotion mechanism (wheels, thrusters, legs).
2. Sensors (cameras, IMU, sonar).
3. Controller (microcontroller / embedded PC).
4. Power supply (battery / tether).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Briefly differentiate between a ROV and an AUV.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle) | AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle) |
|---|---|---|
| Power | Supplied via surface tether | Onboard battery |
| Control | Real-time human tele-operation | Fully autonomous, pre-programmed |
| Communication | Continuous tether (fiber-optic) | Acoustic modem (intermittent) |
| Endurance | Unlimited (tethered) | Limited (battery hours) |
| Depth capability | Very deep (e.g., 6500 m+) | Limited by battery pressure hull |

(2 marks for correct contrast, 1 mark for valid example or depth mention.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major subsystems of a mobile robot. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheelbase $b = 0.30$ m, and the two wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s. Determine the linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot. Also compute the pose update $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta\theta)$ over a time step $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, assuming the robot starts at $(0, 0, 0)$. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Subsystems of a Mobile Robot (7 marks):**

A mobile robot consists of **five** major subsystems (1 mark each, 5 marks; plus 2 marks for the block diagram):

1. **Locomotion Subsystem** — Provides mobility. For ground robots: wheels, tracks, legs. For aerial: propellers, rotors. For underwater: thrusters, fins.

2. **Sensors / Perception Subsystem** — Provides information about the internal state (proprioceptive: IMU, encoders) and external environment (exteroceptive: cameras, sonar, LiDAR).

3. **Controller / Cognition Subsystem** — The "brain". Receives sensor data, processes it using algorithms (SLAM, path planning, PID), and issues actuator commands. Can be reactive, deliberative, or hybrid.

4. **Actuator / Drive Subsystem** — Converts electrical signals into mechanical motion (DC motors, servo motors, thrusters, hydraulic actuators).

5. **Communication Subsystem** — Exchanges data with the operator. Wi-Fi/RF for land/air; acoustic modems underwater.

**Block Diagram** (2 marks): A standard block diagram with sensors → controller → actuators → environment → feedback to sensors (Sense–Think–Act loop, as in Section 4.3 above).

**Part (b) — Kinematic Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1**: Linear velocity $v$ (1 mark):
$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (6 + 4)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 10}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2**: Angular velocity $\omega$ (1 mark):
$$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b} = \frac{0.05 \times (6 - 4)}{0.30} = \frac{0.10}{0.30} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3**: Pose update for $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, starting pose $\theta_0 = 0$ (1 mark):
$$\Delta x = v \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t = 0.25 \times 1 \times 0.1 = 0.025 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta y = v \sin\theta \cdot \Delta t = 0.25 \times 0 \times 0.1 = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta \theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0.333 \times 0.1 = 0.0333 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4**: New pose (1 mark):
$$(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) = (0.025, 0, 0.0333)$$

**Step 5**: Bonus (2 marks) — Verify the dimensions and the sign convention. Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the robot turns *left* (counter-clockwise positive in standard convention), which matches the positive $\Delta\theta$. The result is dimensionally consistent.

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Valuation Pitfall
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks on):**
> 1. **Forgetting to halve** the sum in $v$ — they write $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ instead of $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Mixing up $r$ and $b$** — confusing wheel radius with wheelbase. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not specifying units** in the final answer. (-0.5 mark)
> 4. **Assuming the robot moves in a curve** even for very small $\Delta t$ — the linear approximation $\Delta x \approx v \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t$ is only valid for short steps. (-0.5 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the Sense–Think–Act paradigm in mobile robotics with a suitable block diagram. Why is feedback important? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An AUV displaces a volume of 0.08 m$^3$ of seawater. Compute (i) the buoyant force on the AUV, and (ii) the hydrostatic pressure at a depth of 500 m. Assume the AUV has a mass of 90 kg. Is the AUV positively, neutrally, or negatively buoyant? Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m$^3$, $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$, $P_0 = 101325$ Pa. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Sense–Think–Act Paradigm (7 marks):**

The Sense–Think–Act paradigm is a **3-stage feedback control model** that describes the operational cycle of any mobile robot (2 marks for paradigm description).

- **Sense**: The robot uses onboard sensors (cameras, IMU, sonar, encoders) to acquire raw data about itself and its environment (1.5 marks).
- **Think**: The onboard controller processes sensor data, fuses it with prior knowledge (via SLAM, Kalman filters, neural networks), and decides on the next action (1.5 marks).
- **Act**: The controller issues commands to the actuators (motors, thrusters, steering), which produce physical motion in the environment (1 mark).
- **Feedback** (1 mark): The cycle then repeats. Feedback is critical because (a) the environment is dynamic and uncertain, (b) actuators have errors and disturbances, and (c) without feedback, the robot would be "open-loop" and unable to correct for drift or unexpected events.

**Block Diagram**: A closed-loop block diagram as in Section 4.3 above (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Buoyancy and Pressure Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step (i)** — Buoyant force (2 marks):
$$F_b = \rho g V = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.08 = 804.42 \text{ N}$$

**Step (ii)** — Hydrostatic pressure at 500 m (2 marks):
$$P = P_0 + \rho g h = 101325 + (1025)(9.81)(500)$$
$$P = 101325 + 5,027,625 = 5,128,950 \text{ Pa} \approx 51.29 \text{ bar}$$

**Step (iii)** — Buoyancy state (3 marks):
Weight of AUV:
$$W = mg = 90 \times 9.81 = 882.9 \text{ N}$$

Compare:
$$F_b = 804.42 \text{ N} \quad \text{vs} \quad W = 882.9 \text{ N}$$

Since $F_b < W$ ($804.42 < 882.9$), the AUV is **negatively buoyant** — it will sink if thrusters are turned off.

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Valuation Pitfall
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — particularly leaving $h$ in km instead of m, or $P_0$ in bar instead of Pa. The answer will be off by a factor of 1000. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong conclusion on buoyancy** — students often say "neutral" if $F_b \approx W$. The correct comparison is *strictly* $F_b < W$ or $F_b > W$. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not stating Archimedes' Principle** before computing $F_b$ — examiners want the principle quoted. (-0.5 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist
> - **Mobile robot definition**: an autonomous / semi-autonomous machine that moves through its environment, comprising sensors, controller, actuators, power, and communication.
> - **Four major classes** by medium: **UGV, UAV, USV, UUV** (the focus of PERAT866 is UUV).
> - **6 DOF** for a free rigid body in 3D $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ — 3 translations + 3 rotations.
> - **3 DOF** for a planar mobile robot $(x, y, \theta)$.
> - **Rigid-body 2D rotation matrix**: $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$.
> - **Differential-drive kinematics**: $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$, $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}$.
> - **Hydrodynamic drag**: $F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ (dominant in underwater motion).
> - **Archimedes' Principle**: $F_b = \rho g V$; neutrally buoyant when $F_b = W = mg$.
> - **Seawater density**: $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m$^3$ (memorize).
> - **Standard gravity**: $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$ (memorize).
> - **Hydrostatic pressure**: $P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h$.
> - **Underwater environment is GPS-denied** — use DVL, IMU, acoustic positioning (LBL/USBL).
> - **Sense–Think–Act** is the canonical paradigm; it is a *closed-loop* feedback system.
> - **Communication underwater**: acoustic modems (slow, $\sim$kbps), not RF.
> - **Pose update formula** (kinematic model): $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.

> [!NOTE] Examiner's Final Word
> For Module 1 of PERAT866, the KTU board values **conceptual clarity over numerical complexity**. Make sure you can draw a clean block diagram of a mobile robot, name all five subsystems, and derive the differential-drive kinematics from first principles. Numerical problems are usually simple (one-step) and test your understanding of the kinematic and buoyancy equations rather than your computational speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Locomotion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Locomotion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Underwater Locomotion** refers to the engineered principles and physical mechanisms that enable an autonomous or tethered underwater vehicle (AUV/ROV) to generate controlled motion through a fluid medium. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Underwater Robotics (PERAT866)*, the principle of locomotion is studied as the **conversion of actuator input energy (electrical/hydraulic/mechanical) into controlled thrust and torque**, regulated against the multi-directional resistive forces of the aquatic environment.

The locomotion problem in water is fundamentally different from terrestrial or aerial locomotion because water is a dense, viscous, and incompressible fluid characterized by Reynolds numbers typically in the range of $10^4$ to $10^7$ for mid-sized robotic platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> The principle of locomotion forms the *first foundational pillar* of underwater robotics. Mastery of thrust generation, drag characterization, and bio-inspired fin mechanics is mandatory before progressing to control, navigation, and dynamic positioning modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are swimming in a crowded swimming pool. To move forward, you have two intuitive options:

- **Option A (Drag-based):** You push water backward with your palms. The water resists being pushed (this resistance is "drag"), and by Newton's Third Law, the water pushes you forward. This is how **paddle wheels and slow-moving robots** move.

- **Option B (Lift-based):** You move your hand in a side-to-side sculling motion (like a fish's tail). The angle of your hand creates a "lift" perpendicular to motion, which has a forward component — similar to how an airplane wing works in water. This is how **fast fish, whales, and submarine propellers** move.

A third, increasingly important method is **undulatory/oscillatory propulsion**, where a flexible body (like a sea snake or eel) sends a travelling wave down its body to "push" water backwards. Bio-inspired AUVs mimic this using compliant fins or flexible spines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:**
> *Locomotion = Energy Conversion (Actuator Power) → Mechanical Thrust → Net Forward Motion (after overcoming drag).*

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and dimensional parameters govern every underwater locomotion calculation:

- **Density of seawater:** $\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at the surface, 4°C warmer oceans ≈ 1027 kg/m³)
- **Density of freshwater:** $\rho \approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C:** $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity (submerged):** $g \approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (used for buoyancy stability)
- **Speed of sound in water:** $c \approx 1500 \text{ m/s}$ (relevant for high-speed cavitation studies)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Underwater Locomotion

Underwater robotic locomotion can be systematically classified into **four primary regimes**, each defined by the dominant physics at play:

### 2.1.1 Drag-Based (Reactive) Propulsion
- The actuator pushes a volume of water in a direction opposite to the desired motion.
- Thrust is generated purely from the **momentum change** of the accelerated fluid mass.
- Examples: paddle wheels, oscillating plates, single-jointed flippers.
- Dominant when **Reynolds number (Re)** is moderate and propulsive surfaces are perpendicular to motion.
- Mathematically: $F_T = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta v$ where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate of accelerated water.

### 2.1.2 Lift-Based (Aerodynamic/Hydrodynamic) Propulsion
- A foil angled at an **angle of attack ($\alpha$)** moving through water produces a lift force perpendicular to relative flow.
- This lift is decomposed into a *forward thrust component* and a *lateral side-force component*.
- Examples: submarine propellers, ducted thrusters, oscillating foils with optimal pitch.
- Dominant at **higher Reynolds numbers** and high cruising speeds.

### 2.1.3 Undulatory (Body Wave) Propulsion
- A sinusoidal travelling wave propagates down the body or fin of the vehicle.
- Position of midline: $y(x,t) = a(x)\sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$ where $a(x)$ is the amplitude envelope, $k$ is wavenumber, $\omega$ is angular frequency.
- Examples: lamprey robots, eel-inspired AUVs, knifefish gliders.

### 2.1.4 Oscillatory (Median and Paired Fin) Propulsion
- Rigid or semi-rigid fins oscillate with controlled heave and pitch.
- Two canonical motions: **heaving** (translation normal to flow) and **pitching** (rotation about spanwise axis).
- Examples: manta-ray robots, labriform (lobe-finned) swimmers, bluegill-inspired platforms.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Locomotion Principle

| Principle | Why It Works | How It Is Implemented in Robots |
|-----------|--------------|----------------------------------|
| Drag-based | Newton's 3rd Law: rearward fluid impulse → forward reaction | Paddle wheels, single-DOF flippers, vectored jets |
| Lift-based | Pressure differential across angled foil creates orthogonal force | Propellers, ducted thrusters, controllable-pitch rotors |
| Undulatory | Travelling wave creates continuous rearward momentum flux | Multi-jointed spine with phase-lagged joint angles |
| Oscillatory | Combined heave + pitch replicates optimal fish-fin kinematics | Servo-driven fins with prescribed heave-pitch coupling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Most operational AUVs use a **hybrid approach** — a main propeller (lift-based) for efficient cruise + auxiliary thrusters (drag-based jets) for low-speed manoeuvring and station-keeping.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every governing equation, dimensionless number, and boundary condition required to solve Module 1 locomotion problems. **All absolute values and conditionals are written with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Notes / Validity Range |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \dfrac{vL}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | $Re < 10^5$ for typical AUVs |
| 2 | Hydrodynamic Drag Force | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, v^2\, C_d\, A$ | N | $C_d$ depends on Re and body shape |
| 3 | Lift on a Foil | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, v^2\, S\, C_L(\alpha)$ | N | $S$ = planform area, $\alpha$ = AoA |
| 4 | Thrust from Momentum Change | $F_T = \dot{m}\,(v_e - v_0)$ | N | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate |
| 5 | Propeller Advance Coefficient | $J = \dfrac{v_A}{nD}$ | dimensionless | $v_A$ = advance speed, $n$ = rps, $D$ = diameter |
| 6 | Thrust Coefficient | $C_T = \dfrac{F_T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | dimensionless | Used in open-water propellers |
| 7 | Torque Coefficient | $C_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | dimensionless | Companion to $C_T$ |
| 8 | Propulsive Efficiency (Quasi-steady) | $\eta_p = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 + C_T}}$ | dimensionless | Rankine-Froude actuator disk |
| 9 | Froude Propulsion Efficiency | $\eta_F = \dfrac{C_T^{3/2}}{C_Q}$ | dimensionless | Open-water test efficiency |
| 10 | Total Efficiency | $\eta_0 = \eta_F \cdot \eta_R$ | dimensionless | $\eta_R$ = rotational (mechanical) efficiency |
| 11 | Strouhal Number | $St = \dfrac{f A}{v}$ | dimensionless | Oscillatory fin wake characterisation |
| 12 | Added Mass Coefficient | $m_a = k \cdot \rho \nabla$ | kg | $k \approx 0.5$ for sphere, $\nabla$ = displaced volume |
| 13 | Buoyancy Force | $F_B = \rho_{water}\, g\, \nabla$ | N | $\nabla$ = displaced volume of robot |
| 14 | Net Vertical Force | $F_{net} = F_B - W$ | N | $W$ = submerged weight |
| 15 | Specific Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{F_d}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 \nabla^{2/3}}$ | dimensionless | Hull-form efficiency metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU 2024:** Always carry forward **units** in derivations. Marks are routinely deducted for missing SI units in numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above directly govern:

- **Hull design** of AUVs (streamlined bodies to minimize $C_d$ at cruise Re).
- **Thruster selection** (main propulsion vs. manoeuvring).
- **Endurance estimation** ($E_{battery} / P_{drag}$ gives range).
- **Bio-inspired robot design** (the MIT RoboTuna, UCI Marya, Essex lamprey-bot all derive from these formulas).
- **Mission planning** — turning radius, dive rate, and depth-holding capability all stem from thrust-drag balancing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reynolds Number from Dimensional Analysis

The Reynolds number is the single most important dimensionless group in underwater locomotion. We derive it from the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in the Navier-Stokes equations.

**Step 1 — Start with the Navier-Stokes momentum equation for incompressible flow:**

$$\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla \mathbf{v} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{f}_{body}$$

Here, $\rho$ is fluid density, $\mathbf{v}$ is the velocity vector, $p$ is pressure, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\mathbf{f}_{body}$ represents body forces per unit volume.

**Step 2 — Non-dimensionalize the equation.** Choose characteristic length $L$, characteristic velocity $V$, and time scale $T = L/V$. Define dimensionless variables (denoted by tildes):

$$\mathbf{v} = V \tilde{\mathbf{v}}, \quad \mathbf{x} = L \tilde{\mathbf{x}}, \quad t = \dfrac{L}{V}\tilde{t}, \quad p = \rho V^2 \tilde{p}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the dimensionless variables into the convective term:**

$$\rho \mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla \mathbf{v} \rightarrow \rho\,\dfrac{V^2}{L}\,(\tilde{\mathbf{v}}\cdot\tilde{\nabla}\tilde{\mathbf{v}})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the viscous term:**

$$\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} \rightarrow \mu\,\dfrac{V}{L^2}\,\tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{v}}$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio of inertial to viscous terms (the Reynolds number):**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** When $Re \gg 1$, inertial forces dominate → **turbulent flow regime** (typical of full-scale AUVs). When $Re \ll 1$, viscous forces dominate → **creeping flow** (typical of micro-swimmers like sperm-cell-inspired robots). This dimensionless ratio dictates whether lift-based or drag-based propulsion is more efficient.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Froude Propulsive Efficiency from Actuator Disk Theory

This is the **classic KTU-style 14-mark derivation** for thrust efficiency. We use the Rankine-Froude actuator disk model.

**Step 1 — Define the actuator disk.** Consider an ideal propeller as an infinitely thin disk of area $A_e$ that imparts a uniform axial velocity increment $\Delta v$ to the fluid passing through it. The far-upstream velocity is $v_0$, the far-downstream (slipstream) velocity is $v_2$, and the velocity at the disk is $v_1$.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass:**

$$\rho A_e v_1 = \rho A_0 v_0 = \rho A_2 v_2 = \dot{m}$$

Here, $A_0$ and $A_2$ are the upstream and downstream flow cross-sections, which may differ due to streamtube contraction.

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of momentum in the axial direction. The thrust equals the rate of momentum change:**

$$F_T = \dot{m}(v_2 - v_0)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bernoulli's equation along the outer boundary of the slipstream (where pressure recovers to ambient $p_\infty$):**

$$p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_0^2 = p_{disk,up} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = p_{disk,down} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

**Step 5 — The thrust is also the pressure jump across the disk times its area:**

$$F_T = (p_{disk,down} - p_{disk,up})\,A_e$$

**Step 6 — Using Bernoulli twice and subtracting:**

$$F_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^{\,2} - v_0^{\,2})\,A_e$$

**Step 7 — Equate the two expressions for thrust and solve for $v_1$:**

$$\dot{m}(v_2 - v_0) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^{\,2} - v_0^{\,2})\,A_e$$

Using $\dot{m} = \rho A_e v_1$ and the identity $v_2^{\,2} - v_0^{\,2} = (v_2 - v_0)(v_2 + v_0)$:

$$\rho A_e v_1 (v_2 - v_0) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_e (v_2 - v_0)(v_2 + v_0)$$

$$v_1 = \dfrac{v_0 + v_2}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Define the axial induction factors. Let $v_a$ = far-wake axial velocity, and define $a$ (axial induction factor):**

$$v_1 = v_0 + a\,v_0 = v_0(1 + a)$$

$$v_2 = v_0 + 2a\,v_0 = v_0(1 + 2a)$$

**Step 9 — Compute useful work output (thrust × advance velocity):**

$$P_{useful} = F_T \cdot v_0 = \rho A_e v_0^{\,2} \cdot 2a\,v_0 = 2\rho A_e v_0^{\,3}\, a$$

Wait — recalculating: $F_T = \dot{m}(v_2 - v_0) = \rho A_e v_1 \cdot 2a v_0 = 2\rho A_e v_0^{\,2} a (1+a)$.

**Step 10 — Compute power input (rate of kinetic energy increase in the slipstream):**

$$P_{input} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}(v_2^{\,2} - v_0^{\,2}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_e v_1 (v_2 - v_0)(v_2 + v_0)$$

Substituting $v_1$ and simplifying:

$$P_{input} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_e \cdot (1+a)\,v_0 \cdot 2a v_0 \cdot (2 + 2a) v_0 = 2\rho A_e v_0^{\,3}\,a(1+a)^2$$

**Step 11 — Form the ideal propulsive efficiency ratio:**

$$\eta_p = \dfrac{P_{useful}}{P_{input}} = \dfrac{2\rho A_e v_0^{\,3}\,a(1+a)}{2\rho A_e v_0^{\,3}\,a(1+a)^2} = \dfrac{1}{1+a}$$

**Step 12 — Express in terms of the thrust coefficient $C_T$.** The thrust in terms of disk area and induction factor is:

$$F_T = 2\rho A_e v_0^{\,2}\, a(1+a)$$

Therefore:

$$a(1+a) = \dfrac{C_T}{2} \quad \text{where} \quad C_T = \dfrac{F_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_0^{\,2} A_e}$$

Solving the quadratic $a^2 + a - C_T/2 = 0$:

$$a = -\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{1 + C_T}$$

(Positive root is taken since $a > 0$ for a propulsor.)

**Step 13 — Final compact form of the Rankine-Froude efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\;\eta_p = \dfrac{1}{1+a} = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 + C_T}}\;}$$

This is the **gold-standard KTU derivation** for propulsive efficiency. The result shows that as $C_T$ (loading on the disk) increases, efficiency drops — a key design trade-off for slow-moving, highly-manoeuvrable ROVs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lighthill's Slender-Body Theory for Undulatory Swimming

**Step 1 — Express the midline of the swimming body.** The lateral displacement of the body's centreline is:

$$h(x,t) = b(x)\,\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

where $b(x)$ is the local amplitude envelope, $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wavenumber, and $\omega = 2\pi f$.

**Step 2 — Compute the lateral velocity of each body element:**

$$\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t} = -b(x)\,\omega\,\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Lighthill's reactive force model (1960, 1971).** For a slender body of length $L$ in inviscid flow, the time-averaged thrust power is:

$$\langle P_{thrust} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m_v \int_0^L \overline{\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t}\right)^2 - \left(v_0\,\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)^2}\, dx$$

where $m_v$ is the virtual mass per unit length ($\approx \rho \pi a^2$ for a body of radius $a$).

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrand.** Using $h = b\sin(kx - \omega t)$:

$$\overline{\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t}\right)^2} = \tfrac{1}{2} b^2 \omega^2$$

$$\overline{\left(v_0\,\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)^2} = \tfrac{1}{2} b^2 k^2 v_0^{\,2}$$

**Step 5 — Net thrust power (time-averaged):**

$$\langle P_{thrust} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{4} m_v (\omega^2 - k^2 v_0^{\,2}) \int_0^L b^2(x)\, dx$$

This is positive (i.e., thrust-producing) when $\omega > k v_0$, or equivalently, when the **phase speed of the body wave $c_{wave} = \omega/k$ exceeds the forward swim speed $v_0$**. This is the *Lighthill condition* — a key design rule for undulatory AUVs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Underwater Locomotion Principles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Underwater Locomotion]) --> q1{Dominant Physical Principle}
    q1 -->|Drag / Momentum Exchange| drag[Drag Based Propulsion]
    q1 -->|Pressure Differential| lift[Lift Based Propulsion]
    q1 -->|Travelling Body Wave| und[Undulatory Propulsion]
    q1 -->|Heave and Pitch Coupling| osc[Oscillatory Fin Propulsion]
    drag --> dragEx1[Paddle Wheel]
    drag --> dragEx2[Oscillating Rigid Plate]
    drag --> dragEx3[Vectored Water Jet]
    lift --> liftEx1[Open Propeller]
    lift --> liftEx2[Ducted Thruster]
    lift --> liftEx3[Contra Rotating Rotor]
    und --> undEx1[Lamprey Robot]
    und --> undEx2[Eel Inspired AUV]
    und --> undEx3[Knifefish Glider]
    osc --> oscEx1[Manta Ray Robot]
    osc --> oscEx2[Labriform Swimmer]
    osc --> oscEx3[Tunable Stiffness Fin]
    drag --> engNote1[Used for low speed high manoeuvrability]
    lift --> engNote2[Used for efficient high speed cruise]
    und --> engNote3[Used for slender flexible body AUVs]
    osc --> engNote4[Used for high agility bio mimicry]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical AUV Locomotion System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    power[Power Source Battery Pack] --> ctrl[Controller and Sensor Suite]
    ctrl --> driver[Motor Driver and ESC]
    driver --> motor[Brushless DC Motor]
    motor --> gearbox[Gearbox and Shaft Seal]
    gearbox --> prop[Propeller or Ducted Thruster]
    prop --> thrust[Generated Thrust Vector]
    thrust --> body[AUV Hull]
    body --> drag[Hull and Appendage Drag]
    thrust --> net[(Net Force and Moment)]
    drag --> net
    net --> motion[Vehicle Motion State]
    motion --> fb[Feedback from IMU DVL INS]
    fb --> ctrl
    aux[Auxiliary Thrusters] --> net
    fin[Bio Inspired Fins] --> net
```

## 4.3 Actuator Disk Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    upstream[Far Upstream Flow v0] --> disk[Ideal Actuator Disk Area Ae]
    disk --> downstream[Far Downstream Slipstream v2]
    disk --> v1[Disk Section Velocity v1]
    disk -. induces .-> thrust[Thrust FT Pressure Jump across Disk]
    thrust --> wake[Slipstream Expansion]
    v0 --- v1 --- v2
    upstream --> p0[Ambient Pressure p infinity]
    downstream --> p0
```

## 4.4 Force Balance on a Steady-Cruising AUV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Propulsion
        T[Thrust FT Forward]
    end
    subgraph Resistance
        D[Drag FD Opposes Motion]
    end
    subgraph Net Motion
        N[Net Force Equals Zero at Steady Cruise]
    end
    T --> N
    D --> N
    N --> v[Constant Velocity v0]
    B[Buoyancy FB Upward] --> ver[Vertical Equilibrium]
    W[Submerged Weight W Downward] --> ver
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a physical component or a force. The arrows denote energy flow, control flow, or force application. Use these schematics as quick revision maps before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PERAT866]`
**Differentiate between drag-based and lift-based propulsion in underwater vehicles. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Drag-Based Propulsion | Lift-Based Propulsion |
|---------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| Physical origin | Momentum exchange with rearward fluid | Pressure differential across an angled foil |
| Force direction | Parallel to motion of the surface | Perpendicular to relative flow |
| Efficiency regime | Better at low Re and low speeds | Better at high Re and high speeds |
| Typical mechanism | Paddle wheels, oscillating plates | Propellers, ducted thrusters |
| Bio analogue | Duck feet (paddling) | Fish tail (sculling) |

**Example (1 mark):** A **paddle-wheel driven autonomous lake-explorer robot** is drag-based. A **submarine's main propeller** is lift-based. (1 mark for example of each)

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PERAT866]`
**Define Reynolds number and explain its significance in underwater locomotion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho\,v\,L}{\mu} = \dfrac{vL}{\nu}$$

**Significance (2 marks):** $Re$ governs the flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent), the dominant propulsion mechanism, the magnitude of the drag coefficient $C_d$, and the validity of analytical tools like the actuator disk theory. For typical mid-sized AUVs, $Re \sim 10^5$–$10^7$, meaning turbulent flow and lift-based propulsion dominate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PERAT866]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Rankine-Froude ideal propulsive efficiency expression in terms of the thrust coefficient $C_T$. State the assumptions clearly.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Incompressible, inviscid, steady flow.
- Propeller modeled as an infinitely thin actuator disk.
- Uniform axial velocity induction across the disk.
- Far-field pressure equals ambient pressure.

**Derivation Steps (5 marks, key milestones):**

1. From mass conservation: $\dot{m} = \rho A_e v_1 = \rho A_0 v_0$.
2. From momentum: $F_T = \dot{m}(v_2 - v_0)$.
3. From Bernoulli along slipstream: $p_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_0^2 = p_d + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = p_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$.
4. Combine to obtain $v_1 = (v_0 + v_2)/2$.
5. Define induction factor $a$: $v_1 = v_0(1+a)$, $v_2 = v_0(1+2a)$.
6. Compute $P_{useful} = F_T v_0$ and $P_{input} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}(v_2^2 - v_0^2)$.
7. Form $\eta_p = P_{useful}/P_{input} = 1/(1+a)$.
8. Substitute $a = -\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{1+C_T}$.

**Final Result (1 mark):**

$$\eta_p = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 + C_T}}$$

**(b) [7 marks]** A small inspection ROV cruises at 1.5 m/s using a ducted thruster of diameter 0.25 m. Sea water density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. If the thrust required to overcome drag is 30 N, calculate (i) the thrust coefficient $C_T$, (ii) the ideal Rankine-Froude efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute disk area:**

$$A_e = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.25)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute dynamic pressure × area:**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 A_e = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(1.5)^2(4.909 \times 10^{-2}) = 56.62 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_T$:**

$$C_T = \dfrac{F_T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 A_e} = \dfrac{30}{56.62} = 0.530$$

**Step 4 — Compute efficiency:**

$$\eta_p = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 + 0.530}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1.530}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + 1.237} = \dfrac{2}{2.237} = 0.894$$

**Answer:** $C_T = 0.530$, $\eta_p \approx 89.4\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a):** Students often forget to state the **actuator disk assumptions** (inviscid, uniform induction, etc.) and lose the first mark. Always begin long derivations with an explicit assumption box.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PERAT866]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches and clear derivations, explain the principles of **undulatory propulsion** in bio-inspired underwater robots. Use Lighthill's slender-body theory to derive the thrust-power condition.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Undulatory propulsion mimics fish-like body waves. The midline follows $h(x,t) = b(x)\sin(kx - \omega t)$, and thrust emerges from the continuous lateral momentum imparted to the surrounding fluid.

**Slender-body derivation (4 marks):**

1. Express the midline and its derivatives: $h, \partial h/\partial t, \partial h/\partial x$.
2. Apply Lighthill's reactive force integral: $\langle F_{thrust} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2} m_v \int_0^L (\omega^2 - k^2 v_0^2) b^2(x)\, dx$.
3. State the **Lighthill condition** for positive thrust: $\omega > k v_0$, i.e., the **body-wave phase speed must exceed the vehicle's forward speed**.

**Numerical sketch and design implications (2 marks):**
- A lamprey robot with $f = 1$ Hz, $\lambda = 0.4$ m, $v_0 = 0.2$ m/s satisfies $\omega/k = 0.4$ m/s $> 0.2$ m/s — net thrust produced.
- Design rule: increase wavelength or frequency to increase available thrust at low speeds.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compute the **Reynolds number** for a torpedo-shaped AUV of length 2.0 m cruising at 4 m/s in seawater. Identify the dominant flow regime and recommend the most efficient propulsion principle. Given: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Re:**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \dfrac{(1025)(4)(2.0)}{1.05 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{8200}{1.05 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.81 \times 10^6$$

**Step 2 — Identify regime (1 mark):** $Re \sim 10^6$–$10^7$ → **fully turbulent external flow**.

**Step 3 — Recommend propulsion (1 mark):** **Lift-based propulsion** (submarine-style propeller or ducted thruster) is the most efficient, as $C_d$ is low and lift forces dominate.

**Step 4 — Justification (1 mark):** Drag-based paddles suffer from high $C_d$ at this Re, whereas a well-designed propeller can achieve $\eta_p > 0.85$ with low specific resistance.

**Step 5 — Hull recommendation (1 mark):** Streamlined torpedo form with $L/D \approx 6$–8 minimizes form drag and supports laminar-to-turbulent transition control.

**Step 6 — Practical check (1 mark):** A typical 2-m AUV at 4 m/s draws on the order of 200–400 W of propulsion power — within the budget of a 1–2 kWh Li-ion pack for ~2 hours of endurance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often write the *numerical answer* without a concluding **propulsion recommendation**. The final 2–3 marks are reserved for design justification. Always close numerical problems with a clear engineering recommendation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Locomotion is the conversion of actuator power into controlled thrust** against fluid drag.
- The **Reynolds number $Re = \rho v L/\mu$** is the master dimensionless group — it dictates regime, drag coefficient, and propulsion choice.
- **Drag-based** propulsion (paddles, jets) excels at low speed and high manoeuvrability; **lift-based** propulsion (propellers) excels at efficient high-speed cruise.
- **Undulatory propulsion** requires the **Lighthill condition** $\omega/k > v_0$ for net positive thrust.
- **Oscillatory (fin) propulsion** uses coupled heave and pitch; the **Strouhal number $St = fA/v \approx 0.2$–$0.4$** is the optimal range for high-efficiency swimming.
- The **Rankine-Froude ideal efficiency** is $\eta_p = 2/(1 + \sqrt{1+C_T})$ — always carry forward the assumption list.
- The **buoyancy–weight balance** $F_B - W$ governs vertical equilibrium; it must be near-zero (or deliberately negative for negative-buoyancy descent) for stable cruising.
- For **exam problems**: always include **units**, draw a **force diagram** (even if simple), and **state assumptions** before long derivations.
- For **numerical answers**: finish with a one-line **engineering interpretation** — examiners reward practical insight generously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Checklist:**
> - Memorize the Reynolds number formula and 3 typical $Re$ regimes.
> - Memorize the Rankine-Froude efficiency expression **and its derivation outline**.
> - Know the difference between drag-based and lift-based propulsion with one example each.
> - Be ready to sketch a labelled **actuator disk** diagram showing $v_0$, $v_1$, $v_2$, and $F_T$.
> - Be ready to compute $C_T$, $C_Q$, and $\eta_p$ from a simple numerical problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Mobile Robots: Ground Robot, Aerial Robot, Underwater Robot and Water-Surface Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Mobile Robots: Ground Robot, Aerial Robot, Underwater Robot and Water-Surface Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Types of Mobile Robots

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Mobile Robot?

A **Mobile Robot** is an automatic machine that is capable of locomotion (movement) across a given environment, equipped with sensors for perception, actuators for motion, and an onboard controller (or tele-operated link) for decision-making, without being physically bound to a fixed position. The **IEEE Standard for Robot Taxonomy** classifies mobile robots by the medium in which they operate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** A mobile robot is a mechatronic system whose primary function is **autonomous or semi-autonomous motion** within a defined operational envelope, governed by sensor feedback and control laws.

The operational envelope is the medium:

| Medium | Robot Class | Primary Buoyancy Support |
|---|---|---|
| Solid (terrain) | **UGV** — Unmanned Ground Vehicle | Mechanical contact (wheels/legs/tracks) |
| Air | **UAV** — Unmanned Aerial Vehicle | Aerodynamic lift |
| Submerged water | **ROV / AUV** — Remotely Operated / Autonomous Underwater Vehicle | Hydrostatic buoyancy |
| Water surface | **USV** — Unmanned Surface Vehicle | Surface buoyancy (planing / displacement) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PERAT866 Focus:** The course specifically emphasizes the **Underwater (ROV/AUV)** and **Water-Surface (USV)** categories as the principal domain, with the other two discussed comparatively for contextual engineering grounding.

---

### 1.2 The Four Types — Intuitive Analogies

#### 🚗 Ground Robot (UGV)
**Analogy — The Mars Rover or your Roomba.**
Just as a car must push against the ground to move, a ground robot uses **wheels, tracks, or legs** to generate traction. The contact with a solid surface provides reaction force, so locomotion is essentially a **frictional mechanics problem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sub-Types:** Wheeled (Ackermann, Differential, Omni), Tracked (Skid-Steer), Legged (Bipedal, Quadruped), and Hybrid (Wheel-Leg).

---

#### ✈️ Aerial Robot (UAV / Drone)
**Analogy — A Hummingbird or Helicopter.**
Aerial robots must continuously push air downward to stay aloft. Without ground contact, they rely on **Newton's Third Law**: rotor thrust = weight. The medium (air, ρ ≈ **1.225 kg/m³**) is light, so rotors must spin very fast. This is fundamentally a **fluid-dynamics and aerodynamics** problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sub-Types:** Multirotor (Quadcopter, Hexacopter), Fixed-Wing, Hybrid VTOL (Vertical Take-Off and Landing), and Flapping-Wing (Bio-inspired).

---

#### 🌊 Underwater Robot (ROV / AUV)
**Analogy — A Submarine or a深海 Diver.**
Submerged in water (ρ ≈ **1025 kg/m³** for seawater, ~**800× denser than air**), these robots experience enormous pressure and drag. They use **thrusters for propulsion** and **buoyancy modules** for vertical stability. This is the central subject of PERAT866.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sub-Types:**
> - **ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle):** Tethered, human-controlled, real-time.
> - **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle):** Untethered, self-decision-making.
> - **Glider:** Buoyancy-driven, low-power, long-endurance.
> - **HOV (Human Occupied Vehicle):** Manned submersibles (e.g., Alvin).

---

#### 🛥️ Water-Surface Robot (USV / ASV)
**Analogy — A Remote-Control Toy Boat or Autonomous Catamaran.**
Operating at the **air-water interface**, USVs are partially supported by buoyancy (like a ship) and partially by hydrodynamic lift at speed (hydrofoils). They experience a unique **multi-medium challenge**: air drag above and water drag below, plus wave-induced motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sub-Types:** Monohull, Catamaran (twin hull, high stability), Trimaran, Hydrofoil, and Inflatable-boat platforms.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants Students Must Memorize

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of fresh water | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Density of seawater | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Density of air (sea level) | $\rho_{air}$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Kinematic viscosity of water (20°C) | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶** | m²/s |
| Dynamic viscosity of water (20°C) | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³** | Pa·s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Locomotion Principle — The "Why" and "How" of Each Type

Every mobile robot must overcome a **resistive force** to move. The nature of this force defines the engineering challenge:

- **Ground robots** → overcome **rolling friction** and **gravitational slope resistance** (terramechanics).
- **Aerial robots** → overcome **gravity entirely** via aerodynamic lift, plus form/profile drag.
- **Underwater robots** → overcome **pressure drag + viscous drag** in a dense, incompressible fluid.
- **Surface robots** → overcome a **combination of water drag (hull), air drag (superstructure), and wave forces**.

### 2.2 The Universal Drag Equation (Used in 3 of 4 Domains)

The **drag force** on a body moving through a fluid is given by:

$$F_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, v^{2} \, C_d \, A$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = relative velocity (m/s)
- $C_d$ = dimensionless drag coefficient
- $A$ = characteristic frontal area (m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why drag matters:** Drag scales with the **square of velocity**. Doubling speed quadruples drag, and since Power = Force × velocity, it **octuples** the power requirement. This is why underwater and aerial vehicles are designed for *low-speed efficiency*, not raw velocity.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTE on Table Syntax:** All absolute-value bars use the LaTeX macro `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table structure. Exam invigilators do NOT tolerate broken tables!

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Buoyancy (Archimedes) | $F_b = \rho_{fluid} \, V_{disp} \, g$ | UUV / USV | N | Vehicle weight matched by displaced volume |
| 2 | Net vertical force (submerged) | $F_{net} = F_b - W = (\rho_w V - m) \, g$ | UUV | N | Zero for neutral buoyancy |
| 3 | Hydrodynamic Drag | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} C_d A$ | UUV / USV | N | $C_d$ ≈ 0.04–1.2 |
| 4 | Thrust Required (Hover, UAV) | $T = m \, g$ | UAV | N | $T_{single} = mg/N_{rotors}$ |
| 5 | Lift (Bernoulli, Wing) | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} C_L S$ | UAV (FW) | N | $C_L$ ≈ 0.3–1.5 |
| 6 | Rolling Resistance (Ground) | $F_r = \mu_r \, N$ | UGV | N | $\mu_r$ ≈ 0.01–0.05 (rubber on asphalt) |
| 7 | Power to overcome drag | $P = F_d \, v = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{3} C_d A$ | UUV / UAV | W | Cube-law dominates design |
| 8 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{v L}{\nu} = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | UUV (laminar/turbulent) | dimensionless | $10^{5}$ to $10^{7}$ for submarines |
| 9 | Froude Number (Surface/Wave) | $Fr = \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | USV | dimensionless | $Fr < 0.4$ = displacement; $Fr > 0.4$ = planing |
| 10 | Pressure at depth | $P = P_{atm} + \rho \, g \, h$ | UUV | Pa | At 1000 m, $P \approx 100$ atm |
| 11 | Degrees of Freedom (Heaveave/Sway/Surge + Roll/Pitch/Yaw) | DOF = **6** (rigid body) | All | integer | 3 translational + 3 rotational |
| 12 | Coulomb Efficiency (Battery) | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | All | % | Li-ion ≈ 90–95% |

---

### 2.4 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) — Universal to All Mobile Robots

A rigid body in 3D space has exactly **6 independent motions** (this is a **Newtonian mechanics invariant**):

| # | Translational DOF | Description | Rotational DOF | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Surge** | Forward/backward | **Roll** | Rotation about longitudinal (x) axis |
| 2 | **Sway** | Left/right | **Pitch** | Rotation about lateral (y) axis |
| 3 | **Heave** | Up/down | **Yaw** | Rotation about vertical (z) axis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic:** A robot that can independently control **all 6 DOF** instantaneously is **holonomic** (e.g., a quadcopter). A car is **non-holonomic** — it cannot move sideways without first moving forward (parallel-parking problem). ROVs with vectored thrusters are *often* holonomic.

---

### 2.5 Comparative Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Real Engineering Use Case | Core Engineering Discipline |
|---|---|---|
| Ground (UGV) | Mars Curiosity Rover, agricultural autonomous tractors, Boston Dynamics Spot | Terramechanics, suspension design, SLAM |
| Aerial (UAV) | Aerial mapping, package delivery (Amazon Prime Air), precision agriculture | Aerodynamics, flight control, GNSS fusion |
| Underwater (ROV/AUV) | Oil-rig inspection, ship-hull survey, mine countermeasures, scientific oceanography | Hydrodynamics, pressure vessel design, acoustic comms |
| Surface (USV) | Bathymetric survey, port security, environmental monitoring, ASW (anti-sub warfare) | Naval architecture, wave modeling, multi-domain propulsion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Underwater Robots Need Symmetric Hull Shapes (Drag Analysis)

**Problem Setup:** An AUV of length $L = 2.0$ m and frontal diameter $D = 0.3$ m moves at cruising speed $v = 1.5$ m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Compute the viscous drag force, the pressure drag, and the total power required at cruising speed. Assume a streamlined body with $C_{D,friction} = 0.004$ and $C_{D,pressure} = 0.05$.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the Reynolds Number.**

We use the chord length $L = 2.0$ m as the characteristic length. Water kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

$$Re \;=\; \frac{v \, L}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{1.5 \times 2.0}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 2.988 \times 10^{6}$$

$$Re \;\approx\; 3.0 \times 10^{6}$$

**Textual logic:** This is a high Reynolds number, indicating **turbulent boundary-layer flow** — the pressure-drag component dominates over friction-drag for bluff bodies, but the two are often *added* in empirical submarine design.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the Wetted Surface Area.**

For a streamlined (prolate spheroid) body with length-to-diameter ratio $L/D = 6.67$, the wetted surface area approximation is:

$$A_{wet} \;\approx\; \pi \, D \, L \;\left( 1 - \tfrac{1}{2(L/D)^{2/3}} \right)$$

Substituting $D = 0.3$ m, $L = 2.0$ m:

$$A_{wet} \;\approx\; \pi \times 0.3 \times 2.0 \times \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2 \times (6.67)^{2/3}} \right)$$

$$(6.67)^{2/3} \;\approx\; 3.55 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tfrac{1}{2 \times 3.55} \approx 0.141$$

$$A_{wet} \;\approx\; 1.885 \times (1 - 0.141) \;\approx\; 1.885 \times 0.859 \;\approx\; 1.62 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

**Textual logic:** A long, slender body minimizes wetted area per unit volume — a direct reason why submarines and AUVs are cylindrical/torpedo-shaped, not spherical.

---

**Step 3 — Compute Viscous (Skin-Friction) Drag.**

The **ITTC 1978** correlation for turbulent skin friction gives:

$$C_{f} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(2.988 \times 10^{6}) - 2)^{2}}$$

$$\log_{10}(2.988 \times 10^{6}) \;\approx\; 6.475 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (6.475 - 2)^{2} = 20.0$$

$$C_{f} \;\approx\; \frac{0.075}{20.0} \;=\; 3.75 \times 10^{-3}$$

Now apply the form factor $k = 1 + \tfrac{1.5}{(L/D)^{3/2}} + \tfrac{7}{(L/D)^{3}}$ for streamlined bodies:

$$k \;\approx\; 1 + \frac{1.5}{(6.67)^{1.5}} + \frac{7}{(6.67)^{3}} \;\approx\; 1 + 0.087 + 0.024 \;\approx\; 1.11$$

So effective friction coefficient:

$$C_{D,fric} \;\approx\; k \cdot C_{f} \;\approx\; 1.11 \times 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 4.16 \times 10^{-3}$$

The problem statement provides $C_{D,friction} = 0.004$ which is consistent. Using that value:

$$F_{d,fric} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, v^{2} \, C_{D,fric} \, A_{wet} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(1.5)^{2}(0.004)(1.62)$$

$$F_{d,fric} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1025 \times 2.25 \times 0.004 \times 1.62 \;\approx\; 7.47 \; \text{N}$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute Pressure (Form) Drag.**

$$F_{d,pres} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, v^{2} \, C_{D,pres} \, A_{front}$$

Frontal area: $A_{front} = \pi D^{2}/4 = \pi (0.3)^{2}/4 = 0.0707$ m².

$$F_{d,pres} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1025 \times 2.25 \times 0.05 \times 0.0707$$

$$F_{d,pres} \;\approx\; 4.08 \; \text{N}$$

---

**Step 5 — Total Drag and Power.**

$$F_{d,total} \;=\; F_{d,fric} + F_{d,pres} \;\approx\; 7.47 + 4.08 \;\approx\; 11.55 \; \text{N}$$

Power to overcome drag at cruising speed:

$$P_{cruise} \;=\; F_{d,total} \times v \;=\; 11.55 \times 1.5 \;\approx\; 17.3 \; \text{W}$$

**Textual conclusion:** A 2-metre AUV cruising at 1.5 m/s in seawater requires only ~17 W of propulsive power — this is **why** long-endurance AUVs (e.g., REMUS 600, Autosub Long Range) can operate for **months** on battery power at low speeds. The cube-law in $P \propto v^{3}$ is the governing design constraint.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Buoyancy Tuning for Neutral Equilibrium

**Problem:** A small ROV weighs $W = 480$ N in air. It must be **neutrally buoyant** (neither sinking nor floating) in seawater. Compute the required displaced volume.

**Step 1:** For neutral buoyancy: $F_b = W$.

$$\rho_{sw} \, V_{disp} \, g = W \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{disp} = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \, g}$$

$$V_{disp} = \frac{480}{1025 \times 9.81} \;\approx\; \frac{480}{10055.25} \;\approx\; 0.0477 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

$$V_{disp} \;\approx\; 47.7 \; \text{litres}$$

**Textual logic:** In practice, ROVs are built slightly *positively* buoyant (by 2–5%) so that on tether failure, they **float to the surface** as a safety feature. The pilot trims via expendable syntactic foam blocks or adjustable ballast weights.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Four Mobile-Robot Classes

| Parameter | UGV (Ground) | UAV (Aerial) | ROV/AUV (Underwater) | USV (Surface) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Medium | Solid terrain | Air (ρ = 1.225) | Water (ρ = 1025) | Air-water interface |
| Primary Support | Friction contact | Aerodynamic lift | Hydrostatic buoyancy | Surface buoyancy + hydrodynamic lift |
| Dominant Resistive Force | Rolling friction, slope | Aerodynamic drag | Hydrodynamic drag + pressure | Hull drag + wave-induced forces |
| Typical Speed Range | 0.1 – 30 m/s | 0.5 – 80 m/s | 0.2 – 5 m/s | 1 – 15 m/s (≈ 2–30 knots) |
| Energy Source | Battery / Fuel cell | Battery + solar | Battery (Li-ion) | Diesel / hybrid / solar |
| Endurance | Hours – Days | Minutes – Hours | Hours – Months | Days – Months |
| Communication | Radio (RF) — easy | RF (line-of-sight) | Acoustic (slow, ~kbps) | RF + Acoustic |
| GPS Available? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (acoustic LBL/USBL) | ✅ Yes (on surface) |
| Key Sensor Suite | LiDAR, Camera, IMU | Camera, IMU, Barometer, GPS | DVL, Sonar, Pressure, IMU | Radar, Camera, IMU, GPS |
| Autonomy Class | High (Waymo) | High (Skydio) | Moderate (MBARI AUVs) | Moderate (Saildrone) |
| Design Complexity | Low–Medium | Medium | **Very High** | Medium–High |
| Cost Range | \$500 – \$5M | \$200 – \$2M | \$50K – \$10M | \$20K – \$5M |
| Primary Failure Mode | Stuck / flipped | Battery depletion | Pressure housing leak | Capsize in heavy seas |
| Typical KTU 2024 Exam Weight | 15% | 15% | **50%** | 20% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree — Mobile Robot Taxonomy

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **hierarchical taxonomy** of mobile robots as specified in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Mobile Robots]:::root --> B1[Ground Robot UGV]
    A0 --> B2[Aerial Robot UAV]
    A0 --> B3[Underwater Robot UUV]
    A0 --> B4[Water Surface Robot USV]

    B1 --> C1A[Wheeled Ackermann]
    B1 --> C1B[Wheeled Differential]
    B1 --> C1C[Tracked Skid Steer]
    B1 --> C1D[Legged Bipedal]
    B1 --> C1E[Legged Quadruped]

    B2 --> C2A[Multirotor Quadcopter]
    B2 --> C2B[Multirotor Hexacopter]
    B2 --> C2C[Fixed Wing]
    B2 --> C2D[Hybrid VTOL]
    B2 --> C2E[Flapping Wing Bio Inspired]

    B3 --> C3A[ROV Tethered]
    B3 --> C3B[AUV Autonomous]
    B3 --> C3C[Underwater Glider]
    B3 --> C3D[HOV Manned Submersible]

    C3A --> D3A1[Observation Class]
    C3A --> D3A2[Work Class Heavy]
    C3B --> D3B1[Shallow Water AUV]
    C3B --> D3B2[Deep Water AUV]

    B4 --> C4A[Monohull Hull]
    B4 --> C4B[Catamaran Twin]
    B4 --> C4C[Trimaran]
    B4 --> C4D[Hydrofoil Lifting]
    B4 --> C4E[Inflatable Platform]

    classDef root fill:#003366,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef domain fill:#1f6feb,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Color-Coded Domains (Visual Mnemonic):**
> - 🔵 **Blue Nodes** = Domains (UGV, UAV, UUV, USV)
> - 🟢 **White Nodes** = Specific robot types
> - ⚫ **Root Node** = Universal "Mobile Robots" superclass

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Subsystem Architecture Common to All Four Types

```mermaid
graph LR
    PWR[Power Source Battery or Fuel]:::supply --> CTRL[Central Controller MCU or SBC]:::core
    PWR --> ACT[Actuators Motors Thrusters]:::motion
    PWR --> SENS[Sensor Suite]:::sense
    SENS --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT
    ACT --> MECH[Mechanical Chassis Hull Frame]:::mech
    SENS --> COMM[Communication Module]:::comm
    CTRL --> COMM
    COMM --> OP[Human Operator or Base Station]:::human
    OP --> CTRL

    classDef supply fill:#f9c74f,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#ef476f,color:#fff
    classDef motion fill:#06d6a0,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
    classDef mech fill:#073b4c,color:#fff
    classDef comm fill:#8338ec,color:#fff
    classDef human fill:#fb5607,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Power flows** from the supply to all subsystems (this is the **energy bus**).
- **Sensor → Controller → Actuator** is the canonical **Sense-Plan-Act** control loop.
- **Communication** is bidirectional: the operator issues commands, and the robot reports telemetry.

---

### 4.3 Domain-Specific Actuator Mapping (Block Architecture)

