
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between a **Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV)** and an **Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV)** with respect to power source, communication, and decision-making.

**Model Answer (3 points × 1 mark):**
1. **Power source:** ROVs draw power from the surface ship through a **tether (umbilical cable)** — virtually unlimited endurance. AUVs carry **onboard batteries** — limited endurance.
2. **Communication:** ROVs use **real-time wired (fibre-optic or copper) communication** with high bandwidth (Mbps). AUVs use **acoustic modems** with very low bandwidth (kbps) and high latency.
3. **Decision-making:** ROVs are **pilot-controlled** (tele-operated). AUVs are **self-decision-making** using onboard mission planners and AI.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** List any **three advantages** of an Unmanned Surface Vehicle (USV) over a conventional manned survey vessel.

**Model Answer (3 × 1 mark):**
1. **No human risk** in hazardous, hostile, or contaminated environments (e.g., mine clearance, nuclear zones).
2. **Lower operational cost** — no crew quarters, galley, or life-support systems; smaller displacement.
3. **Longer endurance** — can operate continuously for weeks/months (e.g., Saildrone transoceanic missions) without crew fatigue.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 ESE Pattern — Module 1 Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Classify mobile robots based on the **medium of operation**. With neat diagrams, describe the **locomotion mechanism** of any two underwater robot types.

**(b) (7 Marks)** An AUV of mass $m = 150$ kg is required to cruise at $v = 1.0$ m/s in seawater. The hull has a wetted surface area of $A_{wet} = 2.5$ m² and a total drag coefficient (combined friction + form) of $C_d = 0.08$. Compute the **(i)** total drag force, **(ii)** propulsive power, and **(iii)** energy consumed in **10 hours** of continuous operation. Assume 60% overall propulsive efficiency.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Classification is as given in the **Master Classification Tree (Section 4.1)**. Underwater robot types and their locomotion:

1. **ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle)** — uses **4 to 8 vectored thrusters** (typically BLDC motors in oil-filled housings) for 4–6 DOF motion. Tether provides power & data. Locomotion is **pilot-commanded** in real-time. Diagram: a rectangular frame with thrusters at the corners and a tether rising to the surface.
2. **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle)** — uses **1 main propeller at the stern** for surge and **smaller control fins** (rudders, elevators) for yaw and pitch. Locomotion is **pre-programmed** via waypoint navigation. Energy is from onboard Li-ion battery packs.
3. **Underwater Glider** — uses **buoyancy engines** (oil displacement pumps) to alternately become heavier and lighter than water, converting vertical motion to horizontal via wings. **Zero-thruster** propulsion — extremely energy-efficient.

**(b) [7 marks] Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Drag Force:**

$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} C_d A_{wet} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (1.0)^{2} \times 0.08 \times 2.5$$

$$F_d = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.20 = 102.5 \; \text{N}$$

**(ii) Propulsive Power (at the shaft):**

$$P_{shaft} = F_d \times v = 102.5 \times 1.0 = 102.5 \; \text{W}$$

Accounting for **60% propulsive efficiency** ($\eta = 0.60$), the actual electrical power from batteries is:

$$P_{elec} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta} = \frac{102.5}{0.60} \approx 170.83 \; \text{W}$$

**(iii) Energy in 10 hours:**

$$E = P_{elec} \times t = 170.83 \times (10 \times 3600) = 170.83 \times 36000$$

$$E = 6,149,880 \; \text{J} \approx 6.15 \; \text{MJ} \approx 1.71 \; \text{kWh}$$

**Answer:** $F_d = 102.5$ N, $P_{elec} \approx 171$ W, $E \approx 6.15$ MJ (or 1.71 kWh).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Compare and contrast **Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs)** and **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)** across any **five** engineering parameters. Why is the design of underwater vehicles considered the *most challenging* of the four categories?

**(b) (7 Marks)** A catamaran USV has two hulls, each of length $L = 3$ m and beam $b = 0.4$ m, separated by a centreline distance of $d = 1.8$ m. The total mass of the vessel is $m = 250$ kg. Compute the **(i)** displaced volume for static floatation and **(ii)** the metacentric height $GM$ to assess initial transverse stability. The centre of gravity is $0.4$ m above the keel and the centre of buoyancy is $0.3$ m above the keel.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Comparison across 5 parameters (UGV vs UAV):

| Parameter | UGV | UAV |
|---|---|---|
| Support Mechanism | Wheels/legs on solid ground (reaction force) | Rotor thrust (aerodynamic lift) |
| Energy Efficiency | High (rolling friction low) | Low (must hover against gravity) |
| Endurance | Days | Typically 20–40 min |
| Operating Volume | 2D (constrained to surface) | 3D (full volumetric access) |
| Failure Consequence | Stops safely | Crash; can be catastrophic |
| Sensing Range | LiDAR, GPS easy | Camera + GPS dominant |

**Why underwater is the most challenging** [2 marks]: (1) No GPS underwater — must use expensive acoustic positioning (LBL/USBL). (2) Extreme pressure demands pressure housings (costly materials like titanium). (3) Wireless communication is **severely bandwidth-limited** (acoustic ~kbps vs RF ~Mbps). (4) Water density is **800× air**, so thrusters must be high-thrust and pressure-tolerant.

**(b) [7 marks] Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Displaced Volume:**
For static floatation: $F_b = W$.

$$V_{disp} = \frac{m \, g}{\rho_{sw} \, g} = \frac{m}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{250}{1025} \approx 0.244 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**(ii) Metacentric Height $GM$:**

The second moment of waterline area for a catamaran with two slender hulls of length $L$ and beam $b$ separated by $d$ is:

$$I_T = 2 \left( \frac{L \, b^{3}}{12} + L \, b \, \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} \right)$$

$$I_T = 2 \left( \frac{3 \times (0.4)^{3}}{12} + 3 \times 0.4 \times (0.9)^{2} \right)$$

$$I_T = 2 \left( \frac{3 \times 0.064}{12} + 1.2 \times 0.81 \right) = 2 \left( 0.016 + 0.972 \right) = 2 \times 0.988 = 1.976 \; \text{m}^{4}$$

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V_{disp}} = \frac{1.976}{0.244} \approx 8.10 \; \text{m}$$

$$BG = KB_{height} - KG_{height} = 0.3 - 0.4 = -0.1 \; \text{m}$$

(The negative sign indicates that **B is below G** — at this loading the vessel is **unstable** in pure transverse roll.)

$$GM = BM + BG = 8.10 + (-0.1) = 8.0 \; \text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1 — Sign Errors in $GM$:** Many students incorrectly compute $GM = BM - BG$ or forget that $B$ *can* be below $G$ for surface ships. The correct **KTU-recommended** formula is $GM = KB + BM - KG$. A **positive $GM$** = stable, **negative $GM$** = unstable. Here $GM = 8.0$ m, which is excessively large — students should note this is a *highly stiff* catamaran (typical USV range is $GM \approx 0.5 - 2.0$ m). This indicates the question's $V_{disp}$ is unrealistically small for the given dimensions, but mathematically the calculation follows the formula.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Top 5 Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 1 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ROV and AUV** — ROVs are *tethered*, AUVs are *autonomous*. A student who reverses this loses the very first mark. Always state: **"ROV = cable-powered + tele-operated"** and **"AUV = battery-powered + autonomous."**
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in drag problems** — A numerical answer without units (e.g., writing "102.5" instead of "102.5 N") attracts a **0.5-mark penalty** under KTU 2024 valuation norms. Always annotate N, W, J, m³.
>
> 3. **Mixing air and water densities** — Using $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (air) in an underwater problem is a **3-mark disaster** in 7-mark numericals. Re-read the problem carefully.
>
> 4. **Cube-Law of Power ignored** — In Part B questions, students often compute drag at one speed and assume the same at another. Always apply $P \propto v^{3}$ explicitly.
>
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — A 7-mark question that *asks* for a diagram but is answered with only text gets a **mandatory 2-mark deduction**. Always draw a labelled figure, even if hand-drawn.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-Minute Rapid Revision Block — Memorize This!**

- ☐ A **mobile robot** has 6 DOF: Surge, Sway, Heave, Roll, Pitch, Yaw (3T + 3R).
- ☐ **UGV** = land-based, uses wheels/tracks/legs; powered by battery/fuel.
- ☐ **UAV** = air-based, uses rotors/wings; lift $\propto v^{2}$ (Bernoulli).
- ☐ **ROV** = underwater, **tethered**, real-time control, unlimited power.
- ☐ **AUV** = underwater, **untethered**, autonomous, battery-limited.
- ☐ **USV** = surface, hybrid air-water interface, subject to waves.
- ☐ Universal drag: $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} C_d A$. Power: $P = F_d v \propto v^{3}$.
- ☐ Buoyancy: $F_b = \rho_{fluid} V_{disp} g$. Neutral: $F_b = W = mg$.
- ☐ Standard densities to memorize: $\rho_{air}=1.225$, $\rho_{fw}=1000$, $\rho_{sw}=1025$ kg/m³.
- ☐ Pressure at depth $h$: $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ → at 10 km depth, $P \approx 1000$ atm!
- ☐ Holonomic robot = full 6-DOF instantaneous control; non-holonomic = constrained (car).
- ☐ Acoustic underwater communication is **slow (~kbps)** and **latent** — the fundamental bottleneck of AUV design.
- ☐ Catamaran USVs are preferred over monohulls for **stability** in survey work.
- ☐ Always specify **units** in numerical answers (KTU rule: no units = -0.5 mark).
- ☐ Always provide a **diagram** when the question says "with neat diagram" (KTU rule: missing diagram = -2 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Underwater Vehicle Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Underwater Vehicle Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Underwater Vehicle Construction** refers to the integrated engineering discipline of designing, materialising, and assembling the physical, structural, and fluid-dynamic framework of a vehicle that operates submerged in a liquid medium (predominantly seawater). The construction principles govern **buoyancy management**, **hydrostatic pressure resistance**, **hydrodynamic profile optimisation**, **sealing integrity**, and **subsystem packaging** such that the vehicle can perform its mission (observation, manipulation, intervention, or survey) without structural failure, flooding, or loss of control.

The vehicles are broadly classified by the KTU syllabus into three families:

| Acronym | Full Form | Energy / Control Source | Typical Depth Class |
|---------|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **ROV** | Remotely Operated Vehicle | Tethered, surface-powered | Shallow → Abyssal (6,000 m+) |
| **AUV** | Autonomous Underwater Vehicle | Onboard batteries | Coastal → Deep (6,000 m) |
| **UUV** | Unmanned Underwater Vehicle | Umbrella term for both | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 emphasises the *constructional* aspects — meaning the mechanical, material, and fluid-static decisions — **before** diving into electronics, control, or navigation (which appear in later modules).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Submarine Suitcase"**
>
> Imagine you must build a **watertight suitcase** that must:
> 1. **Not get crushed** when buried under a 100 m water column (≈ 10 atmospheres of pressure pushing inward on every square centimetre).
> 2. **Float neutrally** — neither sink to the bottom nor bob to the surface uncontrollably.
> 3. **Move gracefully** through a fluid 800× denser than air, where every motion creates **drag**.
> 4. **Carry sensitive electronics** (cameras, sonars, computers) that die instantly if a single drop of saltwater touches them.
>
> That "suitcase" is the **hull** of an underwater vehicle. The *principles of construction* are the engineering rules for choosing its shape, its wall thickness, its material, its buoyancy foam, and the placement of its thrusters — so the vehicle can survive, float, and swim.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of seawater (ρ_sw):** **1025 kg/m³** (standard at 4 °C, 35 ppt salinity, surface)
- **Hydrostatic pressure gradient:** **≈ 1 atm (101.325 kPa) per 10 m of depth**
- **Dynamic viscosity of seawater (μ):** **≈ 1.08 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** at 20 °C
- **Sound speed in seawater (c):** **≈ 1500 m/s**
- **Standard gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Drag coefficient range for streamlined bodies:** **0.04 – 0.10**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board examiner expects students to quote **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³** (not 1000 kg/m³) because the vehicle operates in *saltwater*, where dissolved salts raise the density by 2.5 %.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Underwater Vehicle Construction

Every KTU question on this topic is built around five engineering pillars. Master these and you master the module.

### Pillar 1 — Buoyancy (Statics)
The vehicle's weight in water must equal its displaced water weight for **neutral buoyancy**.

### Pillar 2 — Pressure Hull (Structural Integrity)
The hull must withstand **hydrostatic collapse pressure** at the rated operational depth.

### Pillar 3 — Hydrodynamics (Resistance & Propulsion)
The external shape must minimise **form drag** and **friction drag** so limited thruster energy is converted into forward motion.

### Pillar 4 — Materials & Sealing (Survivability)
All exposed parts must resist **corrosion**, **biofouling**, and **water ingress** across pressure cycles.

### Pillar 5 — Component Packaging (Mission Utility)
Thrusters, sensors, batteries, and payloads must be placed to give **static stability** and **dynamic manoeuvrability**.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Buoyancy Management (Archimedes Applied to Submersibles)

The vertical force balance on a submerged vehicle is:

$$F_{net} = F_{buoy} - W_{dry} - W_{payload}$$

For neutral buoyancy, $F_{net} = 0$, so the required **buoyancy foam volume** $V_{foam}$ is found by closing the equation with the foam's effective density.

The vehicle is split into three mass contributions:

1. **Hull + frame + fasteners (structural mass)** — heaviest, made of aluminium, titanium, or HDPE.
2. **Payload (electronics, sensors, batteries)** — must be inside a watertight enclosure.
3. **Ballast / Buoyancy modules** — *adjustable* via syntactic foam or oil-filled bladders.

**Reserve Buoyancy** is the safety margin:

$$\text{Reserve Buoyancy} = \frac{F_{buoy} - W}{F_{buoy}} \times 100\%$$

A KTU-grade ROV typically targets **5 – 10 % positive reserve buoyancy** at the surface so that if power is lost, it returns to the surface rather than sinking (fail-safe behaviour).

---

### 2.2.2 Pressure Hull Design

The hull is a **thin-walled** or **thick-walled** pressure vessel. The KTU syllabus treats the **cylindrical hull with hemispherical end caps** (a "tin-can" geometry) as the canonical case because:
- Cylinders are easy to manufacture (rolled + welded plate, or machined tube).
- Hemispheres are the optimal pressure shape — they have **equal membrane stress in all directions** (the principle behind a submarine's iconic dome ends).

For a **thin-walled cylinder** (wall thickness $t$ < radius $r/10$):

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t} \quad \text{(circumferential stress, governs design)}$$

$$\sigma_{long} = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t} \quad \text{(longitudinal stress, half of hoop)}$$

The **collapse depth** is therefore set by the **hoop stress** reaching the material's yield strength $\sigma_y$:

$$P_{collapse} = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot t}{r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hoop stress dominates:** When you pressurise a cylinder from outside (as the ocean does), the wall wants to *split lengthwise* — hence the *circumferential* (hoop) fibres are the most loaded.

---

### 2.2.3 Hydrodynamic Design

Two drag components dominate:

$$F_{D,total} = F_{D,form} + F_{D,friction} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_f S v^2$$

- $A$ → **frontal area** (minimised by a *streamlined* / *myring*-shaped hull)
- $S$ → **wetted surface area**
- $C_D$ → **form-drag coefficient** (low for streamlined shapes, **0.04 – 0.10**)
- $C_f$ → **friction-drag coefficient** (depends on Reynolds number and surface roughness)

For AUVs that must glide for hours, **laminar-flow bodies of revolution** (axisymmetric, length/diameter ≈ 6–8) are used. For ROVs that hover, a compact, boxy frame is acceptable.

---

### 2.2.4 Thruster Configuration (Degrees of Freedom)

A typical 4-thruster vectored layout:

| Thruster | Function | Force Generated |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| 2 × horizontal (rear, vectored ±45°) | Surge + Yaw | Forward / reverse + turning |
| 1 × horizontal (lateral) | Sway | Sideways translation |
| 1 × vertical (bottom) | Heave | Depth control |

A **6-DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** AUV has independent control of **Surge, Sway, Heave, Roll, Pitch, Yaw**.

---

### 2.2.5 Static Stability (Metacentric Considerations)

For a submerged body, **stability** is governed by the relative vertical positions of:
- **Centre of Gravity (CoG / G)**
- **Centre of Buoyancy (CoB / B)**

The vehicle is **statically stable in roll/pitch** if:

$$G \text{ lies below } B \quad \Rightarrow \quad \overline{GB} = z_B - z_G > 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Designers therefore place **heavy components (batteries, pressure housings) at the bottom** and **buoyancy foam at the top** — just like a ship's keel, but inverted for an underwater vehicle that wants to stay upright.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho_{sw} \, g \, h$ | $h$ = depth | Pa |
| 2 | Buoyant force | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp}$ | $V_{disp}$ = displaced volume | N |
| 3 | Neutral buoyancy | $W_{total} = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp}$ | $W_{total}$ = total weight | N |
| 4 | Reserve buoyancy | $R_b = (F_b - W)/F_b \times 100$ | Safety margin | % |
| 5 | Thin-wall hoop stress | $\sigma_h = P r / t$ | Cylinder | Pa |
| 6 | Thin-wall longitudinal stress | $\sigma_l = P r / (2 t)$ | Cylinder | Pa |
| 7 | Collapse pressure | $P_c = \sigma_y \, t / r$ | Material yield limit | Pa |
| 8 | Total drag | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2$ | Quadratic drag | N |
| 9 | Power for cruising | $P_{req} = F_D \cdot v$ | Useful propulsive power | W |
| 10 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ | $L$ = characteristic length | dimensionless |
| 11 | Static stability | $\overline{GB} = z_B - z_G > 0$ | Metacentric height | m |
| 12 | Volume of syntactic foam needed | $V_{foam} = (F_b - W_{rest}) / (g (\rho_{sw} - \rho_{foam}))$ | Ballast volume | m³ |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula table, note the use of `\vert` / `$\mid$` — never write absolute values with the `$\vert x \vert$` pipe symbol inside a markdown table row, as it breaks the table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These construction principles are not academic — they are the difference between a working deep-sea robot and a **fatal mission failure**. Examples:

- **Titan submersible (OceanGate, 2023):** Loss due to **pressure hull failure** — a textbook case of ignoring hoop-stress / fatigue principles in carbon-fibre construction.
- **Alvin (WHOI, 6,500 m):** A titanium hemisphere — a textbook thick-wall pressure vessel with safety factor **> 2.0**.
- **Sentry (AUV, 6,000 m):** Ceramic syntactic foam blocks provide buoyancy while resisting 600 atm collapse.
- **Saab Seaeye Falcon ROV:** Streamlined polypropylene frame, 4 vectored thrusters, depth rating 300 m — a student-lab favourite.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Foam Volume for Neutral Buoyancy

> **Problem framing (KTU style):**  
> An ROV weighs $W_{dry}$ in air with all hardware installed but **before** adding buoyancy foam. The hull displaces a known water volume $V_{disp}$. Find the volume of syntactic foam (density $\rho_{foam}$) needed so the vehicle is *exactly* neutrally buoyant in seawater ($\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

### Step 1 — Write the vertical force balance at neutral buoyancy

$$F_{buoyancy} = W_{dry} + W_{foam}$$

### Step 2 — Expand each term in terms of density, volume, and gravity

$$\rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp} = W_{dry} + \rho_{foam} \, g \, V_{foam}$$

### Step 3 — Isolate the foam mass term

$$\rho_{foam} \, g \, V_{foam} = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp} - W_{dry}$$

### Step 4 — Cancel $g$ and solve for $V_{foam}$

$$V_{foam} = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, V_{disp} - W_{dry}/g}{\rho_{foam}}$$

### Step 5 — Express the dry weight in terms of the vehicle's dry mass $m_{dry}$

Because $W_{dry} = m_{dry} \, g$:

$$V_{foam} = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, V_{disp} - m_{dry}}{\rho_{foam}}$$

> **Final Working Equation (high-yield):**
>
> $$V_{foam} = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, V_{disp} - m_{dry}}{\rho_{foam}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single equation is the **most-asked constructional problem** in KTU 2024 scheme university exams. Memorise it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Collapse Depth of a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Hull

> **Problem framing:**  
> A cylindrical aluminium pressure hull has inner radius $r_i$, wall thickness $t$, and aluminium yield strength $\sigma_y$. With a design safety factor $n$, derive the maximum rated operational depth.

### Step 1 — Maximum allowable hoop stress

The design must keep the working stress below the yield divided by the safety factor:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

### Step 2 — Thin-wall hoop-stress relation

For a thin-walled cylinder under external pressure $P$:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \, r}{t}$$

### Step 3 — Equate working stress to allowable stress

$$\frac{P_{max} \, r}{t} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $P_{max}$

$$P_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y \, t}{n \, r}$$

### Step 5 — Convert collapse pressure to depth using hydrostatic relation

Hydrostatic pressure grows linearly with depth:

$$P_{max} = \rho_{sw} \, g \, h_{max}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the maximum operational depth

$$h_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y \, t}{n \, r \, \rho_{sw} \, g}$$

> **Final Equation:**
>
> $$h_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y \, t}{n \, r \, \rho_{sw} \, g}$$

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example

Given: $\sigma_y = 240 \text{ MPa}$ (Al 6061-T6), $t = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$, $r = 0.10 \text{ m}$, $n = 2.0$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Compute the numerator:

$$\sigma_y \, t = (240 \times 10^6) \times 0.008 = 1.92 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$n \, r \, \rho_{sw} \, g = 2.0 \times 0.10 \times 1025 \times 9.81$$

$$\phantom{n \, r \, \rho_{sw} \, g} = 2.0 \times 0.10 \times 10055.25 = 2011.05 \text{ N/m}$$

Divide:

$$h_{max} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^6}{2011.05} \approx 954.7 \text{ m}$$

> **Result:** This 8 mm aluminium hull with 2× safety factor is rated to **≈ 955 m** — comfortably covering most continental-shelf ROV missions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Required to Cruise at Constant Velocity

> **Problem framing:**  
> An AUV cruises at velocity $v$. Its drag is given by $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{sw} C_D A v^2$. Derive the propulsive power required.

### Step 1 — Power is force times velocity

$$P_{prop} = F_D \cdot v$$

### Step 2 — Substitute drag

$$P_{prop} = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{sw} C_D A v^2\right) \cdot v$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$P_{prop} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{sw} C_D A v^3$$

> **Key Insight:** Cruise power scales as the **cube of velocity**. Doubling the speed requires **8× the power**. This is why AUVs are designed for slow, steady cruising (1–2 m/s), not sprinting.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Material Selection)

This matrix is the kind of comparative analysis KTU examiners reward with full marks in design questions.

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | Yield Strength (MPa) | Corrosion in SW | Cost | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------------|----------------------|-----------------|------|-------------|
| **Al 6061-T6** | 2700 | 240 | Moderate (needs coating) | Low | Shallow ROV frames |
| **Ti Grade 5** | 4430 | 880 | Excellent | Very High | Deep-sea pressure hulls (Alvin) |
| **HDPE** | 950 | 25 | Excellent | Very Low | Buoyancy block shells, frames |
| **SS 316L** | 8000 | 290 | Good (pitting risk) | High | Fasteners, brackets |
| **Acrylic (PMMA)** | 1190 | 70 | Excellent | Low | Viewports, instrument housings |
| **Syntactic Foam** | 400–700 | 5–25 (compressive) | Excellent | Medium | Buoyancy modules |

> **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Match the **operational depth** to the **hull-material yield**, then choose the **cheapest** material that satisfies it with safety factor ≥ 2.

---

## 3.5 KTU Numerical Practice Set (Worked Solutions)

### Numerical 1 — Foam Volume

> An ROV has dry mass $m_{dry} = 38 \text{ kg}$ and displaces $V_{disp} = 42 \text{ L} = 0.042 \text{ m}^3$. Syntactic foam of density $\rho_{foam} = 520 \text{ kg/m}^3$ is used. Find the volume of foam required for exact neutral buoyancy.

**Solution:**

$$V_{foam} = \frac{\rho_{sw} V_{disp} - m_{dry}}{\rho_{foam}} = \frac{1025 \times 0.042 - 38}{520}$$

$$= \frac{43.05 - 38}{520} = \frac{5.05}{520} \approx 0.0097 \text{ m}^3 \approx 9.7 \text{ L}$$

### Numerical 2 — Reserve Buoyancy

> If 10 L of foam (instead of 9.7 L) is added, what is the reserve buoyancy in %?

**Solution:**

$$F_b = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.042 = 422.2 \text{ N}$$

$$W = 38 \times 9.81 + 0.010 \times 520 \times 9.81 = 372.78 + 51.01 = 423.79 \text{ N}$$

$$R_b = \frac{F_b - W}{F_b} \times 100 = \frac{422.2 - 423.79}{422.2} \times 100 \approx -0.38\%$$

> **Interpretation:** A *negative* reserve buoyancy means the vehicle will **sink** if power fails. 9.7 L gives exact neutral; 10 L slightly over-ballasts. For 5 % reserve buoyancy, solve backwards: required foam ≈ **7.5 L** (i.e. *remove* mass via air-filled bladders — the KTU "trick" question).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Underwater Vehicle

The diagram below maps the **five-pillar constructional architecture** of a generic ROV/AUV, showing how each engineering pillar interacts with the central pressure hull and with mission subsystems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Hull Structure] --> nodeB[Pressure Hull Cylinder]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Hemispherical End Caps]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Frame Skeleton]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Buoyancy Module]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Thruster Bank]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Payload Bay]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Sealing System]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Syntactic Foam Blocks]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Oil Filled Bladder]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Vectored Horizontal Thrusters]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Lateral Sway Thruster]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Vertical Heave Thruster]
    nodeG --> nodeN[Battery Pack]
    nodeG --> nodeO[Control Computer]
    nodeG --> nodeP[IMU and Depth Sensor]
    nodeG --> nodeQ[Camera and LED Array]
    nodeH --> nodeR[O Ring Grooves]
    nodeH --> nodeS[Penetrator Cables]
    nodeH --> nodeT[Compensation Fluid Port]
    nodeA --> nodeU[Hydrodynamic Shell]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Streamlined Fairing]
    nodeU --> nodeW[Anti Fouling Coating]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The **Hull Structure** is the *root*; it branches into the **Pressure Hull**, **Buoyancy Module**, **Thruster Bank**, **Payload Bay**, and **Sealing System**. Each branch is a *constructional subsystem* — together they answer the KTU question: *"How is the vehicle built to survive, float, and swim?"*

---

## 4.2 Thruster Configuration Schematic (4-Thruster Vectored ROV)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[ROV Top View] --> node2[Front Right Vectored Thruster]
    node1 --> node3[Front Left Vectored Thruster]
    node1 --> node4[Rear Right Vectored Thruster]
    node1 --> node5[Rear Left Vectored Thruster]
    node1 --> node6[Center of Gravity G]
    node1 --> node7[Center of Buoyancy B Above G]
    node2 --> node8[Surge Force FX]
    node2 --> node9[Yaw Torque Mz]
    node4 --> node10[Surge Force FX]
    node4 --> node11[Yaw Torque Mz Opposite]
    node7 --> node12[Restoring Moment for Roll Stability]
```

> **Reading aid:** Four vectored thrusters around a central G–B vertical line give **surge (forward)** when fired together and **pure yaw (turning)** when fired differentially. B *above* G gives **passive roll stability**.

---

## 4.3 Constructional Subsystem Interaction Map (Textual Schematic)

This is a **block-level functional flow** showing how construction decisions cascade through the vehicle:

```
[ OPERATIONAL DEPTH TARGET ]
            |
            v
+---------------------------+
|   PRESSURE HULL DESIGN    |  -> Hoop stress -> Wall thickness -> Material
+---------------------------+
            |
            v
+---------------------------+
|   BUOYANCY MODULE SIZING  |  -> Foam volume -> Reserve buoyancy
+---------------------------+
            |
            v
+---------------------------+
|   HYDRODYNAMIC SHELL      |  -> Drag coefficient -> Cruise power -> Battery sizing
+---------------------------+
            |
            v
+---------------------------+
|   THRUSTER PLACEMENT      |  -> DOF coverage -> Static stability check
+---------------------------+
            |
            v
+---------------------------+
|   SEALING & PENETRATORS   |  -> O-ring count -> Cable gland rating
+---------------------------+
```

> This top-down chain is the **canonical KTU design methodology**. A question worded as *"Design an ROV for 500 m depth"* is solved by walking this chain step by step.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define syntactic foam and state TWO reasons why it is preferred as a buoyancy material in underwater vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Syntactic foam** is a composite material consisting of hollow glass or polymer microspheres (microballoons) dispersed in an epoxy or polyester resin matrix, producing a closed-cell, low-density, water-resistant solid.
>
> **Reasons for preference:**
> 1. **Low density (≈ 400 – 700 kg/m³)** with **high compressive strength**, giving positive buoyancy that survives hydrostatic compression at depth.
> 2. **Impervious to water absorption** (closed-cell structure), ensuring buoyancy does not change with depth or time — critical for **long-duration AUV missions**.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the two reasons)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Why is the hoop stress in a cylindrical pressure hull always greater than the longitudinal stress?**

**Model Answer:**

> In a thin-walled cylinder subjected to external pressure $P$, the wall resists by developing **circumferential (hoop) tension**. Force balance over a diametrical cut shows the total hoop force $2 \sigma_h t$ must balance the pressure acting on the projected area $2 r P$ per unit length, giving $\sigma_h = P r / t$. A similar balance along the axis gives only half this value: $\sigma_l = P r / (2 t)$. Thus, **the hoop direction is the most critical** and governs wall-thickness selection, material choice, and weld inspection.

*(3 marks — 2 for the force-balance argument, 1 for stating the design implication)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Buoyancy & Foam Sizing*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** An observation ROV has a dry mass (frame + hull + electronics) of **45 kg** and an external volume of **0.055 m³**. Syntactic foam of density **600 kg/m³** is to be added for buoyancy. Seawater density is **1025 kg/m³** and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

- **(i)** Calculate the volume of foam required for **exact neutral buoyancy**. (4 marks)
- **(ii)** If the designer wants a **6 % positive reserve buoyancy** at the surface, what new foam volume is needed? (3 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **thruster configuration** of a typical 4-thruster vectored ROV. Explain how **surge** and **yaw** motions are produced. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)(i): Neutral Buoyancy Foam Volume

The required foam volume formula (derived in Section 3.1) is:

$$V_{foam} = \frac{\rho_{sw} V_{disp} - m_{dry}}{\rho_{foam}}$$

Substitute values:

$$V_{foam} = \frac{1025 \times 0.055 - 45}{600} = \frac{56.375 - 45}{600} = \frac{11.375}{600}$$

$$\boxed{V_{foam} \approx 0.01896 \text{ m}^3 \approx 19.0 \text{ L}}$$

**Verification:** Mass of 19 L of foam = $0.019 \times 600 = 11.4$ kg.  
Total vehicle mass = $45 + 11.4 = 56.4$ kg.  
Buoyant force = $1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.055 = 553.2$ N.  
Weight = $56.4 \times 9.81 = 553.3$ N. ✓

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)(ii): Foam Volume for 6 % Reserve Buoyancy

**Reserve buoyancy equation:**

$$R_b = \frac{F_b - W}{F_b} \times 100 = 6\%$$

We need to find the **new $V_{foam}$** such that the foam's buoyant contribution exceeds the foam's weight by 6 % of total buoyancy.

Let foam volume be $V_f$. Then:

$$W_{total} = (m_{dry} + \rho_{foam} V_f) g$$
$$F_b = \rho_{sw} V_{disp} \, g$$

Set the relation:

$$F_b - W = 0.06 \, F_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = 0.94 \, F_b$$

So:

$$m_{dry} + \rho_{foam} V_f = 0.94 \, \rho_{sw} V_{disp}$$

$$V_f = \frac{0.94 \times 1025 \times 0.055 - 45}{600} = \frac{53.0 - 45}{600} = \frac{8.0}{600}$$

$$\boxed{V_f \approx 0.01333 \text{ m}^3 \approx 13.3 \text{ L}}$$

> **Counter-intuitive but important:** To get **positive** reserve buoyancy, you actually add **less** foam (or use *air-filled* components). The foam only adds *weight*; the buoyant force is fixed by the displaced volume.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b): 4-Thruster Vectored ROV

**Sketch (descriptive, to be drawn on answer sheet):**

```
            [FRONT]
              |
   TL (vectored 45°)    TR (vectored 45°)
        \                  /
         \    [PAYLOAD]   /
          \    [G]       /
           \   /  \     /
            \ /    \   /
             X      \ /
            / \      X
           /   \    / \
          /     \  /   \
         /       \/     \
   BL (vectored) [B above G] BR (vectored)
              [REAR]
```

*(TL = Top-Left, TR = Top-Right, BL = Bottom-Left, BR = Bottom-Right in the top-view.)*

**Surge (forward) motion:**
- All four thrusters fire **forward** simultaneously.
- Each contributes a **longitudinal force component** $F \cos(45°)$ in the surge direction.
- The lateral components cancel pairwise.
- Net surge force = $4 F \cos(45°) = 2\sqrt{2} F$.

**Yaw (turning) motion:**
- Diagonally opposite thrusters fire in **opposite directions** (e.g., TL forward, BR forward; TR and BR off — or one pair forward, the other pair reverse).
- This creates a **pure torque** about the vertical (z) axis with **no net surge**.
- Net yaw torque = $2 F \cos(45°) \cdot d$, where $d$ is the distance from CoG to the thruster.

**Stability:**
- Buoyancy block **above** CoG → passive restoring moment against roll/pitch disturbances.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Pressure Hull & Depth Rating*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** A cylindrical aluminium pressure hull for an AUV has **inner radius 90 mm** and **wall thickness 7 mm**. Aluminium alloy 6061-T6 has yield strength **240 MPa**. A safety factor of **2.0** is mandated. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

- **(i)** Derive the formula for the **maximum allowable operational depth** of a thin-walled cylinder. (4 marks)
- **(ii)** Calculate the maximum depth for the given hull. (3 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **principles of hydrodynamic design** for an AUV. Compare the streamlined *body-of-revolution* form with a *boxy* ROV frame in terms of drag, energy, and mission suitability. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)(i): Derivation of Maximum Depth Formula

*(This is a re-presentation of the derivation in Section 3.2, tailored to the marks distribution.)*

**Step 1 — Thin-wall hoop stress under external pressure $P$:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \, r}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply safety factor $n$:**

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{P_{max} \, r}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P_{max}$:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y \, t}{n \, r}$$

**Step 4 — Hydrostatic relation $P = \rho g h$:**

$$\rho_{sw} \, g \, h_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y \, t}{n \, r}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $h_{max}$:**

$$\boxed{h_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y \, t}{n \, r \, \rho_{sw} \, g}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)(ii): Numerical Depth Calculation

Substitute values:

$$h_{max} = \frac{(240 \times 10^6) \times 0.007}{2.0 \times 0.090 \times 1025 \times 9.81}$$

**Numerator:** $240 \times 10^6 \times 0.007 = 1.68 \times 10^6$ N/m

**Denominator:** $2.0 \times 0.090 \times 1025 \times 9.81 = 1810.04$ N/m

**Division:**

$$h_{max} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^6}{1810.04} \approx 928 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{max} \approx 928 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b): Hydrodynamic Design Principles

**1. Drag Minimisation:**
- Streamlined *bodies of revolution* (length/diameter ≈ 6 – 8) reduce the **form-drag coefficient $C_D$** to ≈ 0.05 – 0.10.
- Smooth surface finish (polished or coated) reduces **skin-friction drag $C_f$**.

**2. Propulsive Efficiency:**
- Total drag $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2$.
- Cruise power $P = F_D v = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^3$.
- Halving $C_D$ (by streamlining) halves the power needed for the same speed.

**3. Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Streamlined Body-of-Revolution AUV | Boxy ROV Frame |
|--------|------------------------------------|-----------------|
| Form-drag coefficient | 0.05 – 0.10 (very low) | 0.8 – 1.2 (high) |
| Cruise efficiency | Excellent (hours of operation) | Poor (rapid battery drain) |
| Payload volume | Limited (long thin shape) | Large (open frame) |
| Mission profile | Long-range survey, gliding | Station-keeping, intervention |
| Depth rating | Easy to pressurise (single hull) | Frame is open; only electronics sealed |
| Manufacturing | Difficult (precision turning) | Easy (extruded aluminium + bolts) |

> **Conclusion:** AUVs that must **travel far** demand streamlined hulls; ROVs that must **hover and manipulate** benefit from boxy frames with large payload bays. The construction principle is the same; the optimisation trade-off is different.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Using ρ = 1000 kg/m³ instead of 1025 kg/m³** for seawater. *Examiner will deduct 1 full mark silently — this is the most common slip.*
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor $n$** in collapse-depth derivations. Without it, your answer is *theoretically* correct but *engineering-wise* wrong, and the examiner knows it.
> 3. **Mixing up foam density and water density** in the buoyancy equation. Remember: foam **adds weight** while the displaced water volume (fixed) provides **buoyancy**. Adding *more* foam makes the vehicle *heavier*, not lighter.
> 4. **Confusing the roles of Centre of Gravity and Centre of Buoyancy** in stability. For a *submerged* body, G must lie **below** B for stability — this is the *opposite* of a surface ship's metacentre rule.
> 5. **Writing $|x|$ inside markdown tables** — the pipe breaks the table. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX form.
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical answers. KTU examiners allocate 0.5 marks per answer purely for correct units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Rapid-fire revision — print this and read it the night before the exam:**

- ✅ **Density of seawater = 1025 kg/m³** (NOT 1000)
- ✅ **Pressure gradient = 1 atm per 10 m depth** (use $\rho g h$ for precise value)
- ✅ **Buoyancy formula:** $F_b = \rho_{sw} g V_{disp}$
- ✅ **Neutral buoyancy condition:** Total weight = Buoyant force
- ✅ **Reserve buoyancy** = $(F_b - W)/F_b$ — usually **5 – 10 %** for ROVs
- ✅ **Hoop stress dominates** in cylinders: $\sigma_h = Pr/t$ (twice the longitudinal stress)
- ✅ **Collapse depth formula:** $h_{max} = \sigma_y t / (n r \rho_{sw} g)$
- ✅ **Drag scales with $v^2$; power scales with $v^3$**
- ✅ **Syntactic foam** = hollow microspheres in resin matrix — low density, depth-tolerant
- ✅ **For static stability of a submerged body:** $G$ must be **below** $B$ → heavy components at the **bottom**
- ✅ **Aluminium 6061-T6** = common shallow-ROV material; **Titanium Grade 5** = deep-sea
- ✅ **Safety factor $n \geq 2.0$** is a *must* in hull design — examiners always check this
- ✅ **4-thruster vectored ROV:** gives **surge + sway + heave + yaw** (4 DOF; roll and pitch are passively stable)
- ✅ **6-DOF AUV** = surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw
- ✅ **Cruise velocity of AUVs** is intentionally low (1–2 m/s) because of the $v^3$ power law
- ✅ **Wetted surface** governs friction drag; **frontal area** governs form drag
- ✅ **Streamlined body-of-revolution** has $C_D \approx 0.05$–$0.10$; **boxy frame** has $C_D \approx 0.8$–$1.2$
- ✅ **Best design methodology:** Depth target → hull design → buoyancy sizing → hydrodynamic shell → thruster placement → sealing

> **Final KTU Mantra:**  
> *"Survive the pressure, float with reserve, swim with minimum drag, place the heavy stuff low, and always carry a safety factor of two."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations for Moving Frame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equations for Moving Frame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equations for a Moving Frame** refer to the **six degrees-of-freedom (6-DOF) rigid-body equations of motion** of an underwater vehicle expressed in a **body-fixed (moving) reference frame** rather than in an Earth-fixed (inertial) frame. They couple the **kinematic equations** (relating body velocities to inertial rates) with the **kinetic/dynamic equations** (relating body forces and moments to body accelerations), incorporating rigid-body inertia, hydrodynamic added mass, Coriolis–centripetal effects, hydrodynamic damping, and gravitational/buoyancy restoring actions.

In underwater robotics, the moving frame is conventionally attached to the vehicle's center of gravity (CoG) or center of buoyancy (CoB). The standard notation used is the **SNAME (Society of Naval Architects and Marine Engineers) notation**, which KTU examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a moving frame?** Because an underwater vehicle is constantly translating and rotating, sensors (IMU, DVL, sonar), thrusters, and hydrodynamic forces are naturally expressed in the vehicle's own axes. Working in the moving body frame eliminates time-varying rotation matrices inside the dynamics, producing a tractable, time-invariant structure suitable for controller design (PID, LQR, Sliding Mode, MPC).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a small boat on a wavy lake. Your eyes naturally describe motion relative to the **boat** (you feel the wave's push), not relative to the Earth. A passenger in another boat would describe your motion in terms of north–south–east–west coordinates. Both descriptions are correct — they are simply expressed in **different reference frames**.

The **Earth-fixed (inertial) frame** `{n}` (also called the *NED frame* — North-East-Down) is the "passenger's view." The **body-fixed (moving) frame** `{b}` is the "boat's view." The *Equations for a Moving Frame* are the mathematical bridge that lets you:
1. Write **forces** (gravity, thrust, drag) in the boat's own coordinates.
2. Convert the resulting motion back into global coordinates for navigation and tracking.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the moving frame equations as the **robot's own internal diary** of how it is being pushed and turned — perfect for real-time control loops running at 50–1000 Hz onboard the AUV/ROV.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Medium AUV) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seawater density | $\rho$ | **1025** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Vehicle mass | $m$ | 30 – 500 | kg |
| Added-mass coefficient (surge) | $X_{\dot u}$ | 0.05 – 0.10 (non-dim) | — |
| Hydrodynamic damping (linear, surge) | $X_u$ | −70 to −200 | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| Hydrodynamic damping (quadratic, surge) | $X_{u\vert u \vert}$ | −90 to −300 | $\text{kg/m}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reference Frames in Underwater Robotics

Two reference frames dominate the analysis:

* **Earth-fixed (inertial / NED) frame $\{n\}$**: origin on the sea surface or a survey datum; axes aligned with North (X), East (Y), Down (Z).
* **Body-fixed (moving) frame $\{b\}$**: origin at the vehicle's CoG; $x_b$ points **forward (bow)**, $y_b$ points **starboard**, $z_b$ points **downward**.

The **state vectors** used (SNAME notation):

$$
\eta = \begin{bmatrix} x & y & z & \phi & \theta & \psi \end{bmatrix}^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}
$$

$$
\nu = \begin{bmatrix} u & v & w & p & q & r \end{bmatrix}^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}
$$

where:
- $(x, y, z)$ — position of $\{b\}$ in $\{n\}$
- $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ — **roll, pitch, yaw** (Euler angles, ZYX convention)
- $(u, v, w)$ — **surge, sway, heave** linear velocities (in body frame)
- $(p, q, r)$ — roll, pitch, yaw angular rates (in body frame)

## 2.2 The Two Coupled Sets of Equations

The moving-frame formulation is split into **two** complementary sets:

### A. Kinematic Equations (Geometric)
They map body velocities $\nu$ to inertial rates $\dot \eta$ through a rotation/transformation Jacobian $J(\eta)$:

$$
\dot \eta = J(\eta)\,\nu
$$

For an Euler-angle (ZYX) parameterisation:

$$
J(\eta) = \begin{bmatrix} R_b^n(\phi,\theta,\psi) & 0_{3\times 3} \\[4pt] 0_{3\times 3} & T_b^n(\phi,\theta) \end{bmatrix}
$$

with

$$
R_b^n = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

and

$$
T_b^n = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & s\phi t\theta & c\phi t\theta \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ 0 & s\phi/c\theta & c\phi/c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $c \equiv \cos$, $s \equiv \sin$, $t \equiv \tan$.

### B. Kinetic (Dynamic) Equations (Newton–Euler)
In the moving body frame, the **general 6-DOF equation of motion** of an underwater vehicle is:

$$
\underbrace{M_{RB}\,\dot \nu}_{\text{Rigid-body inertia}} + \underbrace{C_{RB}(\nu)\,\nu}_{\text{Rigid-body Coriolis}} + \underbrace{M_A\,\dot \nu}_{\text{Added-mass inertia}} + \underbrace{C_A(\nu)\,\nu}_{\text{Added-mass Coriolis}} + \underbrace{D(\nu)\,\nu}_{\text{Hydrodynamic damping}} + \underbrace{g(\eta)}_{\text{Restoring (gravity/buoyancy)}} = \underbrace{\tau}_{\text{Control inputs + disturbances}}
$$

where $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^6$ contains thruster forces/moments and environmental loads (current, waves).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Term | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinematics | $\dot \eta = J(\eta)\,\nu$ | Body → inertial rate transformation |
| 2 | Rigid-body mass matrix | $M_{RB} = \text{diag}(m I_{3\times 3},\; I_o)$ | Mass + inertia tensor in $\{b\}$ |
| 3 | Rigid-body Coriolis | $C_{RB}(\nu) = \begin{bmatrix} 0_{3\times 3} & -m S(v) \\ -m S(v) & -S(I_o \omega) \end{bmatrix}$ | Centripetal / gyroscopic coupling |
| 4 | Added-mass matrix | $M_A = -\text{diag}(X_{\dot u},Y_{\dot v},Z_{\dot w},K_{\dot p},M_{\dot q},N_{\dot r})$ | Fluid inertia due to acceleration |
| 5 | Total inertia | $M = M_{RB} + M_A$ | Effective mass of vehicle + entrained fluid |
| 6 | Total Coriolis | $C(\nu) = C_{RB}(\nu) + C_A(\nu)$ | Combined rotational effects |
| 7 | Linear damping | $D_{lin} = \text{diag}(X_u, Y_v, Z_w, K_p, M_q, N_r)$ | Skin friction + linear wave damping |
| 8 | Quadratic damping | $D_{nl}(\nu) = \text{diag}(X_{u\vert u \vert}\vert u\vert,\ldots)$ | Form-drag / vortex shedding |
| 9 | Restoring force | $g(\eta) = \begin{bmatrix} (W - B) s\theta \\ -(W - B) c\theta s\phi \\ -(W - B) c\theta c\phi \\ -y_G W c\theta c\phi + z_G W c\theta s\phi \\ z_G W s\theta + x_G W c\theta c\phi \\ -x_G W c\theta s\phi - y_G W s\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | Gravity $W$ minus buoyancy $B$, acting at CoG/CoB offset |
| 10 | Thruster allocation | $\tau = B_u\,u$ | $B_u$ — thruster configuration matrix; $u$ — commanded RPM |
| 11 | Cross-product operator | $S(\lambda) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\lambda_3 & \lambda_2 \\ \lambda_3 & 0 & -\lambda_1 \\ -\lambda_2 & \lambda_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | Skew-symmetric mapping $\lambda \times \cdot$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid:** *KCDMG-τ* → **K**inematics, **C**oriolis, **D**amping, **M**ass, **g** (restoring), $\tau$ (inputs). The KTU paper almost always demands that you write this single line in full.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The moving-frame equations are the **workhorse** of:

1. **On-board guidance, navigation and control (GNC)** of AUVs/ROVs — the controller (PID, backstepping, MPC) uses $\nu$ and $\dot\nu$ computed from these equations at kHz rates.
2. **Dynamic positioning (DP)** of submerged platforms.
3. **Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulation** for marine autonomy (e.g., the Stonefish simulator).
4. **Hydrodynamic identification** — system identification of $M_A$, $D$, and $g(\eta)$ from sea-trials.
5. **Energy-aware path planning** — predicting $\nu(t)$ to estimate thruster energy consumption.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's Law in $\{n\}$ to the Moving-Frame Equation

### Step 1 — Write Newton's second law in the inertial frame

For a rigid body of mass $m$ and inertia tensor $I_o$:

$$
m\,\ddot p^n = f^n, \qquad I_o\,\dot \omega^b + \omega^b \times (I_o \omega^b) = m^b
$$

Here $p^n$ is the position vector in $\{n\}$, $\omega^b$ the angular velocity in $\{b\}$, $f^n$ the total external force in $\{n\}$, and $m^b$ the total external moment in $\{b\}$.

### Step 2 — Transform linear acceleration to $\{b\}$

Using the velocity transformation $\dot p^n = R_b^n(\Theta)\,v^b$, differentiate:

$$
\ddot p^n = R_b^n(\Theta)\left[\dot v^b + \omega^b \times v^b\right]
$$

Substitute back into Newton's law and pre-multiply by $R_n^b = (R_b^n)^{T}$:

$$
m\left[\dot v^b + \omega^b \times v^b\right] = R_n^b\,f^n
$$

Define $f^b = R_n^b\,f^n$ and use the cross-product operator $S(\omega^b)$:

$$
m\,\dot v^b + m\,S(\omega^b)\,v^b = f^b
$$

### Step 3 — Derive the rotational equation in $\{b\}$

Euler's equation in $\{b\}$:

$$
I_o\,\dot \omega^b + S(\omega^b)\,I_o\,\omega^b = m^b
$$

### Step 4 — Assemble into one 6-DOF vector equation

Stack linear and rotational parts into $\nu = [v^b;\,\omega^b]$:

$$
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} m I_{3\times 3} & 0 \\ 0 & I_o \end{bmatrix}}_{M_{RB}} \dot \nu + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} m S(\omega^b) & 0 \\ 0 & S(\omega^b I_o) \end{bmatrix}}_{C_{RB}(\nu)} \nu = \begin{bmatrix} f^b \\ m^b \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **rigid-body moving-frame equation**.

### Step 5 — Add hydrodynamic terms (the underwater-specific step)

In a fluid, an accelerating body drags a portion of fluid with it. By applying potential-flow theory, this gives an **added mass** $M_A$. The body also experiences **Coriolis-like** forces from added mass, $C_A(\nu)$, and viscous damping $D(\nu)$. The full model is therefore:

$$
(M_{RB} + M_A)\,\dot \nu + \left[C_{RB}(\nu) + C_A(\nu)\right]\nu + D(\nu)\,\nu + g(\eta) = \tau
$$

$$
\boxed{\; M\,\dot \nu + C(\nu)\,\nu + D(\nu)\,\nu + g(\eta) = \tau \;}
$$

with $M = M_{RB} + M_A$, $C(\nu) = C_{RB}(\nu) + C_A(\nu)$.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of the Skew-Symmetric Coriolis Operator

For a vector $v = [v_1, v_2, v_3]^T$ and a skew-symmetric $S(v)$:

$$
v \times a = S(v)\,a,\qquad S(v) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -v_3 & v_2 \\ v_3 & 0 & -v_1 \\ -v_2 & v_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Verification:

$$
S(v)\,a = \begin{bmatrix} v_2 a_3 - v_3 a_2 \\ v_3 a_1 - v_1 a_3 \\ v_1 a_2 - v_2 a_1 \end{bmatrix} \;\equiv\; v \times a
$$

For the rotational part, expand $S(\omega^b) I_o \omega^b$ entry-wise. Let $I_o = \text{diag}(I_x, I_y, I_z)$. Then:

$$
S(\omega)\,I_o\,\omega = \begin{bmatrix} (I_z - I_y)\,q\,r \\ (I_x - I_z)\,r\,p \\ (I_y - I_x)\,p\,q \end{bmatrix}
$$

Hence the rotational equation becomes the classical Euler equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_x\,\dot p + (I_z - I_y)\,q\,r &= K^b \\
I_y\,\dot q + (I_x - I_z)\,r\,p &= M^b \\
I_z\,\dot r + (I_y - I_x)\,p\,q &= N^b
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Restoring (Gravitational–Buoyancy) Vector $g(\eta)$

Let $W = mg$ (weight) act at CoG $= [x_G, y_G, z_G]^T$ in $\{b\}$, and $B = \rho g \nabla$ (buoyancy) act at CoB $= [x_B, y_B, z_B]^T$.

The **force component** is simply $f_g = f_W + f_B$, but only the projection onto $\{b\}$ matters:

$$
f_g^b = R_n^b \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ W \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} W s\theta \\ -W c\theta s\phi \\ -W c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -B \end{bmatrix}
$$

The **moment component** is $r_G \times f_W + r_B \times f_B$:

$$
m_g^b = r_G \times R_n^b \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ W \end{bmatrix} + r_B \times \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -B \end{bmatrix}
$$

Carrying out the cross-products entry by entry and setting $W - B$ as a single symbol yields the **canonical SNAME restoring vector** (as listed in row 9 of §2.3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $(W = B)$ — neutrally buoyant — the force terms vanish. When $x_G = y_G = z_G = x_B = y_B = z_B = 0$ (CoG = CoB = origin), the moment terms also vanish. KTU board questions frequently use these neutrally-buoyant, coincident-Center simplifications.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem.** An AUV of mass $m = 50\,\text{kg}$ moves with surge velocity $u = 1.2\,\text{m/s}$. Linear damping in surge is $X_u = -80\,\text{kg/s}$, quadratic damping is $X_{u\vert u \vert} = -120\,\text{kg/m}$. The thruster delivers $X_{\tau} = 90\,\text{N}$. Compute the surge acceleration $\dot u$.

**Solution.** For pure surge motion in still water with no restoring forces:

$$
m\,\dot u + X_u\,u + X_{u\vert u \vert}\,u \vert u\vert = X_{\tau}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
50\,\dot u + (-80)(1.2) + (-120)(1.2)(1.2) &= 90 \\[4pt]
50\,\dot u - 96 - 172.8 &= 90 \\[4pt]
50\,\dot u &= 90 + 96 + 172.8 = 358.8 \\[4pt]
\dot u &= \frac{358.8}{50} = 7.176\,\text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The positive thrust (90 N) and opposing drag (combined 268.8 N) result in a net forward acceleration of **7.18 m/s²**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Moving-Frame Equation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference Inputs\ndesired trajectory eta_d"] --> B["Guidance Module\nproduces eta_ref, nu_ref"]
    B --> C["Controller\nPID / SMC / MPC"]
    C --> D["Thruster Allocation\nBu matrix, RPM commands"]
    D --> E["Actuators\nPropellers / Rudders"]
    E --> F["Vehicle Body 6-DOF\nmoving frame b"]
    F -- "nu, eta" --> G["Sensors\nIMU, DVL, depth, GPS"]
    G -- "measurements" --> C
    F -- "Hydrodynamic Forces" --> H["M(dot nu) + C(nu)nu + D(nu)nu + g(eta) = tau"]
    H -- "feedback" --> C
    F -- "eta" --> B
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal Structure of the Moving-Frame Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Forces["Forces and Moments Acting on Body"]
        F1["Rigid-body inertia\nM_RB * dot nu"]
        F2["Added-mass inertia\nM_A * dot nu"]
        F3["Coriolis-centripetal\nC_RB + C_A times nu"]
        F4["Hydrodynamic damping\nD_lin + D_quadratic"]
        F5["Restoring (gravity/buoyancy)\ng of eta"]
        F6["External disturbances\ncurrents, waves"]
        F7["Control inputs tau\nthrusters, fins"]
    end
    F1 --> SUM((" = "))
    F2 --> SUM
    F3 --> SUM
    F4 --> SUM
    F5 --> SUM
    F6 --> SUM
    F7 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT["nu and eta\n6-DOF motion of AUV"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence: Kinematic and Kinetic Coupling

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant N as Inertial Frame n
    participant J as Transformation J(eta)
    participant B as Body Frame b
    participant M as Mass Matrix M
    participant C as Coriolis C(nu)
    participant D as Damping D(nu)
    participant G as Restoring g(eta)
    participant T as Thrusters tau

    B->>J: supply nu (body rates)
    J->>N: return dot eta (inertial rates)
    N->>J: integrate dot eta
    J->>B: return eta (pose of body)
    B->>M: nu, dot nu
    B->>C: nu
    B->>D: nu
    B->>G: eta
    T->>B: tau
    M->>B: M dot nu (inertia reaction)
    C->>B: C nu (coupling reaction)
    D->>B: D nu (viscous reaction)
    G->>B: g eta (gravity + buoyancy)
```

## 4.4 Physical Diagram Description (Block-Level Schematic)

If a physical figure of an AUV with body axes is required by the KTU paper, the following block-level schematic captures the conceptual geometry:

```mermaid

        direction LR
        XB["+x_b  (forward / bow)"]
        YB["+y_b  (starboard / right)"]
        ZB["+z_b  (downward)"]
        XB --- YB
        YB --- ZB
        ZB --- XB
    end
    subgraph NED["Earth Frame n (NED)"]
        direction LR
        XN["+X_n  (North)"]
        YN["+Y_n  (East)"]
        ZN["+Z_n  (Down)"]
        XN --- YN
        YN --- ZN
        ZN --- XN
    end
    NED -- "R_b_n(phi, theta, psi)" --> AUV
```
