# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the moving reference frame used in the analysis of underwater vehicles. Why is it preferred over the inertial frame for controller design?

**[Model Answer]** The **moving (body-fixed) reference frame $\{b\}$** has its origin at the vehicle's center of gravity (CoG), with $x_b$ pointing forward (bow), $y_b$ pointing starboard, and $z_b$ pointing downward, following the SNAME notation. It is preferred for controller design because:
1. **Forces and moments** from thrusters, hydrodynamics, gravity, and buoyancy are naturally expressed in $\{b\}$.
2. The **inertia tensor** $I_o$ becomes time-invariant (no rotation matrix inside it).
3. **Sensor measurements** (IMU, DVL) are natively in $\{b\}$.
4. The dynamic equations are **time-invariant**, simplifying controller synthesis (e.g., pole placement, LQR).

> **Marks distribution:** Definition (1.5) + reasons (1.5).

---

### Q2. List the SNAME notation for the 6-DOF state variables of an AUV and write the general form of the moving-frame equation.

**[Model Answer]**

Linear velocities: $u$ (surge), $v$ (sway), $w$ (heave).

Angular velocities: $p$ (roll rate), $q$ (pitch rate), $r$ (yaw rate).

Positions/orientations: $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$.

General moving-frame equation:

$$
M\,\dot \nu + C(\nu)\,\nu + D(\nu)\,\nu + g(\eta) = \tau
$$

with $\nu = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$ and $\tau = [X, Y, Z, K, M, N]^T$.

> **Marks distribution:** List (1) + Equation (2).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the rigid-body 6-DOF equation of motion of an underwater vehicle in the moving body-fixed frame, starting from Newton's second law in the Earth-fixed inertial frame. Clearly show the transformation of linear acceleration using the rotation matrix $R_b^n$ and the skew-symmetric operator $S(\cdot)$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Starting from the rigid-body equation, incorporate the hydrodynamic added mass, Coriolis–centripetal, viscous damping, and gravitational–buoyancy restoring terms to obtain the **complete moving-frame equation** for an AUV. Explain the physical meaning of each term. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1.** Newton's law for translation in $\{n\}$:

$$
m\,\ddot p^n = f^n
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $\dot p^n = R_b^n\,v^b$:

$$
\ddot p^n = R_b^n(\dot v^b + \omega^b \times v^b) = R_b^n(\dot v^b + S(\omega^b)v^b)
$$

**Step 3.** Pre-multiply by $R_n^b$:

$$
m\,\dot v^b + m\,S(\omega^b)v^b = f^b
$$

**Step 4.** Euler's rotational equation in $\{b\}$:

$$
I_o\,\dot \omega^b + S(\omega^b)I_o\omega^b = m^b
$$

**Step 5.** Stack into 6-DOF vector form:

$$
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} m I & 0 \\ 0 & I_o \end{bmatrix}}_{M_{RB}}\dot \nu + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} m S(\omega^b) & 0 \\ 0 & S(I_o\omega^b) \end{bmatrix}}_{C_{RB}(\nu)}\nu = \begin{bmatrix} f^b \\ m^b \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Marks distribution:** Newton setup (1.5), acceleration transform (2), skew operator explanation (1.5), Euler rotation (1), final assembly (1).

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

Step-by-step addition of hydrodynamic effects:

| Step | Added term | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_A\,\dot \nu$ | Inertia of fluid entrained with vehicle (added mass) |
| 2 | $C_A(\nu)\,\nu$ | Coriolis-centripetal from added mass |
| 3 | $D(\nu)\,\nu$ | Viscous skin friction + form drag (linear and quadratic) |
| 4 | $g(\eta)$ | Gravity + buoyancy forces/moments |
| 5 | RHS $= \tau$ | Sum of thruster + disturbance forces |

Final combined equation:

$$
\boxed{(M_{RB} + M_A)\dot \nu + [C_{RB}(\nu) + C_A(\nu)]\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau}
$$

Letting $M = M_{RB} + M_A$ and $C = C_{RB} + C_A$:

$$
M\dot \nu + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau
$$

> **Marks distribution:** Each term (1 mark × 5 = 5 marks), explanation (2 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the kinematic equation $\dot \eta = J(\eta)\,\nu$ for an underwater vehicle. Using the ZYX Euler-angle convention, write out the explicit form of the rotation matrix $R_b^n$ and the angular-velocity transformation $T_b^n$. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a neutrally buoyant AUV ($W = B$) with CoG coincident with CoB, derive the restoring vector $g(\eta)$. Show that for small roll and pitch angles it reduces to a linear function of $\phi$ and $\theta$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1.** The body-fixed angular rates $(p,q,r)$ and the inertial Euler-angle rates $(\dot\phi,\dot\theta,\dot\psi)$ are related by:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -s\theta \\ 0 & c\phi & c\theta s\phi \\ 0 & -s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \dot\phi \\ \dot\theta \\ \dot\psi \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2.** Invert to obtain:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot\phi \\ \dot\theta \\ \dot\psi \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & s\phi t\theta & c\phi t\theta \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ 0 & s\phi/c\theta & c\phi/c\theta \end{bmatrix}}_{T_b^n(\phi,\theta)}\begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.** Linear part:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot x \\ \dot y \\ \dot z \end{bmatrix} = R_b^n(\phi,\theta,\psi)\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ w \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4.** Assemble:

$$
\dot\eta = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} R_b^n & 0_{3\times 3} \\ 0_{3\times 3} & T_b^n \end{bmatrix}}_{J(\eta)}\nu
$$

with $R_b^n$ expanded entry-wise as in §2.2.

> **Marks distribution:** Angular relation (2), inversion (2), linear part (1), full Jacobian assembly (2).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1.** Starting from row 9 of §2.3, with $W = B$ and $(x_G, y_G, z_G) = (x_B, y_B, z_B) = 0$:

$$
g(\eta) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 0_{6\times 1}
$$

**Step 2.** Hence for the perfectly neutrally buoyant, coincident-Center case, **all restoring terms vanish identically**, regardless of orientation. The vehicle is in *neutral equilibrium*.

**Step 3.** If we relax the condition to $W \neq B$ but still keep CoG = CoB = origin, then using small-angle approximations ($s\phi \approx \phi$, $s\theta \approx \theta$, $c\phi \approx 1$, $c\theta \approx 1$):

$$
g(\eta) \approx \begin{bmatrix} (W - B)\theta \\ -(W - B)\phi \\ -(W - B) \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

which is **linear** in $\phi$ and $\theta$. This linearisation is widely used in **depth and heading control design**.

> **Marks distribution:** Setting W = B and CoG = CoB (2), evaluating g = 0 (2), small-angle expansion (3).

---

## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on moving-frame questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the skew-symmetric operator** $S(\omega^b)$ in the Coriolis term — marks deducted directly.
> 2. **Mixing up Euler-angle conventions** (ZYX vs. XYZ). KTU follows **ZYX (yaw-pitch-roll)**. Using the wrong matrix is an instant 3-mark cut.
> 3. **Writing SNAME symbols inconsistently** — always use lowercase for body rates $(u,v,w,p,q,r)$ and uppercase for forces/moments $(X,Y,Z,K,M,N)$.
> 4. **Omitting the kinematic equation** $\dot\eta = J(\eta)\nu$ and jumping straight to dynamics. Examiners allocate at least 2 marks to this.
> 5. **Confusing $M_A$ (added mass) with $M_{RB}$ (rigid-body mass)**. Always write $M = M_{RB} + M_A$ explicitly.
> 6. **Failing to define $\tau$** as containing both control inputs and environmental disturbances.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert between frames** in $g(\eta)$ derivation — gravity is in $\{n\}$ but the restoring vector is in $\{b\}$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two coupled equations** define the moving-frame model: kinematic ($\dot\eta = J(\eta)\nu$) and kinetic ($M\dot\nu + C\nu + D\nu + g = \tau$).
- **SNAME notation is sacred:** always $(u,v,w,p,q,r)$ for body rates and $(x,y,z,\phi,\theta,\psi)$ for inertial pose.
- **Rigid-body Coriolis** uses the skew-symmetric operator $S(\omega^b)$; never omit it.
- **Underwater-specific terms** — added mass $M_A$ and added Coriolis $C_A(\nu)$ — differentiate marine vehicles from aerial/ground robots.
- **Restoring vector $g(\eta)$** depends on $W - B$ and on the geometric offset of CoG from CoB.
- **Damping** has both linear ($D_{lin}$) and quadratic ($D_{nl}$) components; the quadratic term dominates at higher speeds.
- **ZYX Euler-angle convention** is the KTU default; expect to draw $R_b^n$ explicitly.
- **Neutral equilibrium** occurs when $W = B$ and CoG = CoB — restoring terms vanish.
- **The moving frame is essential for real-time control** because it keeps the inertia tensor $I_o$ time-invariant.
- **Final compact form** to memorise:
$$
M\dot\nu + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau
$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rigid Motion in a Plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rigid Motion in a Plane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rigid Motion in a Plane (Planar Rigid Body Motion)** is defined as the configuration change of a rigid body in the two-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that the distance between any two points on the body remains invariant at all instants of time. Mathematically, a rigid motion is an isometry of the form $g: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ that preserves both the Euclidean distance metric and the orientation of the body (for proper rigid motions).

The configuration space of an unconstrained rigid body moving in a plane is the **Special Euclidean Group $SE(2)$**, which is the semi-direct product of the rotation group $SO(2)$ and the translation group $\mathbb{R}^2$.

$$
SE(2) = SO(2) \ltimes \mathbb{R}^2
$$

A configuration $q \in SE(2)$ is parameterized as the homogeneous transformation matrix:

$$
q = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $(x, y)$ is the position of the body's reference frame origin in the world frame, and $\theta$ is the orientation (yaw) angle of the body.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a small toy submarine moving across the surface of a calm lake (the 2D plane). A **rigid motion** describes how this toy submarine can *translate* (slide forward/backward, left/right) and *rotate* (turn left or right) without any of its parts bending or deforming. The toy stays exactly the same shape — its propeller is always the same distance from its tail-fin — no matter where it goes or how it turns.

In underwater robotics, **Rigid Motion in a Plane** is the foundation for describing the motion of *surface vehicles* (USVs — Unmanned Surface Vehicles) and for the *horizontal-plane projection* of Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) trajectories. The AUV essentially traces a 2D rigid-body trajectory when viewed from above.

## 1.3 Degrees of Freedom in the Plane

A rigid body moving in a plane possesses exactly **3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**:

| DOF | Symbol | Physical Meaning | KTU-Standard Units |
|:----|:-------|:-----------------|:-------------------|
| Surge | $x$ | Translation along global X-axis | **meters (m)** |
| Sway | $y$ | Translation along global Y-axis | **meters (m)** |
| Yaw | $\theta$ | Rotation about global Z-axis | **radians (rad)** |

## 1.4 Why "Rigid" Matters in Underwater Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rigidity Assumption** is critical in underwater robotics because:
> 1. It allows us to model an AUV hull as a single reference point + orientation, simplifying control laws.
> 2. It ensures kinematic consistency: sensor readings (DVL, IMU) can be rigidly transformed to the vehicle's center of mass.
> 3. It forms the mathematical backbone of **SE(2)-based motion planning** algorithms for subsea intervention tasks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Components of Planar Rigid Motion

Every planar rigid motion can be uniquely decomposed into a **pure rotation** followed by a **pure translation** (or vice versa). This decomposition is non-commutative in general.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic Steps:**
> 1. **Translation Component** — Captures how the body's reference frame origin moves in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
> 2. **Rotation Component** — Captures how the body's local axes align with the world frame.
> 3. **Composition** — The body-fixed frame $B$ transforms to world frame $W$ via $T_{W}^{B} = \text{Translation} \cdot \text{Rotation}$.
> 4. **Closure Property** — The composition of two planar rigid motions is itself a planar rigid motion (group closure under $SE(2)$).
> 5. **Identity Element** — The zero-translation + zero-rotation configuration is the group identity.

## 2.2 Properties of the 2D Rotation Matrix

The $2 \times 2$ rotation matrix $R(\theta) \in SO(2)$ satisfies three fundamental properties:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Property 1 — Orthogonality:**

$$
R(\theta)^T R(\theta) = I_2
$$

**Property 2 — Unit Determinant:**

$$
\det(R(\theta)) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1
$$

**Property 3 — Closure under Multiplication:**

$$
R(\theta_1) R(\theta_2) = R(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

## 2.3 The Twist / Velocity Vector

The instantaneous velocity of a rigid body in the plane is described by a **twist** $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (v_x, v_y, \omega)^T \in \mathbb{R}^3$, where $v_x, v_y$ are translational velocities and $\omega = \dot{\theta}$ is the angular (yaw) rate.

The relationship between the body-fixed twist $\boldsymbol{\xi}^{B}$ and the world-frame twist $\boldsymbol{\xi}^{W}$ is given by the **Adjoint map** of $SE(2)$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\xi}^{W} = \text{Ad}_{g} \, \boldsymbol{\xi}^{B} = \begin{bmatrix} R(\theta) & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In underwater robotics, the twist vector directly maps to **AUV thruster commands** — surge force $\propto v_x$, sway force $\propto v_y$, and yaw moment $\propto \omega$. This is the foundation of the **Newton-Euler equations of motion** used in AUV dynamic simulators.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Domain |
|:--|:--------|:--------|:---------------|
| 1 | Planar configuration | $q = (x, y, \theta) \in SE(2)$ | Position (m), Angle (rad) |
| 2 | 2D Rotation matrix | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | Unitless |
| 3 | Homogeneous transform | $T = \begin{bmatrix} R(\theta) & t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $t = (x,y)^T$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 4 | Inverse transform | $T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 5 | Composition rule | $T_{ac} = T_{ab} \cdot T_{bc}$ | Group product |
| 6 | Twist vector | $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (v_x, v_y, \omega)^T$ | m/s, m/s, rad/s |
| 7 | Adjoint map | $\text{Ad}_g = \begin{bmatrix} R & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 8 | Angular velocity skew | $[\omega]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega \\ \omega & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | rad/s |
| 9 | Pose differential | $\dot{T} = T \cdot \widehat{\boldsymbol{\xi}^{B}}$ | — |
| 10 | Jacobian (chain rule) | $\dot{q} = \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = (v_x, v_y, \omega)^T$ | — |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The 2D Rotation Matrix from First Principles

**Starting Point:** Consider a unit vector $\hat{e}_1 = (1, 0)^T$ attached to a rigid body. After a counter-clockwise rotation by angle $\theta$ about the origin, its new coordinates in the world frame are $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)^T$.

**Step 1 — Express the rotated basis vectors.**

The rotated image of the body-fixed X-axis unit vector $\hat{e}_1^B = (1, 0)^T$ in the world frame is:

$$
\hat{e}_1^{W} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

The rotated image of the body-fixed Y-axis unit vector $\hat{e}_2^B = (0, 1)^T$ in the world frame is:

$$
\hat{e}_2^{W} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Stack the basis vectors as columns of the rotation matrix.**

By the standard convention, the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ is constructed by placing the world-frame coordinates of each body-fixed basis vector as its columns:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \vert & \vert \\ \hat{e}_1^{W} & \hat{e}_2^{W} \\ \vert & \vert \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the orthogonality property.**

Compute $R(\theta)^T R(\theta)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(\theta)^T R(\theta) &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta & -\cos\theta\sin\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta \\ -\sin\theta\cos\theta + \cos\theta\sin\theta & \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms $R(\theta) \in SO(2)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse of a Planar Homogeneous Transform

**Given:** A planar rigid-body configuration

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Partition the matrix.**

Let $R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ and $t = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$. Then $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2 — Propose the inverse form.**

For a block lower-triangular-like matrix, the inverse is:

$$
T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^{-1} & -R^{-1} t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the fact that $R^{-1} = R^T$ (orthogonal).**

$$
R^{-1} = R^T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $-R^{-1} t$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-R^{-1} t &= -\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= -\begin{bmatrix} x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta \\ -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} -x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta \\ x\sin\theta - y\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the full inverse.**

$$
T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & -x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & x\sin\theta - y\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This represents the configuration of the world frame as observed from the body frame.

## 3.3 Derivation: Composition of Two Planar Rigid Motions

**Given:**
- $T_1$ places frame 1 in the world frame.
- $T_2$ places frame 2 in frame 1.

**Goal:** Find $T_3$ that places frame 2 in the world frame.

**Step 1 — Write $T_1$ and $T_2$ explicitly.**

$$
T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} R_1 & t_1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} R_2 & t_2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply the matrices.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= T_1 \cdot T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} R_1 & t_1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} R_2 & t_2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} R_1 R_2 & R_1 t_2 + t_1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret physically.**

- The new orientation $R_3 = R_1 R_2 = R(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ is the sum of the two angles.
- The new position $t_3 = R_1 t_2 + t_1$ shows that the second translation is first rotated by $R_1$ before being added to $t_1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example:** An AUV first rotates by $\theta_1 = 90°$, then translates by $(1, 0)^T$ in its own body frame. The world-frame translation is $R(90°)(1,0)^T + t_1 = (0, 1)^T + t_1$. This demonstrates **why the rotation order matters** — the result is sensitive to whether rotate-then-translate or translate-then-rotate is performed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Flow: Planar Rigid Motion Pipeline for AUVs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["World Frame W Origin at Inertial Reference"] --> B["Global Pose q equals x y theta"]
    C["Body Frame B Fixed to AUV Hull"] --> D["Local Twist vx vy omega"]
    B --> E["Homogeneous Transform T 3x3"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["Rotation Subblock R theta 2x2"]
    E --> G["Translation Subblock t equals x y"]
    F --> H["SO 2 Group Operations"]
    G --> I["R2 Translation Group"]
    H --> J["Adjoint Map for Velocity Transform"]
    I --> J
    D --> J
    J --> K["AUV Thruster Allocation Matrix"]
    K --> L["Underwater Actuator Outputs"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: SE(2) Operations on Underwater Vehicles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Pose Input q in SE 2"] --> M1["Composition Module T ac equals T ab T bc"]
    P2["Pose Input q in SE 2"] --> M1
    M1 --> O1["Output Pose q ac in SE 2"]
    P3["Pose Input q in SE 2"] --> M2["Inverse Module T inv block"]
    M2 --> O2["Output Pose q inverse in SE 2"]
    P4["Pose Input q in SE 2"] --> M3["Interpolation Module SLERP for theta and LERP for xy"]
    M3 --> O3["Intermediate Pose for Trajectory Tracking"]
    O1 --> AUV["AUV Motion Controller"]
    O2 --> AUV
    O3 --> AUV
    AUV --> OUT["Actuator Signals to Thrusters and Rudders"]
```

## 4.3 State-Transition Schematic: Pose Update Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Initial Pose qk at time tk"] --> S1["Compute Twist xi from IMU and DVL"]
    S1 --> S2["Apply Exponential Map q k plus 1 equals qk times exp xi delta t"]
    S2 --> S3["New Pose q k plus 1 in SE 2"]
    S3 --> S4{"Check Mission Goal Reached"}
    S4 -- No --> S1
    S4 -- Yes --> S5["Stop and Surface AUV"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### Q1. Define the configuration space $SE(2)$ for planar rigid body motion and state its dimension.

**Model Answer:**
The configuration space $SE(2)$ is the Special Euclidean Group of the plane, defined as the semi-direct product $SE(2) = SO(2) \ltimes \mathbb{R}^2$. It is the set of all rigid-body configurations in 2D, parameterized by position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$. The dimension of $SE(2)$ as a smooth manifold is **3**, corresponding to the three degrees of freedom of an unconstrained rigid body in a plane: surge $x$, sway $y$, and yaw $\theta$.

### Q2. What is the determinant of the 2D rotation matrix $R(\theta)$? Why is it equal to 1?

**Model Answer:**
The determinant of $R(\theta)$ is:

$$
\det(R(\theta)) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1
$$

It equals 1 because rotation preserves both the area and orientation of objects — it is an orientation-preserving isometry. This constraint (along with orthogonality) defines the special orthogonal group $SO(2)$. A determinant of $-1$ would correspond to an improper rotation (reflection), which is excluded from rigid motions that preserve the chirality (handedness) of the rigid body — a critical property for underwater vehicles whose sensor calibration depends on consistent orientation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice — Select ONE of the following alternatives.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**An Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) starts at world-frame pose $T_1$ with position $(2, 1)$ meters and orientation $\theta_1 = 60°$. It then executes a body-frame translation of $(3, -1)$ meters followed by a rotation of $\theta_2 = 45°$. Compute the final world-frame pose $T_{\text{final}}$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build $T_1$.**

$$
T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 60° & -\sin 60° & 2 \\ \sin 60° & \cos 60° & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 2 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Build $T_2$ (rotation by $45°$ with translation $(3, -1)$ in body frame).**

$$
T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 45° & -\sin 45° & 3 \\ \sin 45° & \cos 45° & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & -0.707 & 3 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_{\text{final}} = T_1 \cdot T_2$.**

Extract $R_1, t_1, R_2, t_2$:

- $R_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$, $t_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- $R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & -0.707 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$, $t_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$

Compute $R_1 R_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 R_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} (0.5)(0.707) + (-0.866)(0.707) & (0.5)(-0.707) + (-0.866)(0.707) \\ (0.866)(0.707) + (0.5)(0.707) & (0.866)(-0.707) + (0.5)(0.707) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.354 - 0.612 & -0.354 - 0.612 \\ 0.612 + 0.354 & -0.612 + 0.354 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} -0.259 & -0.966 \\ 0.966 & -0.259 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to $\theta_{\text{final}} = 105°$ (verified by $\arctan2(0.966, -0.259)$).

Compute $R_1 t_2 + t_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 t_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 + 0.866 \\ 2.598 - 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.366 \\ 2.098 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
R_1 t_2 + t_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.366 + 2 \\ 2.098 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.366 \\ 3.098 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final pose.**

$$
T_{\text{final}} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.259 & -0.966 & 4.366 \\ 0.966 & -0.259 & 3.098 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Final position: $(4.37, 3.10)$ m, Final orientation: $\theta \approx 105°$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Prove that the inverse of a planar homogeneous transformation $T \in SE(2)$ exists, and derive its closed-form expression. Also explain its physical meaning for an AUV.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Existence proof.**

For any $T \in SE(2)$, the rotation block $R \in SO(2)$ satisfies $R^{-1} = R^T$ (because $R^T R = I$). The lower-left block is the zero row vector $0^T$, whose inverse (as a scalar $1$) exists trivially. The lower-right block is the scalar $1$, which is non-zero. By the block-matrix inverse formula for lower-block-triangular matrices, $T^{-1}$ exists and is unique.

**Step 2 — Closed-form derivation.**

Given $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, applying the block inverse formula:

$$
T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^{-1} & -R^{-1} t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical meaning for an AUV.**

$T^{-1}$ represents the pose of the **world frame as observed from the AUV's body frame**. For an AUV, this is essential because:
1. **Sensor Fusion:** Onboard sensors (DVL, IMU) report measurements in the body frame. $T^{-1}$ is used to transform these into world-frame coordinates for navigation.
2. **Relative Localization:** When two AUVs cooperate, $T_1^{-1} T_2$ computes the relative pose — a direct application of the inverse.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the homogeneous transformation matrix representing a pure rotation by angle $\theta$ about the origin in 2D. Then, using this derivation, show that $R(\theta_1) \cdot R(\theta_2) = R(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pure rotation matrix construction.**

A pure rotation (no translation) is represented by the $3 \times 3$ matrix:

$$
R_{\text{hom}}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the composition law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(\theta_1) R(\theta_2) &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2 & -\cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 & 0 \\ \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the angle addition identities:

- $\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2$
- $\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2$

The product reduces to:

$$
R(\theta_1) R(\theta_2) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = R(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

This proves the **closure property** of $SO(2)$ and demonstrates that successive rotations are additive.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An AUV's heading (yaw) is currently $\theta = 30°$. The vehicle applies a rotational velocity of $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s for $t = 10$ seconds. Compute the final orientation using the exponential map for $SO(2)$. Discuss why the exponential map is preferred over matrix multiplication for real-time AUV control.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the exponential map for $SO(2)$.**

For any $\omega \in \mathbb{R}$ and $t \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$
\exp([\omega]_\times t) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\omega t) & -\sin(\omega t) \\ \sin(\omega t) & \cos(\omega t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $[\omega]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega \\ \omega & 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the rotation angle.**

$$
\Delta\theta = \omega \cdot t = 0.2 \times 10 = 2.0 \text{ rad}
$$

Convert to degrees: $2.0 \text{ rad} \times (180/\pi) \approx 114.59°$.

**Step 3 — Compute the final orientation.**

$$
\theta_{\text{final}} = \theta_0 + \Delta\theta = 30° + 114.59° = 144.59°
$$

The final rotation matrix is:

$$
R(144.59°) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(144.59°) & -\sin(144.59°) \\ \sin(144.59°) & \cos(144.59°) \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.812 & -0.583 \\ 0.583 & -0.812 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Why the exponential map is preferred.**

1. **Computational Efficiency:** Only one trig pair computation per integration step, vs. full $3 \times 3$ matrix multiplication.
2. **Numerical Stability:** The exponential map is intrinsically on $SO(2)$ — no drift or renormalization needed.
3. **Direct Twist Integration:** It directly converts a body-frame twist $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (v_x, v_y, \omega)^T$ into a pose update via $q(t+\Delta t) = q(t) \cdot \exp(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\xi}} \Delta t)$.
4. **Real-Time Compatibility:** Embedded AUV controllers running at 50–100 Hz benefit from the reduced floating-point operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Rigid Motion Problems:**
> 1. **Order of Multiplication Error (–3 marks):** Students frequently write $T_2 \cdot T_1$ when the question demands $T_1 \cdot T_2$. Remember: in $SE(2)$ composition, $T_{ac} = T_{ab} \cdot T_{bc}$ reads **right-to-left** in terms of frame transformations.
> 2. **Sign Confusion in $R(\theta)$ (–2 marks):** The off-diagonal $-\sin\theta$ in the top-right position is often misplaced. Always derive from the basis-vector convention.
> 3. **Forgetting Homogeneous Coordinate (–1 mark):** When applying $T$ to a 2D point $p = (x, y)^T$, students forget to use $\tilde{p} = (x, y, 1)^T$.
> 4. **Radian vs. Degree Mix-up (–2 marks):** Trigonometric functions in derivations **must** be in radians; final answers can be reported in either, but the formula sheet usage is strict.
> 5. **Inverse Formula Sign Error (–3 marks):** Forgetting the negation $-R^T t$ in $T^{-1}$ is a high-frequency error — examiners specifically test this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Configuration Space:** Planar rigid motion lives in $SE(2)$, a **3-dimensional** smooth manifold.
- **Degrees of Freedom:** Exactly **3 DOF** — surge ($x$), sway ($y$), and yaw ($\theta$).
- **Homogeneous Transform:** Always $3 \times 3$ with bottom row $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Rotation Block:** Must be orthogonal with $\det = 1$; lives in $SO(2)$.
- **Composition Rule:** $T_{ac} = T_{ab} \cdot T_{bc}$ — **matrix multiplication on the LEFT composes on the RIGHT in terms of frames**.
- **Inverse Formula:** $T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — always derived via block inversion.
- **Closure Property:** $R(\theta_1) R(\theta_2) = R(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$.
- **Twist Vector:** $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (v_x, v_y, \omega)^T$ — links pose derivatives to AUV actuator commands.
- **Exponential Map:** $R(\theta) = \exp([\theta]_\times)$ — preferred for real-time embedded AUV control.
- **Underwater Robotics Link:** Directly applicable to **USV navigation** and the **horizontal-plane projection** of **AUV trajectory tracking**.
- **Group Properties to Memorize:** Closure, associativity, identity ($I_3$), and invertibility — the four group axioms of $SE(2)$.
- **Rigid Body Assumption:** All distances between body points remain constant — this is what makes "rigid motion" **rigid**.
- **Adjoint Map:** Transforms body-frame velocities to world-frame velocities: $\boldsymbol{\xi}^W = \text{Ad}_g \, \boldsymbol{\xi}^B$.
- **Pose Update Loop:** $q_{k+1} = q_k \cdot \exp(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\xi}} \Delta t)$ — the workhorse equation of any AUV simulator.
- **Numerical Safeguard:** Always renormalize $R$ periodically by enforcing $R \leftarrow U V^T$ from SVD, to prevent drift in long missions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of a Rotated Frame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representation of a Rotated Frame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of underwater robotics, a **rotated frame** refers to a coordinate frame whose origin coincides with (or is translated from) a reference frame, but whose axes are rotated by some orientation in 3D space. Mathematically, the orientation of a rotated frame $\{B\}$ with respect to a reference frame $\{A\}$ is described by a **3 × 3 Rotation Matrix** ${}^A_B R \in SO(3)$, where $SO(3)$ denotes the **Special Orthogonal Group** of dimension 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A valid rotation matrix $R$ must satisfy two properties: (1) $R^T R = I$ (orthonormality) and (2) $\det(R) = +1$ (right-handedness). Together these place $R$ in $SO(3)$, the group of all proper rotations in 3D space.

The rotated frame is essential for describing the **6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** pose of an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) or Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) — 3 for position $(x, y, z)$ and 3 for orientation (roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Submarine Steering Wheel**
> Imagine you are sitting inside a submarine moving through the ocean. Your submarine has its own internal coordinate frame — its own "front," "left," and "up." The Earth (or a fixed sonar beacon) has its own frame — say, North, East, and Down. The relationship between *your submarine's orientation* and *the Earth's frame* is exactly a "rotated frame." Every time the pilot tilts the vessel, the rotation matrix updates to reflect the new orientation. The math of the rotated frame simply encodes the answer to the question: **"If I point a sensor forward in my submarine, in which direction in the world does it actually point?"**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Gravity vector (underwater):** $\mathbf{g} \approx \mathbf{9.81 \, m/s^2}$ (effective value is reduced by buoyancy; net specific force is typically **0.2–0.5 m/s²** for a neutrally buoyant AUV).
- **Earth's rotation rate:** $\omega_e \approx \mathbf{7.2921 \times 10^{-5} \, rad/s}$ (negligible for short-duration AUV missions but considered in long-range inertial navigation).
- **Standard underwater frame convention:** Right-handed, with **X** pointing forward (bow), **Y** pointing starboard (right), **Z** pointing down.

### 1.4 Why Do We Need a Rotated Frame Representation?

| Application in Underwater Robotics | Purpose of Rotated Frame |
|---|---|
| **Sonar beam steering** | Transform beam direction from vehicle frame to world frame |
| **Thruster allocation** | Decompose desired world-frame forces into body-frame thruster commands |
| **Manipulator control (ROV arm)** | Express end-effector pose relative to vehicle or world |
| **INS/DVL fusion** | Convert Doppler Velocity Log readings from body frame to navigation frame |
| **Pipeline tracking** | Align forward-looking sonar imagery with world map |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Elementary Rotations

A rotated frame is built from three **elementary rotations**, each about one principal axis of the reference frame. Following the right-hand rule, the standard positive rotations are:

- **Roll ($\phi$)** — rotation about the **X-axis** (longitudinal axis of the AUV)
- **Pitch ($\theta$)** — rotation about the **Y-axis** (lateral/transverse axis)
- **Yaw ($\psi$)** — rotation about the **Z-axis** (vertical axis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these three?** In underwater robotics, the AUV's body frame is conventionally aligned so that the principal axes of rotation coincide with the vehicle's roll (along the hull), pitch (wing/fin axis), and yaw (vertical through the center of buoyancy). This convention simplifies the equations of motion and matches physical sensor placement (IMU, gyros, accelerometers).

### 2.2 Elementary Rotation Matrices

For a rotation by angle $\alpha$ about axis $i \in \{x, y, z\}$:

$$R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.3 Composite Rotations — The Roll-Pitch-Yaw (RPY) Convention

A general rotated frame is described by composing the three elementary rotations. The **ZYX (Yaw-Pitch-Roll)** convention — the standard in mobile robotics and underwater navigation — is:

$$R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$$

Expanded explicitly:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

where $c\alpha = \cos\alpha$ and $s\alpha = \sin\alpha$ are the standard shorthand trigonometric notations.

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic: Why This Order?

1. **Start with the identity frame** (vehicle frame aligned with world frame).
2. **Roll first ($R_x(\phi)$):** Tilt the vehicle about its longitudinal axis. Internal frame now points in a new direction.
3. **Pitch next ($R_y(\theta)$):** Rotate the now-rolled frame about the (already rotated) Y-axis. This corresponds to a *body-fixed* pitch motion.
4. **Yaw last ($R_z(\psi)$):** Finally rotate about the (twice-rotated) Z-axis to point the bow in the desired compass heading.
5. **Result:** The product $R_z R_y R_x$ maps a vector expressed in the body frame into the world (navigation) frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Matrix Multiplication Order Matters:** $R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi) \neq R_x(\phi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi)$. In KTU exams, marks are deducted if students apply rotations in the wrong order. Always remember: **the rightmost matrix is applied first** to the vector.

### 2.5 Properties of the Rotation Matrix

- **Orthonormality:** $R^{-1} = R^T$  →  $R^T R = I$
- **Determinant:** $\det(R) = +1$
- **Identity:** $R(0) = I_{3\times 3}$ (the 3×3 identity matrix)
- **Group closure:** The product of two rotation matrices is again a rotation matrix: $R_1, R_2 \in SO(3) \Rightarrow R_1 R_2 \in SO(3)$

### 2.6 Inverse Mapping: Extracting RPY from a Given Rotation Matrix

Given a rotation matrix $R = [r_{ij}]$, the Euler angles are recovered as:

$$\theta = \text{atan2}(-r_{31}, \sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2})$$

$$\phi = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, r_{33})$$

$$\psi = \text{atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Gimbal Lock Alert:** When $\cos\theta \to 0$ (i.e., $\theta = \pm 90°$), the values of $\phi$ and $\psi$ become coupled and cannot be independently determined. This is the famous **gimbal lock** singularity. In underwater robotics, this corresponds to the AUV pitching vertically — a configuration that must be carefully handled in controller design.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Condition / Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Roll rotation | $R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$ | $\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$ | rad |
| 2 | Pitch rotation | $R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ | rad |
| 3 | Yaw rotation | $R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\psi \in [0, 2\pi)$ | rad |
| 4 | Composite RPY | $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ | All three valid | unitless |
| 5 | Inverse rotation | $R^{-1} = R^T$ | $R \in SO(3)$ | unitless |
| 6 | Vector transform | $^A v = {}^A_B R \cdot {}^B v$ | Rigid rotation | m/s or any |
| 7 | Gimbal lock | $\cos\theta = 0$ | $\theta = \pm \pi/2$ | rad |
| 8 | Position offset | $^A p = {}^A_B R \cdot {}^B p + {}^A p_{B,\text{org}}$ | General 6-DOF | m |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AUV Navigation:** Rotation matrices fuse IMU, DVL, and USBL (Ultra-Short Baseline) acoustic positioning into a consistent world-frame trajectory.
- **ROV Manipulator Kinematics:** The Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) parameters of a robotic arm use sequential rotated frames to compute end-effector pose.
- **Sonar Image Rectification:** Rotated-frame math removes vehicle motion artifacts from side-scan sonar imagery, producing clean seabed maps.
- **Thruster Command Allocation:** 6 thrusters producing forces in body frame are mapped to desired world-frame forces using ${}^B_A R = ({}^A_B R)^T$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Elementary Rotation Matrix $R_z(\psi)$ About the Z-Axis

We want to find the matrix that takes a 2D point $(x, y)$ in the plane and rotates it counter-clockwise by angle $\psi$ to a new point $(x', y')$.

**Step 1: Set up the original coordinates.**

Let the original point be at distance $r$ from the origin, making angle $\alpha$ with the X-axis:

$$x = r\cos\alpha, \quad y = r\sin\alpha$$

**Step 2: After rotation by $\psi$ counter-clockwise, the new angle is $\alpha + \psi$.**

$$x' = r\cos(\alpha + \psi), \quad y' = r\sin(\alpha + \psi)$$

**Step 3: Apply the angle-addition trigonometric identities.**

$$\cos(\alpha + \psi) = \cos\alpha \cos\psi - \sin\alpha \sin\psi$$
$$\sin(\alpha + \psi) = \sin\alpha \cos\psi + \cos\alpha \sin\psi$$

**Step 4: Substitute back to express in terms of $x$ and $y$.**

$$x' = r(\cos\alpha \cos\psi - \sin\alpha \sin\psi) = x\cos\psi - y\sin\psi$$
$$y' = r(\sin\alpha \cos\psi + \cos\alpha \sin\psi) = x\sin\psi + y\cos\psi$$

**Step 5: Write in matrix form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Extend to 3D by leaving the Z-coordinate unchanged.**

$$R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation: The Composite Roll-Pitch-Yaw Matrix

Starting with $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$:

**Step 1: First compute $R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$.**

$$R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

Carrying out the multiplication row by column:

$$= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta\sin\phi & \sin\theta\cos\phi \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Now left-multiply by $R_z(\psi)$.**

$$R_z(\psi) \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta\sin\phi & \sin\theta\cos\phi \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute each of the 9 entries.**

For element $(1,1)$: $\cos\psi \cdot \cos\theta + (-\sin\psi) \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-\sin\theta) = \cos\psi \cos\theta$

For element $(1,2)$: $\cos\psi \cdot \sin\theta\sin\phi + (-\sin\psi) \cdot \cos\phi + 0 \cdot \cos\theta\sin\phi = \cos\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi - \sin\psi \cos\phi$

For element $(1,3)$: $\cos\psi \cdot \sin\theta\cos\phi + (-\sin\psi) \cdot (-\sin\phi) + 0 \cdot \cos\theta\cos\phi = \cos\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi + \sin\psi \sin\phi$

For element $(2,1)$: $\sin\psi \cdot \cos\theta + \cos\psi \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-\sin\theta) = \sin\psi \cos\theta$

For element $(2,2)$: $\sin\psi \cdot \sin\theta\sin\phi + \cos\psi \cdot \cos\phi + 0 \cdot \cos\theta\sin\phi = \sin\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi + \cos\psi \cos\phi$

For element $(2,3)$: $\sin\psi \cdot \sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\psi \cdot (-\sin\phi) + 0 \cdot \cos\theta\cos\phi = \sin\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi - \cos\psi \sin\phi$

For element $(3,1)$: $0 \cdot \cos\theta + 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot (-\sin\theta) = -\sin\theta$

For element $(3,2)$: $0 \cdot \sin\theta\sin\phi + 0 \cdot \cos\phi + 1 \cdot \cos\theta\sin\phi = \cos\theta \sin\phi$

For element $(3,3)$: $0 \cdot \sin\theta\cos\phi + 0 \cdot (-\sin\phi) + 1 \cdot \cos\theta\cos\phi = \cos\theta \cos\phi$

**Step 4: Assemble the final composite matrix.**

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: Vector Transformation in an AUV

**Problem:** A sonar in an AUV's body frame measures a contact at ${}^B p = (10, 0, 2)$ meters. The vehicle has roll $\phi = 0°$, pitch $\theta = 30°$, yaw $\psi = 45°$. Find the world-frame position ${}^A p$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Substitute the angles.**

$\phi = 0°$ implies $\cos\phi = 1, \sin\phi = 0$.
$\theta = 30°$ implies $\cos\theta = \sqrt{3}/2, \sin\theta = 1/2$.
$\psi = 45°$ implies $\cos\psi = \sqrt{2}/2, \sin\psi = \sqrt{2}/2$.

**Step 2: Substitute into the RPY matrix.**

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} (\sqrt{2}/2)(\sqrt{3}/2) & (\sqrt{2}/2)(1/2)(0) - (\sqrt{2}/2)(1) & (\sqrt{2}/2)(1/2)(1) + (\sqrt{2}/2)(0) \\ (\sqrt{2}/2)(\sqrt{3}/2) & (\sqrt{2}/2)(1/2)(0) + (\sqrt{2}/2)(1) & (\sqrt{2}/2)(1/2)(1) - (\sqrt{2}/2)(0) \\ -1/2 & (\sqrt{3}/2)(0) & (\sqrt{3}/2)(1) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Simplify numerically.**

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{6}/4 & -\sqrt{2}/2 & \sqrt{2}/4 \\ \sqrt{6}/4 & \sqrt{2}/2 & \sqrt{2}/4 \\ -1/2 & 0 & \sqrt{3}/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Apply the transformation ${}^A p = R \cdot {}^B p$.**

$$^A p = R \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (\sqrt{6}/4)(10) + (-\sqrt{2}/2)(0) + (\sqrt{2}/4)(2) \\ (\sqrt{6}/4)(10) + (\sqrt{2}/2)(0) + (\sqrt{2}/4)(2) \\ (-1/2)(10) + (0)(0) + (\sqrt{3}/2)(2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate each component.**

$$^A p_x = 10 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{6}}{4} + 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} = \frac{10\sqrt{6} + 2\sqrt{2}}{4} = \frac{5\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 6.82 \text{ m}$$

$$^A p_y = 10 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{6}}{4} + 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} = \frac{5\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 6.82 \text{ m}$$

$$^A p_z = -5 + \sqrt{3} \approx -3.27 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Verify the magnitude is preserved.**

$\vert{}^B p\vert = \sqrt{10^2 + 0^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{104} \approx 10.20 \text{ m}$
$\vert{}^A p\vert = \sqrt{6.82^2 + 6.82^2 + (-3.27)^2} = \sqrt{46.5 + 46.5 + 10.7} = \sqrt{103.7} \approx 10.18 \text{ m}$ ✓

The small discrepancy is due to rounding — the rotation matrix preserves the magnitude exactly, confirming the derivation is correct.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rotated-Frame Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Reference Frame Inertial or Earth Fixed] --> A1[Define World Frame Axes Xw Yw Zw]
    B0[Vehicle Body Frame Origin at AUV CG] --> B1[Define Body Axes Xb Yb Zb aligned with hull]
    A1 --> C1[Compute Elementary Rotations Rx phi, Ry theta, Rz psi]
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Apply Roll about Xb]
    C2 --> C3[Apply Pitch about new Y axis]
    C3 --> C4[Apply Yaw about newest Z axis]
    C4 --> D1[Composite Rotation Matrix R equals Rz Ry Rx]
    D1 --> E1[Transform Sensor Vector Body to World]
    E1 --> F1[Sonar Contact Position in World Frame]
    E1 --> F2[Thruster Force Decomposition]
    E1 --> F3[Manipulator End Effector Pose]
    D1 --> G1[Inverse Mapping R transpose to recover Euler angles]
    G1 --> H1[Extract phi, theta, psi for Controller Feedback]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of 3D Frame Rotations for an AUV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inertial[NED Inertial Frame]
        I1[Xn North]
        I2[Yn East]
        I3[Zd Down]
    end
    subgraph Body[AUV Body Frame after Rotation]
        V1[Xb Bow]
        V2[Yb Starboard]
        V3[Zb Keel]
    end
    I1 -- Yaw psi about Z --> V1
    I2 -- Pitch theta about Y --> V2
    I3 -- Roll phi about X --> V3
```

### 4.3 Orientation Representation Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Orientation of AUV] --> M1{Choose Representation}
    M1 --> M2[Rotation Matrix 3x3 in SO3]
    M1 --> M3[Roll Pitch Yaw three angles]
    M1 --> M4[Axis Angle one axis and one angle]
    M1 --> M5[Quaternion four components]
    M2 --> N1[Pros Smooth, used in transforms]
    M2 --> N2[Cons 9 parameters with 6 constraints]
    M3 --> N3[Pros Intuitive, used by pilots]
    M3 --> N4[Cons Suffers gimbal lock at plus or minus 90 deg pitch]
    M4 --> N5[Pros Compact, no singularity except 180 deg]
    M4 --> N6[Cons Less intuitive]
    M5 --> N7[Pros No gimbal lock, efficient SLERP interpolation]
    M5 --> N8[Cons Non unique q and minus q]
```

### 4.4 Control Flow: From Sensor Reading to World-Frame Output

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[IMU Gyroscope reads angular velocity in body frame] --> S2[Integrate to get orientation increment]
    S2 --> S3[Update rotation matrix R current]
    S3 --> S4{Is pitch near 90 deg}
    S4 --> S5[Yes Apply anti gimbal lock blending]
    S4 --> S6[No Use standard RPY extraction]
    S5 --> S7[Output phi, theta, psi to controller]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Compute desired thrust in world frame]
    S8 --> S9[Apply R transpose to get body frame thruster commands]
    S9 --> S10[Send PWM signals to thrusters]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define a rotation matrix and list its two essential properties that qualify it as a member of the Special Orthogonal Group $SO(3)$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rotation matrix** ${}^A_B R$ is a $3 \times 3$ real matrix that describes the orientation of a rotated frame $\{B\}$ with respect to a reference frame $\{A\}$. The two essential properties are:

1. **Orthonormality:** $R^T R = I_{3\times 3}$ (columns and rows are mutually orthonormal unit vectors).
2. **Right-handedness (proper rotation):** $\det(R) = +1$.

Together these define $R \in SO(3)$, the group of all valid rigid-body rotations in 3D space.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Write the elementary rotation matrix $R_y(\theta)$ representing a rotation by angle $\theta$ about the Y-axis. State the convention used (active vs. passive, right-hand rule).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Convention used:** Right-hand rule with positive $\theta$ following the curl of the fingers of the right hand when the thumb points along the positive Y-axis. This is an **active rotation** — it rotates vectors in a fixed frame, equivalently it transforms a vector expressed in the body frame to the world frame.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the elementary rotation matrix for a rotation of angle $\psi$ about the Z-axis, $R_z(\psi)$, starting from the basic trigonometric identities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An underwater ROV has roll $\phi = 30°$, pitch $\theta = 0°$, yaw $\psi = 60°$. A manipulator end-effector is located at $^B p = (1, 0.5, 0.2)$ m in the body frame. Compute the position of the end-effector in the world frame. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** See Section 3.1 above for the complete derivation. The key steps are:

- Express original coordinates: $x = r\cos\alpha, \, y = r\sin\alpha$.
- Apply rotation by $\psi$: $x' = r\cos(\alpha+\psi), \, y' = r\sin(\alpha+\psi)$.
- Expand using angle-addition formulas: $\cos(\alpha+\psi) = \cos\alpha\cos\psi - \sin\alpha\sin\psi$, $\sin(\alpha+\psi) = \sin\alpha\cos\psi + \cos\alpha\sin\psi$.
- Substitute and write in matrix form, then extend to 3D by leaving Z unchanged.

$$R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

**(b)** Step 1: Substitute the angles.

$\phi = 30°$: $\cos\phi = \sqrt{3}/2, \sin\phi = 1/2$.
$\theta = 0°$: $\cos\theta = 1, \sin\theta = 0$.
$\psi = 60°$: $\cos\psi = 1/2, \sin\psi = \sqrt{3}/2$.

Step 2: Build the composite RPY matrix.

$$R = R_z(60°) R_y(0°) R_x(30°) = R_z(60°) R_x(30°)$$

(since $R_y(0°) = I$).

Step 3: Compute $R_x(30°)$ and $R_z(60°)$.

$$R_x(30°) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{3}/2 & -1/2 \\ 0 & 1/2 & \sqrt{3}/2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_z(60°) = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & -\sqrt{3}/2 & 0 \\ \sqrt{3}/2 & 1/2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 4: Compute the product $R = R_z(60°) R_x(30°)$.

Element $(1,1)$: $(1/2)(1) + (-\sqrt{3}/2)(0) + 0 = 1/2$
Element $(1,2)$: $(1/2)(0) + (-\sqrt{3}/2)(\sqrt{3}/2) + 0 = -3/4$
Element $(1,3)$: $(1/2)(0) + (-\sqrt{3}/2)(-1/2) + 0 = \sqrt{3}/4$
Element $(2,1)$: $(\sqrt{3}/2)(1) + (1/2)(0) + 0 = \sqrt{3}/2$
Element $(2,2)$: $(\sqrt{3}/2)(0) + (1/2)(\sqrt{3}/2) + 0 = \sqrt{3}/4$
Element $(2,3)$: $(\sqrt{3}/2)(0) + (1/2)(-1/2) + 0 = -1/4$
Element $(3,1)$: $0 + 0 + 1 \cdot 0 = 0$
Element $(3,2)$: $0 + 0 + 1 \cdot 1/2 = 1/2$
Element $(3,3)$: $0 + 0 + 1 \cdot \sqrt{3}/2 = \sqrt{3}/2$

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & -3/4 & \sqrt{3}/4 \\ \sqrt{3}/2 & \sqrt{3}/4 & -1/4 \\ 0 & 1/2 & \sqrt{3}/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 5: Apply ${}^A p = R \cdot {}^B p$.

$$^A p = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & -3/4 & \sqrt{3}/4 \\ \sqrt{3}/2 & \sqrt{3}/4 & -1/4 \\ 0 & 1/2 & \sqrt{3}/2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$^A p_x = (1/2)(1) + (-3/4)(0.5) + (\sqrt{3}/4)(0.2) = 0.5 - 0.375 + 0.0866 \approx 0.2116 \text{ m}$$

$$^A p_y = (\sqrt{3}/2)(1) + (\sqrt{3}/4)(0.5) + (-1/4)(0.2) \approx 0.866 + 0.2165 - 0.05 \approx 1.0325 \text{ m}$$

$$^A p_z = (0)(1) + (1/2)(0.5) + (\sqrt{3}/2)(0.2) = 0 + 0.25 + 0.1732 \approx 0.4232 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $^A p \approx (0.212, \, 1.033, \, 0.423)$ m **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of gimbal lock in Euler angle representation. Under what condition does it occur, and what is its physical significance for an underwater vehicle? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the rotation matrix below, extract the roll ($\phi$), pitch ($\theta$), and yaw ($\psi$) angles.

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & 0 & 0.5 \\ 0.25 & 0.866 & -0.433 \\ -0.433 & 0.5 & 0.75 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Gimbal Lock Explanation (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Gimbal lock is a singularity in the Euler-angle (RPY) representation of orientation in which two of the three rotational axes align, causing a loss of one degree of rotational freedom. Mathematically, it occurs when $\cos\theta = 0$, i.e., when the pitch angle $\theta = \pm 90°$.

**Mathematical Condition:** Recall the extraction formulas:

$$\theta = \text{atan2}(-r_{31}, \sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2})$$

When $\theta = \pm 90°$, the term under the square root becomes $r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2 = \cos^2\theta = 0$, and the expression is undefined (or reduces to zero). The formulas for $\phi$ and $\psi$ then both reduce to $\text{atan2}(\text{any}, 0)$, which collapses $\phi$ and $\psi$ into a single combined angle — one degree of freedom is lost.

**Physical Significance for Underwater Vehicles:**

- When an AUV pitches to $\pm 90°$, its bow points straight up or straight down.
- The roll and yaw rotations now both occur about the same effective axis.
- For an ROV performing a vertical pipeline inspection, this configuration is a real and frequent scenario.
- Controllers using RPY feedback may produce wild, oscillatory commands near gimbal lock.
- **Mitigation:** Use quaternions, switch to a different Euler convention (e.g., XYZ) near the singularity, or apply **quaternion-based SLERP (Spherical Linear Interpolation)** for smooth motion.

**(b)** **Extraction of Euler Angles (7 marks):**

Step 1: Identify the matrix elements.

$r_{11} = 0.866, \, r_{21} = 0.25, \, r_{31} = -0.433, \, r_{32} = 0.5, \, r_{33} = 0.75$.

Step 2: Compute the pitch.

$$\theta = \text{atan2}(-r_{31}, \sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2}) = \text{atan2}(0.433, \sqrt{0.866^2 + 0.25^2}) = \text{atan2}(0.433, \sqrt{0.75 + 0.0625})$$

$$= \text{atan2}(0.433, \sqrt{0.8125}) = \text{atan2}(0.433, 0.9014) \approx 0.4482 \text{ rad} \approx 25.66°$$

Step 3: Compute the roll.

$$\phi = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, r_{33}) = \text{atan2}(0.5, 0.75) \approx 0.5880 \text{ rad} \approx 33.69°$$

Step 4: Compute the yaw.

$$\psi = \text{atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11}) = \text{atan2}(0.25, 0.866) \approx 0.2810 \text{ rad} \approx 16.10°$$

**Final Answer:** $\phi \approx 33.69°$, $\theta \approx 25.66°$, $\psi \approx 16.10°$.

Step 5: Verification (sanity check).

Substituting back into the composite RPY formula should reproduce the original matrix. Since $\cos\theta = 0.9014 \neq 0$, the configuration is **not** in gimbal lock, and the extracted angles are valid and unique.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Order of Multiplication:** Many students write $R_x R_y R_z$ instead of $R_z R_y R_x$. This is the most common mistake. **Remember:** in $R_z R_y R_x$, the rightmost rotation is applied first to the vector.
> 2. **Forgetting the Constraint $\det(R) = +1$:** A general orthogonal matrix can also have determinant $-1$ (an *improper* rotation, including a reflection). Always check $\det(R)$.
> 3. **Confusing Active and Passive Rotations:** Active rotation rotates the vector; passive rotation rotates the coordinate frame. The transpose relationship is the giveaway: ${}^B_A R = ({}^A_B R)^T$.
> 4. **Gimbal Lock Blindness:** In numerical problems, students happily divide by $\cos\theta$ even when it is zero. Always test $\cos\theta > \epsilon$ before extracting roll and yaw.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Angles in radians vs. degrees — KTU expects radians in all calculus and trig operations, even if the final answer is reported in degrees.
> 6. **Sign Convention:** Right-hand rule vs. left-hand rule. KTU uses the right-hand rule consistently; an AUV's body frame is **right-handed** with X-forward, Y-starboard, Z-down.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A rotated frame is described by a 3×3 rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$ that maps coordinates from the body frame to the reference (world) frame.
- **Core Properties:** $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$ — memorize both.
- **Three Elementary Rotations:**
  - $R_x(\phi)$ — Roll (about X, longitudinal axis of AUV)
  - $R_y(\theta)$ — Pitch (about Y, transverse axis)
  - $R_z(\psi)$ — Yaw (about Z, vertical axis)
- **Standard Composition (ZYX / Yaw-Pitch-Roll):**
  $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$.
- **Inverse Rotation:** $R^{-1} = R^T$ — this makes rotation matrices computationally cheap to invert.
- **Vector Transformation:** $^A v = {}^A_B R \cdot {}^B v$ — transforms a body-frame vector to the world frame.
- **Extraction Formulas (Euler angles from R):**
  $\theta = \text{atan2}(-r_{31}, \sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2})$,
  $\phi = \text{atan2}(r_{32}, r_{33})$,
  $\psi = \text{atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11})$.
- **Gimbal Lock:** Occurs at $\theta = \pm 90°$ (i.e., $\cos\theta = 0$); use quaternions as an alternative representation.
- **Alternative Representations:** Axis-angle, Euler vectors, and quaternions (4 components) avoid gimbal lock; rotation matrix (9 components, 6 constraints) is preferred for transformations.
- **Order is Sacred:** $R_z R_y R_x \neq R_x R_y R_z$. Write the rightmost rotation first when applying to a vector.
- **Right-Hand Rule:** Thumb along the positive axis, fingers curl in the direction of positive rotation.
- **Numerical Sanity Check:** $\vert R v \vert = \vert v \vert$ — a rotation must preserve the magnitude of any vector.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Holonomic and Non-Holonomic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Holonomic and Non-Holonomic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Holonomic and Non-Holonomic Systems

In the formal study of **Underwater Robotics** (and classical mechanics applied to autonomous marine vehicles), the classification of a system as **Holonomic** or **Non-Holonomic** is determined entirely by the nature of the **kinematic constraints** acting upon the vehicle. A constraint is a mathematical or physical restriction on the possible motions or configurations of a mechanical system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PERAT866 Module 2**
> The classification of constraint equations into holonomic (integrable) and non-holonomic (non-integrable) is a **direct prerequisite** for understanding AUV/ROV path planning, manoeuvring models, and the configuration-space reduction techniques used in autonomous underwater navigation.

### Holonomic Systems — Rigorous Definition

A mechanical system is said to be **holonomic** if all the constraints acting on it can be expressed purely as functions of the **generalized coordinates** $q_i$ and time $t$ alone — that is, the constraints contain **no explicit dependence on the generalized velocities** $\dot{q}_i$.

Mathematically, a holonomic constraint is expressed as:

$$f(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n, t) = 0$$

where $q = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ is the vector of generalized coordinates of the vehicle, and $n$ is the total number of generalized coordinates.

A system of $k$ holonomic constraints reduces the number of independent degrees of freedom from $n$ down to:

$$n_{\text{dof}} = n - k$$

### Non-Holonomic Systems — Rigorous Definition

A mechanical system is called **non-holonomic** if it possesses at least one constraint that **cannot be written** purely in terms of coordinates and time. The constraint must instead involve the **generalized velocities** $\dot{q}_i$ in an *essential* (non-integrable) way. The most general form of a linear non-holonomic constraint is the **Pfaffian form**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i(q_1, \ldots, q_n, t) \dot{q}_i + a_0(q_1, \ldots, q_n, t) = 0$$

If this expression is **not integrable** to a relation $f(q,t) = 0$, it is non-holonomic. Such a constraint restricts the *instantaneous* (velocity-level) allowable motions but **does not reduce the reachable configuration space** — a property of immense importance in underwater vehicle manoeuvrability.

### Scleronomic vs. Rheonomic — The Time Dependency Sub-Classification

| Sub-class | Time dependence | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Scleronomic** | $a_0 = 0$, no explicit $t$ in coefficients | Time-independent / stationary constraints |
| **Rheonomic** | Coefficients explicitly depend on $t$ | Time-varying constraints (e.g., moving water current boundaries) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **rheonomic** underwater robot (e.g., one operating in a tidal channel with time-varying flow) is still classified as holonomic or non-holonomic *based solely on whether its constraints are integrable* — the time-dependence is a *secondary* attribute.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making the Idea Intuitive

### 🧠 The "Shopping Cart" Analogy for Underwater Vehicles

Imagine you are pushing a **standard 4-wheeled car** (an AUV-like body) inside a giant flooded hall:

* **Holonomic vehicle** — Think of a **hovercraft** or a fully-actuated **ROV with 4+ thrusters arranged omnidirectionally**. At any instant, it can move sideways, forward, and rotate *simultaneously*. Its reachable directions are **unrestricted in the instantaneous plane**. The constraint it has (e.g., staying on the floor) is purely positional, so it is holonomic.

* **Non-holonomic vehicle** — Think of a **traditional AUV with a single rear propeller and rudders**, or a car-like submarine. It **cannot move sideways** directly. However, by clever sequencing of forward motions and rotations, it can still *reach any position and orientation* in the workspace. The constraint *"velocity must lie along the vehicle's longitudinal axis"* is a *velocity-level* (Pfaffian) constraint that is *not* integrable, hence non-holonomic.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** A non-holonomic vehicle has *fewer controllable degrees of freedom than the geometric dimension of its task space*. This is why parking a ship is a nightmare — but a holonomic ROV can "strafe" sideways into position effortlessly.

### 🎯 The "Tightrope Walker" Analogy for Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic

* A **tightrope walker** constrained to stay on the rope has a *holonomic* constraint $y = f(x)$ — the position is directly restricted.
* A **person on an icy slope** with the constraint *"feet must point downhill"* has a *non-holonomic* constraint — the velocity must always align with body orientation, yet the person can still reach the bottom of the slope.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Engineering Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following values appear in KTU board-level numerical problems on underwater vehicle kinematics:

* **Degrees of freedom of a 6-DOF rigid body in fluid:** $n_{\text{dof}} = 6$ (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw).
* **Reduced DOF for a non-holonomic AUV (typical torpedo shape):** $n_{\text{ctrl}} = 3$ to $4$ (surge, yaw, sometimes pitch and roll are stabilised passively).
* **Number of independent holonomic constraints on a free-flying ROV:** typically $k = 0$ (fully free 6-DOF), unless moored.
* **Standard notation for Pfaffian form:** $a(q)\dot{q} = 0$ where $a \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$.
* **Reachability check:** Non-holonomic systems satisfy the **Lie algebra rank condition (Chow–Rashevskii theorem)** for full configuration-space reachability.

---

## 1.4 Holonomic and Non-Holonomic Systems in Underwater Robotics — Specific Examples

| System | Constraint type | DOF | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fully-actuated ROV (e.g., 4-thruster) | Holonomic | 6 (full) | Work-class ROVs (e.g., Subsea 7) |
| Torpedo-shaped AUV | Non-holonomic | 3–4 (limited control) | REMUS, Autosub |
| Moored ROV (umbilical) | Holonomic (positional) | 3–4 | Inspection ROVs on TMS |
| Underwater glider | Non-holonomic | 2 (controlled) | Slocum, Spray glider |
| Biomimetic robotic fish | Non-holonomic | 2–3 | SoFi, MIT RoboFish |
| Spherical underwater robot | Holonomic | 3 (rotation) | Omni-directional AUVs |

> [!NOTE]
> A **common KTU exam trap** is to assume a vehicle with *fewer actuators than DOF* is automatically non-holonomic. This is **false** — the classification depends on whether the *constraint equation* can be integrated into a position-only form, not on the number of actuators.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in PERAT866

The distinction between holonomic and non-holonomic systems is the **mathematical foundation** for:

1. **Modelling AUV manoeuvring** — A torpedo AUV's "no-sideways-motion" rule is non-holonomic.
2. **Path planning algorithms** — Reeds–Shepp / Dubins curves (for non-holonomic) vs. straight-line (for holonomic).
3. **Control law synthesis** — Brockett's necessary condition for non-holonomic stabilisability by continuous feedback.
4. **Workspace analysis** — Determining if a tethered ROV can reach all inspection points.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Anatomy of Constraints

Every constraint acting on an underwater vehicle can be written in the **Pfaffian form**:

$$a^T(q, t)\, \dot{q} + a_0(q, t) = 0$$

where:

* $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the generalized coordinate vector (for an underwater vehicle: $q = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ in 6-DOF).
* $a \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is a vector of coefficients.
* $a_0$ is a scalar offset.
* $\dot{q}$ is the generalized velocity vector.

For a system of $k$ such constraints, we can write them compactly as:

$$A(q, t)\,\dot{q} + b(q, t) = 0$$

with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ and $b \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$.

### Decision Tree — Is the System Holonomic?

A constraint $a^T(q,t)\dot{q} + a_0(q,t) = 0$ is **holonomic** if and only if there exists a scalar function $f(q,t)$ such that:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial q} = \lambda(q, t)\, a^T(q, t) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} = \lambda(q, t)\, a_0(q, t)$$

for some non-zero integrating factor $\lambda(q,t)$. Otherwise, it is **non-holonomic**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Test (KTU exam favourite):** Given a Pfaffian constraint, attempt to find an integrating factor. If none exists → non-holonomic. If the constraint can be rewritten as $\frac{d}{dt}g(q,t) = 0$ → holonomic.

---

## 2.2 Generalised Coordinates and Degrees of Freedom in AUVs

For a 6-DOF rigid-body underwater vehicle with position $(x, y, z)$ and orientation $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ (Euler angles: roll, pitch, yaw):

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ \phi \\ \theta \\ \psi \end{bmatrix}, \quad \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ w \\ p \\ q_r \\ r \end{bmatrix}$$

The standard terminology in marine engineering:

| Symbol | Motion | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $u$ | Surge | Forward/backward (along $x_b$) |
| $v$ | Sway | Sideways (along $y_b$) |
| $w$ | Heave | Vertical (along $z_b$) |
| $p$ | Roll | Rotation about $x_b$ |
| $q_r$ | Pitch | Rotation about $y_b$ |
| $r$ | Yaw | Rotation about $z_b$ |

For a **classic torpedo AUV**:

* Surge $u$ is actuated by the propeller.
* Yaw $r$ is actuated by stern planes / rudders.
* Sway $v$ is **constrained to zero** in the body frame (the vehicle cannot translate sideways without first rotating).

This "no-sideways" rule is the **canonical example of a non-holonomic constraint** in marine robotics.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(q, t) = 0$ | Holonomic constraint (position form) | Dimensionless / m / rad |
| 2 | $a^T(q,t)\dot{q} + a_0 = 0$ | General Pfaffian (linear in velocity) | $a$ has units of $1/q$ |
| 3 | $A(q,t)\dot{q} + b = 0$ | Compact form of $k$ constraints | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ |
| 4 | $n_{\text{dof}} = n - k$ | DOF for holonomic system | Integer |
| 5 | $\dim(\text{controllable DOF}) < \dim(\text{task space})$ | Property of non-holonomic systems | Always |
| 6 | $\text{rank}\,\Omega(q) = n$ for all $q$ | Chow–Rashevskii rank condition (full reachability) | $\Omega$ = Lie algebra generated by control vector fields |
| 7 | $\dot{x}\sin\psi - \dot{y}\cos\psi = 0$ | Unicycle/AUV non-holonomic constraint (no sideways slip) | $x, y$ in m, $\psi$ in rad |
| 8 | $\dot{\eta} = J(\eta)\,v$ | Underwater vehicle kinematic equation (NED frame) | $J$ = transformation Jacobian |
| 9 | $\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\phi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & 0 \\ \sin\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$ | Differential-drive / unicycle model | $v, \omega$ are control inputs |
| 10 | Brockett: $\nexists$ continuous $u(x)$ stabilising origin if $B(0)$ not full rank | Necessary condition for non-holonomic stabilisation | — |
| 11 | Holonomic energy: $T + V = E$ (conserved) | Energy conservation in holonomic conservative systems | Joules |
| 12 | $D'Alembert$ virtual work: $\sum (F_i - m_i\ddot{r}_i)\cdot\delta r_i = 0$ | Foundation of holonomic dynamics | N·m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical notation warning (KTU valuation):** In the KTU 2024 scheme, the symbol $q$ is *overloaded*. In mechanics, $q$ = generalized coordinates; in marine engineering, $q_r$ = pitch rate. Do **not** confuse them in equations, or you will lose 2–3 marks on derivations.

---

## 2.4 Why Holonomic / Non-Holonomic Classification Matters in Engineering

1. **Control design:** Non-holonomic systems **cannot be stabilised to a fixed point by any smooth, time-invariant state feedback** (Brockett's theorem). This forces the use of time-varying or discontinuous control — a major KTU exam topic.
2. **Path planning:** A holonomic AUV can follow the **straight-line** path between waypoints. A non-holonomic AUV must use **Reeds–Shepp / Dubins curves** (circular arcs and straight segments).
3. **Energy efficiency:** Underwater gliders exploit non-holonomic dynamics to convert buoyancy changes into forward motion with minimal energy — essential for long-endurance missions.
4. **Workspace identification:** Moored ROVs are *holonomically constrained* (the umbilical is a position constraint), which simplifies kinematic analysis considerably.
5. **Simulation:** Modelling a non-holonomic AUV requires solving a **differential-algebraic equation (DAE)** in the index-1 form $A(q)\dot{q} = b(q)$, not just ODEs.

---

## 2.5 Detailed Step-by-Step Logic — Recognising Holonomic Constraints

**Step 1:** Write the constraint in Pfaffian form $a^T(q,t)\dot{q} + a_0 = 0$.

**Step 2:** Check if the constraint is already in *exact differential* form — i.e., can it be written as $df/dt = 0$ for some scalar $f$?

**Step 3:** If not, attempt to find an integrating factor $\lambda(q,t)$ such that $\lambda a^T \dot{q} + \lambda a_0 = \frac{d}{dt} f(q,t)$.

**Step 4:** If such $\lambda$ exists → **holonomic**. Otherwise → **non-holonomic**.

> [!TIP]
> **Shortcut for KTU exams:** A single Pfaffian constraint in 2D $a_1 \dot{x} + a_2 \dot{y} = 0$ is holonomic iff $\partial a_1 / \partial y = \partial a_2 / \partial x$ (the *exactness* condition for 1-forms). If this fails, the constraint is non-holonomic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reduction of DOF for a Holonomic System

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** Consider an underwater ROV with $n = 6$ generalised coordinates. It is constrained by $k = 2$ holonomic constraints: $f_1(x,y,z,\phi,\theta,\psi) = 0$ and $f_2(\ldots) = 0$. Determine the remaining degrees of freedom.

**Step 1 — Total available coordinates:** The vehicle in unrestricted 3D space has

$$n = 6 \text{ coordinates} \quad (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$$

This corresponds to **6 kinematic degrees of freedom** of a free rigid body in an ideal fluid (treating the body as a 6-DOF rigid body).

**Step 2 — Effect of each holonomic constraint:** Each holonomic constraint $f_i(q,t) = 0$ removes **one** independent degree of freedom, because it fixes the value of one functional combination of the coordinates at every instant.

**Step 3 — Number of remaining DOF:** By direct subtraction,

$$n_{\text{dof}} = n - k = 6 - 2 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Verification via configuration manifold:** The original configuration space is a 6-D manifold $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$. The constraints $f_1 = 0$, $f_2 = 0$ define a codimension-2 sub-manifold. The remaining DOF is the dimension of this sub-manifold:

$$\dim(\mathcal{C}_{\text{constrained}}) = 6 - 2 = 4$$

**Final Answer:** $n_{\text{dof}} = 4$. The system can move in a 4-dimensional sub-space of the original 6-D configuration manifold.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proving a Constraint is Non-Holonomic (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** Show that the rolling-without-slipping constraint of a torpedo-shaped AUV on a horizontal plane is **non-holonomic**.

**Setup:** The AUV body-frame velocity components are $(u, v, r)$. The "no sideways slip" condition in the inertial frame is:

$$-\dot{x}\sin\psi + \dot{y}\cos\psi = 0$$

This is the standard unicycle / car-like constraint.

**Step 1 — Express in Pfaffian form:** With $q = [x, y, \psi]^T$ and $\dot{q} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\psi}]^T$:

$$a^T \dot{q} = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad a^T = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Attempt to integrate:** We seek $f(x, y, \psi) = c$ such that $df/dt = 0$ yields the constraint. Then:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\dot{x} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\dot{y} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \psi}\dot{\psi} = 0$$

Comparing coefficients with the constraint:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = -\lambda \sin\psi, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = \lambda \cos\psi, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial \psi} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Test for consistency:** The first equation gives $f = \lambda \cos\psi + g(y, \psi)$ for some $g$. The second gives $f = \lambda \sin\psi + h(x, \psi)$. Substituting and using $\partial f / \partial \psi = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \psi}(\lambda \cos\psi + g) = 0 \implies -\lambda \sin\psi + \frac{\partial g}{\partial \psi} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \psi}(\lambda \sin\psi + h) = 0 \implies \lambda \cos\psi + \frac{\partial h}{\partial \psi} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Derive contradiction:** From these two:

$$\lambda \cos\psi = -\frac{\partial h}{\partial \psi}, \quad -\lambda \sin\psi = -\frac{\partial g}{\partial \psi}$$

Since $g$ cannot depend on $x$ (it was introduced for the $x$-direction) and $h$ cannot depend on $y$, and they must be consistent, the only solution would require $\lambda$ to be a function of $\psi$ alone. But then cross-checking with the original constraint, no scalar $f$ exists.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Since no $f(q)$ exists with $df/dt = a^T \dot{q} = 0$, the constraint is **non-integrable** → **non-holonomic**. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **standard KTU board proof** of non-holonomy for a wheeled / AUV system. Memorise the structure: assume an integrating factor, derive a contradiction, conclude non-holonomic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Degrees-of-Freedom of a Non-Holonomic AUV

**Setup:** A torpedo AUV has $n = 6$ coordinates. It has $k_1 = 0$ holonomic constraints (the vehicle is unconstrained in position), but $k_2 = 3$ non-holonomic constraints imposed by hydrodynamic body symmetry:

* (i) Roll is uncontrollable / negligible: $p = 0$ (passive stability).
