| 9 | Pitch angle | $\theta_p = \arctan(a_x / a_z)$ | $\text{rad}$ or $\text{deg}$ | Static tilt |
| 10 | Roll angle | $\theta_r = \arctan(a_y / a_z)$ | $\text{rad}$ or $\text{deg}$ | Static tilt |
| 11 | Magnitude check | $\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2} \approx g$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Distinguishes tilt vs motion |
| 12 | Capacitive change | $\Delta C = 2 C_0 x / d_0$ | $\text{F}$ | MEMS differential capacitor |
| 13 | Piezo charge | $q = d_{33} F = d_{33} m a$ | $\text{C}$ | $d_{33}$ = piezo coefficient |
| 14 | Noise-limited resolution | $a_{\min} = N_d \sqrt{BW}$ | $\text{g}$ | $N_d$ in $\text{g}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Underwater Engineering

| Application | Sensor Role | Engineering Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **AUV Dead Reckoning** | Measures linear acceleration; double-integrated (with bias correction) to get velocity and position | Enables navigation during GPS-denied submergence |
| **Tilt / Attitude Control** | Reads gravity vector to infer pitch and roll | Maintains ROV heading against currents |
| **Thruster Vibration Monitoring** | Piezo accelerometer on thruster casing | Detects cavitation, bearing wear, imbalance |
| **Impact / Shock Logging** | High-g accelerometer records collision events | Post-mission forensic analysis of hull strikes |
| **Buoyancy Control Feedback** | Detects vertical acceleration transients | Helps trim ballast system response |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** In KTU answers, always *contextualize* the sensor to underwater use. Generic textbook definitions lose marks; an answer that says "in an AUV, the accelerometer feeds the INS Kalman filter" scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Static Sensitivity of a Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer

**Given:** A MEMS accelerometer has a proof-mass of mass $m$, suspended by four flexures of total equivalent stiffness $k$. The proof-mass sits between two fixed capacitor plates, separated by a nominal gap $d_0$, with each gap having a nominal capacitance $C_0$.

**Step 1 — Nominal capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor:**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

where $A$ is the overlap area of the plates, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ is the vacuum permittivity, and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the dielectric (typically $\approx 1$ for air).

**Step 2 — Capacitance change due to proof-mass displacement $x$:**

When the mass moves by $x$ toward the upper plate, the upper gap becomes $(d_0 - x)$ and the lower gap becomes $(d_0 + x)$. The two capacitances are:

$$C_{\text{upper}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - x} \quad ; \quad C_{\text{lower}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 + x}$$

**Step 3 — Differential capacitance (the measurable quantity):**

$$\Delta C = C_{\text{upper}} - C_{\text{lower}} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[ \frac{1}{d_0 - x} - \frac{1}{d_0 + x} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic simplification (common denominator):**

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{(d_0 + x) - (d_0 - x)}{(d_0 - x)(d_0 + x)} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{2x}{d_0^2 - x^2}$$

**Step 5 — Small-signal approximation ($x \ll d_0$, so $x^2 \ll d_0^2$):**

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \, x}{d_0^2} = \frac{2 C_0 \, x}{d_0}$$

**Step 6 — Relate $x$ to applied acceleration via the static spring equation:**

At steady-state, the proof-mass displacement is governed by:

$$k x_{\text{ss}} = m a \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{\text{ss}} = \frac{m a}{k}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back into the differential capacitance:**

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 C_0}{d_0} \cdot \frac{m a}{k} = \left( \frac{2 C_0 m}{k \, d_0} \right) a$$

**Step 8 — Define the **capacitive sensitivity** $S_C$:**

$$\boxed{\,S_C \;=\; \frac{\Delta C}{a} \;=\; \frac{2 C_0 \, m}{k \, d_0} \quad [\text{F·s}^2/\text{m}]\,}$$

This equation shows that sensitivity is **proportional to proof-mass and nominal capacitance** and **inversely proportional to stiffness and gap**. Engineers tune these parameters to match the desired measurement range — a classic design trade-off.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inclination Angle from Two-Axis Static Acceleration

**Given:** A 3-axis accelerometer is mounted on a submerged ROV. When stationary, the outputs (after removing zero-g bias) are $a_x$, $a_y$, $a_z$.

**Step 1 — Identify the gravity vector in the sensor frame:**

Under static conditions, the accelerometer measures the reaction to gravity along its axes. The Earth's gravity vector $\vec{g}$ projected onto the sensor's axes gives:

$$a_x = g \sin\theta_p \quad ; \quad a_y = g \sin\theta_r \quad ; \quad a_z = g \cos\theta_p \cos\theta_r$$

where $\theta_p$ is the pitch and $\theta_r$ is the roll of the vehicle relative to the Earth frame.

**Step 2 — Solve for pitch angle:**

From $a_x / a_z$:

$$\frac{a_x}{a_z} = \frac{g \sin\theta_p}{g \cos\theta_p \cos\theta_r} = \frac{\tan\theta_p}{\cos\theta_r}$$

For small roll angles ($\theta_r \approx 0$, $\cos\theta_r \approx 1$):

$$\tan\theta_p \approx \frac{a_x}{a_z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_p = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_x}{a_z}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for roll angle similarly:**

$$\boxed{\,\theta_r = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right)\,}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude verification:**

If the vehicle is genuinely at rest, the squared sum must equal $g^2$:

$$a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2 \;\approx\; g^2 \;=\; (9.81)^2 \;\approx\; 96.236\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^4$$

If the computed magnitude is **greater than $g$**, the vehicle is accelerating upward; if **less than $g$**, it is accelerating downward. This is a fundamental *sanity check* used in KTU board numericals.

**Example numerical:** Suppose $a_x = 1.5\ \text{m/s}^2$, $a_y = 0$, $a_z = 9.65\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find the pitch.

$$\theta_p = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1.5}{9.65}\right) = \arctan(0.1554) \approx 8.84^\circ$$

Magnitude check: $\sqrt{1.5^2 + 0 + 9.65^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 93.12} = \sqrt{95.37} \approx 9.766\ \text{m/s}^2$ — slightly below $g$, indicating a mild downward acceleration component.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resonant Frequency for a Quartz Piezoelectric Accelerometer

**Given:** A piezoelectric accelerometer uses a seismic mass $m$ pressing on a piezoelectric crystal stack of stiffness $k_p$ and damping $c_p$.

**Step 1 — Equation of motion (relative displacement $x$):**

$$m \ddot{x} + c_p \dot{x} + k_p x = -m a_{\text{in}}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Natural angular frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{m}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical illustration:** Take $m = 50\ \text{g} = 0.05\ \text{kg}$ and $k_p = 2 \times 10^6\ \text{N/m}$.

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^6}{0.05}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^7} \approx 6324.6\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hz:**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{6324.6}{2\pi} \approx 1006.6\ \text{Hz} \approx 1\ \text{kHz}$$

This is the upper edge of the **usable bandwidth** for the piezo accelerometer. For underwater thruster vibration diagnostics (typical blade-pass frequency 50–300 Hz), this is more than sufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the bandwidth of piezo accelerometers so high?** Because the stiffness $k_p$ of a piezoelectric stack is enormous (typically $\sim 10^6$ to $10^7\ \text{N/m}$), making $\omega_n$ very large. The price paid is **low DC sensitivity** — piezo accelerometers cannot measure constant (zero-frequency) acceleration.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimum Detectable Acceleration (Noise Floor)

**Given:** A MEMS accelerometer datasheet specifies noise density $N_d = 25\ \mu g/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$. The signal-conditioning low-pass filter is set to a bandwidth $BW = 100\ \text{Hz}$.

**Step 1 — RMS noise in the measurement band:**

$$a_{\text{noise,rms}} = N_d \cdot \sqrt{BW} = 25\ \mu g/\sqrt{\text{Hz}} \cdot \sqrt{100\ \text{Hz}} = 25 \cdot 10\ \mu g = 250\ \mu g$$

**Step 2 — Convert to $\text{m/s}^2$:**

$$a_{\text{noise,rms}} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \approx 2.45 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** The smallest acceleration this sensor can resolve, with a 100 Hz measurement bandwidth, is approximately **2.45 mg**. For a slow-moving AUV at survey speeds (0.5 m/s), accelerations are typically $\sim 10\ \text{mg}$, so the signal-to-noise ratio is acceptable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MEMS Capacitive Accelerometer Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Acceleration Input] --> B[Sensor Housing / Anchor Frame]
    B --> C[Suspension Flexure - Stiffness k]
    C --> D[Proof Mass - Mass m]
    D --> E[Differential Capacitor Plates]
    E --> F[Charge Amplifier / C-V Converter]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioning - Filter and Amplify]
    G --> H[ADC - Analog to Digital Conversion]
    H --> I[Digital Processor / Microcontroller]
    I --> J[INS Kalman Filter]
    J --> K[Vehicle Navigation Output]
    I --> L[Tilt and Attitude Output]

    subgraph Sensing_Element
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph Analog_Front_End
        F
        G
    end

    subgraph Digital_Back_End
        H
        I
    end

    subgraph Vehicle_Application_Layer
        J
        K
        L
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Acceleration enters from the housing; the proof-mass lags behind due to inertia; the resulting displacement changes the capacitance of the differential plates; the analog front-end converts the capacitance change to voltage; the digital back-end filters and applies sensor-fusion; finally, the navigation and tilt layers produce usable outputs for the underwater vehicle.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Flow of an Underwater IMU Using Accelerometer Data

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[3-Axis MEMS Accelerometer] --> stepB[Raw Signal Conditioner]
    stepC[3-Axis Gyroscope] --> stepB
    stepD[3-Axis Magnetometer] --> stepB
    stepB --> stepE[Kalman Filter - Sensor Fusion]
    stepE --> stepF[Roll Pitch Yaw Output]
    stepE --> stepG[Linear Acceleration Output]
    stepF --> stepH[ROV Attitude Controller]
    stepG --> stepI[AUV Dead Reckoning Module]
    stepI --> stepJ[Position Estimate]

    style stepA fill:#cce5ff
    style stepB fill:#e6f2ff
    style stepE fill:#b3d9ff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept for KTU Board:** Accelerometers alone **cannot** yield reliable long-term orientation because the integration of bias drift causes attitude to wander. They are therefore *always* fused with gyroscopes (short-term accurate) and magnetometers (long-term heading reference) in a **Kalman filter** within the AUV/ROV navigation stack.

---

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Equivalent Circuit Model of Piezoelectric Accelerometer

```
            ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
            │                                            │
   INPUT    │      ┌───────┐         ┌──────────────┐    │  OUTPUT
   (accel)  │      │ mass  │         │ piezoelectric│    │  (charge
   ───────► │ ───► │   m   │ ──F──►  │  crystal qp  │ ──►│   q, V)
            │      └───────┘         └──────────────┘    │
            │          ▲                    │            │
            │          │ k (spring)         │ C (clamp) │
            │          │                    │            │
            │          └────────────────────┘            │
            └────────────────────────────────────────────┘

   Equivalent source:      Equivalent circuit:
       F = m·a                      
       q = d33·F = d33·m·a         [ C_p ]──[ V = q/C_p ]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Drawing this ASCII in the answer sheet** during a KTU exam demonstrates clear understanding and earns full methodology marks. Always label the force path ($F$), the spring ($k$), and the piezo element explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define an accelerometer. Differentiate between static and dynamic acceleration measurement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An accelerometer is an electromechanical sensor that measures proper acceleration along one or more axes and converts it into an electrical signal.

- **Static acceleration** is constant in time (DC component) — e.g., the constant $1g$ component due to gravity when the sensor is tilted. Measurement requires a DC-coupled transducer such as a **capacitive MEMS** or **piezoresistive** accelerometer.
- **Dynamic acceleration** varies rapidly with time (AC component) — e.g., vibration, shock, or motion-induced transients. Measurement can use **capacitive** or **piezoelectric** accelerometers with an AC-coupled amplifier.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark static, 1 mark dynamic — total 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any four performance parameters used to specify an accelerometer used in underwater robotics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Range** — maximum measurable acceleration (e.g., $\pm 5g$) — *1 mark*
2. **Sensitivity** — output per unit $g$ (e.g., $200\ \text{mV/g}$) — *1 mark*
3. **Bandwidth** — usable frequency range (e.g., 0–1 kHz) — *0.5 mark*
4. **Noise density** — minimum resolvable signal (e.g., $25\ \mu g/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$) — *0.5 mark*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **MEMS capacitive accelerometer**. State the role of the differential capacitor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram:** (draw the Mermaid architecture from Section 4.1 in your answer sheet)

**Working principle — step-by-step:**

1. The sensor consists of a silicon proof-mass suspended by compliant flexures between two fixed capacitor plates. *(1 mark)*
2. When the housing accelerates, inertia causes the proof-mass to lag, producing a relative displacement $x$ with respect to the housing. This displacement is governed by the SDOF equation $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a_{\text{in}}$. *(1 mark)*
3. The displacement reduces the gap on one side and increases the gap on the other, changing the two capacitances in opposite directions. *(1 mark)*
4. The differential capacitance $\Delta C = C_{\text{upper}} - C_{\text{lower}} \approx 2C_0 x / d_0$ is measured by a charge amplifier or C-V converter circuit. *(1 mark)*
5. The signal is conditioned, digitized by an ADC, and processed by a microcontroller. *(1 mark)*
6. The output is a digital acceleration value proportional to the input $a_{\text{in}}$. *(1 mark)*
7. **Role of differential capacitor:** It converts the small mechanical displacement into a *larger, linear, and temperature-stable* electrical signal, while rejecting common-mode noise and common-mode acceleration. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** Derive the **transfer function** of an accelerometer modelled as a mass-spring-damper system. State the significance of natural frequency and damping ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equation of motion in relative coordinate:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a_{\text{in}}(t) \quad \text{...(1)} \quad (1\ \text{mark})$$

**Step 2 — Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):**

$$(ms^2 + cs + k) X(s) = -m A_{\text{in}}(s) \quad \text{...(2)} \quad (1\ \text{mark})$$

**Step 3 — Transfer function:**

$$H(s) = \frac{X(s)}{A_{\text{in}}(s)} = \frac{-m}{ms^2 + cs + k} \quad \text{...(3)} \quad (1\ \text{mark})$$

**Step 4 — Standard second-order form:**

Define $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$:

$$H(s) = \frac{-1/k}{\,s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2\,} \quad \text{...(4)} \quad (1\ \text{mark})$$

**Step 5 — Significance:**

- **Natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$** sets the upper edge of the usable bandwidth. Above this frequency, the sensor cannot respond. Stiffer suspensions give higher bandwidth but lower sensitivity. *(1 mark)*
- **Damping ratio $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$** controls the transient response. Critical value $\zeta = 1$ yields fastest settling without overshoot. Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$) produces resonance peak. Typical design value for accelerometers is $\zeta \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ for wide bandwidth with minimal ringing. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\,H(s) = \frac{-1/k}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}, \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}, \quad \zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}\,}$$

*(1 mark for the boxed summary with both definitions)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **piezoelectric accelerometer**. Derive the expression for charge output in terms of applied acceleration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (1 mark each, 3 marks total):**
- A heavy seismic mass (typically tungsten) is bonded to a piezoelectric crystal stack (often quartz or PZT ceramic).
- The stack is preloaded between the mass and the sensor base via a central bolt.
- The whole assembly is housed in a sealed metal case with a mounting stud.

**Working (2 marks):**
- When the housing accelerates, inertia of the seismic mass applies a force $F = ma$ on the piezo crystal.
- This force produces an electrical charge $q$ proportional to the applied force via the piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33}$.
- The charge is converted to a low-impedance voltage by an internal charge amplifier (IEPE / ICP type).

**Derivation (2 marks):**

From the piezoelectric constitutive relation:

$$q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a$$

Define the **charge sensitivity** $S_q = d_{33} \cdot m$:

$$\boxed{\,q = S_q \cdot a \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_q = d_{33} \cdot m \quad [\text{C·s}^2/\text{m}]\,}$$

---

**(b)** Discuss the **role of accelerometers in underwater vehicle navigation**, listing at least four specific applications. Justify why MEMS capacitive type is preferred for INS applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Four specific applications (1.25 marks each, 5 marks total):**

1. **Inertial Navigation (Dead Reckoning):** Linear acceleration is integrated once to yield velocity and twice to yield displacement. During GPS-denied underwater transit, this provides continuous position estimates when fused with a DVL (Doppler Velocity Log) in a Kalman filter.

2. **Attitude and Tilt Sensing:** Under static conditions, the accelerometer measures the projection of gravity on its axes, allowing direct computation of roll and pitch via the $\arctan$ relations. This is essential for ROV pilots to maintain vehicle orientation against underwater currents.

3. **Heave Compensation:** Vertical-axis accelerometer data is high-pass filtered to remove gravity bias, isolating the *heave* (vertical bobbing) due to surface waves. This is fed back to active heave-compensation systems on ROVs to stabilize tooling payloads.

4. **Thruster Health Monitoring:** Piezo accelerometers mounted on thruster casings detect vibration signatures indicative of cavitation, bearing failure, or propeller imbalance — enabling predictive maintenance.

**Justification — why MEMS capacitive for INS (2 marks):**

- **Low power consumption** (sub-mW) — critical for long-endurance battery-powered AUVs.
- **DC response** — measures constant gravity and slow tilt, which piezo sensors cannot.
- **Small size and CMOS compatibility** — enables compact IMU modules inside the pressure hull.
- **Good bias stability** (sub-mg) and **low noise density** — supports long-term integration drift reduction.
- **Cost-effective** at volume, allowing redundant multi-axis arrays for fault tolerance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse *acceleration* with *velocity* or *position*.** Many students write $a = dv/dt$ and try to integrate without stating *initial conditions* or *bias compensation* — losing 2 marks.
> - **Do NOT claim piezo accelerometers measure DC.** They do not — there is no static charge without a changing force. Always qualify with "AC-coupled / vibration only."
> - **Always carry units in numericals.** Forgetting $\text{m/s}^2$ vs $g$ conversion ($1g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$) is the single most common mark-deduction error in KTU board papers.
> - **For tilt questions**, students often write $\arcsin$ instead of $\arctan$ when the wrong axis ratio is chosen. Mark the *axes clearly* in your diagram before deriving.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Accelerometer (Module 3, PERAT866)**

- **Definition:** Accelerometer = sensor that measures proper acceleration (not "force of gravity" — measures contact force on proof-mass).
- **SDOF Model:** Equation of motion $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a_{\text{in}}$ is the bedrock.
- **Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — sets the upper bandwidth limit.
- **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ — optimum $\approx 0.7$ for flat magnitude response.
- **Transfer Function:** $H(s) = -1/k \,/\, (s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$.
- **MEMS Capacitive:** DC-responsive, low-power, ideal for INS and tilt — preferred for AUV/ROV navigation.
- **Piezoelectric:** AC-only, very high bandwidth, ideal for thruster vibration diagnostics.
- **Piezoresistive:** High-g shock survival — used in ROV manipulator impact loggers.
- **Capacitive Sensitivity:** $S_C = 2 C_0 m / (k d_0)$.
- **Charge Sensitivity (Piezo):** $q = d_{33} m a$.
- **Tilt Equations:** $\theta_p = \arctan(a_x/a_z)$, $\theta_r = \arctan(a_y/a_z)$ — valid under static conditions.
- **Magnitude Sanity Check:** $\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2} \approx g$ only at rest.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Accelerometer + Gyroscope + Magnetometer → Kalman filter → INS attitude.
- **Units Rule:** $1g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ — always convert when computing $F = ma$ in Newtons.
- **High-Yield Board Keywords:** *proper acceleration, proof-mass, flexure, differential capacitance, charge amplifier, IEPE, bias stability, noise density, sensor fusion, dead reckoning, heave compensation*.
- **Real-World Recall:** AUV = INS + DVL + Accelerometer; ROV = Tilt sensor + Vibration monitor on thrusters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Compass Inclinometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Compass Inclinometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Magnetic Compass — Formal Definition

A **Magnetic Compass** is a non-acoustic orientation sensor that determines the heading (yaw angle) of an underwater vehicle (UUV/ROV/AUV) by sensing the direction of the **Earth's geomagnetic field vector** relative to the vehicle's body frame. In underwater robotics, the most widely deployed variant is the **Fluxgate Magnetometer**, which measures the three orthogonal components ($B_x$, $B_y$, $B_z$) of the local magnetic flux density and computes the heading using trigonometric projection.

The geomagnetic field has a typical intensity of approximately **25 – 65 µT** depending on geographic latitude and declination angle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERAT866 – Module 3):** Magnetic compasses and inclinometers form the foundational **attitude and heading reference** stack for low-cost underwater vehicles where high-grade inertial measurement units (IMUs) are either unaffordable or unavailable.

### 1.2 Inclinometer — Formal Definition

An **Inclinometer** (also called a **tilt sensor** or **clinometer**) is a device that measures the angular deviation of a reference axis from the local gravity vector (the true vertical). In underwater robotics, MEMS-based inclinometers using **3-axis MEMS accelerometers** are standard, providing **pitch (θ)** and **roll (φ)** by resolving the static gravity component from the accelerometer triad.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definitions to Memorise for KTU ESE:**
> - **Heading (ψ):** Rotation about the body's vertical (z) axis, measured clockwise from magnetic North.
> - **Pitch (θ):** Rotation about the body's lateral (y) axis — nose up/down.
> - **Roll (φ):** Rotation about the body's longitudinal (x) axis — wing tilt.
> - **Heeling Error:** Compass error introduced when the sensor is not level (corrected using inclinometer data).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Diver's Wristwatch

Imagine a deep-sea diver carrying a **smartwatch with a digital compass app**:
- The compass needle doesn't point to the **true geographic North** (the spin axis of Earth), but to the **magnetic North** — where Earth's iron-nickel core magnet pulls the needle. The diver must mentally correct for **magnetic declination** (the angle between true and magnetic north at that location).
- Now imagine the diver tilts their wrist 30° sideways while reading the compass. The reading becomes **wrong** because the compass now mixes the *vertical* magnetic component with the horizontal one. This is exactly why a **co-located inclinometer is mandatory** in any practical underwater compass module — it tells the compass how tilted it is, allowing software correction.
- The same diver's smartwatch also has a **tilt meter** showing how steeply they are oriented. This tilt is computed by a tiny accelerometer that feels Earth's gravity pulling downward — a mass on a microscopic spring stretches more when tilted.

In a **ROV/AUV**, identical principles apply: a **3-axis magnetometer triad + 3-axis accelerometer triad** together yield a complete **6-DOF attitude solution** at low cost.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Faced)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Earth's Magnetic Flux Density | $B_E$ | **25 µT – 65 µT** |
| Standard Reference Field (US/UK) | $B_{ref}$ | **≈ 50 µT** |
| Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| Standard Heading Range | ψ | **0° to 360°** |
| Standard Pitch Range | θ | **−90° to +90°** |
| Standard Roll Range | φ | **−180° to +180°** |
| Fluxgate Noise Floor (typical) | — | **0.5 – 5 nT/√Hz** |
| MEMS Accelerometer Noise (typical) | — | **300 – 500 µg/√Hz** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Magnetic Compass (Fluxgate Type)

A fluxgate magnetometer uses two parallel ferromagnetic cores driven into saturation by an AC excitation coil. When an external field (Earth's field) is present, the cores saturate asymmetrically, inducing a **second-harmonic voltage** in a sense coil proportional to the external field component along the core axis.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Triad Sensing:** Three mutually orthogonal fluxgate sensors measure $B_x$, $B_y$, $B_z$ in the **sensor (body) frame**.
2. **Tilt Compensation:** Using pitch (θ) and roll (φ) from the inclinometer, the magnetic field vector is **rotated** into the horizontal (level) plane.
3. **Heading Computation:** The horizontal components are passed through an $\arctan 2$ function to yield the magnetic heading.
4. **Declination Correction:** The local magnetic declination (δ) is added to obtain true heading.
5. **Deviation Compensation:** A multi-term **soft-iron / hard-iron calibration** matrix removes distortions caused by the vehicle's own ferrous components.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Inclinometer (MEMS Accelerometer Type)

A MEMS accelerometer contains a **proof mass suspended by compliant springs**. When the device tilts, the mass deflects; this displacement is sensed capacitively or piezoresistively. At **quasi-static** conditions (no acceleration, only gravity), the accelerometer output directly reads the projection of $\vec{g}$ on each axis.

**Operational Logic:**

1. Static gravity vector $\vec{g}_b = [A_x, A_y, A_z]^T$ measured in body frame.
2. Pitch = angle between $\vec{g}$ projection and X-axis in YZ plane.
3. Roll = angle between $\vec{g}$ projection and Y-axis in XZ plane.
4. Heading-independent tilt extraction (no gyroscope fusion required for static tilt).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the *tilt-compensated compass* with a *gyrocompass*. A fluxgate compass **cannot** measure heading during sustained rotation in the absence of a magnetic reference (e.g., near the poles where the horizontal field is weak). It is fundamentally a **magnetic-field sensor**, not an inertial rotation sensor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The pipe character is intentionally avoided in tables; absolute value is denoted using $\vert \cdot \vert$.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta = \arctan 2\!\left(-A_x,\; \sqrt{A_y^{\,2}+A_z^{\,2}}\right)$ | Pitch from accelerometer triad | radians |
| 2 | $\phi = \arctan 2\!\left(A_y,\; A_z\right)$ | Roll from accelerometer triad | radians |
| 3 | $X_h = B_x\cos\theta + B_y\sin\phi\sin\theta + B_z\cos\phi\sin\theta$ | Tilt-compensated X (horizontal) | µT |
| 4 | $Y_h = B_y\cos\phi - B_z\sin\phi$ | Tilt-compensated Y (horizontal) | µT |
| 5 | $\psi_{mag} = \arctan 2\!\left(-Y_h,\; X_h\right)$ | Magnetic heading (CW from North) | radians |
| 6 | $\psi_{true} = \psi_{mag} + \delta$ | True heading after declination | radians |
| 7 | $\begin{bmatrix} B_x^{cal} \\ B_y^{cal} \\ B_z^{cal} \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{W}\!\begin{bmatrix} B_x - B_{x0} \\ B_y - B_{y0} \\ B_z - B_{z0} \end{bmatrix}$ | Hard-iron offset + soft-iron matrix | µT |
| 8 | $\delta_{correction} = A + B\sin\psi + C\cos\psi + D\sin 2\psi + E\cos 2\psi$ | Deviation table (NAVARCH style) | radians |
| 9 | $B_h = \sqrt{X_h^{\,2}+Y_h^{\,2}}$ | Horizontal field magnitude | µT |
| 10 | $\tan I = \dfrac{B_z^{level}}{B_h}$ | Magnetic inclination (dip angle) | radians |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AUV Navigation:** Combined magnetic compass + inclinometer provides heading + attitude at **< 1° RMS accuracy** at low cost (typical module: **$50 – $300 USD** vs. **>$10,000 USD** for a FOG-based IMU).
- **ROV Pilot Display:** Real-time HUD overlay of vehicle heading and tilt to assist the pilot during station-keeping and manipulation tasks.
- **Underwater Survey (Pipeline/Cable Tracking):** Heading-stabilised multibeam sonar mapping requires sub-degree heading accuracy — fluxgate + DVL fusion is the industry-standard fallback when GPS is unavailable.
- **Marine Geophysics:** Magnetic surveys of seabed iron-ore deposits rely on the same magnetometer physics, with declination correction mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tilt Extraction from a 3-Axis Accelerometer

We begin with the gravity vector resolved in the body frame. When the body is level (θ = φ = 0), the accelerometer triad reads $[0,\;0,\;g]^T$. When tilted, gravity projects onto all three axes.

**Step 1: Decompose gravity using the standard aerospace rotation order (yaw–pitch–roll, ZYX intrinsic).**

The direction-cosine matrix from body to navigation frame is:

$$
\mathbf{C}_b^n = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta c\psi & s\phi s\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\ c\theta s\psi & s\phi s\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi & c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\ -s\theta & s\phi c\theta & c\phi c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $s \equiv \sin$, $c \equiv \cos$.

**Step 2: The accelerometer triad measures gravity in the body frame. At quasi-static equilibrium (no linear acceleration):**

$$
\vec{A}_b = \mathbf{C}_n^b \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ g \end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding, we obtain:

$$
A_x = -g\sin\theta
$$

$$
A_y = g\sin\phi\cos\theta
$$

$$
A_z = g\cos\phi\cos\theta
$$

**Step 3: Solve for pitch θ. Divide $A_x$ by the magnitude of the YZ-plane projection.**

$$
\sqrt{A_y^{\,2}+A_z^{\,2}} = g\cos\theta
$$

$$
\frac{A_x}{\sqrt{A_y^{\,2}+A_z^{\,2}}} = \frac{-g\sin\theta}{g\cos\theta} = -\tan\theta
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta = \arctan 2\!\left(-A_x,\; \sqrt{A_y^{\,2}+A_z^{\,2}}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Solve for roll φ using $A_y$ and $A_z$.**

$$
\frac{A_y}{A_z} = \frac{g\sin\phi\cos\theta}{g\cos\phi\cos\theta} = \tan\phi
$$

Therefore:

$$
\phi = \arctan 2\!\left(A_y,\; A_z\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The use of $\arctan 2$ (two-argument form) is **mandatory** in KTU numerical solutions because it preserves the correct quadrant — unlike $\arctan$ which is restricted to $(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2})$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tilt-Compensated Magnetic Heading

**Step 1: Body-frame magnetic field vector.** The fluxgate triad directly measures $\vec{B}_b = [B_x,\;B_y,\;B_z]^T$.

**Step 2: Rotate the magnetic vector into the level (horizontal) frame using the pitch and roll DCM.**

We apply the inverse of the body-to-level rotation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_h \\ Y_h \\ Z_h \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\phi\sin\theta & \cos\phi\sin\theta \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \sin\phi\cos\theta & \cos\phi\cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} B_x \\ B_y \\ B_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Extract the explicit forms of $X_h$ and $Y_h$.**

$$
X_h = B_x\cos\theta + B_y\sin\phi\sin\theta + B_z\cos\phi\sin\theta
$$

$$
Y_h = B_y\cos\phi - B_z\sin\phi
$$

**Step 4: Compute the heading using the two-argument arctangent.**

The horizontal plane has its X-axis aligned with the vehicle's forward direction (which, when level, is the heading reference). The magnetic heading is:

$$
\psi_{mag} = \arctan 2\!\left(-Y_h,\; X_h\right)
$$

The negative sign convention is needed because headings are measured **clockwise** from magnetic North, while the arctangent follows the standard mathematical (counter-clockwise) convention.

**Step 5: Apply local magnetic declination δ to convert to true heading.**

$$
\psi_{true} = \psi_{mag} + \delta
$$

where δ is obtained from the **World Magnetic Model (WMM)** or the **International Geomagnetic Reference Field (IGRF)**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hard-Iron and Soft-Iron Calibration Model

**Step 1: Hard-iron distortion** arises from permanently magnetised materials on the vehicle (thruster motors, steel pressure housings). It adds a **constant offset vector** $\vec{B}_0$.

$$
\vec{B}_{hard} = \vec{B}_{true} + \vec{B}_0
$$

**Step 2: Soft-iron distortion** arises from ferromagnetic materials that distort the field directionally. It is modelled as a **3×3 matrix** $\mathbf{W}$.

$$
\vec{B}_{soft} = \mathbf{W}\,\vec{B}_{true}
$$

**Step 3: Combined model.**

$$
\vec{B}_{measured} = \mathbf{W}\,\vec{B}_{true} + \vec{B}_0
$$

**Step 4: Inversion to recover the true field.**

$$
\vec{B}_{true} = \mathbf{W}^{-1}\!\left(\vec{B}_{measured} - \vec{B}_0\right)
$$

**Step 5: Calibration procedure** — the vehicle is rotated through 360° in heading (and ideally varied in pitch/roll). The locus of $(B_x, B_y)$ should ideally trace a **circle** centred at the origin. Distortions shift the centre (hard-iron) and stretch the locus into an **ellipse** (soft-iron). The matrix $\mathbf{W}^{-1}$ is solved by **ellipsoid fitting** (e.g., least-squares on $\vec{B}_{measured}^T\mathbf{Q}\vec{B}_{measured} = B_{ref}^{\,2}$).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Fluxgate Compass | MEMS Inclinometer |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Quantity Sensed | Magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ | Specific force (gravity + acceleration) |
| Output Quantities | Heading ψ (3D field vector) | Pitch θ, Roll φ |
| Bandwidth | DC to **~1 kHz** (typical) | DC to **~50 Hz** (typical) |
| Noise Density | **0.5 – 5 nT/√Hz** | **300 – 500 µg/√Hz** |
| Drift | None (absolute reference) | None for tilt, but affected by linear acceleration |
| Heading at Poles | Degraded (weak horizontal field) | Independent of location |
| Susceptibility to Local Distortion | **HIGH** (must calibrate) | Low |
| Cost (typical marine module) | **$80 – $400** | **$20 – $150** |
| Suitability for UUVs | Excellent when properly calibrated | Excellent when vehicle is quasi-static |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The complementary filter
> $\psi_{fused}(k) = \alpha\,\psi_{gyro}(k) + (1-\alpha)\,\psi_{compass}(k)$
> fuses a low-bandwidth but drift-free compass reading with a high-bandwidth gyroscope. Typical $\alpha \approx 0.02$ gives a **time constant of ~50 s** for compass correction — a value students must know for the KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Compass-Inclinometer Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sB[Body Frame Magnetic Vector Bx By Bz] --> rot1[Rotation Matrix C theta phi]
    sA[Body Frame Acceleration Ax Ay Az] --> rot1
    sA --> tilt[Pitch and Roll Computation]
    tilt --> rot1
    rot1 --> comp[Compensated Xh Yh Zh]
    comp --> head[Heading Computation arctan2]
    head --> dec[Declination Correction Add delta]
    dec --> cal[Hard Soft Iron Calibration Matrix W]
    cal --> out1[True Heading psi deg]
    tilt --> out2[Pitch theta deg]
    tilt --> out3[Roll phi deg]
    out1 --> fusion[Complementary Filter Fusion]
    out2 --> fusion
    out3 --> fusion
    fusion --> nav[Navigation Stack Output]
```

### 4.2 Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Collect Magnetometer Data 360 deg Rotation] --> n2[Plot Bx vs By Locus]
    n2 --> n3{Is Locus Circular and Centered at Origin?}
    n3 -- No --> n4[Apply Hard Iron Offset Correction B0]
    n4 --> n5[Apply Soft Iron Matrix Correction W]
    n5 --> n6[Recompute and Validate]
    n6 --> n3
    n3 -- Yes --> n7[Calibration Complete Store W and B0]
    n7 --> n8[Deploy in Vehicle]
```

### 4.3 Sensor Fusion Logic — Complementary Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gyr[Gyroscope Angular Rate omega z] --> int[Integration]
    int --> pred[Predicted Heading psi gyro]
    cmp[Compass Heading psi mag] --> err[Error e psi mag minus psi gyro]
    pred --> err
    err --> gain[Gain alpha approx 0.02]
    gain --> corr[Corrected Heading]
    pred --> corr
    corr --> out[Output Heading psi final]
    out --> fb[Feedback for Next Cycle]
    fb --> int
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — 6-DOF Attitude Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensor Layer
        magT[Fluxgate Triad 3 axis Magnetometer]
        accT[MEMS Accelerometer Triad]
        gyrT[MEMS Gyroscope Triad]
    end
    subgraph Processing Layer
        tiltComp[Tilt Computation from Acc]
        magComp[Magnetic Tilt Compensation]
        headCalc[Heading via arctan2]
        complFilt[Complementary Filter]
        declCorr[Declination Correction IGRF]
        calMat[Calibration Matrix Application]
    end
    subgraph Output Layer
        psiOut[True Heading psi]
        thetaOut[Pitch theta]
        phiOut[Roll phi]
        rateOut[Body Rates p q r]
    end
    magT --> calMat
    accT --> tiltComp
    gyrT --> complFilt
    calMat --> magComp
    tiltComp --> magComp
    magComp --> headCalc
    headCalc --> declCorr
    declCorr --> complFilt
    tiltComp --> thetaOut
    tiltComp --> phiOut
    complFilt --> psiOut
    gyrT --> rateOut
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The diagrams above follow a strict block-architecture form because physical schematics of fluxgate cores and MEMS proof masses would require custom drawing tools beyond Mermaid's capability. The block diagrams faithfully capture all functional interactions and are accepted by KTU evaluators for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define *magnetic declination* and *magnetic inclination* (dip angle). Why is declination correction mandatory for an AUV operating in survey mode?

**Model Answer:**
Magnetic declination (δ) is the horizontal angle between **true geographic north** and **magnetic north** at a given location. Magnetic inclination (I) is the vertical angle the geomagnetic field vector makes with the local horizontal plane. Declination correction is mandatory because the fluxgate compass senses **magnetic** north only; without adding δ to the measured heading, the AUV's survey track will drift laterally by up to several hundred metres per kilometre of travel, producing unacceptable position error.

**(3 marks — 1 mark definition δ, 1 mark definition I, 1 mark engineering justification)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
List two sources of magnetic compass error in an underwater vehicle and one technique to mitigate each.

**Model Answer:**

| Error Source | Mitigation Technique |
|---|---|
| Hard-iron distortion (motor magnets, steel hull) | Offset compensation via calibration rotation; mount magnetometer on a non-magnetic stalk away from thrusters |
| Soft-iron distortion (ferrous structural parts) | Apply 3×3 correction matrix derived from ellipsoid fitting during in-water calibration |
| Magnetic declination | Use IGRF/WMM model lookup table for the operating area |
| Vehicle-induced EMI | Shielded cabling, differential signalling, common-mode chokes |

**(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **pitch (θ)** and **roll (φ)** from a 3-axis MEMS accelerometer at quasi-static conditions, clearly stating any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ROV carries a magnetometer whose uncorrected triad readings during a 360° rotation yield the offset vector $\vec{B}_0 = [2.5,\; -1.8,\; 0.6]^T\;\mu T$. The vehicle's true magnetic field at the operating site has magnitude $50\;\mu T$ and declination $\delta = -8.5°$. If the measured field at an instant is $\vec{B}_m = [22.0,\; -15.0,\; 38.0]^T\;\mu T$, compute the **true heading** assuming a level vehicle (θ = φ = 0) and a unity soft-iron matrix. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a):**

Assumption: the vehicle undergoes **no linear acceleration** (only gravity acts on the proof mass). The accelerometer triad measures $\vec{g}$ projected onto body axes.

Direction-cosine matrix (ZYX intrinsic rotation):

$$
\mathbf{C}_n^b = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta c\psi & s\phi s\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\ c\theta s\psi & s\phi s\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi & c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\ -s\theta & s\phi c\theta & c\phi c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply $\vec{g}_n = [0,\;0,\;g]^T$:

$$
A_x = -g\sin\theta
$$

$$
A_y = g\sin\phi\cos\theta
$$

$$
A_z = g\cos\phi\cos\theta
$$

Pitch derivation:

$$
\tan\theta = -\frac{A_x}{\sqrt{A_y^{\,2}+A_z^{\,2}}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta = \arctan 2\!\left(-A_x,\; \sqrt{A_y^{\,2}+A_z^{\,2}}\right)
$$

Roll derivation:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{A_y}{A_z} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \phi = \arctan 2\!\left(A_y,\; A_z\right)
$$

Note the explicit use of $\arctan 2$ to preserve quadrant information. **(7 marks — 2 marks assumptions, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks final expression)**

---

**Solution to A(b):**

Given: $\vec{B}_0 = [2.5,\; -1.8,\; 0.6]^T\;\mu T$, $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}_3$, $\vec{B}_m = [22.0,\; -15.0,\; 38.0]^T\;\mu T$.

**Step 1:** Apply hard-iron correction:

$$
\vec{B}_{cal} = \vec{B}_m - \vec{B}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 22.0-2.5 \\ -15.0-(-1.8) \\ 38.0-0.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 19.5 \\ -13.2 \\ 37.4 \end{bmatrix}\;\mu T
$$

**Step 2:** Since vehicle is level (θ = φ = 0), $X_h = B_x = 19.5\;\mu T$, $Y_h = B_y = -13.2\;\mu T$.

**Step 3:** Compute magnetic heading:

$$
\psi_{mag} = \arctan 2\!\left(-Y_h,\; X_h\right) = \arctan 2\!\left(13.2,\; 19.5\right)
$$

Numerical evaluation:

$$
\psi_{mag} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{13.2}{19.5}\right) = \arctan(0.6769) \approx 34.07°
$$

Since both $X_h > 0$ and $-Y_h > 0$, the heading is in the first quadrant — confirmed by $\arctan 2$. So $\psi_{mag} = 34.07°$.

**Step 4:** Apply declination $\delta = -8.5°$ (West declination is negative by WMM convention):

$$
\psi_{true} = \psi_{mag} + \delta = 34.07° + (-8.5°) = 25.57°
$$

**Verification of magnitude:**

$$
\sqrt{X_h^{\,2}+Y_h^{\,2}+Z_h^{\,2}} = \sqrt{19.5^2 + 13.2^2 + 37.4^2} = \sqrt{380.25 + 174.24 + 1398.76} = \sqrt{1953.25} \approx 44.20\;\mu T
$$

The deviation from the nominal 50 µT is consistent with the inclination effect at non-equatorial latitudes — acceptable.

**Final Answer:** $\psi_{true} \approx 25.57°$ (CW from true North). **(7 marks — 1 mark setup, 2 marks hard-iron step, 2 marks arctan 2 evaluation, 2 marks declination)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** With a labelled block diagram, explain the working principle of a **fluxgate magnetometer**. Why is it preferred over a Hall-effect sensor for underwater heading applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AUV's tilt-compensated horizontal magnetic field components are $X_h = 25.3\;\mu T$ and $Y_h = -12.8\;\mu T$. The local declination is $\delta = +6.2°$ (East). The vehicle's pitch is $\theta = 12°$ and roll is $\phi = -5°$, with the body-frame magnetometer reading $B_z = 36.5\;\mu T$. Calculate the **true heading** in degrees. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a):**

A fluxgate magnetometer consists of two identical, high-permeability ferromagnetic cores (e.g., permalloy) wound with a primary **excitation coil** carrying an AC signal (typically 1–10 kHz). A secondary **sense coil** is wound around both cores in opposing series.

**Operating sequence:**

1. The AC excitation drives each core alternately into **positive and negative saturation**.
2. In the absence of an external field, the sense coil sees two perfectly symmetric flux reversals → net induced voltage at the **second harmonic** (2ω) is **zero**.
3. When an external DC field $\vec{H}_{ext}$ (Earth's field) is present, one core saturates earlier than the other in each half-cycle. The symmetry breaks, and the sense coil produces a **second-harmonic voltage** whose amplitude is **proportional to $\vert H_{ext} \vert$** and whose phase encodes the **sign/direction** of $H_{ext}$.
4. Three such orthogonal sensors yield $B_x$, $B_y$, $B_z$.

**Why fluxgate over Hall-effect for UUVs:**

| Criterion | Fluxgate | Hall-effect |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity (nT-level) | **0.1 – 1 nT** typical | ~50 – 500 nT |
| Heading accuracy | **< 0.5°** achievable | ~2 – 5° typical |
| Temperature stability | Good with compensation | Poor (drift ~100 ppm/°C) |
| Cost | Moderate | Lower |

Hall-effect sensors are insufficient for survey-grade heading due to their much higher noise floor. **(7 marks — 3 marks principle, 2 marks diagram explanation, 2 marks comparison)**

---

**Solution to B(b):**

Given: $X_h = 25.3\;\mu T$, $Y_h = -12.8\;\mu T$, $\delta = +6.2°$.

The values of $X_h$ and $Y_h$ are already tilt-compensated, so we proceed directly.

**Step 1:** Compute magnetic heading using the convention $\psi_{mag} = \arctan 2(-Y_h, X_h)$:

$$
\psi_{mag} = \arctan 2\!\left(-(-12.8),\; 25.3\right) = \arctan 2\!\left(12.8,\; 25.3\right)
$$

Numerical evaluation:

$$
\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12.8}{25.3}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.5059) \approx 26.83°
$$

Both arguments are positive, so the result lies in the **first quadrant**:

$$
\psi_{mag} \approx 26.83°
$$

**Step 2:** Apply declination correction (East positive):

$$
\psi_{true} = \psi_{mag} + \delta = 26.83° + 6.20° = 33.03°
$$

**Step 3 (Verification using $B_z$ for consistency):**

We can re-derive $X_h$ and $Y_h$ from the body-frame components using the tilt-compensation equations to confirm consistency. Given the problem provides pre-tilt-compensated values, we simply confirm the horizontal field magnitude:

$$
B_h = \sqrt{25.3^2 + (-12.8)^2} = \sqrt{640.09 + 163.84} = \sqrt{803.93} \approx 28.35\;\mu T
$$

Total field:

$$
B_{tot} = \sqrt{B_h^{\,2} + B_z^{\,2}} = \sqrt{803.93 + 1332.25} = \sqrt{2136.18} \approx 46.22\;\mu T
$$

This is reasonable for a mid-latitude geomagnetic total field (~46 µT vs. the nominal 50 µT — consistent with regional variation).

**Final Answer:** $\psi_{true} \approx 33.03°$ (CW from true North). **(7 marks — 2 marks arctan 2 setup, 2 marks numerical evaluation, 1 mark declination, 2 marks verification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Using `atan` instead of `atan2`:** Loses quadrant info → wrong heading by 90° or 180°. (–2 marks typical)
> 2. **Forgetting declination sign convention:** East is **positive**, West is **negative** per WMM. Wrong sign → entire answer off by 12.4° in Q-B(b). (–1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing magnetic heading with true heading:** Some students report $\psi_{mag}$ as the final answer. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Neglecting tilt compensation:** At θ = 12°, φ = –5°, the uncompensated compass error can exceed 5° — examiners expect the tilt-correction DCM or pre-corrected values to be used. (–2 marks if ignored)
> 5. **Mixing up sin/cos in the DCM:** Pitch rotation involves $-s\theta$ in the third row — a frequent transposition error. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Forgetting to subtract $\vec{B}_0$ before computing heading:** Hard-iron offset causes systematic error of up to several degrees. (–2 marks)
> 7. **Reporting heading without units or in radians when degrees are asked:** Loses 0.5 mark formatting penalty.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Heading** (ψ) is rotation about the vertical Z-axis, measured **clockwise from North** in navigation convention.
- **Pitch** (θ) and **Roll** (φ) come from a 3-axis accelerometer assuming **zero linear acceleration** (quasi-static).
- The governing pitch equation: $\theta = \arctan 2(-A_x,\sqrt{A_y^2+A_z^2})$.
- The governing roll equation: $\phi = \arctan 2(A_y, A_z)$.
- Tilt-compensated horizontal magnetic field: $X_h = B_x\cos\theta + B_y\sin\phi\sin\theta + B_z\cos\phi\sin\theta$, $Y_h = B_y\cos\phi - B_z\sin\phi$.
- Magnetic heading: $\psi_{mag} = \arctan 2(-Y_h, X_h)$.
- True heading: $\psi_{true} = \psi_{mag} + \delta_{declination}$.
- **Hard-iron error** = constant offset $\vec{B}_0$; correct by **subtracting** the calibration offset.
- **Soft-iron error** = directional distortion; correct by **multiplying** with calibration matrix $\mathbf{W}^{-1}$.
- **Always use $\arctan 2$** (two-argument) — never single-argument $\arctan$ — for quadrant correctness.
- Earth's field: **25 – 65 µT** depending on latitude; weakest horizontal component near magnetic poles.
- Fluxgate sensitivity **0.1 – 1 nT** vs. Hall-effect **50 – 500 nT** → fluxgate preferred for survey-grade UUVs.
- MEMS accelerometer cannot distinguish gravity from sustained linear acceleration — fusion with gyroscope or compass is required during dynamic manoeuvres.
- Complementary filter gain $\alpha \approx 0.02$ gives a ~50 s correction time constant (commonly tested numerical value).
- **Declination lookup:** World Magnetic Model (WMM) or IGRF — East positive, West negative.
- The **declination table / deviation card** approach uses: $\delta_{corr} = A + B\sin\psi + C\cos\psi + D\sin 2\psi + E\cos 2\psi$.
- An inclinometer **cannot measure yaw**; only a magnetometer (or gyro) can.
- Calibration procedure: rotate vehicle through full 360° in horizontal plane and observe $B_x$ vs $B_y$ locus — should be a circle centred at origin.
- For underwater vehicles, mount the magnetometer on a **non-magnetic stalk** (e.g., aluminium or polymer) at least **0.5 – 1 m** from thrusters and the main pressure housing to reduce hard-iron effects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tactile and Proximity Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tactile and Proximity Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tactile Sensors — Formal Definition

A **tactile sensor** is a contact-based transduction device that converts mechanical stimuli (force, pressure, vibration, texture, or slip) acting on its surface into a measurable electrical signal. In underwater robotics, tactile sensors are integrated onto **robotic fingers, gripper pads, hull-crawling skins, and manipulator end-effectors** to enable compliant interaction with the unstructured subsea environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):** A tactile sensor is "a device that measures parameters of physical contact between the sensor and an object, including contact force, pressure distribution, and surface texture, typically producing a spatially distributed pressure map over an array of sensing elements."

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blind Hand of an Octopus"

Imagine an octopus exploring a dark shipwreck. It cannot see, but its arms are covered with millions of chemical and mechanical receptors. When a sucker presses against a rusty iron railing, the octopus *feels* the texture, hardness, and shape — without needing light. **An underwater tactile sensor does exactly this for a robot:** it gives the machine a "blind sense of touch" when vision (due to murky water, low light, or turbidity) fails.

## 1.2 Proximity Sensors — Formal Definition

A **proximity sensor** is a non-contact transducer that detects the presence, distance, or approach velocity of a target object within a defined sensing range, without any physical contact. In underwater robotics, proximity sensors are the **first line of defense** for obstacle avoidance, station-keeping, autonomous docking, and pipeline inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):** A proximity sensor is "a non-contact device that emits an interrogating field (acoustic, electric, magnetic, or optical) and detects perturbations in that field caused by the presence of a target, thereby inferring the target's range or existence."

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — "A Bat in a Subsea Cave"

A bat flying in total darkness emits ultrasonic chirps and listens to the echoes to know how close a wall is — it never has to *touch* the wall. **A subsea proximity sensor (typically acoustic/ultrasonic) does the same thing underwater**, where light attenuates within a few meters but sound propagates for kilometers.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Speed of sound in seawater:** $c \approx \mathbf{1500 \ m/s}$ (≈ 4.5× faster than in air).
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho \approx \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$.
- **Sensitivity of a tactile array:** typically expressed in **kPa** (kilopascals) or **mN** (millinewtons).
- **Proximity sensor range bands:** near-field (< 1 m), mid-field (1–10 m), far-field (> 10 m).
- **Spatial resolution of tactile arrays:** typically **1–5 mm** center-to-center pitch in modern AUV/ROV skins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Whenever a numerical is asked on acoustic ranging, the **1500 m/s** value in seawater is the canonical figure examiners expect. Using 340 m/s (air) will be marked wrong.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (Mandatory Board Diagram in Words)

| Family | Contact? | Typical Subsea Use |
|---|---|---|
| Tactile | **Yes** (physical touch required) | Grasping, hull inspection, compliant manipulation |
| Proximity | **No** (field-based detection) | Obstacle avoidance, docking, mapping |

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tactile Sensor — Operating Principles

A tactile sensor operates on the principle of **mechanotransduction**: an applied mechanical quantity $F$ (force) is converted into a measurable signal $V$ (voltage) through one of the following transduction mechanisms.

### 2.1.1 Piezoresistive Tactile Sensors
- **Mechanism:** Applied force changes the resistance of a conductive elastomer (e.g., carbon-loaded silicone).
- **Equation:** $R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$, where change in geometry (L, A) or resistivity $\rho$ due to strain yields a measurable $\Delta R$.
- **Why popular underwater:** Simple, low-cost, robust to salt-water corrosion when encapsulated.

### 2.1.2 Piezoelectric Tactile Sensors
- **Mechanism:** Applied stress $T$ generates an electric charge $Q$ across a crystal (e.g., PVDF, PZT).
- **Equation:** $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge constant (C/N).
- **Why preferred for vibration:** Dynamic response only — ideal for slip detection and texture sensing.

### 2.1.3 Capacitive Tactile Sensors
- **Mechanism:** Force changes the gap $d$ between two parallel plates.
- **Equation:** $C = \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{A}{d}$.
- **Why favored in arrays:** CMOS-compatible, high spatial resolution (down to sub-mm pitch).

### 2.1.4 Optical Tactile Sensors
- **Mechanism:** An elastomer surface is illuminated internally; contact with an object deforms the surface and modulates reflected light, captured by an internal camera.
- **Example:** **GelSight**-type sensors, which produce a high-resolution 3D micro-geometry of the touched object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** For a 14-mark question, students are expected to explain **at least 3 transduction principles** with their governing equations. Memorizing only the names without equations costs 4–5 marks.

## 2.2 Proximity Sensor — Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Ultrasonic / Acoustic Proximity (Dominant in Underwater)
- **Mechanism:** A transducer emits a short acoustic pulse; the time-of-flight (ToF) to the target echo gives the range.
- **Dominant because:** Sound attenuates only ~0.04 dB/m in seawater vs. light which attenuates ~4 dB/m (in clear ocean water).

### 2.2.2 Capacitive Proximity
- **Mechanism:** A target entering the electric field changes the sensor's capacitance to ground.
- **Use:** Non-metallic object detection; effective through glass or thin polymer windows.

### 2.2.3 Inductive Proximity
- **Mechanism:** An eddy-current loop in the sensor is damped by a metallic target.
- **Limitation underwater:** Limited to ~few mm range; used only on manipulator fingertips near metal fixtures.

### 2.2.4 Optical / LIDAR Proximity
- **Mechanism:** ToF of a laser pulse, or triangulation.
- **Limitation underwater:** Severe attenuation in turbid water; effective only at < 5 m in clear water.

### 2.2.5 Magnetic Proximity (Hall Effect / Magnetoresistive)
- **Mechanism:** Detects a permanent magnet on the target.
- **Use:** Docking of AUVs into a subsea dock with magnetic fiducials.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acoustic range from ToF | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | c = speed of sound in water (1500 m/s), t = round-trip time | m |
| 2 | Piezoresistive sensitivity | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | GF = gauge factor, $\varepsilon$ = strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | d = charge constant, F = applied force | C |
| 4 | Capacitive tactile | $C = \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon$ = permittivity, A = area, d = gap | F |
| 5 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho$ = water density, g = 9.81, h = depth | Pa |
| 6 | Acoustic attenuation | $I(x) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha x / 10}$ | $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (dB/m) | W/m² |
| 7 | Beam spread (sonar) | $\theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength, D = aperture diameter | rad |
| 8 | Sensor SNR | $SNR = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$ | signal and noise voltages | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** The expression $\dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ uses \vert or \mid only if needed; here we used a fraction to avoid the pipe character entirely. Always substitute the vertical pipe `\vert` (or fractions) in tables to keep the markdown parser happy.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tactile sensors on ROV grippers** are used to grasp delicate biological samples (cold-water corals, sponges) without crushing them.
- **Acoustic proximity sensors** on AUVs enable safe navigation under Antarctic ice shelves where optical sensors are useless.
- **Capacitive tactile skins** on inspection robots detect micro-cracks in oil-riser pipes by "feeling" surface roughness.
- **Proximity-augmented docking:** AUVs use a downward-looking echo-sounder to land softly on a subsea dock.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 CONTENT: Deeper Analytical Mechanics (continued from above)

## 2.5 Underwater-Specific Challenges (Why Subsea Sensing is Harder)

1. **Pressure:** At 1000 m depth, ambient pressure is ≈ 100 atm. Sensors must be pressure-compensated or housed in oil-filled compliant bladders.
2. **Corrosion:** Saltwater degrades exposed metals; titanium or polymer encapsulation is mandatory.
3. **Biofouling:** Marine growth can mask or short sensor surfaces — anti-fouling coatings required.
4. **Acoustic crosstalk:** Multiple AUVs operating in proximity can interfere with each other's sonars.

## 2.6 Tactile Sensor Performance Parameters (Board-Favorite)

- **Sensitivity:** $\Delta V / \Delta F$ in mV/N.
- **Spatial resolution:** center-to-center distance of sensing elements (taxels).
- **Hysteresis:** difference in output between loading and unloading at the same force (should be < 5 %).
- **Response time:** typically 1–10 ms.
- **Dynamic range:** ratio between maximum and minimum detectable force, often expressed in dB.

## 2.7 Proximity Sensor Performance Parameters

- **Sensing range:** minimum and maximum detection distance.
- **Repeatability:** standard deviation of repeated range measurements at fixed target distance.
- **Beam angle:** angular spread of emitted field.
- **Update rate:** measurements per second (Hz).
- **False-positive rate:** probability of detecting a target that does not exist (e.g., due to bubbles).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Acoustic Range from Time-of-Flight

The acoustic proximity sensor emits a pulse at time $t_0$. The pulse travels through water, strikes the target, and returns as an echo, received at time $t_1$. The total round-trip time is:

$$t = t_1 - t_0$$

The pulse travels a total path of length $c \cdot t$, where $c$ is the speed of sound in seawater. Because this path covers the **outbound and inbound legs**, the one-way distance to the target is half the total path:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

### Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** An ROV-mounted forward-looking sonar emits an acoustic pulse. The echo from a subsea pipeline returns in $t = 12 \ \text{ms}$. Compute the range $R$, given $c = 1500 \ \text{m/s}$ in seawater.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.** Acoustic range is given by the one-way distance formula:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Convert time to SI units.** Convert milliseconds to seconds:

$$t = 12 \ \text{ms} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$R = \frac{1500 \ \text{m/s} \times 12 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and divide by 2.**

$$R = \frac{1500 \times 0.012}{2} = \frac{18}{2} = 9 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude with units.** The pipeline is located **9 metres** in front of the ROV. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure at Sensor Depth

A subsea robot descending experiences increasing hydrostatic pressure. Derive the pressure on a sensor located at depth $h$.

### Step 1 — Free-body concept.
Consider a column of seawater of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$. The weight of this column is:

$$W = m \cdot g = (\rho \cdot A \cdot h) \cdot g$$

### Step 2 — Pressure definition.
Pressure is force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A}$$

### Step 3 — Cancel A.

$$P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

### Step 4 — Substitute seawater values at $h = 500$ m.

$$P_h = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2 \times 500 \ \text{m}$$

$$P_h = 5{,}027{,}625 \ \text{Pa} \approx 5.03 \ \text{MPa} \approx 50.3 \ \text{atm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always carry units through the algebra. Final answer must have units of **Pa (or MPa)**. A bare number with no unit is marked **strictly zero** by KTU examiners.

## 3.3 Derivation: Capacitive Tactile Sensor Sensitivity

For a parallel-plate capacitor with area $A$, plate separation $d$, and dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon$:

$$C = \varepsilon \cdot \frac{A}{d}$$

Let an applied force reduce the gap from $d_0$ to $d = d_0 - \Delta d$. The change in capacitance is:

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon \cdot A \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right)$$

Using the small-displacement approximation $d_0 - \Delta d \approx d_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}\right)$ and the first-order Taylor expansion $\dfrac{1}{1-x} \approx 1 + x$:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot \Delta d}{d_0^2} = C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$

The **sensitivity** is therefore:

$$S = \frac{\Delta C / C_0}{\Delta d} = \frac{1}{d_0}$$

Smaller initial gap → higher sensitivity. This is why sub-mm capacitive tactile arrays are so responsive.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Choosing the Right Sensor for a Subsea Task)

| Task | Best Sensor Type | Justification (with Key Equation) |
|---|---|---|
| Grasping a fragile sponge | **Capacitive tactile** | $\Delta C \propto \Delta d$, sub-mN resolution |
| Detecting a 20-m distant subsea pipeline | **Acoustic proximity** | $R = c t/2$ with $c = 1500$ m/s, long range |
| Detecting a metallic docking pin at 2 cm | **Inductive proximity** | Eddy-current damping, mm-scale range |
| Slip detection in a wet gripper | **Piezoelectric tactile** | $Q = d F$, dynamic response to vibration |
| Mapping hull texture (1 mm features) | **Optical tactile (GelSight)** | Sub-mm micro-geometry via internal camera |
| Avoiding a 5-m distant wall in clear water | **Optical / LIDAR** | $R = c t/2$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (light) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Tactile Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Stimulus Force or Pressure] --> B[Transduction Layer Piezoresistive Capacitive or Piezoelectric]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    C --> D[ADC Conversion]
    D --> E[Microprocessor or FPGA]
    E --> F[Decision Output Grasp Slip or Release]
    style A fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style F fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The leftmost node (blue) represents the physical contact, while the rightmost node (orange) is the digital control decision. The chain follows KTU's expected *signal-flow* answer format for sensor descriptions.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Acoustic Proximity Sensor (Echosounder)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Trigger Pulse from Controller] --> T[Xmit Transducer]
    T -->|Acoustic wave at 1500 m/s| W[Water Column]
    W -->|Echo reflected| R[Recv Transducer]
    R --> AMP[Low Noise Amplifier]
    AMP --> GD[Time of Flight Detector]
    GD --> CALC[Range Calculator R = c t over 2]
    CALC --> OUT[Digital Range Output metres]
    style T fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style R fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style OUT fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#1a4d1a
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Tactile vs Proximity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stimulus]:::stim --> Q1{Contact or No Contact}
    Q1 -->|Yes Contact| TACT[Tactile Sensor Array]
    Q1 -->|No Contact Field based| PROX[Proximity Sensor Acoustic Capacitive Inductive]
    TACT --> OUT1[Force Pressure Texture Map]
    PROX --> OUT2[Range Presence Absence]
    classDef stim fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (T, R, AMP, etc.). No reserved keywords (end, subgraph, style) appear as standalone node names. All labels are plain text without markdown bold tags.

## 4.4 Sensor Selection Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Is the target in contact with the robot?}
    Q -->|Yes| Q2{Is force or texture the primary measurand?}
    Q -->|No| Q3{What is the expected range?}
    Q2 -->|Force| PIEZ[Piezoelectric or Piezoresistive Array]
    Q2 -->|Texture| OPT[Optical Tactile GelSight type]
    Q3 -->|Less than 5 cm| IND[Inductive or Capacitive Proximity]
    Q3 -->|5 cm to 50 m| ACO[Acoustic Ultrasonic Proximity]
    Q3 -->|Greater than 50 m| SONAR[Long Range Sonar]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define a tactile sensor. List any two transduction principles used in underwater tactile sensing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A tactile sensor is a contact-based device that converts mechanical stimuli such as force, pressure, vibration, or texture into a measurable electrical signal when its surface physically contacts an object (1 mark).
Two transduction principles used underwater are (1 mark each):
1. **Piezoresistive** — change in resistance of a conductive elastomer under force.
2. **Capacitive** — change in capacitance due to gap reduction between parallel plates.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Why are acoustic proximity sensors preferred over optical sensors in deep-sea applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Acoustic proximity sensors use sound waves that propagate through seawater with very low attenuation (≈ 0.04 dB/m) and a speed of **1500 m/s** (1 mark). In contrast, optical signals attenuate rapidly (≈ 4 dB/m even in clear ocean water) due to absorption and scattering by suspended particles (1 mark). Therefore, acoustic sensors provide reliable non-contact ranging up to hundreds of metres, making them the preferred choice for deep-sea obstacle avoidance and navigation (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric tactile sensor with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between applied force and generated charge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ROV uses a forward-looking acoustic proximity sensor. The echo from a subsea wall returns in $t = 8$ ms. Calculate the distance to the wall. Given the speed of sound in seawater $c = 1500$ m/s, also find the time taken for the next pulse if the maximum unambiguous range is 50 m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Working principle (3 marks).**
A piezoelectric tactile sensor uses a piezoelectric material (e.g., PVDF polymer or PZT ceramic) sandwiched between two electrodes. When an external force $F$ is applied normal to the surface, the material undergoes mechanical strain. Due to the piezoelectric effect, this strain produces an electric charge $Q$ on the electrodes, which is then conditioned by a charge amplifier into a measurable voltage.

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks).**
The constitutive piezoelectric equation relating applied force $F$ to generated charge $Q$ is:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge constant** in coulombs per newton (C/N). For PVDF, $d \approx 33$ pC/N; for PZT, $d \approx 250$ pC/N. The output voltage across a capacitance $C_p$ of the piezoelectric element is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

**Step 3 — Block diagram description (1 mark).** Signal flow: *Applied Force → Piezoelectric Element → Charge Amplifier → Filter → ADC → Microcontroller.*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the range (3 marks).**
The acoustic range formula is:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

Substituting $c = 1500$ m/s and $t = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ s:

$$R = \frac{1500 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6 \ \text{m}$$

The wall is **6 m** ahead of the ROV.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum unambiguous range constraint (2 marks).**
For a maximum unambiguous range of 50 m, the round-trip time is:

$$t_{max} = \frac{2 R_{max}}{c} = \frac{2 \times 50}{1500} = 0.0667 \ \text{s} \approx 66.7 \ \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the next pulse must be transmitted at least 66.7 ms after the previous one (2 marks).** Hence the pulse repetition interval (PRI) is 66.7 ms, corresponding to a pulse repetition frequency (PRF) of:

$$PRF = \frac{1}{PRI} = \frac{1}{0.0667} \approx 15 \ \text{Hz}$$

---

### Question B — [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]

**(a)** Compare the working principles of **capacitive** and **piezoresistive** tactile sensors. State one underwater application of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the operation of an acoustic proximity sensor used in an AUV for obstacle avoidance. Mention the role of the time-of-flight detector. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

| Parameter | Capacitive Tactile | Piezoresistive Tactile |
|---|---|---|
| **Transduction** | Change in capacitance $C = \varepsilon A / d$ due to gap change | Change in resistance $R = \rho L/A$ due to strain |
| **Sensitivity Driver** | Gap reduction $\Delta d$ | Strain $\varepsilon$ and gauge factor $GF$ |
| **Spatial Resolution** | Sub-mm (CMOS compatible) | mm-scale (elastomer grid) |
| **Dynamic Response** | Static and dynamic | Mainly static (DC-coupled) |
| **Hysteresis** | Low | Moderate (elastomer creep) |
| **Underwater Use** | Delicate gripper pads on ROV for grasping biological samples (3.5 marks) | Hull-inspection crawlers measuring pressure distribution on ship hulls (3.5 marks) |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Block diagram (already in Section 4.2 of these notes).** The flow is:
**Controller → Transmit Transducer → Water Column → Echo from obstacle → Receive Transducer → LNA → ToF Detector → Range Calculator → Output (m).**

**Operation (5 marks):** The controller triggers the transmit transducer, which emits a short acoustic pulse. The pulse travels through seawater at 1500 m/s, reflects from the obstacle, and is captured by the receive transducer. The low-noise amplifier boosts the weak echo. The **time-of-flight detector** measures the elapsed time $t$ between transmission and reception.

**Role of ToF detector (2 marks):** The ToF detector is the heart of the system — it provides the precise time interval that is fed to the range calculator, which then computes $R = c t / 2$. Without accurate ToF measurement, the entire ranging function fails. Typical ToF detectors use a leading-edge threshold circuit with sub-microsecond resolution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to halve the time** in the acoustic range equation. Writing $R = c t$ instead of $R = c t/2$ costs 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Using $c = 340$ m/s** (air) instead of **1500 m/s** (seawater). This is the most common error; examiners treat it as a *conceptual* error worth 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Memorising the sensor name but not the governing equation** — at least one equation per sensor is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Omitting units** in the final answer. Marks are deducted even if the numerical value is correct.
> 5. **Confusing tactile (contact) with proximity (non-contact)** in classification questions. The examiner's key explicitly tests this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✔ **Tactile sensors REQUIRE contact**; **proximity sensors do NOT**.
- ✔ The **speed of sound in seawater is 1500 m/s** — always use this in numericals.
- ✔ Acoustic range formula: $R = c t / 2$ — the **division by 2** is for round-trip.
- ✔ **Piezoelectric** is best for **dynamic** (vibration, slip) tactile sensing.
- ✔ **Piezoresistive** is best for **static** pressure mapping on elastomer skins.
- ✔ **Capacitive** tactile gives **highest spatial resolution** (sub-mm).
- ✔ **Acoustic proximity** dominates underwater because light attenuates rapidly.
- ✔ **Hydrostatic pressure** at depth $h$ is $P_h = \rho g h$; affects sensor packaging.
- ✔ For **docking**, magnetic or acoustic proximity sensors are preferred.
- ✔ Always state the **governing equation** in long answers; examiners allocate 2–3 marks for it.
- ✔ Always state the **units** in numericals; KTU deducts marks for unit-less answers.
- ✔ **Beam spread** $\theta = \lambda / D$ is a common 2-mark sub-question for sonars.
- ✔ **Hysteresis, sensitivity, dynamic range** are key tactile performance metrics.
- ✔ **Range, beam angle, PRF, false-positive rate** are key proximity performance metrics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasound Range Finder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultrasound Range Finder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ultrasound Range Finder (URF)** is an active acoustic sensing instrument that emits a high-frequency pressure wave (typically 20 kHz to 2 MHz) into the water column and measures the round-trip time-of-flight (ToF) of the reflected echo from submerged obstacles, the seabed, or other acoustic reflectors. The two-way travel time is multiplied by the local **speed of sound in water** (~1500 m/s) and divided by two to compute the slant range to the target. In the KTU 2024 scheme context of underwater robotics, the URF is classified as a *primary short-to-medium range obstacle detection and terrain mapping sensor* for AUVs, ROVs, and bottom-crawling vehicles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Ultrasound range finders are covered under the *Acoustic Sensors and Perception* unit of Module 3, with a strong emphasis on the time-of-flight principle, speed of sound in water, transducer beam geometry, and signal processing for obstacle avoidance.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine a **blind person in a foggy swimming pool** holding a stick. They push the stick forward; when it hits something, the tap vibrates back to their hand, and the delay tells them how far the wall is. The ultrasound range finder works the same way, but instead of a stick, it sends a *chirp of sound* (a pressure wave) and listens for the echo. Because sound travels about **4.3 times faster in water (≈1500 m/s) than in air (≈343 m/s)**, sonar is the most efficient long-range sensing modality underwater — radio waves and light get absorbed within meters, but sound can travel kilometers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants for KTU Examinations**
> - Speed of sound in freshwater (20°C): **c ≈ 1481 m/s**
> - Speed of sound in seawater (20°C, 35 ppt salinity): **c ≈ 1522 m/s**
> - Standard reference value used in problems: **c ≈ 1500 m/s**
> - Density of seawater: **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³**
> - Acoustic impedance of seawater: **Z ≈ 1.54 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s) (Rayl)**

### 1.3 Classification by Operating Frequency

| Class | Frequency | Typical Range | Application in UWR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low-frequency (LF) | 10–50 kHz | 1–10 km | Long-range AUV obstacle warning |
| Medium-frequency (MF) | 50–200 kHz | 100 m – 1 km | General AUV/ROV navigation |
| High-frequency (HF) | 200 kHz – 1 MHz | 1–100 m | Altimeter, docking, short-range |
| Very-high (VHF) | > 1 MHz | < 5 m | Precision homing, gap detection |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step
