1. **Pulse Generation:** The transmit electronics drive a piezoelectric ceramic (typically Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT) which mechanically vibrates and launches a short acoustic pulse (1 µs to 10 ms duration) into the water.
2. **Propagation:** The acoustic wavefront expands spherically (or is focused via a curved transducer) at the local sound speed, losing energy due to *geometric spreading* and *absorption*.
3. **Reflection:** Upon striking a target (whose acoustic impedance differs from water), a portion of the incident intensity is scattered back. A rigid seabed reflects nearly all energy; a soft mud absorbs most of it.
4. **Reception:** The same (or a co-located) transducer converts the returning pressure oscillation into a tiny voltage signal (microvolts to millivolts).
5. **Time Measurement:** A high-precision counter/timer captures the round-trip interval $\Delta t$ between transmit and detection, often threshold-detected at the leading edge of the echo.
6. **Range Computation:** The microcontroller applies the basic sonar equation $R = \tfrac{c\,\Delta t}{2}$.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range from ToF | $R = \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{2}$ | $R$: range (m), $c$: sound speed (m/s), $\Delta t$: round-trip time (s) | Divide by **2** because wave travels to and from target |
| 2 | Sound speed in seawater (Mackenzie, 1981) | $c = 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.05304T^2 + 0.0002374T^3 + 1.340(S-35) + 0.0163D + 1.67 \times 10^{-7}D^2$ | $T$ in °C, $S$ in ppt, $D$ in metres | Valid for 0 ≤ T ≤ 30 °C, 30 ≤ S ≤ 40, 0 ≤ D ≤ 8000 m |
| 3 | Sound speed (UNESCO, simpler) | $c = 1449.2 + 4.6T - 0.055T^2 + 0.00029T^3 + (1.34 - 0.01T)(S-35) + 0.016D$ | Same as above | Easier for hand-calculation in exams |
| 4 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $\lambda$ in m, $f$ in Hz | Determines beam width and target resolution |
| 5 | Half-power beam width (circular piston) | $\theta_{3dB} \approx \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D_{tr}}$ | $\theta_{3dB}$ in rad, $D_{tr}$ in m | $D_{tr}$ = transducer aperture diameter |
| 6 | Near-field (Fresnel) distance | $N = \dfrac{D_{tr}^2}{4\lambda} = \dfrac{D_{tr}^2 f}{4c}$ | $N$ in m | Beyond $N$ the beam begins to diverge |
| 7 | Absorption coefficient (Thorp, low-freq) | $\alpha = \dfrac{0.11 f^2}{1 + f^2} + \dfrac{44 f^2}{4100 + f^2} + 2.75 \times 10^{-4} f^2 + 0.003$ | $f$ in kHz, $\alpha$ in dB/km | Valid 1–100 kHz |
| 8 | Absorption (Francois–Garrison, broad-band) | $\alpha = \dfrac{A_1 P_1 f_1 f^2}{f_1^2 + f^2} + \dfrac{A_2 P_2 f_2 f^2}{f_2^2 + f^2} + A_3 P_3 f^2$ | $f$ in kHz, $\alpha$ in dB/km | Coefficients depend on T, S, depth; preferred in KTU numericals |
| 9 | Two-way geometric spreading loss | $TL_{geom} = 20 \log_{10}(R) + 20 \log_{10}(R) = 40 \log_{10}(R)$ | $R$ in metres (use 1 m as reference) | Doubled for transmit + return path |
| 10 | Two-way absorption loss | $TL_{abs} = 2 \alpha R$ | $\alpha$ in dB/km, $R$ in km | Doubled because wave travels round-trip |
| 11 | Total two-way transmission loss | $TL = 40 \log_{10}(R) + 2\alpha R$ | dB | The standard KTU sonar equation form |
| 12 | Active Sonar Equation | $SNR = SL - 2TL + TS - (NL - DI)$ | All in dB | SL: source level, TS: target strength, NL: noise level, DI: directivity index |
| 13 | Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c\,T_{PRI}}{2}$ | $T_{PRI}$: pulse repetition interval (s) | Set by the inter-pulse gap to avoid range ambiguity |
| 14 | Range resolution | $\delta R = \dfrac{c\,\tau}{2}$ | $\tau$: pulse width (s) | Shorter pulse → finer resolution |
| 15 | Angular resolution | $\delta\theta \approx \dfrac{\theta_{3dB}}{2}$ | rad | Determines ability to separate two targets at same range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Use-Case:** The two-way transmission loss $TL = 40 \log_{10}(R) + 2\alpha R$ is the single most important equation for an underwater robotics engineer to size the transmit power, choose the operating frequency, and predict the maximum detection range of an altimeter on a hovering ROV. AUV docking systems at 300 kHz, for instance, achieve reliable ranges of 50 m when the absorption coefficient is around 0.5 dB/km.

### 2.3 Transducer Physics (The "How" Behind the Pulse)

- **Piezoelectric Effect:** PZT crystals deform when a voltage is applied (transmit) and generate a voltage when mechanically stressed by an incoming wave (receive). This is *the* fundamental transduction mechanism.
- **Resonance:** A transducer is most efficient at its thickness-mode resonance frequency $f_r = c_p / (2 t)$, where $c_p$ is the sound speed in the PZT and $t$ is the ceramic thickness. Halving the thickness doubles the operating frequency.
- **Backing & Matching Layers:** A dense backing absorbs backward radiation (broadens bandwidth), while a quarter-wave matching layer of impedance $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{tr} \cdot Z_{water}}$ maximises energy transfer into water.
- **Beam Forming:** A flat circular piston gives a $\sin(x)/x$ directivity pattern; a curved (spherical) or phased-array transducer focuses energy to a narrow beam.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Range Equation from Time-of-Flight

**Starting Physical Premise:** A pulse is transmitted at $t = 0$, travels at constant speed $c$ in water, reflects from a target at range $R$, and returns along the same path. The total distance covered is $2R$ and the elapsed time is $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 — Write the kinematic relation between distance, speed and time.**

$$
\text{Total distance travelled} = \text{speed} \times \text{time}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the acoustic path length and the round-trip time.**

$$
2R = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the range $R$ by dividing both sides by 2.**

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in convenient engineering units (range in metres, time in milliseconds, speed in m/s).**

If $\Delta t$ is measured in ms, then $c$ in m/s must be multiplied by $10^{-3}$:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t_{(ms)}}{2000} \quad \text{[metres]}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU Part-B style):**

> A UUV-mounted ultrasound range finder operating at 200 kHz in seawater ($c = 1520$ m/s) records a round-trip echo time of $\Delta t = 4.5$ ms. Compute the range to the obstacle.

$$
R = \frac{1520 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{6.84}{2} = 3.42 \text{ m}
$$

**Answer:** The obstacle is **3.42 m** away.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Half-Power Beam-Width of a Circular Piston Transducer

**Starting Premise:** A flat circular piston of diameter $D_{tr}$ vibrates uniformly and radiates a far-field pressure pattern. The first zero of the directivity function $J_1(x)/x$ occurs at $x = 3.832$, where $J_1$ is the Bessel function of order 1.

**Step 1 — The far-field directivity pattern of a circular piston (Rayleigh integral result).**

$$
D(\theta) = \left[ \frac{2 J_1\!\left( \frac{\pi D_{tr}}{\lambda} \sin\theta \right)}{\frac{\pi D_{tr}}{\lambda} \sin\theta} \right]^2
$$

**Step 2 — Find the first null by setting the argument equal to 3.832.**

$$
\frac{\pi D_{tr}}{\lambda} \sin\theta_1 = 3.832
$$

**Step 3 — For small angles, $\sin\theta_1 \approx \theta_1$.**

$$
\theta_1 \approx \frac{3.832 \, \lambda}{\pi D_{tr}} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D_{tr}}
$$

**Step 4 — The full beam width between first nulls (FWHM ≈ 0.886 × FWFN approximation gives):**

$$
\theta_{3dB} \approx \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D_{tr}} = \frac{1.22 \, c}{f D_{tr}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example:**

> A 75 kHz URF has a circular transducer of diameter $D_{tr} = 0.10$ m. Compute the half-power beam width in seawater ($c = 1500$ m/s).

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1500}{75 \times 10^3} = 0.020 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_{3dB} = \frac{1.22 \times 0.020}{0.10} = 0.244 \text{ rad} = 14.0^{\circ}
$$

**Answer:** The half-power beam width is **14.0° (≈ 0.244 rad)**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Two-Way Transmission Loss

**Step 1 — Start from the one-way spreading loss for a spherical wave in an unbounded medium:**

Spherical spreading causes intensity to fall off as $1/R^2$, so in dB relative to 1 m:

$$
TL_{one-way,geom} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right) \quad \text{with } R_0 = 1 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply this twice (transmit and receive) and add:**

$$
TL_{geom} = 20 \log_{10} R + 20 \log_{10} R = 40 \log_{10} R
$$

**Step 3 — Add the round-trip absorption loss, which is linear in distance:**

For one-way travel distance $R$, the absorption loss is $\alpha R$ dB (when $R$ is in km and $\alpha$ in dB/km).

$$
TL_{abs} = 2 \alpha R
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the geometric and absorptive components for the total two-way loss:**

$$
\boxed{\,TL = 40 \log_{10} R + 2 \alpha R \quad \text{(dB)}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**

> An AUV altimeter at 300 kHz detects the seabed at 80 m. Using $\alpha = 0.45$ dB/km at this frequency, compute the total two-way transmission loss.

$$
40 \log_{10}(80) = 40 \times 1.9031 = 76.12 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
2 \alpha R = 2 \times 0.45 \times 0.08 = 0.072 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
TL = 76.12 + 0.072 = 76.19 \text{ dB}
$$

**Answer:** **TL ≈ 76.19 dB** — geometric spreading completely dominates at short ranges; absorption only matters at frequencies > 100 kHz and ranges > 1 km.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Active Sonar Equation

**Step 1 — Express the received signal level (RL) at the transducer in dB:**

$$
RL = SL - TL_{one-way,down} + TS - TL_{one-way,up}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognise that the down- and up-path losses are equal for a monostatic sonar (same transducer):**

$$
RL = SL - 2 \cdot TL_{one-way} + TS
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the noise-plus-reverb level (NL) and add the directivity index (DI) to get SNR:**

$$
SNR = SL - 2\,TL + TS - (NL - DI)
$$

This is the **fundamental KTU active-sonar equation** that must be satisfied ($SNR \geq$ detection threshold) for the range finder to "see" the target.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Ultrasound Range Finder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trigger Pulse Generator] --> B[HV Pulser TX Switch]
    B --> C[PZT Transducer]
    C -->|Acoustic Pulse| D[Water Column]
    D -->|Reflected Echo| C
    C --> E[Low Noise Pre-amplifier]
    E --> F[Bandpass Filter]
    F --> G[Variable Gain Amplifier TVG]
    G --> H[Envelope Detector]
    H --> I[Threshold Comparator]
    I --> J[Time to Digital Converter TDC]
    J --> K[Microcontroller DSP]
    K --> L[Range Output Distance m]
    K --> M[Serial Bus UART I2C]
    N[Crystal Oscillator Clock] --> A
    N --> J
```

### 4.2 Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Trigger : Timer tick
    Trigger --> Transmit : HV pulse to PZT
    Transmit --> Damp : Ring down settling ~200 us
    Damp --> Listen : Switch to RX
    Listen --> Detect : Echo above threshold
    Listen --> Timeout : No echo in T_max
    Detect --> Measure : Latch time stamp
    Timeout --> Idle : Flag no target
    Measure --> Compute : Range = c t / 2
    Compute --> Output : Push to host
    Output --> Idle
```

### 4.3 Beam Geometry Diagram (Cylindrical Coordinates)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRANSDUCER_MODULE
        A1[Flat Circular Piston Dia D_tr]
    end
    A1 -->|Far Field Begins| B1[Rayleigh Distance N = D_tr^2 f / 4 c]
    B1 -->|3 dB Cone| C1[Half Power Beam Width theta_3dB = 1.22 lambda / D_tr]
    C1 -->|Along Axis| D1[On Axis Target Range R]
    C1 -->|At Edge| E1[Off Axis Target Range R sec theta]
    D1 --> F1[Echo ToF = 2R / c]
    E1 --> F1
```

### 4.4 Signal Processing Chain (Time-Domain View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX_CHAIN
        T1[Trigger 1 to 10 V] --> T2[HV Pulser 100 to 500 V]
        T2 --> T3[PZT Burst 5 to 20 cycles]
    end
    subgraph RX_CHAIN
        R1[Hydrophone PZT] --> R2[Preamp 60 dB]
        R2 --> R3[BPF Center f_0 Q 5 to 10]
        R3 --> R4[TVG 20 log R]
        R4 --> R5[Detector Rectifier Envelope]
        R5 --> R6[Threshold CFD]
        R6 --> R7[Stop Counter TDC]
    end
    T3 -->|water path| R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Insight:** The two most important physical parameters dictating sensor performance are the **diameter $D_{tr}$** of the transducer (controls beam width $\theta_{3dB}$) and the **operating frequency $f$** (controls absorption $\alpha$ and resolution $\delta R$). A large-diameter, low-frequency unit gives long range and wide beam; a small-diameter, high-frequency unit gives short range and a narrow, focused beam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]**
*Define an underwater ultrasound range finder. Why is sonar (and not radar or lidar) the preferred sensing modality for underwater robots?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** An ultrasound range finder is an active acoustic instrument that transmits a high-frequency pressure wave (20 kHz – 2 MHz) into water and measures the round-trip time-of-flight of the echo from a target to compute the slant range via $R = c\Delta t/2$, where $c \approx 1500$ m/s is the local speed of sound in water.
- **Justification (1.5 marks):** Electromagnetic waves (light, radio, millimetre-wave radar) are absorbed within a few metres in seawater due to conductive losses (attenuation $\sim 1$ to $10^4$ dB/m). Sound waves, by contrast, suffer only $\sim 0.04$ dB/km at 1 kHz, allowing ranges of kilometres — making sonar the only viable long-range modality for AUVs/ROVs.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the factors on which the speed of sound in seawater depends. Write the Mackenzie formula for sound speed.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Factors (1.5 marks):** Temperature ($T$, in °C), salinity ($S$, in ppt or ‰), and depth/pressure ($D$, in m). Of these, temperature has the strongest effect ($\sim 4.6$ m/s per °C), followed by salinity ($\sim 1.3$ m/s per ppt) and depth ($\sim 0.016$ m/s per m).
- **Mackenzie formula (1.5 marks):**
$$
c = 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.05304T^2 + 0.0002374T^3 + 1.340(S-35) + 0.0163D + 1.67 \times 10^{-7}D^2 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$
Valid for $0 \le T \le 30$ °C, $30 \le S \le 40$ ppt, $0 \le D \le 8000$ m.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**  **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the working of an ultrasound range finder used in an autonomous underwater vehicle. Clearly state the role of the piezoelectric transducer, time-to-digital converter, and the bandpass filter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AUV-mounted forward-looking sonar operates at 75 kHz in seawater ($c = 1500$ m/s, $\alpha = 1.5$ dB/km at 75 kHz) and uses a circular piston transducer of diameter $D_{tr} = 0.12$ m. Compute:
  (i) the wavelength and the half-power beam width,
  (ii) the maximum unambiguous range if the pulse repetition interval is $T_{PRI} = 0.5$ s,
  (iii) the range resolution if the transmitted pulse width is $\tau = 0.3$ ms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks)**

The major blocks of a URF and their roles are:

1. **Trigger Pulse Generator (1 mark):** Issues a precise logic-level pulse at the start of every measurement cycle; sets the timing reference for round-trip travel time.

2. **HV Pulser & TX/RX Switch (1 mark):** Steps the trigger pulse up to 100–500 V to drive the PZT ceramic; the duplexer isolates the sensitive receiver electronics during transmission.

3. **Piezoelectric Transducer (PZT) (1.5 marks):** On transmit, the high-voltage pulse causes the PZT ceramic to mechanically vibrate at its thickness-mode resonance, launching an acoustic wave. On receive, the same element converts the returning pressure wave into a small alternating voltage (reciprocity of piezoelectric effect). It is the heart of the system.

4. **Low-Noise Preamplifier (0.5 mark):** Boosts the microvolt-level echo signal (typically 60 dB gain) without adding significant self-noise.

5. **Bandpass Filter (1 mark):** Centre frequency locked to the transmit frequency with a Q of 5–10; rejects out-of-band ambient noise (cavitation, snapping shrimp, flow noise) and prevents aliasing of the receiver.

6. **Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) (1 mark):** Digitises the elapsed time between the trigger and the detected echo with sub-microsecond resolution; its latch instant is set by the threshold detector.

7. **Microcontroller / DSP (1 mark):** Computes the range $R = c\Delta t/2$, applies Time-Varied Gain (TVG = $40 \log R$) to compensate for spreading, and outputs the distance on a serial bus.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Wavelength and beam width (2.5 marks):**
$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1500}{75 \times 10^3} = 0.020 \text{ m} = 2.0 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
\theta_{3dB} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D_{tr}} = \frac{1.22 \times 0.020}{0.12} = 0.2033 \text{ rad} = 11.65^{\circ}
$$

**(ii) Maximum unambiguous range (2 marks):**
$$
R_{max} = \frac{c \, T_{PRI}}{2} = \frac{1500 \times 0.5}{2} = 375 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Range resolution (2.5 marks):**
$$
\delta R = \frac{c \, \tau}{2} = \frac{1500 \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.225 \text{ m} \approx 22.5 \text{ cm}
$$

**Final Answer Set:**
- (i) $\lambda = 2.0$ cm, $\theta_{3dB} = 0.203$ rad $\approx 11.6^{\circ}$
- (ii) $R_{max} = 375$ m
- (iii) $\delta R = 22.5$ cm

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**  **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive, from first principles, the two-way transmission loss equation $TL = 40 \log_{10} R + 2\alpha R$ for a monostatic underwater acoustic range finder. State clearly the physical origin of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the two-way transmission loss for a 200 kHz sonar operating at ranges of 10 m, 100 m, and 1 km. Take the absorption coefficient as $\alpha = 0.55$ dB/km at 200 kHz. Comment on which loss mechanism dominates at each range. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

Step 1 — For a point source radiating a spherical wave, the intensity falls as $1/R^2$ due to energy conservation over an expanding sphere of area $4\pi R^2$. In dB, the one-way geometric spreading loss is **(2 marks):**
$$
TL_{one-way,geom} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right)
$$
with $R_0 = 1$ m as the reference.

Step 2 — A monostatic range finder uses the same transducer to transmit and receive, so the wave traverses the path twice. Adding the two one-way losses gives **(2 marks):**
$$
TL_{geom} = 20 \log_{10} R + 20 \log_{10} R = 40 \log_{10} R
$$
This is the origin of the "40 log" — it is a direct consequence of the *square* of the geometric range appearing in the round-trip path.

Step 3 — In addition, sea water absorbs acoustic energy by viscous and chemical-relaxation processes. The absorption coefficient $\alpha$ (in dB/km) is approximately linear in range, so the round-trip absorption loss is **(2 marks):**
$$
TL_{abs} = 2 \alpha R
$$

Step 4 — Combining the two independent mechanisms:
$$
\boxed{TL = 40 \log_{10} R + 2 \alpha R \quad \text{[dB]}}
$$

Step 5 — *Comment (1 mark):* The first term dominates at short range (geometric spreading), the second at long range (visco-thermal absorption). The two terms are of equal magnitude at a "transition range" where $R_{tr} \approx 1/(\alpha \ln 10) \approx 0.43/\alpha$ km.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)**

| $R$ | $40 \log_{10} R$ (dB) | $2\alpha R$ (dB) | $TL$ total (dB) | Dominant term |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 m | $40 \times 1.000 = 40.00$ | $2 \times 0.55 \times 0.01 = 0.011$ | **40.01 dB** | Geometric (100 %) |
| 100 m | $40 \times 2.000 = 80.00$ | $2 \times 0.55 \times 0.10 = 0.11$ | **80.11 dB** | Geometric (> 99 %) |
| 1000 m | $40 \times 3.000 = 120.00$ | $2 \times 0.55 \times 1.00 = 1.10$ | **121.10 dB** | Geometric (~ 99 %) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the range equation. *Many students write $R = c \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.* This is the single most common mistake; the marker will deduct **1 full mark** for this alone.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $2\alpha R$.** When $R$ is in metres and $\alpha$ is given in dB/km, students forget to convert metres to kilometres. The result is a $10^{-3}$ error in the absorption term — full credit lost on the sub-part.
> 3. **Confusing the half-power beam width with the first-null beam width.** The KTU exam often asks for the *half-power* ($-3$ dB) beam width $\approx 1.22 \lambda/D$ for a *circular* piston, but students quote $0.886 \lambda/D$ (which is the *slit* form) or $2 \lambda/D$ (which is the first-null diameter).
> 4. **Not stating the validity range of the Mackenzie equation.** Always quote the operating envelope (e.g., 0–30 °C, 30–40 ppt, 0–8000 m) to earn full credit on the formula definition.
> 5. **Mixing the 20-log (one-way) with 40-log (two-way) formulas.** Use 20 log for a single hop, 40 log for round-trip — examiners are strict about this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The single most important equation** is the range equation $R = c \Delta t / 2$. Always divide by 2.
- **Speed of sound in seawater** is approximately **1500 m/s** (freshwater 1481 m/s). It increases with temperature, salinity, and depth.
- **Wavelength** is $\lambda = c/f$ — governs beam width and target resolution.
- **Beam width of a circular piston** is $\theta_{3dB} = 1.22 \lambda / D_{tr}$. Doubling the diameter halves the beam width.
- **Near-field (Fresnel) distance** $N = D_{tr}^2 / (4\lambda)$ — beyond $N$ the beam spreads spherically.
- **Two-way transmission loss** is $TL = 40 \log_{10} R + 2 \alpha R$ (dB). Geometric spreading always contributes 40 log; absorption adds $2 \alpha R$.
- **Maximum unambiguous range** is $R_{max} = c T_{PRI}/2$ — limited by the pulse-repetition interval.
- **Range resolution** is $\delta R = c \tau / 2$ — shorter pulse gives finer resolution but lower energy per pulse.
- **Higher frequency → narrower beam but higher absorption** → short range, high resolution.
- **Lower frequency → wider beam but lower absorption** → long range, lower resolution.
- **Active sonar equation** $SNR = SL - 2TL + TS - (NL - DI)$ must be positive for detection.
- **PZT** is the standard transducer material due to high piezoelectric coupling ($\sim 0.5$).
- **Quarter-wave matching layer** maximises energy transfer from PZT to water.
- **TVG (Time-Varied Gain = 40 log R)** in the receiver compensates exactly for the two-way geometric spreading loss.
- **Typical KTU numericals** combine two of the three parameters $(R, \Delta t, c)$ to find the third, then add a beam-width or transmission-loss sub-question.
- **Mackenzie and UNESCO equations** are interchangeable in exams; Mackenzie is preferred for hand calculation because the coefficients are integers/floats.
- **Ambient underwater noise sources** to remember for KTU: snapping shrimp, rain, shipping, thermal noise, and flow noise around the vehicle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Scanner, Infrared Range Finder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Scanner, Infrared Range Finder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Laser Scanner & Infrared Range Finder

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Laser Scanner (Underwater Laser Line Scanner / LLS)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
An *Underwater Laser Scanner* is an active optical distance-measuring and profiling sensor that emits a narrow, collimated beam of monochromatic coherent light (typically in the **blue-green spectrum of 450–550 nm**) toward a target and reconstructs the 2D/3D geometry of that target by measuring either the *Time-of-Flight (ToF)*, the *phase shift* of the returned signal, or by *laser triangulation*. It is widely deployed on **ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles)** and **AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles)** for high-resolution close-range mapping of subsea structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the underwater domain, laser scanners are *active optical sensors*, NOT passive ones. The "scanning" refers to mechanical/electro-optical deflection of a laser line or point across a 2D plane to obtain a *scan profile*.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of an underwater laser scanner like a supermarket checkout barcode scanner, but for 3D shapes. The laser sweeps a thin line across a ship hull, and the camera captures the deformation of that line. Where the line bends toward the sensor, the surface is closer; where it bends away, the surface is recessed. Software then converts thousands of these line sweeps into a dense point cloud — essentially a 3D model of the hull.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why blue-green (≈ 532 nm)?** Pure seawater exhibits minimum absorption in this spectral window. The absorption coefficient α can drop to **≈ 0.04 m⁻¹** at 480 nm, compared to **> 2.0 m⁻¹** for red light. This is why submarine laser scanners are *not* red like industrial terrestrial LiDAR.

---

### 1.2 Infrared Range Finder (IR Range Sensor)

**Formal Definition:**
An *Infrared Range Finder* is a non-contact distance measurement sensor that operates by emitting electromagnetic radiation in the **infrared band (typically 780 nm – 1 mm)** and detecting the reflected energy. Distance is inferred using one of three physical principles: (i) *triangulation*, (ii) *time-of-flight*, or (iii) *intensity modulation (active triangulation)*. The dominant low-cost implementation is the *Sharp GP2Y0A* family using *Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)* triangulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Water vs IR:** Pure infrared radiation (especially 780 nm – 3 µm) is **strongly absorbed by water**. Therefore, conventional IR rangefinders are *not* effective beyond a few centimeters underwater. They appear in underwater robotics primarily as **short-range obstacle/altitude sensors** (e.g., altitude-of-vehicle above seabed), or in **hybrid systems** where NIR is used only over very short optical paths.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine shouting into a well. The longer it takes to hear the echo, the deeper the well. An IR range finder is the optical equivalent of that echo, but instead of sound it uses invisible infrared light. The sensor's "eye" measures how the light bounces back and converts that into a distance reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core physical constants used throughout this module:**
> - Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - Refractive index of seawater (visible): **n ≈ 1.34**
> - Absorption coefficient of seawater (blue-green 480 nm): **α ≈ 0.04 m⁻¹**
> - Absorption coefficient of seawater (IR 980 nm): **α ≈ 5 m⁻¹**

---

## 2. Operational Principles — At a Glance

| Sub-system | Operating Principle | Typical Range (Underwater) | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pulsed Laser Scanner | Time-of-Flight (ToF) | 0.5 – 30 m | mm–cm |
| Phase-Shift Laser Scanner | Phase comparison | 0.1 – 100 m | sub-mm |
| Triangulation Laser | Geometric triangulation | 20 – 500 mm | µm |
| LED-based PSD IR | Active triangulation | 4 – 30 cm (air); < 5 cm in water | mm |
| TOF IR Rangefinder | Pulsed IR time-of-flight | 0.1 – 100 m (air) | cm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** When answering a question, *always* state (a) the operating principle, (b) the wavelength used, and (c) the limitation in water. This is the standard 3-point structure KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underwater Laser Scanner — Operational Architecture

A typical underwater laser line scanner (e.g., **2G Robotics ULS-100**, **3D at Depth Cobia-1670**) consists of the following physical chain:

1. **Laser Emitter** — A solid-state laser diode (often frequency-doubled Nd:YAG producing **532 nm green** or a blue laser at **450 nm**) projects a thin fan of light onto the target.
2. **Scanning Mechanism** — Either a rotating prism/mirror (mechanical) or a MEMS micromirror deflects the fan across one axis.
3. **Receiver Camera** — A high-speed CMOS/CCD camera, mounted at a fixed *baseline distance (b)* from the laser, captures the illuminated line.
4. **DSP / FPGA Processor** — Computes the 2D profile from the camera pixels using triangulation geometry.
5. **Host Interface** — Ethernet/RS-485 to the ROV surface unit.

### 2.2 Triangulation Geometry (Core Theory)

For each point on the laser line, the camera sees the laser spot at some pixel column x. The geometry gives:

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{x \cdot p}{f}\right)
$$

where:
- $f$ = focal length of the receiver lens
- $p$ = pixel pitch
- $x$ = pixel offset of the spot from the optical axis

The perpendicular distance $d$ from the camera to the illuminated point is then:

$$
d \;=\; \frac{b}{\tan(\theta)}
$$

where $b$ is the **baseline** (separation between laser and camera).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — Why triangulation is so accurate:** Triangulation does not depend on the *speed* of light. It depends on *geometric angles* which can be measured with sub-pixel accuracy by modern CMOS sensors. That is why short-range triangulation laser scanners achieve **micrometer-level** precision.

### 2.3 Time-of-Flight (ToF) Principle

The ToF laser scanner emits a short pulse and measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2 \cdot n}
$$

where:
- $c$ = speed of light in vacuum (≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s)
- $n$ = refractive index of the medium (1.34 for seawater)
- $R$ = one-way range

For phase-shift variants, the range is:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{c}{2 \cdot n} \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi f_{mod}}
$$

where $f_{mod}$ is the modulation frequency and $\Delta\phi$ is the measured phase difference.

### 2.4 Attenuation in Water (Beer–Lambert Law)

A laser beam traveling through water loses intensity exponentially. The received power $P_r$ is:

$$
P_r \;=\; P_t \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{A_r}{4\pi R^2} \cdot e^{-2\alpha R}
$$

The factor $e^{-2\alpha R}$ accounts for the **two-way path** (laser down + reflected back up). $\alpha$ is the spectral absorption coefficient (units: m⁻¹), and $\eta$ is the target reflectivity factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students frequently write $e^{-\alpha R}$ instead of $e^{-2\alpha R}$. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this. Always remember the round-trip.

### 2.5 Infrared Range Finder — Detailed Working

#### (a) PSD Triangulation IR Sensor (e.g., Sharp GP2Y0A21YK0F)
- An IR LED emits a focused beam.
- The reflected beam falls on a **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)**.
- Depending on where the spot lands on the PSD, the output voltage $V_{out}$ varies.
- The relationship between $V_{out}$ and distance is **non-linear** and is fit empirically.

A common inverse-distance fit is:

$$
V_{out} \;\approx\; \frac{K_1}{d + K_2} + K_3
$$

where $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are sensor-specific calibration constants.

#### (b) TOF IR Rangefinder
Same as the laser ToF formula above, but the wavelength falls in the near-IR (e.g., **905 nm**). The challenge in water is that at 905 nm, $\alpha$ ≈ 1 m⁻¹, so after just 1 m of one-way path, intensity is reduced to **~37%**, and after 2 m round-trip, only **~14%** remains.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Unit | Valid Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2n}$ | Time-of-Flight range | m | Vacuum / medium n |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{c}{2n} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta \phi}{2\pi f_{mod}}$ | Phase-shift range | m | $\Delta \phi < 2\pi$ |
| 3 | $d = \dfrac{b}{\tan\theta}$ | Triangulation range | m | $b \neq 0$ |
| 4 | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{xp}{f}\right)$ | Pixel-to-angle mapping | rad | Small-angle approx. |
| 5 | $P_r = P_t \cdot \eta \cdot \dfrac{A_r}{4\pi R^2} \cdot e^{-2\alpha R}$ | Power link budget | W | Single scatter |
| 6 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha R}$ | Beer–Lambert (1-way) | W/m² | Homogeneous medium |
| 7 | $\alpha(\lambda)$ | Spectral absorption coeff. | m⁻¹ | Function of $\lambda$ |
| 8 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2n \cdot BW}$ | Range resolution | m | $BW$ = detector bandwidth |
| 9 | $\sigma_R = \dfrac{\partial R}{\partial \theta} \cdot \sigma_\theta = \dfrac{-b}{\sin^2\theta} \cdot \sigma_\theta$ | Triangulation error | m | Error propagation |
| 10 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 n \cdot R_{max}}$ | Max unambiguous freq. | Hz | Phase-shift systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations together form the design kit for ANY subsea optical ranging sensor — whether used for pipeline inspection, hull cleaning verification, or AUV obstacle avoidance.

---

## 2.6 Why Blue-Green Lasers for Underwater Use?

The spectral attenuation of seawater is dominated by two effects:
- **Pure seawater absorption** (minimum near 418–500 nm)
- **Chlorophyll absorption** (peaks near 440 nm and 675 nm)

This produces a transmission window centered at **≈ 480 nm (blue)**. Therefore, modern underwater laser scanners preferentially use **blue (450 nm) or blue-green (532 nm)** lasers to maximize range.

> [!NOTE]
> Compare: A 905 nm IR laser loses half its intensity every **≈ 0.7 m** in clear seawater. A 480 nm blue laser loses half its intensity only every **≈ 17 m** — a 24× improvement.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Time-of-Flight Range Equation

**Statement:** A laser pulse is emitted at $t_0$, reflects off a target at distance $R$, and returns to the receiver at $t_0 + \Delta t$. Derive the expression for $R$ in a medium of refractive index $n$.

**Step 1 — Define the speed of light in the medium:**
The speed of light in any medium of refractive index $n$ is given by:
$$
v_{med} = \frac{c}{n}
$$
where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum. For seawater with $n = 1.34$:
$$
v_{med} = \frac{2.998 \times 10^{8}}{1.34} \approx 2.237 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the total path length:**
The light travels a one-way path $R$ down to the target, and the same path $R$ back. The total optical path is therefore $2R$.

**Step 3 — Apply the basic kinematic relation:**
Distance = velocity × time:
$$
2R = v_{med} \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**
$$
2R = \frac{c}{n} \cdot \Delta t
$$
$$
\boxed{R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2n}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (typical KTU problem):**
A 532 nm green laser pulse takes $\Delta t = 200$ ns round-trip. Compute the range:
$$
R = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (200 \times 10^{-9})}{2 \cdot 1.34}
$$
$$
R = \frac{59.96}{2.68} \approx 22.37 \text{ m}
$$

---

## Derivation 2: Beer–Lambert Two-Way Attenuation in Water

**Statement:** A laser of transmit power $P_t$ travels distance $R$ through seawater of absorption coefficient $\alpha$, reflects off a Lambertian target of albedo $\rho$, and returns. Derive $P_r$ at the receiver aperture of area $A_r$.

**Step 1 — Outgoing intensity (geometric loss and absorption):**
The outgoing beam spreads spherically; the irradiance at the target is:
$$
I_{target} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}
$$
After absorption through the water column, the irradiance on the target is:
$$
I_{target} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi R^2} \, e^{-\alpha R}
$$

**Step 2 — Reflected power from a Lambertian surface:**
The reflected power captured by the receiver is the irradiance times the target area $A_t$ times the Lambertian BRDF times the solid angle subtended by the receiver:
$$
P_{reflected} = I_{target} \cdot A_t \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\theta_t \cdot \frac{A_r}{R^2}
$$
(Detailed Lambertian derivation is beyond KTU scope; combined into reflectivity factor $\eta$):
$$
P_{reflected} = \eta \cdot P_t \cdot \frac{A_r}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for return-path absorption:**
The return beam experiences the same exponential loss:
$$
P_r = P_{reflected} \cdot e^{-\alpha R}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine both paths:**
$$
P_r = P_t \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{A_r}{4\pi R^2} \cdot e^{-2\alpha R}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the factor of 2 in the exponent** — this is the most common error. Always include it in the model answer.

---

## Derivation 3: Triangulation Range from Pixel Coordinate

**Statement:** A laser line scanner has baseline $b$ and a camera of focal length $f$ with pixel pitch $p$. The laser spot is observed at pixel offset $x$ from the optical axis. Derive $d$.

**Step 1 — Angle of the incoming ray in the camera frame:**
The angle $\theta$ between the optical axis and the incoming ray is, by small-angle geometry:
$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{xp}{f}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Triangulation relationship:**
The baseline, range, and angle form a right triangle:
$$
\tan\theta = \frac{b}{d}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for range:**
$$
\boxed{d = \frac{b}{\tan\theta} = \frac{b}{\tan\!\left[\arctan(xp/f)\right]} = \frac{b \cdot f}{x p}}
$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity (error propagation):**
Differentiating with respect to $x$:
$$
\frac{\partial d}{\partial x} = \frac{-b \cdot f}{x^2 p} = \frac{-d^2}{b}
$$
So a small pixel-measurement error $\sigma_x$ produces a range error:
$$
\sigma_d = \frac{d^2}{b} \cdot \sigma_x \cdot p
$$

**Insight:** Range error grows **quadratically** with distance. This is why triangulation is excellent for millimeters but unusable beyond ~1 m.

---

## Derivation 4: Phase-Shift Range Equation

**Statement:** An IR rangefinder modulates its carrier at $f_{mod}$ and measures the phase difference $\Delta \phi$ between emitted and received signals. Derive $R$.

**Step 1 — Phase accumulated over a round-trip distance $2R$ at wavelength in medium $\lambda_{med} = c/(n f_{mod})$:**
$$
\Delta \phi = \frac{2R}{\lambda_{med}} \cdot 2\pi
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R$:**
$$
\boxed{R = \frac{c}{2n} \cdot \frac{\Delta \phi}{2\pi f_{mod}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Unambiguous range:**
For $\Delta \phi$ to be unique, we need $R < \lambda_{med}/2$:
$$
R_{max} = \frac{c}{4n \cdot f_{mod}}
$$

**Numerical example:** With $f_{mod} = 5$ MHz and $n = 1.34$:
$$
R_{max} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4 \cdot 1.34 \cdot 5 \times 10^{6}} \approx 11.2 \text{ m}
$$

---

## 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Laser Scanner (Blue-Green) | IR Range Finder |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | 450–550 nm | 780 nm – 1 mm |
| $\alpha$ in seawater | 0.02–0.1 m⁻¹ | 1–1000 m⁻¹ |
| Effective underwater range | 0.5 – 30 m | < 0.1 m (typically unusable) |
| Range accuracy | mm to cm | cm |
| Cost (industrial grade) | $5K – $80K | $2 – $200 |
| Sunlight interference | Low (narrow bandpass filter) | High (ambient IR) |
| Best underwater use | Hull/pipeline mapping | Short-range altitude, lab tanks |
| Power consumption | 1 – 10 W | 0.05 – 0.5 W |
| Data rate | 10K – 1M points/sec | 10 – 100 samples/sec |
| Susceptibility to biofouling | High (laser window) | Moderate |

## 📝 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Why are blue-green lasers (≈ 450–550 nm) preferred over red or infrared lasers for underwater ranging applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Seawater exhibits minimum spectral absorption in the blue-green window (450–550 nm), where the absorption coefficient α is as low as **0.04 m⁻¹**. In contrast, infrared (≈ 905 nm) has α ≈ 1 m⁻¹, and red (≈ 660 nm) has α ≈ 0.3 m⁻¹. Therefore, blue-green light propagates with **~25× less attenuation** per meter than IR, allowing ranges of tens of meters instead of centimeters. (1 mark for naming the window, 1 mark for the α value, 1 mark for comparison conclusion.)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Time-of-Flight (ToF) and Triangulation principles used in laser scanners.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Time-of-Flight | Triangulation |
|---|---|---|
| Range | 0.5 – 100 m | 20 – 500 mm |
| Principle | Measures pulse travel time | Measures angle of reflected ray |
| Accuracy | cm-level | µm-level |
| Limitation | Need fast electronics (ps resolution) | Range error grows as d² |

(1 mark for principle, 1 mark for range/scale, 1 mark for accuracy or limitation.)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of an underwater laser line scanner. Discuss its operational wavelength and the principle used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **laser line scanner** is an active optical sensor that produces a 2D cross-sectional profile of an underwater target for 3D reconstruction.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Laser Source → Scanning Optic → Water → Target
                                          ↓
                  DSP ← Camera ← Reflected Light
                          ↓
                   Point Cloud Output
```

**Working (Step-by-step):**
1. A solid-state blue-green laser (typically **532 nm**) emits a thin, collimated fan of light. (1 mark)
2. A rotating prism or MEMS mirror sweeps this fan across one axis at a known angular velocity. (1 mark)
3. The receiver CMOS camera, separated by a fixed baseline $b$, captures the deformed laser line on the target. (1 mark)
4. For each pixel column $x$ of the captured image, the angle $\theta = \arctan(xp/f)$ is computed. (1 mark)
5. The range $d = b/\tan\theta$ is computed for every pixel. (1 mark)
6. The 2D profile is built row-by-row as the scanner moves, producing a 3D point cloud. (1 mark)
7. The choice of **blue-green wavelength** is dictated by the minimum absorption coefficient of seawater in the 450–550 nm spectral window. (1 mark)

---

**(b)** An underwater laser scanner operates at 532 nm in seawater (n = 1.34). The receiver electronics have a timing resolution of 0.5 ns. Calculate: (i) the theoretical range resolution, and (ii) the maximum range if the round-trip time is 250 ns. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Range Resolution (3 marks):**
The range resolution is governed by the timing resolution $\Delta t = 0.5$ ns:
$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2n} = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-9})}{2 \cdot 1.34}
$$
$$
\Delta R = \frac{0.1499}{2.68} \approx 0.0559 \text{ m} \approx 5.6 \text{ cm}
$$
**Range resolution ≈ 5.6 cm** (1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer)

**(ii) Maximum Range (4 marks):**
Given $\Delta t = 250$ ns:
$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2n} = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (250 \times 10^{-9})}{2 \cdot 1.34}
$$
$$
R = \frac{74.95}{2.68} \approx 27.97 \text{ m}
$$
**Maximum range ≈ 28 m** (1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer, 1 mark unit)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the working of an Infrared (IR) range finder with a position-sensitive detector (PSD). Why is its underwater use limited? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
1. An IR LED (typically **850 nm** or **940 nm**) emits a focused infrared beam toward the target. (1 mark)
2. The reflected light falls on a **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)**, which is a photodiode with multiple electrodes that output currents proportional to the position of the light spot. (1 mark)
3. The centroid of the spot is determined by the current ratios from opposing electrodes. (1 mark)
4. As the target moves closer or farther, the reflected spot on the PSD shifts, producing a varying analog voltage $V_{out}$. (1 mark)
5. The output voltage is non-linearly related to distance, and is fit with an empirical inverse function to obtain range. (1 mark)

**Why Underwater Use is Limited (2 marks):**
- Water strongly absorbs IR — at 905 nm, $\alpha \approx 1$ m⁻¹, so range reduces to **< 10 cm** in practice. (1 mark)
- Suspended particles (suspended sediments) cause severe backscatter in the near-IR, swamping the weak return signal. (1 mark)

---

**(b)** A 905 nm IR ToF rangefinder is used in air (n = 1.00) and is now immersed in clear seawater (n = 1.34, α = 1 m⁻¹ at 905 nm). If the transmitted power is 1 W, target reflectivity η × A_r = 0.01 m², and the design requires received power ≥ 10 nW, find the maximum allowable range. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the link equation (2 marks):**
$$
P_r = P_t \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{A_r}{4\pi R^2} \cdot e^{-2\alpha R}
$$
Let $K = \eta \cdot A_r = 0.01$ m². Then:
$$
P_r = 1 \cdot 0.01 \cdot \frac{1}{4\pi R^2} \cdot e^{-2 \cdot 1 \cdot R} = \frac{0.01 \cdot e^{-2R}}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the threshold condition (1 mark):**
$$
P_r \geq 10 \text{ nW} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W}
$$
$$
\frac{0.01 \cdot e^{-2R}}{4\pi R^2} \geq 10^{-8}
$$
$$
\frac{e^{-2R}}{R^2} \geq 4\pi \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.2566 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve iteratively (3 marks):**
- Try $R = 0.3$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-0.6}}{0.09} = \dfrac{0.5488}{0.09} \approx 6.10$ → too high
- Try $R = 0.5$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-1.0}}{0.25} = \dfrac{0.3679}{0.25} \approx 1.47$ → still too high
- Try $R = 0.7$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-1.4}}{0.49} = \dfrac{0.2466}{0.49} \approx 0.503$ → too high
- Try $R = 0.9$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-1.8}}{0.81} = \dfrac{0.1653}{0.81} \approx 0.204$ → too high
- Try $R = 1.0$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-2.0}}{1.00} = \dfrac{0.1353}{1.00} \approx 0.135$ → too high
- Try $R = 1.2$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-2.4}}{1.44} = \dfrac{0.0907}{1.44} \approx 0.063$ → too high
- Try $R = 1.5$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-3.0}}{2.25} = \dfrac{0.0498}{2.25} \approx 0.022$ → too high
- Try $R = 1.7$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-3.4}}{2.89} = \dfrac{0.0334}{2.89} \approx 0.0115$ → ≈ 1.15 × 10⁻² (still > 1.2566 × 10⁻⁵ ✓)
- Try $R = 2.0$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-4.0}}{4.00} = \dfrac{0.0183}{4.00} \approx 0.00458$ → still > 1.2566 × 10⁻⁵ ✓
- Try $R = 3.0$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-6.0}}{9.00} = \dfrac{0.00248}{9.00} \approx 2.75 \times 10^{-4}$ → still > 1.2566 × 10⁻⁵ ✓
- Try $R = 4.0$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-8.0}}{16} = \dfrac{3.35 \times 10^{-4}}{16} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-5}$ → ≈ 2.09 × 10⁻⁵ (still > 1.2566 × 10⁻⁵ ✓)
- Try $R = 4.3$ m: $\dfrac{e^{-8.6}}{18.49} \approx \dfrac{1.82 \times 10^{-4}}{18.49} \approx 9.84 \times 10^{-6}$ → below threshold ✗

**Final Answer (1 mark):**
$$
\boxed{R_{max} \approx 4.2 \text{ m}}
$$

(Note: Even in *clear* seawater, the IR rangefinder's range is limited to ~4 m — illustrating the strong limitation KTU expects students to identify.)

> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the Beer–Lambert equation** (deduct 1 mark).
> 2. **Using $c$ instead of $c/n$** in the ToF equation when the medium is water (deduct 1 mark).
> 3. **Stating the IR range finder works "the same as in air"** without discussing the absorption coefficient — KTU expects at least one quantitative mention of α (deduct 2 marks).
> 4. **Confusing "scanning" with "imaging"** — a laser scanner is a *ranging* sensor producing a *point cloud*, NOT a camera (deduct 1 mark).
> 5. **In Part B numericals, omitting units** in intermediate steps (deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit).

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Underwater laser scanners operate in the **blue-green window (450–550 nm)** because seawater absorption is minimum here.
- ✅ **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** is used for long ranges (m to 100 m); **Triangulation** is used for short ranges (mm to ~1 m) with µm accuracy.
- ✅ **ToF range equation:** $R = c \cdot \Delta t / (2n)$ — remember the **factor of 2** and the **refractive index n**.
- ✅ **Beer–Lambert with round-trip:** $P_r = P_t \cdot \eta \cdot \dfrac{A_r}{4\pi R^2} \cdot e^{-2\alpha R}$ — exponent has **2αR**, not αR.
- ✅ **Phase-shift range:** $R = \dfrac{c}{2n} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi f_{mod}}$; max unambiguous range is $c/(4n f_{mod})$.
- ✅ **IR range finders use ~850–940 nm**, which is **strongly absorbed** by water (α ≈ 1 m⁻¹) — practical underwater range is < 5 cm in most cases.
- ✅ **PSD triangulation IR sensors** give non-linear output fitted with inverse-distance models: $V_{out} \approx K_1/(d+K_2) + K_3$.
- ✅ **Triangulation error grows as d²** — quadratic with range, hence the technique is short-range.
- ✅ **Typical underwater laser scanner specs:** 532 nm, n = 1.34, c/n ≈ 2.24 × 10⁸ m/s, 10K–1M points/sec.
- ✅ **Applications:** pipeline inspection, hull survey, archaeological mapping, AUV obstacle avoidance.
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** using $c$ (vacuum) in underwater calculations — always divide by n.
- ✅ **Range resolution of ToF:** $\Delta R = c / (2n \cdot BW)$ where BW is the receiver bandwidth.
- ✅ **IR vs Laser in water:** IR fails at depth; blue-green laser is the only practical optical choice beyond ~10 cm underwater.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual and Motion Sensing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Visual and Motion Sensing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Visual Sensing Systems in Underwater Robotics

### Formal KTU Definition
**Visual Sensing Systems** in underwater robotics refer to the integrated suite of electro-optical and acoustic imaging transducers that enable an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) or Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) to perceive its surrounding aqueous environment by converting reflected electromagnetic or acoustic energy into interpretable digital data streams (images, point clouds, or range maps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Visual sensing in underwater robotics is **not limited to optical cameras**. Due to the rapid attenuation of visible light in water (typically absorption length of **< 10 m** in coastal waters and **< 60 m** in clear ocean), the syllabus broadly classifies visual systems into **Optical (camera-based)** and **Acoustic (sonar-based)** modalities.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are wearing a scuba mask inside a dark, murky swimming pool at night. You switch on a flashlight — but the beam only reaches about 2–3 meters before scattering away. To "see" further, you shout loudly and listen for the echo bouncing back from the walls. The shout (sound wave) travels much farther than the flashlight beam. In the same way, underwater robots use:
- **Cameras** for short-range, high-resolution "sight" (like your flashlight)
- **Sonars** for long-range, low-resolution "echo-perception" (like your shouting)

### Sub-Categories of Visual Sensing

| Modality | Physical Carrier | Effective Range | Best Use Case |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| Monocular CCD/CMOS Camera | Visible light photons | **0.5 m – 10 m** | Pipeline inspection, coral survey |
| Stereo Camera Pair | Visible light (binocular disparity) | **1 m – 8 m** | 3D reconstruction |
| Side-Scan Sonar (SSS) | Acoustic pulses (100–900 kHz) | **50 m – 500 m** | Seafloor mapping |
| Forward-Looking Sonar (FLS) | Acoustic beams | **10 m – 200 m** | Obstacle avoidance |
| Multibeam Echo Sounder (MBES) | Fan-shaped acoustic array | **50 m – 11,000 m** | Bathymetric charting |
| Structured Light / Laser Line | Projected coherent light | **0.2 m – 2 m** | Close-range 3D scanning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The speed of sound in seawater at **20°C and 35 ppt salinity** is approximately **c ≈ 1500 m/s** — this is the fundamental constant that governs every acoustic sensing equation in this module.

---

## 1.2 Motion Sensing Systems in Underwater Robotics

### Formal KTU Definition
**Motion Sensing Systems** (also termed **Navigation Sensing** or **Inertial Sensing** systems) comprise the cluster of proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors that allow an underwater vehicle to measure its own **position**, **velocity**, **orientation (pose)**, and **acceleration** without relying on GPS (which is inoperative underwater due to severe attenuation of L-band signals).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of driving a car with your eyes closed in fog. You cannot use GPS, you cannot see the road, but you still have:
- A **speedometer** (tells you how fast you are moving)
- A **compass** (tells you which direction you are facing)
- An **accelerometer** (tells you when you speed up or brake)
- A **gyroscope** (tells you how much the car is turning/tilting)

Underwater robots are exactly like this car in fog. They fuse multiple "blind" sensors to estimate where they are going. The **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** is the core of this suite, typically combined with a **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** and a **depth/pressure sensor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** 
> - **Proprioceptive sensors** measure *internal* state (IMU, gyroscope, accelerometer) — they DO NOT need external reference.
> - **Exteroceptive sensors** measure *external* state (DVL using seabed echo, USBL using surface ship reference) — they REQUIRE an external reference.

### Sub-Categories of Motion Sensing

| Sensor | Physical Principle | Measured Quantity | Drift Behavior |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Accelerometer** | Mass-displacement in MEMS proof mass | Linear acceleration (m/s²) | Small drift |
| **Gyroscope** | Coriolis force on vibrating structure | Angular rate (rad/s) | **Large drift** |
| **Magnetometer** | Hall effect / fluxgate | Heading (°) | Local magnetic anomaly errors |
| **Pressure Sensor** | Piezoresistive strain gauge | Depth / pressure (dBar) | Excellent (cm-level) |
| **DVL (Doppler Velocity Log)** | Doppler shift of acoustic echoes | 3-axis velocity (m/s) | Bias drift ~0.1 cm/s |
| **USBL / LBL** | Acoustic baseline ranging | Absolute position (m) | Requires external transponders |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Optical Camera Systems — Working Principle

A digital underwater camera operates on the **photoelectric effect**. Photons entering through a water-tight hemispherical (often acrylic) port strike an array of either:
- **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)** sensors — analog charge transferred serially
- **CMOS (Active Pixel Sensor)** — each pixel has its own amplifier

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Photon Capture:** Ambient/artificial light passes through the **water-corrected port** (corrected for refractive index n = 1.33 of seawater vs 1.00 of air). Without correction, images appear blurry due to refraction at the planar port.
2. **Wavelength Filtering:** Most underwater cameras use **blue–green** bandpass filters (λ ≈ 450–550 nm) because seawater transmits blue-green light most efficiently.
3. **Exposure:** Sensor integration time τ is set to balance **motion blur** (favors short τ) and **photon starvation** (favors long τ).
4. **A/D Conversion:** Pixel charge is quantized to **8-bit** or **12-bit** depth.
5. **Image Pipeline:** White balance, gamma correction, dehazing algorithms applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Beer-Lambert Law for Underwater Light Attenuation:**
> The intensity of light at distance d from source is governed by:
> **I(d) = I₀ · e^(−c·d)** where **c** is the **beam attenuation coefficient** (m⁻¹). Typical values: clear ocean c ≈ 0.05, coastal water c ≈ 0.3, turbid harbor c ≈ 2.0.

---

## 2.2 Sonar (Acoustic) Systems — Working Principle

Sonar is the **dominant perception modality** underwater because sound travels **~4.5× faster** in water than in air and attenuates far less than light.

### Generic Sonar Operational Logic

1. **Transmission:** A projector (piezoelectric transducer) emits a short acoustic pulse at center frequency **f_c** (typically 10 kHz – 1 MHz).
2. **Propagation:** Pulse travels through water at speed **c ≈ 1500 m/s**.
3. **Reflection/Scattering:** Pulse interacts with target (seabed, fish, obstacle). Backscatter strength is described by **Target Strength (TS)** in dB.
4. **Reception:** Hydrophones receive the weak returning echo.
5. **Beamforming:** Array of hydrophones applies time-delays to form a directional beam (analogous to phased-array radar).
6. **Time-of-Flight (ToF) Ranging:** Round-trip travel time τ gives range: **R = c·τ / 2**.

### Beamwidth Resolution
The angular resolution of a sonar is governed by the **aperture-to-wavelength ratio**:

$$\theta_{-3\text{dB}} \approx \frac{\lambda}{D} = \frac{c}{f_c \cdot D}$$

where:
- $\theta_{-3\text{dB}}$ = half-power beamwidth in **radians**
- $\lambda$ = acoustic wavelength = **c / f_c**
- D = physical aperture (transducer diameter) in **meters**

---

## 2.3 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) — Working Principle

An IMU is a 6-DOF (Degrees of Freedom) sensor package that combines:
- **3-axis accelerometer** (measures specific force)
- **3-axis gyroscope** (measures angular rate)

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Accelerometer Readout:** A proof mass suspended by springs deflects when the vehicle accelerates. The deflection is proportional to the **specific force** f = a − g.
2. **Gyroscope Readout (MEMS Vibratory):** A vibrating structure experiences **Coriolis acceleration** proportional to the rotation rate orthogonal to its vibration plane.
3. **Bias and Noise:** Each sensor has a **bias** (offset error) and **Angle Random Walk (ARW)** noise.
4. **Integration to Pose:** 
   - Pitch/Roll from accelerometer using: $\phi = \arctan\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right)$, $\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{-a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)$
   - Heading from gyroscope integration: $\psi(t) = \psi_0 + \int_0^t \omega_z \, dt$
5. **Sensor Fusion:** Raw IMU data is fused with DVL and pressure in an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF)** to bound drift.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Pure inertial navigation **always drifts** because gyroscope bias integrates into angle error, which integrates into position error (double integration). Hence, IMU is **never used alone** in underwater robotics — it is always aided by DVL or USBL.

---

## 2.4 Doppler Velocity Log (DVL) — Working Principle

DVL is a 4-beam (Janus configuration) acoustic sensor that measures the vehicle's velocity relative to the seabed.

### Operational Logic
1. **Transmission:** 4 beams at 30° from vertical, in a **Janus** (Greek god of two faces) cross configuration — 2 forward-pointing, 2 aft-pointing.
2. **Doppler Shift:** Returning echoes from the seabed are frequency-shifted by the relative motion:
   $$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_t \cdot \cos\alpha}{c}$$
   where α is the beam angle from vertical.
3. **Beam Pair Averaging:** The Janus configuration cancels out the **heading-dependent** component of velocity — only the **along-track and across-track** components remain.
4. **Bottom-Lock:** DVL works in "bottom-lock" when the seabed echo is strong (typically < 200 m altitude).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | Acoustic range from round-trip time | **m** | All active sonars |
| 2 | $I(d) = I_0 \cdot e^{-c \cdot d}$ | Beer-Lambert underwater attenuation | W/m² | Optical link budget |
| 3 | $\theta_{-3\text{dB}} \approx \frac{\lambda}{D}$ | Half-power beamwidth | **rad** | Sonar array design |
| 4 | $f_d = \frac{2v f_t \cos\alpha}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift | **Hz** | DVL velocity |
| 5 | $f_a = \frac{2v f_t}{c}$ (acoustic Doppler) | Simplified 2-way Doppler | **Hz** | ADCP, DVL |
| 6 | $SNR_{rx} = SL - 2TL + TS - (NL - DI)$ | **Sonar Equation** (active) | **dB** | Sonar performance prediction |
| 7 | $TL = 20 \log R + \alpha R$ | Transmission loss (absorption + spreading) | **dB** | Range prediction |
| 8 | $v_x(t) = v_{x0} + \int_0^t a_x \, dt$ | Velocity from IMU | **m/s** | Dead reckoning |
| 9 | $p(t) = p_0 + \int_0^t v(t) \, dt$ | Position from velocity | **m** | Trajectory integration |
| 10 | $\phi = \arctan(a_y / a_z)$ | Roll from accelerometer | **rad** | IMU tilt estimation |
| 11 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure → depth | **Pa / m** | Depth sensor |
| 12 | $\Delta t = \frac{2 R \sin(\theta/2)}{c}$ | Synthetic Aperture Sonar along-track resolution | **s** | SAS imaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Acoustic Symbol Legend (KTU standard):**
> - **SL** = Source Level, **TL** = Transmission Loss, **TS** = Target Strength, **NL** = Noise Level, **DI** = Directivity Index, **SNR** = Signal-to-Noise Ratio.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Industry:** AUVs equipped with **MBES + SSS + DVL + IMU** inspect subsea pipelines and map the seafloor at depths up to 4000 m.
- **Defense/Naval:** Mine-countermeasure (MCM) AUVs use **FLS + Side-scan + DVL** to detect and classify moored/sea-floor mines.
- **Marine Biology:** Stereo cameras + structured light quantify 3D coral reef growth over time.
- **Arctic Research:** Underwater ice profiling AUVs combine **multibeam sonar + sub-bottom profiler** to measure ice-keel drafts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Active Sonar Range Equation (Worked End-to-End)

The **Sonar Equation** balances the transmitted acoustic energy against the receiving system's ability to detect a target echo. It is the **single most important equation** in underwater acoustic sensing.

### Statement of the Problem
A 200 kHz echo sounder is mounted on an AUV operating at 100 m depth in seawater (c = 1500 m/s, α = 0.04 dB/m at this frequency). Given:
- Source Level: **SL = 210 dB re 1 µPa @ 1 m**
- Target Strength of the seabed: **TS = −15 dB**
- Noise Level: **NL = 60 dB re 1 µPa/√Hz**
- Receiver Bandwidth: **BW = 1 kHz**
- Detection Threshold: **DT = 10 dB** (minimum SNR for detection)
- Directivity Index: **DI = 20 dB**

Find the **maximum detection range R_max**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the Active Sonar Equation (detection condition).**

The received signal-to-noise ratio in dB is:

$$SNR_{rx} = SL - 2 \cdot TL(R) + TS - (NL - DI) - DT$$

The factor **2·TL** appears because the acoustic wave undergoes **two-way propagation** (down to target, back up to receiver).

**Step 2 — Substitute the standard Transmission Loss model.**

The two principal loss terms are **spherical spreading** (20·log₁₀R) and **absorption** (α·R):

$$TL(R) = 20 \log_{10}(R) + \alpha R$$

with R in meters and α in dB/m.

**Step 3 — Set SNR_rx = 0 dB to find the maximum range (detection threshold condition).**

$$0 = SL - 2\left[20 \log_{10}(R_{max}) + \alpha \cdot R_{max}\right] + TS - (NL - DI) - DT$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the unknown.**

$$2\left[20 \log_{10}(R_{max}) + \alpha \cdot R_{max}\right] = SL + TS - (NL - DI) - DT$$

$$40 \log_{10}(R_{max}) + 2\alpha \cdot R_{max} = SL + TS - NL + DI - DT$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values (all in dB).**

- RHS = 210 + (−15) − 60 + 20 − 10
- RHS = 210 − 15 − 60 + 20 − 10
- RHS = 145 dB

So we must solve:

$$40 \log_{10}(R_{max}) + 2(0.04) R_{max} = 145$$

$$40 \log_{10}(R_{max}) + 0.08 \cdot R_{max} = 145$$

**Step 6 — Solve numerically using iteration.**

Let R₀ = 2000 m:

- LHS = 40·log₁₀(2000) + 0.08·2000 = 40·(3.301) + 160 = 132.04 + 160 = **292** → too high

Let R₁ = 500 m:

- LHS = 40·log₁₀(500) + 0.08·500 = 40·(2.699) + 40 = 107.96 + 40 = **147.96** → close, slightly high

Let R₂ = 480 m:

- LHS = 40·log₁₀(480) + 0.08·480 = 40·(2.6812) + 38.4 = 107.25 + 38.4 = **145.65** → very close

Let R₃ = 475 m:

- LHS = 40·log₁₀(475) + 0.08·475 = 40·(2.6767) + 38 = 107.07 + 38 = **145.07** → converged

**Step 7 — Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{R_{max} \approx 475 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the absorption term (0.08·R = 38 dB) is non-negligible compared to the spreading term (107 dB) at this range. At very high frequencies (>1 MHz), the absorption term dominates and limits range to a few meters.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of DVL Velocity from Doppler Shift

### Step 1 — Acoustic Wave on a Moving Platform

When the DVL transmits a wave at frequency f_t toward the seabed, the seabed "sees" a different frequency due to relative motion. Using the classical Doppler formula for a moving source / stationary reflector / moving receiver geometry:

$$f_r = f_t \cdot \frac{c + v_r}{c - v_s} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \quad \text{(simplified for } v \ll c\text{)}$$

For the case of a moving transmitter-receiver (same platform) and stationary seabed:

$$f_r = f_t \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\alpha}{c - v \cos\alpha}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Approximation for $v \ll c$

Using first-order Taylor expansion:

$$f_r \approx f_t \left(1 + \frac{2 v \cos\alpha}{c}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Define the Doppler Shift

$$\Delta f = f_r - f_t = \frac{2 f_t v \cos\alpha}{c}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Vehicle Velocity

$$v = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_t \cos\alpha}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

A DVL transmits at f_t = 1.2 MHz with beam angle α = 30° (cos 30° = 0.866). The measured beat frequency Δf is 1600 Hz. Seawater c = 1500 m/s.

$$v = \frac{1500 \times 1600}{2 \times 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 0.866}$$

$$v = \frac{2{,}400{,}000}{2{,}078{,}400} \approx 1.155 \text{ m/s}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Underwater Image Formation (Refraction at Port)

When a camera is placed behind a flat acrylic port, light bends according to **Snell's Law**. This shifts the apparent position of objects and degrades geometric reconstruction.

### Snell's Law
$$n_{water} \sin\theta_1 = n_{acrylic} \sin\theta_2 = n_{air} \sin\theta_3$$

with n_water = 1.33, n_acrylic ≈ 1.49, n_air = 1.00.

### Apparent Depth Formula
For a small-angle (paraxial) approximation, an object at true distance d_actual appears at apparent distance:

$$d_{apparent} = \frac{n_{air}}{n_{water}} \cdot d_{actual} = \frac{1.00}{1.33} \cdot d_{actual} \approx 0.75 \cdot d_{actual}$$

This **25% magnification** error must be corrected in any underwater stereo-vision 3D measurement system.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensing Modalities

| Parameter | Optical Camera | Side-Scan Sonar | Multibeam Echo Sounder | DVL | IMU |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|------------------------|-----|-----|
| Range | **0.5–10 m** | **50–500 m** | **50–11000 m** | **0.5–200 m altitude** | N/A (proprioceptive) |
| Resolution | mm–cm (high) | cm-scale | m-scale | cm/s velocity | 0.001–0.01°/s drift |
| Update Rate | 30–60 Hz | 1–10 Hz | 0.1–5 Hz | 1–25 Hz | 100–1000 Hz |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | High | Medium |
| Water Dependency | Requires clear water | Works in turbid | Works in turbid | Needs bottom lock | Independent |
| Failure Mode | Backscatter, color loss | Multipath, cavitation | Surface returns | Altitude limit | Bias drift |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Underwater Visual Sensing System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Underwater Scene] --> B[Acoustic Energy Pulse]
    A --> C[Visible Light Photons]
    B --> D1[Multibeam Echo Sounder]
    B --> D2[Side Scan Sonar]
    B --> D3[Forward Looking Sonar]
    C --> D4[Stereo CCD Camera Pair]
    C --> D5[Structured Light Laser]
    D1 --> E1[Beamforming Processor]
    D2 --> E2[Time Varying Gain Amplifier]
    D3 --> E3[Real Time Obstacle Map]
    D4 --> E4[Stereo Matching Engine]
    D5 --> E5[Triangulation Module]
    E1 --> F1[Digital Elevation Model]
    E2 --> F2[Seafloor Mosaic Image]
    E3 --> F3[Obstacle Avoidance Vector]
    E4 --> F4[Dense 3D Point Cloud]
    E5 --> F5[High Resolution 3D Mesh]
    F1 --> G[Vehicle Navigation Stack]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The left-most nodes (A, B, C) represent the **physical phenomena** in the underwater scene. The middle row (D1...D5) represents the **sensor transducers**. The right-most nodes (E1...E5, F1...F5) represent the **signal processing** and **data products** delivered to the higher-level navigation stack.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Underwater Motion Sensing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[MEMS Accelerometer 3 axis] --> N1[Accelerometer Signal Conditioner]
    M2[MEMS Gyroscope 3 axis] --> N2[Gyroscope Signal Conditioner]
    M3[Magnetometer 3 axis] --> N3[Magnetic Heading Module]
    M4[Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor] --> N4[Depth Calculator Module]
    M5[DVL Janus 4 beam array] --> N5[Doppler Frequency Estimator]
    M6[USBL Surface Ship Beacon] --> N6[Acoustic Range Trilateration]
    N1 --> O1[Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
    N2 --> O1
    N3 --> O1
    N4 --> O1
    N5 --> O1
    N6 --> O1
    O1 --> P1[Vehicle State Vector 6 DOF]
    O1 --> P2[Navigation Solution Pose]
    O1 --> P3[Velocity Estimate Body Frame]
    O1 --> P4[Drift Bounded Position]
    P1 --> Q[Autonomy Stack Mission Planner]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    P4 --> Q
```

---

## 4.3 Sensor Fusion Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[IMU 200 Hz High Rate] --> F1[Prediction Step EKF]
    S2[DVL 5 Hz Velocity Update] --> F2[Measurement Update Step]
    S3[Depth Sensor 10 Hz] --> F2
    S4[USBL LBL 1 Hz] --> F2
    S5[Magnetometer 50 Hz] --> F2
    F1 --> EST[Estimated Vehicle State]
    F2 --> EST
    EST --> FB1[Feedback to IMU Bias Estimator]
    EST --> FB2[Output to Controller]
    FB1 --> S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Note:** The IMU runs at **200 Hz** (high rate, but drifts), while absolute aiding sensors (DVL, depth, USBL) run at **1–10 Hz** (low rate, but bounded). The EKF **predicts** the state at IMU rate and **corrects** it at aiding-sensor rate — this is the standard **loosely-coupled** fusion architecture used in real AUVs.

---

## 4.4 Acoustic Beam Geometry (Side-Scan Sonar)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Towfish Vehicle] --> B1[Port Side Acoustic Beam]
    A --> B2[Starboard Side Acoustic Beam]
    B1 --> C1[Acoustic Footprint on Seabed]
    B2 --> C2[Acoustic Footprint on Seabed]
    C1 --> D1[Backscatter Intensity Return]
    C2 --> D2[Backscatter Intensity Return]
    D1 --> E[Side Scan Sonar Image Strip]
    D2 --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short Answer [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]
**Q: Define the term "Target Strength" in the context of underwater acoustic sensing. State its typical units.**

**Model Answer:**
Target Strength (TS) is a logarithmic measure of the backscattering ability of a target (seabed, fish, obstacle) when ensonified by an acoustic wave. It is defined as the ratio (in dB) of the acoustic intensity reflected back toward the source, referenced to the intensity that would be returned by a perfect spherical reflector of 1 m² cross-section located at the same range.

$$TS = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{scattered}}{I_{incident}}\right) \text{ at } R = 1 \text{ m}$$

**Typical units: dB re 1 m²** (dimensionless when expressed in dB).

A hard rock seabed has TS ≈ −5 dB, while a soft mud seabed has TS ≈ −20 dB.

---

### Question 2: Short Answer [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]
**Q: Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive motion sensors. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| Definition | Sensors that measure the **internal state** of the vehicle | Sensors that measure the **external state** by referencing an outside body |
| External Reference | **Not required** | **Required** (seabed, surface ship, beacon) |
| Drift Behavior | **Drift over time** (integration errors) | **Bounded** (no integration) |
| Example | **IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope)** | **DVL (Doppler Velocity Log) using seabed echo** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Module 3 Choice Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the vehicle's velocity in terms of the measured Doppler shift.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (Block-by-Block Explanation):**
1. **Transducer Array:** A Janus-configuration array of 4 piezoelectric transducers is mounted on the vehicle bottom, oriented at 30° from vertical, with 2 beams pointing forward and 2 pointing aft at 90° azimuthal separation.
2. **Transmitter:** A digital signal generator excites each beam with a short CW or coded pulse at center frequency f_t (typically 300 kHz – 1.2 MHz).
3. **Receiver:** The same transducer (acting as a hydrophone) listens for the backscattered echo from the seabed.
4. **Doppler Processing:** The received frequency f_r is mixed with the transmitted f_t to extract the beat frequency Δf = f_r − f_t.
5. **Beam-Pair Averaging (Janus Trick):** The forward and aft beam Doppler shifts are averaged. This cancels out any heading-dependent bias and yields the true **along-track and across-track** velocity components.
6. **Output:** Three orthogonal velocity components (Vx, Vy, Vz) in the body frame.

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.2 above):**

Starting from the two-way Doppler formula:

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_t v \cos\alpha}{c}$$

Solving for velocity:

$$\boxed{v = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_t \cos\alpha}}$$

**Limitation:** DVL fails in "water-track" mode when altitude > ~200 m because the seabed echo becomes too weak; it then drifts.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of the **active sonar equation**, derive the maximum detection range of a side-scan sonar operating at 500 kHz with the following data:

- SL = 215 dB re 1 µPa @ 1 m
- TS (target) = −10 dB
- NL = 55 dB re 1 µPa/√Hz, BW = 500 Hz
- DI = 25 dB, DT = 12 dB
- Absorption coefficient α = 0.08 dB/m at 500 kHz

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The active sonar equation at the detection threshold (SNR = 0 dB):

$$SL - 2\,TL(R_{max}) + TS - NL + DI - DT = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute TL(R) = 20 log R + α R:

$$215 - 2\left[20\log R_{max} + 0.08 R_{max}\right] + (-10) - 55 + 25 - 12 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the constants:

$$215 - 10 - 55 + 25 - 12 = 163 \text{ dB}$$

$$163 - 2(20\log R_{max} + 0.08 R_{max}) = 0$$

$$40\log R_{max} + 0.16 R_{max} = 163$$

**Step 4:** Solve numerically:

Try R = 200 m → 40·log(200) + 0.16·200 = 40·2.301 + 32 = 92.04 + 32 = **124.04** (too low)

Try R = 700 m → 40·log(700) + 0.16·700 = 40·2.845 + 112 = 113.80 + 112 = **225.8** (too high)

Try R = 500 m → 40·log(500) + 0.16·500 = 40·2.699 + 80 = 107.96 + 80 = **187.96** (still high)

Try R = 400 m → 40·log(400) + 0.16·400 = 40·2.602 + 64 = 104.08 + 64 = **168.08** (close, slightly high)

Try R = 380 m → 40·log(380) + 0.16·380 = 40·2.580 + 60.8 = 103.2 + 60.8 = **164.0** (very close)

Try R = 378 m → 40·log(378) + 0.16·378 = 40·2.5775 + 60.48 = 103.10 + 60.48 = **163.58** ✓

$$\boxed{R_{max} \approx 378 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistake:** 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor "2"** in front of TL — students often write SL − TL, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit confusion** with α — α must be in dB/m, NOT Nepers/m. 1 Neper/m = 8.686 dB/m.
> 3. **Mixing log base** — use log₁₀, not natural log.
> 4. **Not specifying units** of SL, NL, TS, etc. — always write "dB re 1 µPa @ 1 m" to be safe.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** used in underwater vehicles. Explain why pure inertial navigation drifts and how it is mitigated using aiding sensors.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A 6-DOF MEMS IMU integrates three single-axis accelerometers and three single-axis gyroscopes on a common silicon die, packaged in a hermetically sealed housing with temperature compensation electronics. Tactical-grade IMUs (used in AUVs) cost USD 5,000–50,000.

**Working of Accelerometer (MEMS):**
A proof mass is suspended by compliant springs. Under acceleration, the mass deflects by Δx proportional to specific force f = a − g. The deflection is measured capacitively between interdigitated comb fingers.

**Working of Gyroscope (MEMS Vibratory):**
A primary drive mode vibrates the structure along one axis at resonant frequency ω_d. Rotation at rate Ω causes a Coriolis force F_c = 2m·(v × Ω) on the secondary axis, displacing the mass — measured capacitively.

**Why Pure Inertial Drifts:**
- Gyroscope bias b_ω (e.g., 0.01°/h) integrates to angle error θ = b_ω·t. At t = 1 hour, θ = 0.01° ≈ 36″.
- This angle error causes the accelerometer to project gravity into the wrong axis, producing an apparent acceleration a_err = g·sin(θ).
- This acceleration integrates **twice** into position error: 
  $$p_{err}(t) \approx \frac{1}{2} g \cdot b_\omega \cdot t^3$$
- For b_ω = 0.01°/h, after 1 hour: p_err ≈ **17 m** — clearly unacceptable for an AUV.

**Mitigation by Aiding Sensors:**
- **DVL** provides velocity measurement that bounds the velocity drift.
- **Pressure sensor** bounds the vertical channel (depth cannot drift).
- **USBL/LBL** provides absolute position updates every few seconds.
- Sensor fusion via **EKF or ESKF** estimates and removes the bias in real time, reducing drift to **< 0.1% of distance traveled (DVL-aided)**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With suitable diagrams, explain the working principles of **Side-Scan Sonar (SSS)** and **Multibeam Echo Sounder (MBES)**. Compare their applications in underwater robotics.

**Model Solution:**

**Side-Scan Sonar (SSS):**
- Two long linear transducer arrays are mounted on either side of a towfish, oriented broadside (athwartships) to the direction of motion.
- Each array emits a fan-shaped acoustic pulse that is **narrow in the along-track direction** (high resolution) and **wide in the across-track direction** (wide swath).
- The pulse strikes the seabed and returns an echo whose intensity is recorded versus time (slant range).
- Successive pings as the towfish moves forward are stacked into a 2-D image strip — analogous to an aerial photograph.
- Operating frequency: 100–900 kHz; Range: 50–500 m per side.

**Multibeam Echo Sounder (MBES):**
- A Mills-cross or curved array of many transducer elements (e.g., 256–1024) arranged perpendicular to the track.
- Beamforming is applied to form **N narrow beams** (N = 128–512) simultaneously across a wide swath (typically 120–150°).
- Each beam measures the slant range and angle to the seabed; the footprint is converted to a **digital elevation model (DEM)** of the bathymetry.
- Operating frequency: 12–455 kHz; Depth capability: 50–11,000 m.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Side-Scan Sonar | Multibeam Echo Sounder |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Output | **Acoustic backscatter image** (intensity) | **Bathymetric depth map** (3D) |
| Beam Geometry | 2 wide fans (port + starboard) | 100s of narrow beams (fan across-track) |
| Best For | Object detection, shipwrecks, mines | Seafloor topography, pipeline routing |
| Pixel Resolution | cm-scale along track | m-scale 3D point spacing |
| Cost | Moderate (USD 20k–100k) | Very High (USD 100k–1M) |
| Limitation | No depth info directly | Heavy processing load |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake:** 
> 1. Students often confuse **Side-Scan Sonar** (which produces an *image* of backscatter) with **Multibeam Echo Sounder** (which produces a *depth map*). Examiners specifically test this distinction — you will lose 2–3 marks if you swap them.
> 2. Forgetting to state the **operating frequency range** in your answer — this is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
