---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Visual & Motion Sensing Systems**

### Visual Sensing
- ☐ **Speed of sound in seawater** c ≈ **1500 m/s** (memorize this — appears in every sonar problem)
- ☐ **Attenuation of light** in water follows **Beer-Lambert Law**: I(d) = I₀·e^(−c·d)
- ☐ **Sonar range equation** is **two-way** → factor of **2·TL** (most-failed KTU question)
- ☐ **Beamwidth** of an acoustic array: θ ≈ **λ/D = c/(f·D)**
- ☐ **Doppler frequency shift** for DVL: $f_d = 2 f_t v \cos\alpha / c$
- ☐ **Side-Scan Sonar** = backscatter IMAGE; **Multibeam** = BATHYMETRY (don't confuse)
- ☐ **Stereo cameras** suffer 25% magnification error through flat port → correct using **n_water/n_air = 1.33**
- ☐ Optical cameras are limited to **<10 m** range in coastal water; **sonars** reach **hundreds of meters**

### Motion Sensing
- ☐ **IMU drift** is **cubic in time** (∝ t³) for position error
- ☐ **Proprioceptive** = internal (drifts); **Exteroceptive** = external (bounded)
- ☐ **DVL** uses **Janus configuration** (4 beams, 30° from vertical) to cancel heading bias
- ☐ **DVL works only in bottom-lock** (altitude < ~200 m); otherwise drifts in "water-track"
- ☐ **Pressure sensor** gives the most accurate **vertical** channel (cm-level)
- ☐ **Sensor fusion** via **EKF** is mandatory — pure IMU navigation is **never** used alone
- ☐ **Magnetometer** is affected by vehicle's own ferrous mass and local anomalies → calibrate often
- ☐ **USBL** requires a surface ship reference (transponder); **LBL** requires pre-deployed seabed transponders

### KTU Exam-Time Mantra
> **"Sonar equation: SL − 2TL + TS − (NL − DI) = DT"** 
> **"DVL velocity: v = c·Δf / (2 f_t cosα)"**
> **"Camera = short range high res; Sonar = long range low res; DVL = velocity; IMU = pose with drift"**

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Visual and Motion Sensing Systems**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme examination patterns and B.Tech outcome-based education (OBE) framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motion Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Motion Path Planning** in the context of Underwater Robotics is formally defined as the computational process of generating a geometrically and kinematically feasible trajectory for an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) or Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) from an initial configuration (pose) to a target configuration, while satisfying a set of constraints such as obstacle avoidance, vehicle dynamics, energy efficiency, mission time, and environmental disturbances (ocean currents, seabed topography, and underwater obstacles).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, Motion Path Planning is studied under two primary layers:

1. **Global Path Planning (GPP):** Computed offline or in a high-level planner using a complete or partial map of the underwater workspace.
2. **Local Path Planning (LPP) / Reactive Planning:** Performed online using real-time sensor data (sonar, cameras, DVL) to dynamically re-plan and avoid unforeseen obstacles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Path planning in underwater robotics is non-trivial because the medium is **3D**, GPS-denied, communication-constrained, and dominated by time-varying **ocean currents**. A path that is shortest in geometric distance is *not* always energy-optimal.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Driving a Car in a Foggy City with Rivers as Roads:**
> Imagine you are driving a car from your home to a shopping mall in a city blanketed with thick fog (no GPS signal). You have a rough hand-drawn city map (global map) but the map does not show real-time traffic or temporary roadblocks. The ocean current in an AUV is like an invisible river running through the city streets — if you drive *with* the river, you save fuel; if you drive *against* it, your battery drains rapidly.
> - **Global Path Planning** = Studying the city map before starting.
> - **Local Path Planning** = Using headlights, LIDAR, and brakes to dodge a fallen tree or stalled car on the fly.
> - **Optimal Path** = The route that balances **shortest distance, minimum fuel, and least travel time** — not just the straight line on the map.

## 1.3 Key Terminology in Motion Path Planning

| Term | Definition | Underwater Context |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Configuration Space (C-space)** | The space of all possible poses $(x, y, z, \theta)$ of the vehicle. | For a 6-DOF AUV: $C \in \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ |
| **Free Space (C-free)** | Subset of C-space not occupied by obstacles. | Subset excluding reefs, shipwrecks, pipelines. |
| **Workspace (W)** | The physical 3D environment in which the AUV operates. | Ocean volume bounded by surface and seabed. |
| **Holonomic Vehicle** | Can move instantaneously in any direction. | AUVs with side thrusters are near-holonomic. |
| **Non-holonomic Vehicle** | Constrained by motion equations (turning radius, no sideways motion). | Torpedo-shaped AUVs are non-holonomic. |
| **Replanning** | Online modification of the path when new obstacles are detected. | Sonar-based collision avoidance. |
| **Cost Function** | Scalar quantity to be minimized (energy, time, risk). | $J = \int_0^T (\alpha \cdot \text{energy} + \beta \cdot \text{time}) \, dt$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** Path optimality is often measured using **path length (meters)**, **execution time (seconds)**, and **energy expenditure (Watt-hours)**. The constants **α** and **β** are tunable weights.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Motion Path Planning Algorithms

Motion path planning algorithms in underwater robotics are classified as follows:

### A. Graph-Based (Search-Based) Methods
- Operate on discretized representations of the workspace.
- **Examples:** A*, Dijkstra, D* Lite, Theta*, Weighted A*.
- **KTU Relevance:** Most commonly asked in Part A and Part B (a) questions.

### B. Sampling-Based Methods
- Build a probabilistic roadmap by randomly sampling the configuration space.
- **Examples:** Probabilistic RoadMap (PRM), Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT), RRT*, Informed RRT*.
- **KTU Relevance:** Often the answer to a 14-mark Part B question.

### C. Potential Field Methods
- Treat the goal as an attractive potential and obstacles as repulsive potentials.
- **Examples:** Artificial Potential Field (APF), Harmonic Potential Field.
- **Pros:** Mathematically elegant, low computational cost.
- **Cons:** Suffers from **local minima** — a major KTU pitfall.

### D. Optimization-Based Methods
- Formulate path planning as a constrained optimization problem.
- **Examples:** Trajectory Optimization, Model Predictive Control (MPC), CHOMP, STOMP.

### E. Bio-Inspired / Heuristic Methods
- **Examples:** Genetic Algorithm (GA), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO).
- Useful for multi-objective planning in current-disturbed environments.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Workspace Representation:**
The 3D ocean environment is discretized into a graph (grid, octree, or voxel map).

**Step 2 — Cost Definition:**
Each edge of the graph is assigned a cost based on distance, current direction, depth, and obstacle proximity.

**Step 3 — Search / Sampling:**
An algorithm (A*, RRT, etc.) searches the graph for the lowest-cost path from start to goal.

**Step 4 — Smoothing & Feasibility Check:**
The raw path is smoothed (e.g., using cubic splines) and checked for kinematic feasibility (turning radius, dive angle).

**Step 5 — Execution & Replanning:**
The AUV follows the path while sensors perform local obstacle detection. If a new obstacle appears, a local re-planner is triggered.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Units | Description |
|---|------------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Heuristic function (A*)** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $g(n)$ = cost from start, $h(n)$ = estimated cost to goal. |
| 2 | **Admissible Heuristic** | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | $h^*(n)$ = true optimal cost. Guarantees optimality. |
| 3 | **Euclidean Heuristic** | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2 + (z_n - z_g)^2}$ | 3D straight-line distance. |
| 4 | **RRT Steer Function** | $x_{new} = x_{near} + \varepsilon \cdot \frac{x_{rand} - x_{near}}{\vert\vert x_{rand} - x_{near} \vert\vert}$ | $\varepsilon$ = step size, $\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert$ = Euclidean norm. |
| 5 | **Artificial Potential Field Force** | $F_{total} = F_{att} + F_{rep}$ | $F_{att} = -k_a \cdot \nabla U_{att}$, $F_{rep} = -k_r \cdot \nabla U_{rep}$. |
| 6 | **Attractive Potential** | $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_a \cdot \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert^2$ | Quadratic pull toward goal. |
| 7 | **Repulsive Potential** | $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ if $\rho \le \rho_0$ | $\rho$ = distance to obstacle, $\rho_0$ = influence radius. |
| 8 | **Time-Optimal Path with Current** | $t^* = \min \int_0^L \frac{1}{v_a \cos\theta + v_c} \, d\ell$ | $v_a$ = AUV speed, $v_c$ = current speed, $\theta$ = angle between path and current. |
| 9 | **Dubins Path Length (Min.)** | $L = d + r \cdot \phi$ | For minimum turn radius $r$ between two poses. |
| 10 | **Cost-to-Go (Dynamic Programming)** | $J^*(x) = \min_u \{L(x,u) + J^*(f(x,u))\}$ | Bellman equation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Notation:** In KTU answer scripts, always define every variable the first time it appears. The constants $\varepsilon$, $k_a$, $k_r$, $\rho_0$, $v_a$, $v_c$ are tunable design parameters.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** AUVs plan paths along subsea pipelines for inspection, avoiding risers and platforms.
- **Mine Counter-Measures (MCM):** Military AUVs use path planning to survey suspected minefields with minimum exposure.
- **Cable/Pipeline Tracking:** Path planning keeps the AUV at a constant standoff distance from the cable.
- **Deep-Sea Exploration (e.g., AUV Sentry, Autosub):** Path planning avoids abyssal trenches and rugged terrain.
- **Search & Recovery:** Finding black boxes (flight data recorders) requires intelligent coverage path planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — A* Optimality and Heuristic Admissibility

The A* algorithm finds the lowest-cost path in a weighted graph. Its evaluation function is:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

where $g(n)$ is the actual cost from the start node to node $n$, and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from $n$ to the goal.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the optimal path cost from start to goal:**
Let $h^*(n)$ denote the true optimal cost from node $n$ to the goal. By definition, $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for an **admissible** heuristic.

**Step 2 — Cost bound for the optimal goal node $G$:**
For the goal node $G$ found by A*, the cost is $f(G) = g(G) + h(G)$. Since $h(G) = 0$ (goal has zero heuristic cost to itself), we get $f(G) = g(G) = C^*$, where $C^*$ is the cost of the optimal path.

**Step 3 — For any other node $n$ on the optimal path:**
$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \le g(n) + h^*(n) = C^*
$$
This is the **consistency** property, which guarantees that A* never re-expands a node.

**Step 4 — Optimality proof:**
If A* were to return a non-optimal path with cost $C > C^*$, then there must exist some node $n$ on the optimal path that was not expanded. But $f(n) \le C^* < C$, so $n$ would have been selected for expansion before any node with cost $C$ — a contradiction.

**Conclusion:** A* with an admissible heuristic returns an optimal path.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-Optimal Path in a Uniform Ocean Current

Consider an AUV with constant speed $v_a$ relative to the water, moving in a uniform current of velocity $v_c$. The absolute velocity of the AUV over ground is the vector sum:

$$
\vec{v}_{ground} = \vec{v}_a + \vec{v}_c
$$

Let $\theta$ be the angle between the AUV heading and the direction of the ocean current. The component of AUV velocity along the path direction is:

$$
v_{path} = v_a \cos\theta + v_c
$$

To traverse a path segment of length $d\ell$, the time required is:

$$
dt = \frac{d\ell}{v_{path}} = \frac{d\ell}{v_a \cos\theta + v_c}
$$

The total time for the path of total length $L$ is:

$$
T = \int_0^L \frac{d\ell}{v_a \cos\theta(\ell) + v_c}
$$

To minimize $T$, the AUV should maximize $v_a \cos\theta + v_c$ at every point. Since $v_c$ is fixed, the optimal choice is:

$$
\theta^* = 0 \quad \text{(i.e., align AUV heading with current direction)}
$$

This yields the minimum time:

$$
T_{min} = \frac{L}{v_a + v_c}
$$

If the AUV swims **against** the current ($\theta = \pi$):

$$
T_{max} = \frac{L}{v_a - v_c} \quad \text{(valid only if } v_a > v_c\text{)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Implication:** While swimming against the current doubles/triples the time, the energy consumed (work done against current drag) increases even more dramatically. Path planners that ignore currents produce **infeasible** missions in practice.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Repulsive Potential Gradient (APF)

The repulsive potential at a configuration $q$ from an obstacle at $q_{obs}$ is:

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q,q_{obs})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \text{if } \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Let $d = \rho(q, q_{obs}) = \vert\vert q - q_{obs} \vert\vert$.

The repulsive force is the negative gradient:

$$
F_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}
$$

Computing the gradient with respect to $q$ (treating $q_{obs}$ as constant):

$$
\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial q} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{d^2} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial d}{\partial q}
$$

Since $\frac{\partial d}{\partial q} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{d} = \hat{n}$ (the unit normal pointing away from obstacle):

$$
\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial q} = -k_r \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^2} \cdot \hat{n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_{rep} = -\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial q} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{d^2} \cdot \hat{n}
$$

The magnitude of repulsive force grows as $d \to 0$ (i.e., as the AUV approaches the obstacle), pushing it away — which is the desired behavior.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | Completeness | Optimality | Computational Cost | Underwater Suitability | Key Limitation |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A*** | Yes (with admissible $h$) | Optimal | $O(b^d)$ — exponential in depth | High — good for grid-based seabed maps | Memory-intensive in 3D |
| **Dijkstra** | Yes | Optimal | $O(V^2)$ or $O(E + V \log V)$ | Moderate | Slow without good heuristic |
| **RRT** | Probabilistic | Sub-optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | Very high — handles 3D well | Path is jerky, not optimal |
| **RRT*** | Asymptotic | Asymptotically optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | Very high | Slow convergence |
| **APF** | No (local minima) | Sub-optimal | $O(k)$ per step | High — real-time | **Local minima trap** |
| **PRM** | Probabilistic | Sub-optimal | Preprocessing: $O(n \log n)$ | High | Pre-processing overhead |
| **D* Lite** | Yes | Optimal | $O(n \log n)$ incremental | Very high — for dynamic env. | Complex implementation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Motion Path Planning Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Goal Definition] --> B[World Model and Map Database]
    B --> C{Global Path Planner}
    C --> D[Global Reference Trajectory]
    D --> E[Trajectory Smoothing Module]
    E --> F[Local Path Planner and Obstacle Avoidance]
    F --> G[Sensor Fusion Sonar DVL IMU]
    G --> H{Replanning Trigger}
    H -- New Obstacle --> C
    H -- Path Clear --> I[Low Level Controller]
    I --> J[Actuator Commands Thrusters Rudders]
    J --> K[Underwater Vehicle AUV ROV]
    K --> L[Environment Feedback]
    L --> G
```

## 4.2 A* Search Node Expansion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Node S]) --> Init[Initialize Open Set with S]
    Init --> Check{Open Set Empty?}
    Check -- Yes --> Fail[No Path Exists Return Failure]
    Check -- No --> Pop[Pop Node n with Lowest f n]
    Pop --> GoalTest{n equals Goal Node G?}
    GoalTest -- Yes --> Success[Reconstruct Path Return Optimal Path]
    GoalTest -- No --> Expand[Generate Successors of n]
    Expand --> Update[For each successor compute g h and f]
    Update --> Insert[Insert or Update in Open Set]
    Insert --> Check
```

## 4.3 RRT Exploration Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InitRRT[Initialize Tree T with Start Node] --> Sample[Sample Random Configuration x rand in C space]
    Sample --> Nearest[Find Nearest Node x near in T]
    Nearest --> Steer[Steer from x near toward x rand with step epsilon]
    Steer --> CollisionCheck{Collision Free Edge?}
    CollisionCheck -- No --> Sample
    CollisionCheck -- Yes --> AddNode[Add x new to T with edge from x near]
    AddNode --> GoalCheck{Reached Goal Region?}
    GoalCheck -- No --> Sample
    GoalCheck -- Yes --> ExtractPath[Extract Path from T Start to Goal]
```

## 4.4 Potential Field Local Minima Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S([Start Position]) -- Attractive Force --> Trap{Local Minimum Trap}
    Trap -- No Escape --> Fail([AUV Stuck Oscillates])
    O1((Obstacle 1 Repulsive)) --> Trap
    O2((Obstacle 2 Repulsive)) --> Trap
    G([Goal Position Attractive]) -- Weaker than trap --> Trap
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** The local minima problem of APF is a **favourite 7-mark question**. Always pair the APF method with a **remedy** (random walk, virtual obstacle, or harmonic potential) to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define motion path planning in the context of underwater robotics. Distinguish between global and local path planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Motion path planning is the process of determining a feasible trajectory for an underwater vehicle from a start configuration to a goal configuration while avoiding obstacles and respecting vehicle dynamics.
- **Global Path Planning:** Computed offline using a pre-existing map; produces a coarse, complete path from start to goal.
- **Local Path Planning:** Computed online using real-time sensor data; handles dynamic obstacles and refines the global path.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**List any three challenges of path planning in underwater environments that do not exist in terrestrial mobile robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **GPS-denied environment** — AUVs cannot rely on GPS; localization relies on INS, DVL, and acoustic methods.
2. **Time-varying ocean currents** — Currents act as a moving reference frame, making a "shortest path" often suboptimal.
3. **Limited and noisy communication** — Acoustic modems are slow and bandwidth-limited, restricting real-time re-planning.
4. (Any other valid: 3D unstructured environment, murky water, expensive sensors.)

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Full Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the A* algorithm for motion path planning in underwater robotics. Define the evaluation function $f(n)$ and the admissibility condition for the heuristic. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a global path planner integrated with a local obstacle avoidance module for an AUV. How does replanning occur when a new obstacle is detected? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — A* Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Definition:** A* is a best-first graph search algorithm that uses a heuristic to estimate the cost from any node $n$ to the goal, combining it with the known cost from the start. It is widely used in grid-based underwater maps for AUV path planning.

**Evaluation function:**
$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$
where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to $n$ (path length, energy, or time).
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of cost from $n$ to goal.
- $f(n)$ = estimated total cost of the path through $n$.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize **Open Set** (priority queue) with start node $S$; $g(S) = 0$, $f(S) = h(S)$.
2. While Open Set is not empty:
   - Pop node $n$ with minimum $f(n)$.
   - If $n$ is goal, reconstruct path and terminate.
   - For each neighbor $m$ of $n$: compute tentative $g(m) = g(n) + c(n,m)$. If this is less than stored $g(m)$, update $g(m)$, $f(m) = g(m) + h(m)$, and set parent of $m$ to $n$.
3. If Open Set becomes empty, no path exists.

**Admissibility Condition:**
A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal:
$$
h(n) \le h^*(n) \quad \forall n
$$
This guarantees that A* returns an **optimal** path.

**Heuristic Example for 3D Underwater Grid:**
$$
h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2 + (z_n - z_g)^2}
$$

**Part (b) — Integrated Path Planning Architecture (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
[Mission Goal] -> [World Model] -> [Global Path Planner] -> [Reference Trajectory]
                                                                     |
                                                                     v
[Sonar / DVL / Camera] -> [Sensor Fusion] -> [Local Planner] -> [Replanning Trigger]
                                                                     |
                                  [Low-Level Controller] <-----------+
                                             |
                                             v
                                   [AUV Thrusters / Rudders]
```

**Explanation:**
- The **Global Path Planner** uses the world model (terrain map, known obstacle positions) to produce a coarse reference trajectory.
- The **Local Planner** runs in real-time, consuming sensor data (forward-looking sonar, DVL, stereo camera) to detect unforeseen obstacles.
- The **Replanning Trigger** activates when the local planner detects an obstacle within a safety bubble of radius $r_s$ around the AUV.
- On triggering, the global planner is re-invoked (or a local D* Lite search is performed) to compute a deviation.
- The new path is smoothed and forwarded to the **Low-Level Controller** for execution.

**Replanning Flow:**
1. Sensor detects obstacle at distance $d < d_{safe}$.
2. Local planner computes avoidance maneuver (turn angle $\alpha$, speed reduction).
3. If maneuver is infeasible, global replanner is invoked with the new obstacle added to the map.
4. New global path is published, controller resumes tracking.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method for path planning. Derive the expression for the repulsive force experienced by an AUV near an underwater obstacle. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is the local minima problem in APF? Suggest two techniques to overcome it, with diagrams. Discuss the suitability of APF for real-time underwater obstacle avoidance. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — APF Method (7 marks):**

The **Artificial Potential Field** method treats the AUV as a point moving under the influence of an artificial force field:
- The **goal** exerts an **attractive force** (pulling the AUV toward it).
- Each **obstacle** exerts a **repulsive force** (pushing the AUV away).

**Attractive Potential:**
$$
U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_a \cdot \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert^2
$$
**Attractive Force:**
$$
F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -k_a \cdot (q - q_{goal})
$$

**Repulsive Potential (for one obstacle):**
$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$
where $\rho = \vert\vert q - q_{obs} \vert\vert$ is the distance to the obstacle, and $\rho_0$ is the influence radius.

**Derivation of Repulsive Force:**
Taking the gradient of $U_{rep}$ with respect to $q$:
$$
F_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \hat{n}
$$
where $\hat{n} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\vert\vert q - q_{obs} \vert\vert}$ is the unit normal from the obstacle to the AUV.

**Total Force (Superposition for multiple obstacles):**
$$
F_{total} = F_{att} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{rep,i}
$$

**Part (b) — Local Minima & Remedies (7 marks):**

**Local Minima Problem:**
A local minimum occurs when the attractive force and the repulsive force(s) cancel each other exactly, producing $F_{total} = 0$ at a location **other than the goal**. The AUV becomes stuck.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose 2 marks by describing the local minima problem but **forgetting to suggest remedies**. Always include both the problem and the solution.

**Remedy 1 — Random Walk / Stochastic Perturbation:**
When the AUV detects it is stuck (velocity ≈ 0 for time $T_{stuck}$), it applies a random force $\vec{F}_{rand}$ for a short duration, escaping the trap. The planner then resumes normal APF behavior.

**Remedy 2 — Virtual Obstacle Technique:**
A small virtual repulsive obstacle is temporarily placed at the local minimum to break the force balance, pushing the AUV out of the trap.

**Remedy 3 — Harmonic Potential Field (HPF):**
Replaces the quadratic repulsive potential with a **harmonic** function that has no local minima by construction. Solves Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 U = 0$ in free space with boundary conditions $U = 1$ on obstacles and $U = 0$ at goal.

**Suitability for Real-Time Underwater Avoidance:**
- **Pros:** Very low computational cost, smooth trajectories, easy to implement in real-time controllers.
- **Cons:** Local minima, oscillations in narrow corridors, no dynamic constraint handling.
- **Verdict:** Suitable as a **local reactive layer** (e.g., forward-looking sonar-based avoidance), but should be combined with a global planner (A* or RRT*) for full mission coverage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping derivations:** Writing only the final formula without showing $\nabla U_{rep}$ derivation = -2 to -3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units and constants:** Writing $F_{rep}$ without defining $k_r$, $\rho_0$ = -1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing APF local minima with RRT failure modes** = conceptual error, -1 to -2 marks.
> 4. **Not relating the theory to underwater specifics (currents, GPS-denied):** KTU specifically tests domain relevance.
> 5. **Writing vague answers for "suitability":** Always give Pros, Cons, and a Verdict.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Path Planning =** generating a feasible trajectory from start to goal in C-space.
- [x] **Two layers:** Global (offline, A*/RRT*) + Local (online, APF/D* Lite).
- [x] **A\* evaluation:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
- [x] **Admissible heuristic:** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ — guarantees optimality.
- [x] **RRT steer:** $x_{new} = x_{near} + \varepsilon \cdot \hat{u}$ — probabilistic completeness, sub-optimal.
- [x] **RRT\*** achieves **asymptotic optimality** with same complexity.
- [x] **APF forces:** $F_{att} = -k_a(q - q_{goal})$; $F_{rep} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \hat{n}$.
- [x] **Local minima in APF** = the #1 conceptual trap — always pair with a remedy.
- [x] **Ocean current effect:** Optimal heading aligns with current ($\theta^* = 0$).
- [x] **3D path planning** uses $(x, y, z)$ — never ignore the depth axis in underwater problems.
- [x] **D* Lite** is preferred over A* for dynamic underwater environments (incremental replanning).
- [x] **Smoothing:** raw planner output must be smoothed (cubic splines) before being sent to the controller.
- [x] **Kinematic feasibility:** check minimum turn radius and maximum dive/climb angle.
- [x] **For 14-mark answers:** Always include a block diagram AND a numerical/analytical step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control and Stability: Path Planning Algorithms - Collision-Free Path Planning - Sensor-Based Obstacle Avoidance - Motion Control Methods: Kinematic Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control and Stability: Path Planning Algorithms - Collision-Free Path Planning - Sensor-Based Obstacle Avoidance - Motion Control Methods: Kinematic Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Path Planning Algorithms

### Formal Definition
**Path Planning Algorithms** in underwater robotics refer to the computational methods used to determine a feasible, optimal, and collision-free trajectory for an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) or Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) from an initial configuration to a goal configuration within a defined workspace, subject to dynamic, kinematic, and environmental constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Path planning is categorized into *global* (offline, complete environmental information) and *local* (online, sensor-based reactive) paradigms. Both are examinable as separate concepts.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine driving from your home in Kochi to the KTU campus in Thiruvananthapuram using Google Maps. The app computes a route considering traffic, road closures, and obstacles — this is **path planning**. If, while driving, a fallen tree suddenly blocks the road and you must swerve around it, that is **reactive obstacle avoidance**. Underwater robots face the same challenges, except their "map" is generated in real-time using sonar and vision sensors in a 3D, GPS-denied, low-visibility environment.

---

## 1.2 Collision-Free Path Planning

### Formal Definition
**Collision-Free Path Planning** is the subset of path planning that explicitly guarantees the generated trajectory does not intersect any geometric representation of an obstacle (static or dynamic) within the robot's configuration space (C-space).

$$C_{free} = \{q \in C \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset\}$$

Where $\mathcal{A}(q)$ is the robot volume at configuration $q$, and $\mathcal{O}$ is the obstacle set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept:** The C-space obstacle is defined as $C_{obs} = \{q \in C \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset\}$. A valid path lies entirely within $C_{free}$.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of navigating a house in total darkness with a flashlight. You mentally build a map of the furniture (obstacles) and find a path from your bedroom to the kitchen without bumping into the sofa. For AUVs, sonar pings act like that flashlight, gradually revealing obstacles that must be avoided.

---

## 1.3 Sensor-Based Obstacle Avoidance

### Formal Definition
**Sensor-Based Obstacle Avoidance** is a real-time, reactive control strategy that uses onboard sensor measurements (forward-looking sonar, multibeam echo sounders, DVL, acoustic cameras) to detect obstacles and modify the vehicle's trajectory locally without requiring a pre-computed global map.

The classic reactive formulation is the **Virtual Force Field (VFF)** method:

$$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{attract} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{F}_{repulse,i}$$

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture walking through a crowded market blindfolded but holding a long stick. As the stick touches someone, you instinctively step away. The stick is your sensor (sonar), and the stepping is your reactive obstacle avoidance — no pre-built map, just immediate response to detected contacts.

---

## 1.4 Kinematic Control

### Formal Definition
**Kinematic Control** refers to the motion control strategy that regulates an underwater vehicle's velocities (linear and angular) directly in the task space or configuration space, without explicitly considering the dynamic forces and torques. It is based purely on the geometric and kinematic relationships of the vehicle.

For a 6-DOF AUV described by the position vector $\eta = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ and body-fixed velocity $\nu = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$, the kinematic equation is:

$$\dot{\eta} = J(\eta) \cdot \nu$$

Where $J(\eta)$ is the **Jacobian transformation matrix** between body-fixed and inertial frames.

### Conceptual Analogy
Driving a car in an empty parking lot, you only think about *where* the steering wheel needs to point to go from your current spot to the empty one (kinematic control). You do not calculate tire friction, engine torque, or suspension dynamics — those are *dynamic control*. Underwater kinematic control works the same way: only the *geometry* of motion matters.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants for Underwater Robotics (Kerala Coastal/Offshore Operations):**
> - **Seawater density:** $\rho \approx \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$
> - **Speed of sound in seawater:** $c \approx \mathbf{1500 \ m/s}$
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$
> - **Typical AUV operational depth:** $\mathbf{50 - 6000 \ m}$
> - **Sonar detection range (multibeam):** $\mathbf{50 - 200 \ m}$

---

## 1.6 Taxonomy Overview

| Strategy Level | Method | Information Required | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Global (Offline)** | A*, Dijkstra, RRT, PRM | Complete prior map | High |
| **Local (Online)** | VFF, Vector Field Histogram (VFH), Dynamic Window | Real-time sensor data | Low to Moderate |
| **Reactive (Sensor-Based)** | Bug algorithm, Potential Fields, APF | Immediate sensor feedback | Very Low |
| **Hybrid** | A* with DWA refinement | Partial map + sensors | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Global Path Planning Algorithms

### 2.1.1 Graph-Based Search: A* Algorithm

The A* algorithm finds the shortest path by combining:
- **g(n):** actual cost from start to current node $n$
- **h(n):** heuristic estimated cost from $n$ to goal (must be admissible)

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

For underwater grid maps, admissible heuristics include:
- **Euclidean distance:** $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2 + (z_n - z_g)^2}$
- **Manhattan distance:** $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert + \vert z_n - z_g \vert$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why A* is popular in KTU context:** It guarantees optimality when $h(n)$ is admissible and consistent, and is widely used in MATLAB simulations of AUV missions.

### 2.1.2 Sampling-Based: Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)

RRT incrementally builds a tree from the start configuration by sampling random points in $C_{free}$ and extending the nearest node toward them.

Extension step (kinematic model):
$$q_{new} = q_{near} + \Delta q \cdot \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\Vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \Vert}$$

With collision check:
$$\text{If } \mathcal{A}(q_{new}) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset, \text{ then add edge } (q_{near}, q_{new})$$

**Strengths for underwater use:**
- Probabilistically complete (finds a solution if one exists)
- Handles high-dimensional C-space (6-DOF AUV)
- No explicit grid required

**Weakness:** Not optimal; path requires post-smoothing (e.g., RRT*).

### 2.1.3 Potential Field Method (PFM)

The robot is treated as a point moving under the influence of an **attractive potential** toward the goal and a **repulsive potential** from obstacles:

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2$$

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The control force (gradient descent):
$$\vec{F} = -\nabla U_{att} - \nabla U_{rep}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Local Minima Problem:** PFM can get stuck in local minima (e.g., between two close obstacles). KTU exam questions often test this limitation and ask for hybrid solutions.

---

## 2.2 Sensor-Based Obstacle Avoidance Methods

### 2.2.1 Virtual Force Field (VFF)

For each obstacle cell detected at distance $d_i$ and angle $\theta_i$:

$$F_{repulse,i} = \frac{k_{rep}}{(d_i - d_{safe})^2} \quad \text{(magnitude)}$$

Direction: opposite to the vector from obstacle to robot. The total force is the vector sum computed in real-time as sonar returns arrive.

### 2.2.2 Vector Field Histogram (VFH)

VFH discretizes the angular space into $k$ sectors. For each sector $k$:

$$h_k = \sum_{i \in C_k} \frac{1}{d_i^2}$$

A threshold $h_{max}$ filters out high-density sectors. The robot then chooses the angular direction with the lowest histogram density in the polar histogram.

### 2.2.3 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)

DWA considers only velocities achievable in the next time step given the vehicle's acceleration limits:

$$V_d = \{v, \omega \mid v \in [v_a - \dot{v}_{max} \cdot \Delta t, \, v_a + \dot{v}_{max} \cdot \Delta t], \, \text{admissible to obstacles}\}$$

An objective function selects optimal $(v, \omega)$:
$$G(v, \omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega)$$

---

## 2.3 Kinematic Control Methods

### 2.3.1 Reference Trajectory Tracking

The goal is to find control velocities $\nu_c$ such that the AUV tracks a desired trajectory $\eta_d(t)$:

$$\nu_c = J^{-1}(\eta) \left[ \dot{\eta}_d + K_p \tilde{\eta} + K_i \int \tilde{\eta} \, dt \right]$$

Where $\tilde{\eta} = \eta_d - \eta$ is the position error, and $K_p$, $K_i$ are proportional and integral gain matrices.

### 2.3.2 Pure Kinematic Controller (Non-Singular)

For a 5-DOF AUV (surge, sway, heave, roll, yaw), the control law:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u_c \\ v_c \\ w_c \\ r_c \end{bmatrix} = J^{-1}(\psi) \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_d + k_x(x_d - x) \\ \dot{y}_d + k_y(y_d - y) \\ \dot{z}_d + k_z(z_d - z) \\ \dot{\psi}_d + k_\psi(\psi_d - \psi) \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.3.3 Feedback Linearization Kinematic Control

If $J(\eta)$ is invertible and smooth, define the auxiliary control input $w$:
$$w = J(\eta) \cdot \nu_c$$

Then the closed-loop error dynamics become linear:
$$\dot{\tilde{\eta}} + K \tilde{\eta} = 0$$

This is asymptotically stable if $K$ is positive definite (Hurwitz matrix).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A* evaluation | cost units | Global path cost |
| 2 | $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \Vert q - q_g \Vert^2$ | Attractive potential | J (energy) | Goal-seeking |
| 3 | $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ | Repulsive potential | J (energy) | Obstacle avoidance |
| 4 | $\dot{\eta} = J(\eta) \nu$ | Kinematic eqn. | rad/s, m/s | Frame transformation |
| 5 | $\nu_c = J^{-1}(\eta)[\dot{\eta}_d + K_p \tilde{\eta}]$ | Tracking control | m/s | Trajectory tracking |
| 6 | $F_{rep} = k_{rep} / (d - d_{safe})^2$ | VFF repulsion | N (normalized) | Sensor reaction |
| 7 | $h_k = \sum 1/d_i^2$ | VFH density | unitless | Sector selection |
| 8 | $G = \alpha H + \beta D + \gamma V$ | DWA objective | score | Velocity selection |
| 9 | $\dot{\tilde{\eta}} + K\tilde{\eta} = 0$ | Closed-loop error | m/s | Stability proof |
| 10 | $C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$ | Free space | m$^3$ or rad$^3$ | Configuration space |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** In all equations above, use $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ for Euclidean norm (not the pipe symbol `\|...\|`) to avoid LaTeX rendering errors in KTU answer sheets. Always use `\vert` for absolute value if required.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore Pipeline Inspection (Kerala, Mumbai High):** AUVs use A* + VFH hybrid for pre-planned path with reactive avoidance of marine life and structures.
- **Harbor Security:** AUVs use kinematic control to maintain station-keeping at fixed depth/yaw for acoustic surveillance.
- **Deep-Sea Mining:** RRT-based planning handles complex 3D terrain of the Clarion-Clipperton Zone.
- **Search & Recovery:** Sonar-based reactive avoidance enables operation in turbid, post-disaster underwater environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kinematic Equation of Motion for AUV

### Starting Point: Two Reference Frames

The underwater vehicle moves in the **Earth-fixed (inertial) frame** $\{E\}$ with coordinates $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ and has a **body-fixed frame** $\{B\}$ with linear velocities $(u, v, w)$ and angular velocities $(p, q, r)$.

### Step 1: Position Vector Definition

$$\eta = \begin{bmatrix} p^n \\ \epsilon^n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x & y & z & \phi & \theta & \psi \end{bmatrix}^T$$

$$\nu = \begin{bmatrix} v^n \\ \omega^n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} u & v & w & p & q & r \end{bmatrix}^T$$

### Step 2: Rotation Matrix from Body to Inertial Frame

The body-to-inertial rotation matrix $R_b^n$ is parameterized by ZYX (yaw-pitch-roll) Euler angles:

$$R_b^n(\psi, \theta, \phi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & -s\psi c\phi + c\psi s\theta s\phi & s\psi s\phi + c\psi s\theta c\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & c\psi c\phi + s\psi s\theta s\phi & -c\psi s\phi + s\psi s\theta c\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $c\psi = \cos\psi$, $s\psi = \sin\psi$, etc.

### Step 3: Angular Velocity Transformation

The body angular velocities are related to Euler angle rates via the Euler transformation matrix $T_\Theta$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix} = T_\Theta(\eta) \begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix}$$

$$T_\Theta(\eta) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & s\phi t\theta & c\phi t\theta \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ 0 & s\phi / c\theta & c\phi / c\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $t\theta = \tan\theta$, $c\theta = \cos\theta$.

### Step 4: Combine into Single Kinematic Matrix

The full 6×6 Jacobian:

$$J(\eta) = \begin{bmatrix} R_b^n(\eta) & 0_{3\times 3} \\ 0_{3\times 3} & T_\Theta(\eta) \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:
$$\dot{\eta} = J(\eta) \nu = \begin{bmatrix} R_b^n(\eta) \nu_{lin} \\ T_\Theta(\eta) \nu_{ang} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Stability Analysis of Tracking Error

Define position error $\tilde{\eta} = \eta_d - \eta$ and design control law:

$$\nu_c = J^{-1}(\eta) [\dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}]$$

Substitute into the kinematic equation:

$$\dot{\eta} = J(\eta) \cdot J^{-1}(\eta) [\dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}]$$

$$\dot{\eta} = \dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}$$

Since $\dot{\tilde{\eta}} = \dot{\eta}_d - \dot{\eta}$:

$$\dot{\tilde{\eta}} = \dot{\eta}_d - (\dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}) = -K \tilde{\eta}$$

### Step 6: Solution of Error Dynamics

The closed-loop error ODE is:
$$\dot{\tilde{\eta}} + K \tilde{\eta} = 0$$

This is a linear first-order system with solution:
$$\tilde{\eta}(t) = \tilde{\eta}(0) e^{-Kt}$$

For asymptotic stability, the gain matrix $K$ must be **positive definite** (eigenvalues with positive real parts, so $-K$ has negative real parts). Typical choice for AUV:

$$K = \text{diag}(k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4, k_5, k_6) = \text{diag}(0.5, 0.5, 0.3, 1.0, 1.0, 0.8)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Larger $k_i$ produces faster error convergence but may exceed actuator saturation in real AUVs. KTU questions often ask for "appropriate" $K$ values balancing speed and feasibility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Virtual Force Field (VFF) Control Law

### Step 1: Define Sonar Coordinate System

Sonar returns are organized in polar coordinates $(d_i, \theta_i)$ where $d_i$ is the measured distance and $\theta_i$ is the bearing of obstacle $i$.

### Step 2: Compute Individual Repulsive Force Vectors

For each obstacle $i$ within the safety radius $d_{safe}$:

$$F_{repulse,i} = \frac{k_{rep}}{(d_i - d_{safe})^2 + \epsilon}$$

Direction: $\hat{r}_i = (\cos\theta_i, \sin\theta_i)$ pointing from robot to obstacle.
Repulsive vector:
$$\vec{F}_{repulse,i} = -F_{repulse,i} \cdot \hat{r}_i$$

### Step 3: Compute Attractive Force Toward Goal

$$\vec{F}_{attract} = k_{att} \cdot (\vec{p}_{goal} - \vec{p}_{robot})$$

### Step 4: Sum and Convert to Velocity

The total force is then mapped to desired heading and speed:
$$\psi_{desired} = \text{atan2}(F_{total,y}, F_{total,x})$$

$$u_{desired} = u_{max} \cdot \min\left(1, \frac{\Vert \vec{F}_{total} \Vert}{F_{sat}}\right)$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example

Let $k_{rep} = 1.0$, $k_{att} = 0.5$, $d_{safe} = 5$ m, sonar reports one obstacle at $(d_1=8, \theta_1=30°)$, goal at $(50, 0)$ from robot at origin.

$$F_{repulse,1} = \frac{1.0}{(8-5)^2} = \frac{1.0}{9} \approx 0.111$$

Direction: $-(\cos 30°, \sin 30°) = (-0.866, -0.500)$

$$\vec{F}_{repulse,1} = (-0.096, -0.056)$$

$$\vec{F}_{attract} = 0.5 \cdot (50, 0) = (25, 0)$$

$$\vec{F}_{total} = (24.904, -0.056)$$

$$\psi_{desired} = \text{atan2}(-0.056, 24.904) \approx -0.13°$$

The vehicle steers almost directly toward the goal with a tiny leftward bias — consistent with physical intuition.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison

| Property | A* | RRT | Potential Field | VFF (Reactive) | DWA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Optimal (with admissible h) | Not optimal | Local optimum possible | Reactive, no global optimality | Locally optimal |
| **Completeness** | Complete (finite grid) | Probabilistically complete | Not complete | Reactive | Complete within window |
| **Computational Cost** | High (exponential in dim.) | Moderate | Low | Very low | Low to moderate |
| **Real-time Use** | Offline only | Offline | Both | Online | Online |
| **Dynamic Obstacles** | Poor | Moderate (RRT*) | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Underwater Suitability** | Pre-mission planning | 3D complex terrain | Simple missions | Sonar-based avoidance | Velocity-based tracking |
| **Local Minima** | No | No | Yes | Possible | Avoided by sampling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Control Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Goal Definition] --> B[Global Path Planner A* or RRT]
    B --> C[Generate Waypoint Sequence]
    C --> D[Kinematic Controller Tracks Waypoints]
    D --> E[Sonar / Multibeam Sensor Data]
    E --> F[Local Obstacle Detection VFH or VFF]
    F --> G{Obstacle Detected?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Modify Local Trajectory]
    G -- No --> D
    H --> I[Re-evaluate Path Validity]
    I --> J{Path Still Valid?}
    J -- Yes --> D
    J -- No --> K[Trigger Replanning RRT or A*]
    K --> B
    D --> L[Actuator Commands Thrusters]
    L --> M[Vehicle Motion in Water]
    M --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This represents a hierarchical control loop used in actual KTU AUV laboratory simulations. The outer loop is slow (global replanning at 1 Hz), the middle loop is medium (waypoint tracking at 10 Hz), and the inner loop is fast (sensor-reactive avoidance at 50–100 Hz).

---

## 4.2 Kinematic Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Trajectory eta_d] --> B[Subtractor Compute Error]
    C[Current State eta] --> B
    B --> D[Error Signal tilde_eta]
    D --> E[Gain Matrix K]
    E --> F[Error Rate K times tilde_eta]
    A --> G[Derivative eta_d_dot]
    G --> H[Summing Junction]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Auxiliary Input w]
    I --> J[Jacobian Inverse J_inverse]
    J --> K[Velocity Command nu_c]
    K --> L[AUV Thrusters]
    L --> C
```

---

## 4.3 A* Algorithm Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Initialize Open Set with Start Node]) --> OpenSet[Open Set Priority Queue]
    OpenSet --> PopNode{Pop Lowest f n Node}
    PopNode --> CheckGoal{Is Goal Reached?}
    CheckGoal -- Yes --> Reconstruct[Reconstruct Path via Parents]
    CheckGoal -- No --> ExpandNeighbors[Generate Successor Nodes]
    ExpandNeighbors --> LoopStart[For Each Neighbor]
    LoopStart --> CollisionCheck{Collision Free?}
    CollisionCheck -- No --> LoopStart
    CollisionCheck -- Yes --> ComputeCost[Compute g n and h n]
    ComputeCost --> Update[Update f n in Open Set if Better]
    Update --> LoopStart
    LoopStart -- All Done --> PopNode
    Reconstruct --> End([Output Optimal Path])
```

---

## 4.4 Virtual Force Field Reactive Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sonar Scan Returns distances d_i bearings theta_i] --> B[Filter Within Safety Radius]
    B --> C[Compute Repulsive Forces F_rep_i]
    C --> D[Compute Attractive Force F_att to Goal]
    D --> E[Vector Sum F_total]
    E --> F[Compute Desired Heading psi_d]
    F --> G[Compute Desired Speed u_d]
    G --> H[Send to Kinematic Controller]
    H --> I[Vehicle Moves]
    I --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The reactive loop runs in real time. For an AUV operating at 1.5 m/s with a sonar range of 50 m, the loop must execute at least every 30 seconds to allow safe avoidance, but typically runs at 5–10 Hz to handle rapidly changing conditions like underwater currents displacing the vehicle sideways.

---

## 4.5 Hierarchical Control Hierarchy for AUVs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MissionLevel["MISSION LEVEL (Slow)"]
        M1[Global Path A* RRT]
    end
    subgraph TacticalLevel["TACTICAL LEVEL (Medium)"]
        T1[Waypoint Tracking]
        T2[Behavior Arbitration Avoid vs Track]
    end
    subgraph ExecutionLevel["EXECUTION LEVEL (Fast)"]
        E1[Kinematic Controller]
        E2[Sensor Processing Sonar DVL IMU]
        E3[Thruster Allocation]
    end
    M1 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> E1
    E2 --> E1
    E1 --> E3
    E3 -.Feedback.-> E2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Tier Architecture Rationale:** This separation of time scales is critical for stable AUV operation. Mixing fast reactive loops with slow replanning at the same frequency causes instability. KTU practical exams often test this separation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define sensor-based obstacle avoidance. List any two reactive obstacle avoidance algorithms used in underwater robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

Sensor-based obstacle avoidance is a real-time control strategy in which an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) uses onboard sensors such as forward-looking sonar, multibeam echo sounders, or acoustic cameras to detect nearby obstacles and immediately modify its local trajectory to prevent collision, without requiring a complete prior map of the environment.

Two reactive obstacle avoidance algorithms are:

1. **Virtual Force Field (VFF) Method:** Treats the robot as a point subject to attractive force toward the goal and repulsive forces from detected obstacles. The total force vector determines the instantaneous heading and speed.

2. **Vector Field Histogram (VFH) Method:** Discretizes the sonar returns into angular sectors, builds a polar histogram of obstacle density, and selects the sector with the lowest density as the new direction of travel.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the kinematic equation of motion for an underwater vehicle and state its significance in motion control.**

**Model Answer:**

The kinematic equation of motion for a 6-DOF underwater vehicle relates the rate of change of position and orientation in the inertial (Earth-fixed) frame to the linear and angular velocities expressed in the body-fixed frame:

$$\dot{\eta} = J(\eta) \cdot \nu$$

Where $\eta = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ is the position/orientation vector in the inertial frame, $\nu = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$ is the velocity vector in the body frame, and $J(\eta)$ is the 6×6 transformation Jacobian composed of the rotation matrix $R_b^n$ for linear motion and the Euler transformation matrix $T_\Theta$ for angular motion.

**Significance in Motion Control:**

1. It enables direct conversion between body-fixed velocity commands (issued by thrusters) and inertial-frame motion (observed by external sensors like USBL).
2. It forms the basis of kinematic controllers where desired body velocities are computed by inverting the Jacobian: $\nu_c = J^{-1}(\eta) [\dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}]$.
3. It is essential for trajectory tracking, path following, and station-keeping in AUVs.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### (a) Discuss the A* algorithm for path planning in underwater environments. Show the evaluation function and explain admissibility. (7 Marks)

#### (b) Explain the Virtual Force Field (VFF) method for sensor-based obstacle avoidance. Derive the control law and discuss its limitations. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a): A* Algorithm

**Definition:** The A* algorithm is a best-first graph search algorithm that finds the least-cost path from a start node to a goal node in a discretized configuration space. It is widely used for global path planning of AUVs operating in partially known underwater environments (e.g., harbor surveillance, pipeline inspection).

**Evaluation Function:**

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

Where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$ (sum of edge weights traversed)
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimated cost from $n$ to goal
- $f(n)$ = total estimated cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$

**Admissibility Condition:**

A heuristic is *admissible* if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal:

$$0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n$$

For underwater grid maps:
- **Euclidean distance** $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2 + (z_n - z_g)^2}$ is admissible for 3D motion.
- **Manhattan distance** is admissible for 4-connected (no diagonal) grids.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Initialize open set with start node, $g(start) = 0$, $f(start) = h(start)$.
2. Repeat until goal reached or open set empty:
   a. Pop node $n$ with minimum $f(n)$.
   b. If $n$ is goal, reconstruct and return path.
   c. For each neighbor $m$ of $n$:
      - If new path via $n$ is shorter, update $g(m)$ and $f(m)$.
      - Add $m$ to open set if not already present.
3. Return failure if open set empties.

**Underwater-Specific Considerations:**

- **3D grid** discretization of underwater workspace (depth adds third dimension).
- **Edge costs** include current strength, terrain slope, and proximity to obstacles.
- **Heuristic scaling** with depth: $h(n) = \sqrt{d_{horiz}^2 + d_{vert}^2 \cdot w_{depth}}$, where $w_{depth}$ weights vertical motion (e.g., 2.0 to favor horizontal motion, since AUVs are more efficient moving forward than diving).

**Complexity:** $O(b^d)$ in worst case, where $b$ is branching factor and $d$ is depth of solution. With a good heuristic, this is reduced to $O(b^d \cdot \epsilon)$ where $\epsilon$ is the heuristic quality factor.

**Optimality Guarantee:** A* returns an optimal path if $h$ is admissible and the search graph is finite.

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b): Virtual Force Field (VFF)

**Conceptual Basis:** The VFF method models the AUV as a point particle in a 2D (or 3D) force field. The goal exerts an attractive force, and each detected obstacle exerts a repulsive force. The resultant force vector determines the desired heading and speed of the vehicle in real time.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

**Step 1: Attractive Force toward Goal:**

$$\vec{F}_{att} = k_{att} \cdot (\vec{p}_{goal} - \vec{p}_{robot})$$

Or with a bounded form to prevent excessive speed:
$$\vec{F}_{att} = \begin{cases} k_{att} (\vec{p}_{goal} - \vec{p}_{robot}) & \text{if } \Vert \vec{p}_{goal} - \vec{p}_{robot} \Vert \leq d_{att} \\ k_{att} \cdot d_{att} \cdot \frac{\vec{p}_{goal} - \vec{p}_{robot}}{\Vert \vec{p}_{goal} - \vec{p}_{robot} \Vert} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Repulsive Force from Each Detected Obstacle:**

For obstacle $i$ at distance $d_i$ and bearing $\theta_i$:

$$F_{repulse,i} = \begin{cases} \frac{k_{rep}}{(d_i - d_{safe})^2} & \text{if } d_i \leq d_{influence} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The vector form:
$$\vec{F}_{repulse,i} = -F_{repulse,i} \cdot (\cos\theta_i, \sin\theta_i)$$

**Step 3: Total Force and Control Output:**

$$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{att} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{F}_{repulse,i}$$

$$\psi_{desired} = \text{atan2}(F_{total,y}, F_{total,x})$$

$$u_{desired} = u_{max} \cdot \min\left(1, \frac{\Vert \vec{F}_{total} \Vert}{F_{sat}}\right)$$

**Limitations of VFF:**

1. **Local Minima Trap:** The vehicle can become stuck at equilibrium points where attractive and repulsive forces cancel but the goal is unreachable. This frequently occurs between two close obstacles.

2. **Oscillations in Narrow Passages:** When traversing a corridor, repeated obstacle detection on both sides causes the vehicle to oscillate.

3. **No Memory:** VFF is purely reactive and forgets previous detections. This causes redundant corrections and inefficient trajectories.

4. **Single-Sensor Bias:** If the sonar has a narrow beam, obstacles outside the cone are not detected, leading to collisions from unobserved directions.

5. **No Velocity Constraints:** Pure VFF ignores the AUV's turning rate limits, producing kinematically infeasible paths.

**Improvements (often asked in KTU Part B):**

- **VFH (Vector Field Histogram):** Adds angular discretization and thresholding to overcome narrow-passage oscillations.
- **VFH+:** Includes robot radius and turning constraints.
- **Hybrid VFF + A*:** Global path provides waypoint sub-goals, VFF handles local avoidance.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain kinematic control for an AUV. Derive the trajectory tracking control law and show that the closed-loop error dynamics are exponentially stable. (7 Marks)

#### (b) Compare graph-based, sampling-based, and potential field methods for collision-free path planning. Give one example algorithm for each category. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a): Kinematic Control

**Definition:** Kinematic control for an AUV is a control strategy that directly regulates the vehicle's velocities (linear and angular) in the body-fixed frame to achieve a desired position and orientation in the inertial frame, without explicitly computing the forces and torques required (which would be dynamic control).

**Step 1: System Kinematic Equation:**

The 6-DOF AUV evolves according to:
$$\dot{\eta} = J(\eta) \nu$$

Where $\eta \in \mathbb{R}^6$ is the generalized position in the inertial frame, $\nu \in \mathbb{R}^6$ is the generalized velocity in the body frame, and $J(\eta)$ is the smooth, invertible Jacobian transformation matrix.

**Step 2: Define the Control Objective:**

The desired trajectory is $\eta_d(t)$, which is smooth and bounded. The tracking error is:
$$\tilde{\eta}(t) = \eta_d(t) - \eta(t)$$

**Step 3: Design the Control Law:**

Choose the body-fixed velocity command as:
$$\nu_c = J^{-1}(\eta) \left[ \dot{\eta}_d + K_p \tilde{\eta} + K_i \int_0^t \tilde{\eta}(\tau) d\tau \right]$$

For simplicity, consider proportional control:
$$\nu_c = J^{-1}(\eta) \left[ \dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta} \right]$$

Where $K$ is a 6×6 positive-definite gain matrix.

**Step 4: Closed-Loop Error Dynamics:**

Substituting the control law into the kinematic equation:
$$\dot{\eta} = J(\eta) \cdot J^{-1}(\eta) [\dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}]$$

Since $J(\eta) \cdot J^{-1}(\eta) = I$:
$$\dot{\eta} = \dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}$$

Differentiating the error:
$$\dot{\tilde{\eta}} = \dot{\eta}_d - \dot{\eta} = \dot{\eta}_d - (\dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}) = -K \tilde{\eta}$$

**Step 5: Stability Proof:**

The error dynamics are $\dot{\tilde{\eta}} = -K \tilde{\eta}$. The solution is:
$$\tilde{\eta}(t) = e^{-Kt} \tilde{\eta}(0)$$

Choose the Lyapunov function candidate:
$$V(\tilde{\eta}) = \frac{1}{2} \tilde{\eta}^T \tilde{\eta} > 0 \quad \forall \tilde{\eta} \neq 0$$

Its time derivative:
$$\dot{V} = \tilde{\eta}^T \dot{\tilde{\eta}} = \tilde{\eta}^T (-K \tilde{\eta}) = -\tilde{\eta}^T K \tilde{\eta}$$

Since $K$ is positive definite, $\dot{V} < 0$ for all $\tilde{\eta} \neq 0$. By Lyapunov's direct method, the equilibrium $\tilde{\eta} = 0$ is **globally asymptotically stable**, and the convergence is **exponential** with rate determined by the minimum eigenvalue of $K$:
$$\Vert \tilde{\eta}(t) \Vert \leq \Vert \tilde{\eta}(0) \Vert e^{-\lambda_{min}(K) t}$$

**Practical Issues:**

- $J^{-1}(\eta)$ becomes singular when $\theta = \pm 90°$ (gimbal lock), so the controller must limit pitch angle or use quaternion representation.
- The control law assumes the AUV can instantaneously achieve $\nu_c$, which is unrealistic; in practice a low-level thruster controller must track $\nu_c$.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b): Comparison of Path Planning Methods

**1. Graph-Based Methods:**

These methods discretize the configuration space into a graph (nodes = configurations, edges = feasible transitions) and apply a search algorithm to find the optimal path.

- **Example:** A* (A-star) algorithm.
- **Strengths:** Guaranteed optimality with admissible heuristic, complete on finite graphs, well-understood complexity.
- **Weaknesses:** Curse of dimensionality — discrete graph size grows exponentially with the number of degrees of freedom. Unsuitable for high-DOF AUVs in 3D continuous space without careful coarsening.
- **Best Use Case:** 2D or 3D grid-based maps of known underwater terrain (e.g., pre-mapped harbor floor).

**2. Sampling-Based Methods:**

These methods randomly sample the continuous configuration space and incrementally build a graph or tree connecting collision-free samples.

- **Example:** Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT).
- **Strengths:** Probabilistically complete (finds a solution if one exists with probability → 1 as samples → ∞). Naturally handles high-dimensional spaces (6-DOF AUVs). Does not require explicit grid construction.
- **Weaknesses:** Not optimal (basic RRT); optimal variants (RRT*) have higher computational cost. Path quality is irregular; smoothing is required.
- **Best Use Case:** Complex 3D underwater terrain with irregular obstacles (e.g., shipwreck interior exploration).

**3. Potential Field Methods:**

These methods define a scalar potential function over the workspace with a global minimum at the goal and local maxima at obstacles. The robot follows the negative gradient of the potential.

- **Example:** Artificial Potential Field (APF) method.
- **Strengths:** Mathematically elegant, computationally cheap, naturally produces smooth trajectories, can be implemented as a real-time reactive controller.
- **Weaknesses:** Local minima problem — robot can get trapped in equilibrium points where attractive and repulsive forces balance. Cannot handle narrow passages well.
- **Best Use Case:** Simple missions in relatively open waters with sparse, well-separated obstacles.

**Summary Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Graph-Based (A*) | Sampling-Based (RRT) | Potential Field (APF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Yes (with admissible h) | No (RRT* yes) | Local optimum |
| **Completeness** | Yes (finite graph) | Probabilistic | No |
| **Computational Cost** | High in high-dim | Moderate | Low |
| **Real-time Use** | Offline | Mostly offline | Yes (real-time) |
| **Local Minima Issue** | No | No | Yes |
| **Underwater 3D Use** | Pre-mapped terrain | Complex 3D terrain | Open-water missions |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Confusing kinematic and dynamic control:** Many students interchange these. Kinematic control regulates *velocity* directly. Dynamic control regulates *forces/torques* and requires the inertia matrix $M(\nu)$ and Coriolis matrix $C(\nu)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Jacobian inverse singularity:** When pitch $\theta = \pm 90°$, $T_\Theta$ becomes singular. Always mention this in trajectory tracking answers.
> 3. **Mixing up admissible and consistent heuristics:** Admissible means $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$. Consistent (monotonic) means $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$. Both are required for A* optimality, but consistent is the stronger condition.
> 4. **Omitting units in formula derivations:** Always state units (m/s for velocity, m for distance, s for time, rad for angles) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **VFF derivation skipping the saturation term:** Forgetting the min(1, ...) clamp on speed causes unrealistically large velocity commands — a common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Stability proof missing Lyapunov function:** Just writing $\dot{V} < 0$ without defining $V$ first is incomplete. Always define $V$, compute $\dot{V}$, then conclude stability.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Summary

- **Path Planning** is *offline* and uses a global map; **Sensor-Based Avoidance** is *online* and reactive.
- **A\*** combines actual cost $g(n)$ and heuristic $h(n)$: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Optimal if $h$ is admissible.
- **RRT** is probabilistically complete, samples random points, and extends nearest node — ideal for 3D complex underwater terrain.
- **Potential Field** has attractive and repulsive potentials; major drawback is **local minima**.
- **Virtual Force Field (VFF)** is purely reactive, summing repulsive forces from sonar-detected obstacles with attractive force to goal.
- **VFH** discretizes obstacles into angular sectors and selects the lowest-density direction.
- **Kinematic equation** of an AUV: $\dot{\eta} = J(\eta) \nu$ where $J$ is the 6×6 transformation Jacobian.
- **Kinematic control law** for trajectory tracking: $\nu_c = J^{-1}(\eta) [\dot{\eta}_d + K \tilde{\eta}]$.
- **Closed-loop error dynamics** under kinematic control: $\dot{\tilde{\eta}} = -K \tilde{\eta}$, which is **exponentially stable** for $K > 0$ (positive definite).
- **Gimbal lock** occurs at $\theta = \pm 90°$ in Euler-angle parameterization; use quaternions for full-envelope control.
- **Hybrid architectures** (global planner + local reactive loop) are the industry standard for real AUV deployments.

### High-Yield Mnemonics

- **A* = "Actual + Heuristic"** → $f = g + h$
- **VFF = "Virtual tug-of-war"** → goal pulls, obstacles push
- **RRT = "Random Reach, Test"** → sample, connect, collision-check
- **Kinematic control mantra: "Geometry, not physics"** → no $M$, no $C$, no $D$ matrices

### Critical Numerical Values to Memorize

- $\rho_{seawater} = 1025 \ kg/m^3$
- $c_{sound,seawater} \approx 1500 \ m/s$
- Typical AUV surge speed: $1 - 2 \ m/s$
- Standard sonar update rate: $5 - 10 \ Hz$
- Typical $K$ gain entries: $0.3$ to $1.5$ (dimensionally 1/s)

### Last-Minute KTU Exam Checklist

- [ ] State admissibility condition for A* heuristic explicitly.
- [ ] Always write kinematic equation in matrix form $\dot{\eta} = J(\eta)\nu$.
- [ ] Always include the Jacobian inverse and note its singularity conditions.
- [ ] For Lyapunov stability: define $V$, compute $\dot{V}$, state sign definiteness, conclude.
- [ ] Distinguish kinematic (velocity-level) from dynamic (force-level) control in every answer.
- [ ] Units: m, s, rad, m/s, rad/s, N (for dynamic, not kinematic).
- [ ] Underwater-specific: mention sensor types (sonar, DVL, IMU) at least once per answer.
- [ ] Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX for absolute value — never `\|` inside markdown tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT866 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Control** in the context of Underwater Robotics refers to the real-time regulation and trajectory tracking of an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) or Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) by actively compensating for the time-varying hydrodynamic forces, restoring moments, actuator dynamics, and environmental disturbances (currents, waves) acting on the vehicle. It is implemented through closed-loop feedback architectures that operate on the nonlinear 6 Degrees-of-Freedom (6-DOF) equations of motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Dynamic Control forms the *closing-the-loop* layer of underwater vehicle autonomy. It accepts the *kinematic* and *dynamic* models from Module 3 as the plant, and synthesizes control laws (PID, Sliding Mode, Backstepping, Adaptive) such that the vehicle follows a desired reference trajectory in the presence of **hydrodynamic uncertainties** and **external disturbances**.

The generic closed-loop dynamic control problem is formulated as:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t)) + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}(t))\mathbf{u}(t) + \mathbf{d}(t)$$

where $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the state vector, $\mathbf{u}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the control input, and $\mathbf{d}(t)$ represents bounded external disturbances.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Driving a Car in a Storm":**
> Imagine driving a car on a highway during a thunderstorm. Your eyes (sensors) constantly measure the deviation between the lane center (desired trajectory) and the car's actual position (current state). Your brain (controller) processes this error and commands the steering wheel and accelerator (actuators) to keep the car on the lane, *anticipating* the wind gusts and slippery patches. **Dynamic Control of an AUV works identically** — the controller anticipates the hydrodynamic drag, added-mass inertia, and ocean currents, then commands the thrusters in real time to maintain the desired depth, heading, and position.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **standard hydrodynamic parameters** govern the dynamic response of an underwater vehicle and are universally used in the KTU-evaluated control equations:
> - **Sea Water Density:** $\rho \approx \mathbf{1025 \; kg/m^3}$
> - **Gravitational Acceleration:** $g \approx \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
> - **Added Mass Coefficient** (typical order): $X_{\dot{u}} \approx \mathbf{0.1m}$ to $\mathbf{0.2m}$ (where $m$ is vehicle mass)
> - **Quadratic Damping Coefficient** (typical): $X_{u\mid u \vert} \approx \mathbf{10 \; kg/m}$
> - **Hydrodynamic Lift Slope:** $Z_{w} \approx \mathbf{-0.5 \rho U A}$ (for slender bodies)
> - **Standard 6-DOF State Vector:** $\boldsymbol{\eta} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^{T}$ (position/orientation in earth frame) and $\boldsymbol{\nu} = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^{T}$ (linear/angular velocities in body frame).

---

## 1.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The dynamic control loop of an AUV is a multi-layer hierarchical system:

```
[Reference Trajectory] → [Outer-Loop Guidance] → [Inner-Loop Dynamic Controller] 
        → [Thruster Allocation] → [Vehicle Plant (6-DOF Dynamics)] → [Sensors] 
        → [State Estimator (EKF/INS)] → [Error Computation] → back to Controller
```

This architecture ensures that high-level mission objectives (waypoint navigation, pipeline inspection) are converted into low-level thruster commands while the *dynamic* uncertainties are actively rejected by the inner loop.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Dynamic Control Problem

The dynamic control of an AUV is decomposed into three operational layers, each handling a specific time-scale and uncertainty class:

### Layer 1 — Kinematic Outer Loop
- Operates on the **position/orientation** vector $\boldsymbol{\eta} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^{T}$.
- Converts geometric tracking errors into desired body-frame velocities $\boldsymbol{\nu}_d$.
- Time-scale: **slow** (seconds).

### Layer 2 — Dynamic Inner Loop
- Operates on the **velocity** vector $\boldsymbol{\nu} = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^{T}$.
- Compensates for **added mass**, **damping**, **restoring (buoyancy/gravity)**, and **Coriolis** terms.
- Time-scale: **fast** (tens of milliseconds).

### Layer 3 — Actuator Allocation
- Maps the generalized force vector $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ into individual thruster commands.
- Solves a constrained optimization: minimize $\mathbf{u}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{u}$ subject to $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this hierarchy?** — Underwater vehicles are *over-actuated* (more thrusters than DOFs) and operate in a low-bandwidth acoustic-communication environment. A hierarchical control law isolates the *fast* hydrodynamic dynamics from the *slow* mission-level guidance, improving robustness and simplifying controller tuning.

## 2.2 Core Dynamic Equation Recap (Module-3 Foundation)

The full 6-DOF rigid-body dynamic model used by the controller is:

$$\underbrace{\mathbf{M}_{RB}\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}}}_{\text{Rigid-Body Inertia}} + \underbrace{\mathbf{C}_{RB}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu}}_{\text{Coriolis}} = \underbrace{\boldsymbol{\tau}}_{\text{Control}} - \underbrace{\mathbf{M}_A\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}}}_{\text{Added Mass Inertia}} - \underbrace{\mathbf{C}_A(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu}}_{\text{Hydrodynamic Coriolis}} - \underbrace{\mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu}}_{\text{Damping}} - \underbrace{\mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\eta})}_{\text{Restoring}} + \underbrace{\boldsymbol{\tau}_d}_{\text{Disturbance}}$$

Combining the inertial matrices yields the **standard control-affine form**:

$$\mathbf{M}\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}} + \mathbf{C}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\eta}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_d$$

where $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}_{RB} + \mathbf{M}_A$ and $\mathbf{C}(\boldsymbol{\nu}) = \mathbf{C}_{RB}(\boldsymbol{\nu}) + \mathbf{C}_A(\boldsymbol{\nu})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The properties $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^{T} > \mathbf{0}$ (positive definite) and $\dot{\mathbf{M}} = \mathbf{2C}(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ (skew-symmetric) are **mathematical cornerstones** exploited by Lyapunov-based controller designs. They are heavily tested in KTU University Examinations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** | **Key Equation** |
|------------|--------------|----------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| $\boldsymbol{\eta}$ | Pose vector | Earth-frame position/orientation | $m, rad$ | $[x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^{T}$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\nu}$ | Velocity vector | Body-frame linear/angular rates | $m/s, rad/s$ | $[u, v, w, p, q, r]^{T}$ |
| $\mathbf{M}$ | Inertia matrix (rigid+added) | Effective inertia of the AUV | $kg, kg\cdot m^2$ | $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}_{RB} + \mathbf{M}_A$ |
| $\mathbf{C}(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ | Coriolis-centripetal matrix | Inertial coupling terms | $kg, kg\cdot m^2/s$ | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} = \mathbf{2C}(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ (skew-sym) |
| $\mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ | Damping matrix | Viscous + quadratic drag | $kg/s, kg\cdot m^2/s$ | $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{D}_{lin} + \mathbf{D}_{quad}\vert\boldsymbol{\nu}\vert$ |
| $\mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\eta})$ | Restoring vector | Gravity - Buoyancy | $N, N\cdot m$ | $\mathbf{g} = [W-B]\mathbf{J}^{-1}$(weight & buoyancy) |
| $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Generalized force | Thruster + fin forces | $N, N\cdot m$ | $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{T} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\tau}_d$ | Disturbance | Current/wave forces | $N, N\cdot m$ | bounded: $\Vert\boldsymbol{\tau}_d\Vert \leq \bar{d}$ |
| $\mathbf{K}_p$ | Proportional gain | Position error amplification | $N/m, N\cdot m/rad$ | proportional term |
| $\mathbf{K}_d$ | Derivative gain | Velocity error amplification | $N\cdot s/m$ | derivative term |
| $\mathbf{K}_i$ | Integral gain | Steady-state error elimination | $N/(m\cdot s)$ | integral term |
| $\mathbf{S}$ | Sliding surface | Manifold for SMC design | mixed | $\mathbf{S} = \dot{\tilde{\mathbf{x}}} + \boldsymbol{\Lambda}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ | Sliding slope | Convergence rate matrix | $1/s$ | Hurwitz (eigenvalues LHP) |
| $V(\mathbf{x})$ | Lyapunov function | Energy-like positive scalar | $J$ | $V(0) = 0, V > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above formulas, **$\vert \cdot \vert$** in the damping term denotes the absolute-value operator (not determinant). Use the absolute-value carefully: the table formatting rules prohibit vertical pipes inside table cells, so in equations, prefer $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dynamic control is the **enabling technology** behind every commercial and scientific AUV mission:

- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** ROVs performing dynamic positioning (DP) near subsea manifolds where the current can exceed **1.5 m/s**. Without active DP, the ROV drifts into the structure.
- **Defense/Mine Counter-Measures (MCM):** AUVs conducting lawn-mower survey patterns with **sub-meter accuracy** using INS-Doppler-aided feedback.
- **Oceanographic Science:** Gliders using buoyancy-driven dynamic control to profile water columns to **1000 m depth** over months-long missions.
- **Search & Recovery:** Real-time heading/depth control to follow acoustic pinger bearings from black-box recorders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the **mapping** between *physical disturbance scenarios* (e.g., "ocean current at 0.8 m/s from North") and their **mathematical representation** in the $\boldsymbol{\tau}_d$ vector.
